thesaurus: <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7fdc"><Section><Exponent><EXP>about</EXP><DEF>used when saying what the subject of something is</DEF><THESEXA>She’s always talking about you.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In her novels she writes about life in South Africa.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There’s something I wanted to ask you about.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>on</EXP><DEF>about a particular subject</DEF><THESEXA>a book on English grammar </THESEXA><THESEXA>a report on poverty in rural areas</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>concerning/regarding</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>about</DEF><THESEXA>Prince Saiid answered questions concerning Kuwait’s future.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The report raises a number of questions regarding food safety.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>with regard to</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>about – used especially when you want to start talking or writing about something</DEF><THESEXA>Dear Sir, I’m writing with regard to your advertisement in The Times.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>re</EXP><DEF>used in business letters and in emails to introduce the subject that you are going to write about</DEF><THESEXA>Re: Friday’s meeting</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7fbe"><Section><Exponent><EXP>accident</EXP><DEF>an event in which a vehicle is damaged and often someone is hurt</DEF><THESEXA>Her father died in a car accident.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Hugh had an accident on his way to work.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>crash</EXP><DEF> a serious accident in which a vehicle hits something else</DEF><THESEXA>Rees-Jones was the only person to survive the crash.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a car/plane/train crash</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was killed in a plane crash.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>collision</EXP><DEF>an accident in which two or more cars, trains etc hit each other</DEF><THESEXA>His car was involved in a collision with a train.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>a head-on collision <GLOSS>between vehicles that are driving towards each other</GLOSS></THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The actor was killed in a head-on collision while driving his new sports car.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>disaster</EXP><DEF>a serious accident involving a train, plane, or boat, in which a lot of people are killed or injured</DEF><THESEXA>It was Britain’s worst air disaster.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wreck</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF> an accident in which a car or train is badly damaged</DEF><THESEXA>Ben nearly died in a car wreck.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pileup</EXP><DEF> an accident that involves several cars or trucks</DEF><THESEXA>The pileup happened in thick fog.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There was a 12-car pileup on the motorway.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fender-bender</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><EXP>prang</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> a car accident in which little damage is done</DEF><THESEXA>Atkinson was involved in a fender-bender in the hotel parking lot.</THESEXA><THESEXA>At 15, he borrowed his parents’ car and had a prang.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-3ac8af84.14195636192.-7d3c"><Section><SECHEADING>words meaning accidentally</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>accidentally/by accident</EXP><DEF>in a way that was not planned or intended</DEF><THESEXA>He accidentally shot his friend.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Fleming discovered the drug by accident, when he was researching something else.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I met the man purely by accident, and we began a conversation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>by mistake</EXP><DEF>if you do something by mistake, you intend to do one thing, but you accidentally do something else instead</DEF><THESEXA>Michelle must have picked up my keys by mistake.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Gary wandered into the wrong hotel room by mistake.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be an accident</EXP><DEF>if you say that something was an accident, you mean that you are sorry it happened, but it was not done deliberately</DEF><THESEXA>Marris told the police the killing was an accident.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Don’t blame yourself – it was an accident.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unintentionally</EXP><DEF>if you do something unintentionally, especially something bad, you do it even though you do not intend to do it</DEF><THESEXA>Teachers often unintentionally favour certain students.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Several scenes in the movie are unintentionally funny.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>inadvertently</EXP><DEF>if you inadvertently do something, you do it without realizing you are doing it, because you are not careful enough</DEF><THESEXA>In a panic, I inadvertently pushed the accelerator instead of the brake.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The Finance Minister inadvertently revealed budget secrets to reporters.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7f7f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>account</EXP><DEF>a written or spoken description that says what happens in an event or process</DEF><THESEXA>He provided the police with a detailed account of what he saw.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>description</EXP><DEF>something you say or write that gives details about what something or someone is like</DEF><THESEXA>The report gave a brief description of the problem.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>story</EXP><DEF>an account of something that has happened which may not be true – used especially when people are telling each other about something</DEF><THESEXA>I don’t think he was honest enough to tell us the full story.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her parents did not believe her story.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>report</EXP><DEF>a written or spoken account of a situation or event, giving people the information they need, especially after studying something as part of your job</DEF><THESEXA>The UN will issue a report on the incident.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Initial police reports suggest she was murdered.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>version</EXP><DEF>a particular person’s account of an event, which is different from the account that another person gives</DEF><THESEXA>Her <COLLOINEXA>version of events</COLLOINEXA> has been contradicted by other witnesses.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7f4d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>accusation</EXP><DEF>a statement saying that you believe someone is guilty of a crime or of doing something bad</DEF><THESEXA>The army has faced accusations of racism.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>allegation</EXP><DEF>a public statement accusing someone of doing something although it has not been proved</DEF><THESEXA>He denied allegations that he received a bribe.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police are investigating allegations of corruption.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>charge</EXP><DEF>an official statement by the police accusing someone of a crime and saying that they must go to court, or a statement saying that someone has done something bad, made by anyone</DEF><THESEXA>He appeared in court on a murder charge.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She denied the charge that the article had been misleading. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>indictment</EXP><REGISTERLAB>law</REGISTERLAB><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><DEF>an official written statement accusing someone of a crime so that they will be judged under the American legal system</DEF><THESEXA>He is under indictment for credit card fraud.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7f3c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>accuse</EXP><DEF>to say that you believe that someone is guilty of a crime or of doing something bad</DEF><THESEXA>Two women have been accused of kidnapping a newborn baby.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Are you accusing me of lying?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>allege</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>əˈledʒ</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to accuse someone of doing something, although this has not been proved</DEF><THESEXA>He alleged that the other man had attacked him first.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>charge</EXP><DEF>if the police charge someone, they officially tell that person that they are believed to be guilty of a crime and that they must go to court</DEF><THESEXA>She was charged with murder.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>indict</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>law</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to officially accuse someone of a crime so that they will be judged in court under the American legal system</DEF><THESEXA>He was indicted on charges of fraud.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7f2c"><HEADING>sense 2</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>action</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>something that someone does</DEF><THESEXA>He is responsible for his own actions.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They refused to give a reason for their actions.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>act</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a particular type of action</DEF><THESEXA>violent acts</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>act of violence/kindness/defiance odd.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>move</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>something that you do in order to achieve something</DEF><THESEXA>Her decision to sell the shares had been a smart move.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>step</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>one of a series of things that you do in order to deal with a problem or to succeed</DEF><THESEXA>The first step is to make sure we have got funding for the project.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We must <COLLOINEXA>take steps </COLLOINEXA>to make sure that this does not happen again.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This is an important step towards peace.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>measure</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>an official action that is intended to deal with a particular problem</DEF><THESEXA>There are increased security measures at airports.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The school was closed as a precautionary measure following a chemical leak.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gesture</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>something that you do to show how you feel about someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>Do you think it would be a nice gesture to send her some flowers?</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>gesture of goodwill/solidarity/defiance</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The company gave us £100 as a gesture of goodwill.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>deed</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>especially literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an action, especially one that is very good or very day</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>exploits</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>plural</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>exciting or brave actions</DEF><THESEXA>daring skill, courage, or strength to do it</DEF><THESEXA>Completing a marathon is a remarkable feat for a six-year-old.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The bridge is a great feat of engineering.</THESEXA><THESEXA /></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7f06"><Section><Exponent><EXP>actor</EXP><DEF>someone who performs in a play or film</DEF><THESEXA>Her son wants to be an actor.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Both my parents are actors.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>actress</EXP><DEF>a woman who performs in a play or film. Many women prefer to be called <DEFBOLD>actors</DEFBOLD> rather than <DEFBOLD>actresses</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Who was the actress who played Jane Eyre?</THESEXA><THESEXA>She’s one of my favourite actresses.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>star</EXP><DEF>a famous actor</DEF><THESEXA>Julia Roberts is a famous Hollywood star.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The hotel is popular with movie stars.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the star</EXP><DEF>someone who plays the most important part in a play or film</DEF><THESEXA>Daniel Radcliffe is the star of the ‘Harry Potter’ films.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>co-star</EXP><DEF>one of two or more famous actors who have important parts in a play or film</DEF><THESEXA>Her co-star Jodie Foster won the Best Actress Award.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the lead</EXP><DEF>the main acting part in a play or film</DEF><THESEXA>He will <COLLOINEXA>play the lead</COLLOINEXA> in a new version of ‘Dracula’.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>extra</EXP><DEF>an actor in a film who does not say anything but is part of a crowd</DEF><THESEXA>She started her career as an extra in TV soap operas.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>understudy</EXP><DEF>an actor who learns a part in a play so that they can act the part if the usual actor is ill</DEF><THESEXA>Vanessa had flu, and her place was taken by her understudy, Miss Lisa Fennell.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>a group of actors</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>the cast</EXP><DEF>all the actors in a play or film</DEF><THESEXA>Other members of the cast include Johnny Depp and Danny DeVito.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She will head the cast <GLOSS>she will have the most important part</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>company</EXP><DEF>a group of actors who perform plays together</DEF><THESEXA>In 2006, he joined the Royal Shakespeare Company.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The play will be performed by an all-female company.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7f05"><Section><Exponent><EXP>admire</EXP><DEF>to like someone because they have achieved something special, or they have skills or qualities that you would like to have</DEF><THESEXA>I admire your courage.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She admired him for the way he dealt with the situation. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>respect</EXP><DEF>to have a good opinion of someone, even if you do not agree with them, for example because they have achieved a lot or have high standards</DEF><THESEXA>She is respected by all her colleagues at the university.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She’s an actor who is not prepared to compromise, and her audience loves and respects her for that.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>revere</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>rɪˈvɪə</PRON><AMEVARPRON>-ˈvɪr</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to greatly admire someone because of their achievements and personal qualities, especially someone famous</DEF><THESEXA>Mandela is revered as one of the great leaders of our time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>look up to sb</EXP><DEF>to admire someone who is older or who has more experience than you</DEF><THESEXA>All the young comedians look up to him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>think highly of sb</EXP><DEF>to think that someone is good at what they do</DEF><THESEXA>His teachers seem to think very highly of him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>idolize</EXP><DEF>to admire someone so much that you think they are perfect – used especially about famous people or people in your family</DEF><THESEXA>He idolized his brother.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Jane grew up idolizing Princess Diana.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hero-worship</EXP><DEF>to admire someone a lot and want to be like them – often used when this seems unreasonable or extreme</DEF><THESEXA>She hero-worshipped John to such an extent that she was blind to his faults.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7ef7"><Section><SECHEADING>admit something is true</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>admit</EXP><DEF>to agree unwillingly that something is true</DEF><THESEXA>He admitted that the company was having financial difficulties.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>I must admit</COLLOINEXA> I was disappointed by their reaction.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>concede</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to admit something in a discussion or argument</DEF><THESEXA>‘You may be right,’ Bridget conceded.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a decision which he now concedes was incorrect.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>acknowledge</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>əkˈnɒlɪdʒ</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to say that something is true or that a situation exists</DEF><THESEXA>The report acknowledges that research on animals is not always a reliable guide when it comes to humans.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They do not want to acknowledge the fact that things have changed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>confess</EXP><DEF>to admit something that you feel embarrassed or ashamed about</DEF><THESEXA>Bradley confessed that he struggled to finish the race.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>I must confess</COLLOINEXA> I don’t like his wife at all.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>Granted/I grant you</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when admitting that something is true, although you do not think it makes much difference to the main point. <DEFBOLD>Granted</DEFBOLD> is usually used at the beginning of a sentence, or on its own</DEF><THESEXA>She has a lot of experience, I grant you, but she’s not good at managing people.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Granted he did play well in the last game, but generally his form hasn't been very good recently. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>admit you have done something wrong</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>admit</EXP><DEF>to say that you have done something wrong, especially something criminal</DEF><THESEXA>He admitted charges of theft and false accounting.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Bennett admitted killing his wife.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>confess</EXP><DEF>to tell the police or someone in authority that you have done something bad, especially when they have persuaded you to do this</DEF><THESEXA>He finally confessed that he had stolen the money. </THESEXA><THESEXA>They told him that if he confessed he would get a lighter sentence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>own up</EXP><DEF>to admit that you have done something wrong, usually something that is not very serious. <DEFBOLD>Own up</DEFBOLD> is more informal than <DEFBOLD>admit</DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD>confess</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>He owned up to the mistake straight away.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fess up</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to admit that you have done something wrong that is not very serious</DEF><THESEXA>Come on, fess up! Where were you last night? </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come clean</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to finally admit something bad that you have been trying to hide</DEF><THESEXA>They want the government to come clean on where all the money has gone.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7eeb"><Section><Exponent><EXP>advanced</EXP><DEF>using very modern technology and ideas</DEF><THESEXA>technologically advanced nations</THESEXA><THESEXA>Their equipment isn’t as advanced as ours.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sophisticated</EXP><DEF>very advanced, and working in a better but often more complicated way than other things</DEF><THESEXA>highly sophisticated weapons</THESEXA><THESEXA>As machines become more sophisticated, they become more likely to break down.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>high-tech</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˌhaɪ ˈtek◂</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>using very advanced technology, especially electronic equipment and computers</DEF><THESEXA>high-tech industries in Silicon Valley</THESEXA><THESEXA>High-tech listening equipment was used to find survivors in the rubble.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>state-of-the-art</EXP><DEF> using the newest and most advanced features, ideas, and materials that are available</DEF><THESEXA>The football club has invested £40 million in state-of-the-art training facilities.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The sound system is state-of-the-art.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cutting-edge</EXP><DEF>cutting-edge technology or research is the most advanced that there is at this time</DEF><THESEXA>The system uses cutting-edge technology to identify and eliminate viruses. </THESEXA><THESEXA>cutting-edge medical research</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7ed9"><HEADING>Sense 2</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>advantage</EXP><DEF>a good feature that something has, which makes it better, more useful etc than other things</DEF><THESEXA>The great advantage of digital cameras is that there is no film to process.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>benefit</EXP><DEF>a feature of something that has a good effect on people’s lives</DEF><THESEXA>Regular exercise has many benefits, including reducing the risk of heart disease.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>merit</EXP><DEF>a good feature that something has, which you consider when you are deciding whether it is the best choice</DEF><THESEXA>The committee will consider the merits of the proposals.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The merits and demerits of<GLOSS>the good and bad features of</GLOSS>alternative funding systems were widely discussed in the newspapers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The chairman saw no great merit in this suggestion<GLOSS>he did not think that it was a good idea</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>virtue</EXP><DEF>an advantage that makes you believe that something is a good thing</DEF><THESEXA>They believed in the virtues of culture, civilization, and reason. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s always <COLLOINEXA>extolling the virtues </COLLOINEXA>of hard work<GLOSS>saying that hard work is a good thing</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the good/great/best thing about sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when mentioning a good feature of something. This phrase is rather informal and you should not use it in formal essays</DEF><THESEXA>The good thing about cycling is that you don’t have to worry about getting stuck in a traffic jam.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the beauty of sth is that</EXP><DEF>used when you want to emphasize that something has a very good or useful feature</DEF><THESEXA>The beauty of the plan is that it is so simple.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7ea7"><Section><Exponent><EXP>advertise</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I,T</GRAM><DEF>to tell people about a product or service and try to persuade them to buy it, for example in a newspaper, television, or Internet advertisement</DEF><THESEXA>Some universities advertise on television.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She has signed a deal to advertise the company's haircare products.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>promote</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to try to increase the sales or popularity of a product or event, for example by selling it at a lower price or talking about it on television</DEF><THESEXA>He's in London to promote his new album.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>market</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to try to sell a product or service by deciding which type of people are likely to buy it and by making it interesting to them</DEF><THESEXA>The collection is being marketed as clothing for climbers and skiers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Most companies have agreed not to market products to children under 12.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>publicize</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><ORTHVAR>publicise</ORTHVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to tell the public about something by writing about it in newspapers, speaking about it on television etc</DEF><THESEXA>He had done a lot of interviews to publicize his new book.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The hostages' case has been widely publicized.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hype</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to try to make people think something is good or important by advertising or talking about it a lot on television, the radio etc. <DEFBOLD>Hype</DEFBOLD> is often used when you do not trust the information</DEF><THESEXA>The boxing match was being hyped as the biggest fight of the decade.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>plug</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to advertise a book, film etc by talking about it on television or radio</DEF><THESEXA>Marc was on the show to plug his new play.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7e95"><Section><Exponent><EXP>advertisement</EXP><THESEXA>an <COLLOINEXA>advertisement for</COLLOINEXA> shampoo</THESEXA><THESEXA>They placed an advertisement in the newspaper.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ad</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an advertisement</DEF><THESEXA>She’s been in several <COLLOINEXA>TV <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-58387a81.11836165248.-7fa3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>after</EXP><POS>prep</POS><DEF>after something happens, or after a period of time has passed. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7e5c"><Section><SECHEADING>to say yes</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>agree</EXP><DEF>to say that you think that someone’s plan or suggestion <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7e25"><Section><Exponent><EXP>aim</EXP><DEF>something you hope to achieve by doing something</DEF><THESEXA>The main aim of the plan was to provide employment for local people.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>goal</EXP><DEF>something important that you hope to achieve in the future, even though it may take a long time</DEF><THESEXA>The country can still achieve its goal of reducing poverty by a third.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>target</EXP><DEF>a particular amount or total that you want to achieve</DEF><THESEXA>The company is on track to meet its target of increasing profits by 10%.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>objective</EXP><DEF>the specific thing that you are trying to achieve – used especially about things that have been officially discussed and agreed upon in business, politics etc</DEF><THESEXA>Their main objective is to halt the flow of drugs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We met to set the business objectives for the coming year.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ambition</EXP><DEF>something that you very much want to achieve in your future career</DEF><THESEXA>Her ambition was to go to law school and become an attorney.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Earlier this year, he achieved his ambition of competing in the Olympic Games.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7dbd"><Section><Exponent><EXP>allow</EXP><DEF>to say that someone can do something – used about parents, teachers, or people in <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7d62"><Section><Exponent><EXP>always</EXP><DEF>all the time, at all times, or every time</DEF><THESEXA>I will always love you.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He always carries his medicine wherever he goes. </THESEXA><THESEXA>People will always need houses. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>forever</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>for ever</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>if something lasts or continues forever, it remains or continues for all future time</DEF><THESEXA>Nothing lasts forever.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He seemed to think he would live forever.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>permanently</EXP><DEF>always, or for a very long time – used about changes that you expect to last forever. <DEFBOLD>Permanently </DEFBOLD> can be used with a verb or with an adjective</DEF><THESEXA>His eyesight may be permanently damaged.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They decided to move to Portugal permanently.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>for life</EXP><DEF>for the rest of your life</DEF><THESEXA>Marriage is supposed to be for life.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was sent to jail for life.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Remarks like that can affect someone for life.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>for good</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>forever – used to talk about a permanent change</DEF><THESEXA>This time, he’s coming back for good.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Once a species dies out, it is gone for good.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>for all time</EXP><DEF>forever – used when saying that something will last or be remembered forever because it is very good or special</DEF><THESEXA> Their deeds will be remembered for all time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>to/until your dying day</EXP><DEF>for the rest of your life – used when something has affected you very deeply</DEF><THESEXA>I’ll remember what he said to my dying day.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7d4c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>amount</EXP><DEF>how much of something there is</DEF><THESEXA>Try to reduce the amount of fat in your diet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a tiny amount of poison</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>quantity</EXP><DEF>a particular amount of food, liquid, or another substance that can be measured – used especially in written descriptions and instructions</DEF><THESEXA>Make sure that you add the right quantity of milk.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They buy the wood in large quantities.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>volume</EXP><DEF>the amount of something such as business activity or traffic, especially when this is large or increasing</DEF><THESEXA>The volume of traffic on our roads has risen sharply.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the huge volume of trade with China</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>level</EXP><DEF>the exact amount of something at one time, which can go up or down at other times</DEF><THESEXA>They measured the level of alcohol in his blood.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is a high level of unemployment.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>proportion</EXP><DEF>the amount of something, compared with the whole amount that exists</DEF><THESEXA>the proportion of road accidents caused by drunk drivers</THESEXA><THESEXA>A high proportion of the students were from poor families.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>quota</EXP><DEF>a maximum amount of something that can be produced, sold, brought into a country etc</DEF><THESEXA>import quotas on Japanese cars</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>yield</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>jiːld</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>the amount of something that is produced, especially crops</DEF><THESEXA>this year’s cotton yield</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7d0c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>anger</EXP><DEF>a strong emotion that you feel because someone has behaved badly or because a situation <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7cb1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>animal</EXP><DEF>a living thing such as a dog, cow, or tiger, but not an insect, fish, or bird</DEF><THESEXA>farm animals such as cows, sheep, and pigs</THESEXA><THESEXA>People are becoming more concerned about animal welfare.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>creature </EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈkriːtʃə</PRON><AMEVARPRON>-ər</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>an animal – used especially when describing a particular animal’s characteristics or when referring to animals generally</DEF><THESEXA>The cheetah is a magnificent creature.</THESEXA><THESEXA>all the living creatures in the sea</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>beast</EXP><DEF>an animal, especially a large or dangerous one</DEF><THESEXA>To most people, lions are savage beasts.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a wild beast</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>living thing</EXP><DEF>an animal considered as one of a group of things that grow and then die, including humans and plants</DEF><THESEXA>The only living things we saw were dogs and cats.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>species</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈspiːʃiːz</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>a group of animals whose members are similar and can breed together to produce young animals</DEF><THESEXA>Seven species of birds of prey have been observed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mammal</EXP><DEF>an animal that drinks milk from its mother’s body when it is young</DEF><THESEXA>Humans, dogs, and whales are all mammals.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>vertebrate</EXP><DEF>a living creature that has a backbone</DEF><THESEXA>viruses that affect chickens, monkeys, and most other vertebrates</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>invertebrate</EXP><DEF>a living creature that does not have a backbone</DEF><THESEXA>earthworms and other small invertebrates</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7c97"><Section><Exponent><EXP>answer</EXP><DEF>something you say when someone asks you a question or speaks to you</DEF><THESEXA>I asked if he wanted to come, but I didn’t get an answer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If you’re asking me for money, the answer’s no!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reply</EXP><DEF>an answer – used especially in written English to report what someone said</DEF><THESEXA>She asked how he felt, and received the reply, ‘Awful!’</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Finished now?’ ‘No,' came the reply.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>response</EXP><DEF>an answer that clearly shows your reaction to a question, suggestion etc</DEF><THESEXA>Wagner’s responses showed that he had thought carefully about the issues.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Sure. Why not?’ was his response to most of Billie’s suggestions.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>retort</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an angry answer given when someone has annoyed you or criticized you</DEF><THESEXA>Isabelle began an angry retort and then stopped herself.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>comeback</EXP><DEF>a quick answer that is clever, funny, or rude</DEF><THESEXA>He walked out before I could think of a snappy comeback.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>riposte</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a quick and clever answer</DEF><THESEXA>Anna produced the perfect riposte.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rejoinder</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a quick answer, especially a clever or rude one</DEF><THESEXA>If he confronts them, he’ll run the risk of a sharp rejoinder.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7c6f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>answer</EXP><DEF>to say something to someone when they have asked you a question or spoken to you</DEF><THESEXA>You don’t have to answer the question if you don’t want to.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I said hello to her, but she didn’t answer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reply</EXP><DEF>to answer someone – used especially in written English to report what someone said</DEF><THESEXA>‘I’m so sorry,’ he replied.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We asked Jane to help, but she replied that she was too busy.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>respond</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to answer someone</DEF><THESEXA>How do you respond to the allegation that you deliberately deceived your employers?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give sb an answer</EXP><DEF>to answer someone by telling them what you have decided or giving them a piece of information they asked for</DEF><THESEXA>He asked me to marry him, but I didn’t give him an answer straight away.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get back to sb</EXP><DEF>to answer someone at a later time especially by telephoning them, usually because you need to think about their question or find out more information</DEF><THESEXA>She’s promised to get back to me as soon as she hears any more news from the hospital.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>retort</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to answer someone angrily, especially because they have annoyed you or criticized you</DEF><THESEXA>‘You’re not afraid?’ Brenda asked. ‘Of course not,’ he retorted angrily.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.1180d18c85a.-60b4"><Section><Exponent><EXP>appear</EXP><DEF>to start to be seen, especially suddenly</DEF><THESEXA>A few small white clouds appeared on the horizon.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pop up</EXP><DEF>to appear very suddenly</DEF><THESEXA>A woman’s face popped up from the other side of the fence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>become visible</EXP><DEF>to gradually start to be able to be seen</DEF><THESEXA>The shape of the baby’s head gradually became visible on the screen.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come into view</EXP><DEF>if something comes into view, you start to see it as you move closer to it, or it gets closer to you</DEF><THESEXA>The white cliffs of Dover came into view.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come out</EXP><DEF>if the sun, moon, or the stars come out, they suddenly appear</DEF><THESEXA>The sun came out from behind a cloud. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>loom/loom up</EXP><DEF>if a large frightening object or person looms, they appear suddenly</DEF><THESEXA>The dark shape of the castle loomed up out of the mist.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The man’s face suddenly loomed over him. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>resurface</EXP><DEF>to appear again after being lost or missing</DEF><THESEXA>The girl’s father has resurfaced after six years of no contact.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7c3b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>appearance</EXP><DEF>the way someone or something looks to other people</DEF><THESEXA>She’d had plastic surgery to change her appearance.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The mushrooms are similar in appearance to poisonous ones, so you have to be careful.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>looks</EXP><DEF>someone’s appearance, especially when considering how good-looking they are. <DEFBOLD>Looks</DEFBOLD> is more informal than <DEFBOLD>appearance</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Girls of that age are always worried about their looks.</THESEXA><THESEXA>People generally describe him as having boyish <COLLOINEXA>good looks</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>image</EXP><DEF>the appearance and character that a person, organization, product etc deliberately tries to produce</DEF><THESEXA>She wowed fans with her sleek new image.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The scandal has damaged her image as an honest politician.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The car has a racy new image.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>aspect</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the appearance of someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>The costume gives his character an even more sinister aspect.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7c02"><HEADING>Sense 1</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>approve</EXP><DEF>to officially accept a plan, proposal etc</DEF><THESEXA>The Medical Research Council has approved the use of a new drug for breast cancer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pass</EXP><DEF> to approve a law or proposal, especially by voting</DEF><THESEXA>Many anti-smoking laws have been passed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ratify</EXP><DEF> to make a written agreement official by signing it</DEF><THESEXA>The treaty was ratified by the Senate in 1988.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rubber-stamp</EXP><DEF>to approve something without really thinking about it – used to show disapproval</DEF><THESEXA>Parliament merely rubber-stamped the president’s decisions.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-3ac8af84.14195636192.-7c7d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>approximately</EXP><DEF>more or less than a number or amount – used especially in technical or scientific argueto speak angrily to someone because you disagree with them about somethingThose two are always arguing.We rarely argue with each other.have an argumentto argue with someone for a period of time about a particular thingShe had a long argument with the man who was selling the tickets.have a rowraʊBrEhave a fightespecially AmEto have an argument with someone, especially with your boyfriend, girlfriend, or a member of your familyShe was upset because she’d had a fight with her boyfriend.The couple at the next table were having a row. quarrelespecially BrEto argue with someone, especially for a long time and about many different thingsThe children quarrel all the time.squabbleˈskwɒbəlˈskwɑː-to argue about unimportant thingsThe kids were squabbling over what to watch on TV.fall out with sbBrEto have a big argument with someone that results in you stopping having a friendly relationship with themI’ve fallen out with my best friend.be at each other’s throatsif two people are at each other’s throats, they are always arguing in a very angry wayHis parents are constantly at each other’s throats.

Sense 1
argumenta situation in which people speak angrily to each other because they disagree about somethingan argument between two drivers over who had right of wayA 29-year-old man was shot and killed today after an argument over a gambling debt.rowraʊBrEfightespecially AmEa loud angry argument with someone, especially your boyfriend, girlfriend, or someone in your family. Row is also used about a serious disagreement between politicians about important public issuesThere were always fights between my parents.the continuing row over tax increasesA few months ago they had a big row, and Steve drove off and spent the weekend in London.disagreementa situation in which people disagree with each other, but without shouting or getting angryThere were the occasional disagreements about money, but mostly we got on well.Ginny had left the company after a disagreement with her boss.quarrelespecially BrEan argument, especially one in which people get angry and that lasts a long time. Quarrel sounds more formal and more serious than argument or rowa bitter family quarrelfeudfjuːda very bitter argument between two groups, especially families, which lasts for many years and causes people to hate each otherThe feud between the Hatfields and the McCoys raged for 20 years.disputea public or legal argument about something, especially one which continues for a long timeMorris has been involved in a long legal dispute with his publisher.The settlement will resolve a long-running dispute over the country’s nuclear program.war/battle of wordsan argument in which two people or groups criticize each other continuously in publicThe war of words over construction delays at the airport has erupted again.bust-upBrEinformala very bad argument, especially one in which people decide to separate from each otherHe had a bust-up with the team manager.shouting matchan angry argument in which people shout at each otherHe got into a shouting match with another driver.slanging matchBrEinformalan argument in which people insult each otherHe was sacked after a slanging match with a colleague.
an argument that is not very serioussquabbleˈskwɒbəlˈskwɑː-an argument about something that is not importantThere were the usual squabbles between brothers and sisters.Voters are tired of petty squabbles between party leaders.tiffinformalan argument that is not very serious, between people who are in loveGary had a bit of a tiff with his girlfriend.misunderstandinga slight argument – a rather formal word which is often used humorouslyThere was a slight misunderstanding over the bill, but everything’s been sorted out now.skirmisha short argument, especially between politicians or sports opponentsEvans and O'Brien had several political skirmishes.

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7b19"><Section><Exponent><EXP>arrive</EXP><DEF> to get to the place you are going to</DEF><THESEXA>I arrived at the party at around 7 home.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reach</EXP><DEF>to arrive somewhere, especially after a long journey</DEF><THESEXA>When we finally reached the port, we were all very tired. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come</EXP><DEF>if someone comes, they arrive at the place where you are</DEF><THESEXA>She came home yesterday.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What time did the plumber say he’d planes taking off and landing.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7afa"><Section><SECHEADING>types of art</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>modern art</EXP><DEF>art from the late 19th century until now</DEF><THESEXA>I’m not very keen on modern art.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>contemporary art</EXP><DEF>art that is being created now</DEF><THESEXA>an exhibition of contemporary art</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>Western art</EXP><DEF>art in Europe and North America</DEF><THESEXA>Velasquez was one of the greatest figures in the history of Western art.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fine art</EXP><DEF>art, especially painting, which is made to be beautiful or affect your emotions, rather than to be useful</DEF><THESEXA>She wants to study fine art at college.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the Royal Academy of Fine Arts</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>abstract art</EXP><DEF>paintings or images that consist of shapes or lines which do not look like people, places, or objects</DEF><THESEXA>it’s almost impossible for any artist not to be influenced by Picasso’s abstract art.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>figurative art</EXP><DEF>art that shows pictures of people, places, or objects</DEF><THESEXA>In this country, we always feel more comfortable with figurative art. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conceptual art</EXP><DEF>a type of modern art, which shows ideas about the world</DEF><THESEXA>In conceptual art, the idea behind the work of art holds as much importance as the artwork itself.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>applied art</EXP><DEF>the use of artistic principles in the design of objects and images for people to use. This includes subjects such as fashion, jewellery design, architecture, and photography</DEF><THESEXA> Van de Velde taught at the new school of applied art.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>primitive art</EXP><DEF>art by people who live in societies where there is a very simple way of life and no modern technology or industry</DEF><THESEXA>The interest in primitive art came about largely through the work of Gauguin.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pop art</EXP><DEF>art that shows ordinary objects that you find in people’s homes, which was made popular in the 1960s by artists such as Andy Warhol</DEF><THESEXA>The relationship of advertising to art was part of the pop art phenomenon in the 1960s.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the visual arts</EXP><DEF>painting, sculpture, and other art forms that you look at, not literature or music</DEF><THESEXA>The approach to the visual arts has changed in the last two decades.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the decorative arts</EXP><DEF>the design and production of beautiful things for the home, such as furniture, pottery, and cloth</DEF><THESEXA>The exhibition will also cover the decorative arts.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the performing arts</EXP><DEF>dance, music, or drama, done to entertain people</DEF><THESEXA>San Diego’s School for the Creative and Performing Arts </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the plastic arts</EXP><DEF>art that involves producing objects such as sculptures or pots</DEF><THESEXA>the decline of the plastic arts in the fifth century</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>performance art</EXP><DEF>a type of art that can combine acting, dance, and other actions to express an idea</DEF><THESEXA>It will be a mixture of live performance art, music and poetry in an informal club setting.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7ae5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>article</EXP><DEF>a piece of writing in a newspaper or magazine about a particular subject</DEF><THESEXA>Greg began his career writing articles for the college magazine.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an interesting newspaper article</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>story</EXP><DEF>an article in a newspaper about a recent event, especially one that a lot of people find interesting or exciting</DEF><THESEXA>a front-page story</THESEXA><THESEXA>I read a newspaper story about the crash.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The local paper <COLLOINEXA>ran a story</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>published a story</GLOSS> about the festival.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>piece</EXP><DEF>a short article in a newspaper or magazine</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve written a couple of pieces for the New York Times magazine.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The piece was first published in the Observer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>feature</EXP><DEF>a special article about a particular subject, often with photographs and continuing for several pages</DEF><THESEXA>They had a special feature on Croatia.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The paper did a big feature on growing your own food. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>review</EXP><DEF>an article in a newspaper or magazine about a book, play, product, hotel etc, giving someone’s opinion of it</DEF><THESEXA>The play had <COLLOINEXA>rave reviews</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>people liked it a lot - an informal use</GLOSS>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Did you see the review of the new Coldplay album?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>column</EXP><DEF>an article by a particular writer that appears regularly in a newspaper or magazine</DEF><THESEXA>He has a weekly column in the Times.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She writes a newspaper column on gardening.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP> editorial</EXP><DEF>a piece of writing in a newspaper that gives the personal opinion of the editor about something that is in the news</DEF><THESEXA>Several years ago the New York Times published an editorial stating that the appropriate minimum wage is $0.00.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cutting</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>an article that has been cut from a newspaper or magazine</DEF><THESEXA>His mother has kept all his old press cuttings.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7ace"><Section><Exponent><EXP>artificial</EXP><DEF>not real or natural, but made to look or work like something real or natural</DEF><THESEXA>artificial flowers</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was given an artificial heart.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>synthetic</EXP><DEF>made using chemical processes rather than by natural processes</DEF><THESEXA>synthetic fabrics</THESEXA><THESEXA>a synthetic version of the drug</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>man-made</EXP><DEF>made by people – used especially about geographical features and materials</DEF><THESEXA>a man-made lake</THESEXA><THESEXA>man-made fibres</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fake</EXP><DEF>made to look or seem like something else, especially in order to deceive people</DEF><THESEXA>a fake passport</THESEXA><THESEXA>fake fur</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>imitation</EXP><DEF>made to look like something, but not real – used especially about guns, jewellery, and leather</DEF><THESEXA>imitation firearms</THESEXA><THESEXA>imitation pearls</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>false</EXP><DEF>artificial – used especially about teeth, <NonDV><REFHWD>eyelash</REFHWD><SUFFIX>es</SUFFIX></NonDV>, and <NonDV><REFHWD>beard</REFHWD><SUFFIX>s</SUFFIX></NonDV></DEF><THESEXA>a set of false teeth</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>simulated</EXP><DEF>not real, but made to look, sound, or feel real, especially by means of special computers or machines</DEF><THESEXA>a simulated space flight</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>virtual</EXP><DEF>made, done, seen etc on a computer, rather than in the real world</DEF><THESEXA>a virtual tour of the garden</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7aa9"><Section><Exponent><EXP>artist</EXP><DEF>someone who produces art, especially paintings or drawings</DEF><THESEXA>an exhibition of paintings by 50 contemporary artists</THESEXA><THESEXA>a wildlife artist who specializes in drawing birds</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>painter</EXP><DEF>someone who paints pictures</DEF><THESEXA>the Dutch painter, Johannes Vermeer</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sisley was principally a landscape painter.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>photographer</EXP><DEF>someone who takes photographs, as an art or as a job</DEF><THESEXA>Cartier-Bresson was one of the greatest photographers of all time.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a fashion photographer</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>potter</EXP><DEF>someone who makes pots or other objects from clay</DEF><THESEXA>In Spain, local potters produce ceramics with beautiful brightly-coloured designs. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sculptor</EXP><DEF>someone who makes <NonDV><REFHWD>sculpture</REFHWD><SUFFIX>s</SUFFIX></NonDV><GLOSS>a work of art that is made from stone, metal, or wood</GLOSS></DEF><THESEXA>The museum has several pieces by the great English sculptor, Sir Henry Moore.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>performance artist</EXP><DEF>someone who uses acting, dance and other actions to create art that expresses an idea</DEF><THESEXA>a one-man show by the French performance artist, Orlan</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7a96"><Section><Exponent><EXP>ashamed</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>feeling very sorry and embarrassed because of something you have done, or someone connected with you has done</DEF><THESEXA>You should be ashamed of yourself.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She felt deeply ashamed of her son’s behaviour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>humiliated</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>very ashamed and upset, because someone has made you look weak or stupid, especially in front of other people</DEF><THESEXA>I came out of the class feeling humiliated.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mortified</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈmɔːtɪfaɪd</PRON><AMEVARPRON>ˈmɔːr-</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>extremely ashamed and embarrassed, especially about something you have done accidentally</DEF><THESEXA>She’ll be mortified when she realizes her mistake.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a mortified expression</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shamefaced</EXP><DEF>showing by the expression on your face that you are ashamed about something</DEF><THESEXA>Paul came into my office looking shamefaced and apologized for what he had done.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>feel guilty</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also </LINKWORD><LEXVAR>feel bad</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>to feel worried and unhappy because you know that you have done something wrong. <DEFBOLD>Feel bad </DEFBOLD> is more informal than <DEFBOLD>feel guilty</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>He felt guilty about lying to his parents.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I felt bad about letting the team down.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lose face</EXP><DEF>to lose people’s respect for you, especially by doing something that makes you look weak or stupid in front of other people</DEF><THESEXA>He feels he’ll lose face if he admits to his staff that he was wrong.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7a49"><Section><Exponent><EXP>astrology</EXP><DEF>the study of the positions and movements of the stars and how they might influence people and events</DEF><THESEXA>Do you believe in astrology?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The science of astrology has existed for thousands of years.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>horoscope</EXP><DEF>a description of people’s characters and the things that will happen to them, based on the position of the stars and planets at the time of their birth – especially a description that is printed in a newspaper or magazine</DEF><THESEXA>I sometimes read my horoscope in the daily paper.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My horoscope says that I should be careful about investing money.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the signs of the zodiac</EXP><DEF>the twelve signs that you can belong to according to your birth date and the position of the stars then, which some people believe influence our characters and our lives</DEF><THESEXA>The carving shows the twelve signs of the zodiac.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>star sign/sign</EXP><DEF>one of the 12 signs of the <NonDV><REFHWD>zodiac</REFHWD></NonDV></DEF><THESEXA>What sign are you?</THESEXA><THESEXA>My favourite star sign is Aquarius.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>Aries</EXP><DEF>the first sign of the zodiac, which is represented by a <NonDV><REFHWD>ram</REFHWD></NonDV>. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7a13"><Section><Exponent><EXP>attack</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>an act of using weapons against an enemy in a counterattack.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>onslaught</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a large violent attack by an army</DEF><THESEXA>In 1544 there was a <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7a01"><Section><SECHEADING>to attack a person</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>attack</EXP><DEF>to use violence against someone and try to such as a street</DEF><THESEXA>He was mugged on his way home from school.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stab</EXP><DEF>to attack someone with a knife</DEF><THESEXA>The victim had been stabbed in the neck.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assault</EXP><DEF>to attack and hurt someone – used especially when talking about this as a criminal <NonDV><REFHWD>offence</REFHWD></NonDV></DEF><THESEXA>He assaulted a flight attendant who refused to serve him more drinks.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be set upon by sb/sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be attacked by a group of people</DEF><THESEXA>He died outside his home after being set upon by a gang of a place</DEF><THESEXA>The village was attacked by enemy warplanes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We will attack at dawn.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>invade</EXP><DEF>to enter a country and try to get control of it using force</DEF><THESEXA>The Romans invaded Britain 2,000 years ago.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>storm</EXP><DEF>to suddenly attack a city or building that is well defended by getting inside it and taking control</DEF><THESEXA>Elite troops stormed the building and rescued the hostages.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>besiege</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>bɪˈsiːdʒ</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to surround a city or building with soldiers in order to stop the people inside from getting <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-79b3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>pay attention</EXP><DEF>to carefully listen to, look at, or think about something or someone</DEF><THESEXA>She told the class to stop talking and pay attention.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In any relationship, it helps to pay attention to each other’s needs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>concentrate</EXP><DEF>to think carefully about something that you are doing and not let other things disturb your thoughts</DEF><THESEXA>I tried to read a few pages, but I was too upset to concentrate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Children need their own quiet space where they can concentrate on their homework.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>keep your mind on sth</EXP><DEF>to continue to pay attention to something, especially when you are very excited or worried about something else</DEF><THESEXA>On Friday, the day of the party, I had a dreadful time keeping my mind on my work.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Good football players keep their minds on the game at all times.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>keep your eyes on sth</EXP><DEF>to keep watching something carefully, so that you do not make any mistakes</DEF><THESEXA>The first rule of driving is to keep your eyes on the road. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Keep your eyes on the ball – now swing!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be engrossed in sth</EXP><DEF>to be paying so much attention to a book, a conversation, some work etc, that you do not notice what is happening around you</DEF><THESEXA>Helen was sitting up in bed, engrossed in a novel.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was so engrossed in what he was doing he didn’t even hear the doorbell ring.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be absorbed in sth</EXP><DEF>to be giving all your attention to something, especially something you are reading, watching, or thinking about</DEF><THESEXA>The little boy sat at the edge of the pond, totally absorbed in watching the fish.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was a quiet man who often seemed to be absorbed in his own thoughts.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Matilda was curled up in an armchair, absorbed in a book.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give sth/sb your undivided attention</EXP><DEF>to give all your attention to something or someone and not do anything else – used especially when this is something you want or need to do</DEF><THESEXA>Come round when the kids are in bed so I can give you my undivided attention.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Once I’ve finished my exams, I’ll be able to give my undivided attention to looking for a job.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7985"><HEADING>Sense 3</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>attraction</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a feature or quality that makes people like, want, or feel interested in something</DEF><THESEXA>The excitement is part of the attraction of the job. </THESEXA><THESEXA>For many mothers, the attraction of childcare in the workplace is the chance to be near their children.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The idea of living in another country does have a certain attraction.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>appeal</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a quality that makes people like something or someone</DEF><THESEXA>Much of Corfu’s appeal lies in its lively night life.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t understand the appeal of this kind of music.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the lure of sth</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>singular</GRAM><DEF>a very attractive quality that makes you want something very much – often used about something that has a bad effect or influence</DEF><THESEXA>It’s hard to resist the lure of credit cards and easy money.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The lure of urban life is especially powerful for the young.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>charm</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a pleasant quality that someone or something has that makes people like them, feel attracted to them, or be influenced by them</DEF><THESEXA>He was physically attractive and possessed considerable personal charm.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The book captures Savannah’s old Southern charm perfectly.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>glamour</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>the attractive and exciting quality that is connected with wealth and success</DEF><THESEXA>the glamour of Monte Carlo</THESEXA><THESEXA>Hollywood glamour</THESEXA><THESEXA>The world of modelling is famous for its glitz and glamour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>draw</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>singular</GRAM><DEF>something that makes people want to do something or go somewhere</DEF><THESEXA> The money is a big draw for many players.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The island’s main draw is its wonderful beaches.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-794a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>available</EXP><DEF>if something is available, you can buy it, get it, or use it</DEF><THESEXA>Are there period.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>vacant</EXP><DEF>if a house or room is vacant, it is available for someone to use or rent</DEF><THESEXA>If you’re looking for somewhere to rent, I think there’s a vacant apartment in my building.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ll ask around and see if there’s a room vacant somewhere.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The sign on the toilet said ‘vacant’. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spare</EXP><DEF>a spare room, key, tyre etc is one that you have in addition to the ones that you normally use, and is therefore available to be used</DEF><THESEXA>We have a spare room you can stay in.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There’s a spare key in this drawer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I got the spare tyre out of the back of the car.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>empty</EXP><DEF>an empty room, building etc has no one in it</DEF><THESEXA>The house was empty for two months before it was sold.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They have three empty rooms now that the kids have moved out.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>on offer</EXP><DEF>if something is on offer, it is available for people to choose from</DEF><THESEXA>There is a huge network of cycle tracks on offer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Other facilities on offer in this excellent hotel include a hairdressing salon, a coffee shop, and a children's play room. </THESEXA><THESEXA>the variety of delicious fruits on offer</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>taken</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>if a seat or room is taken, it is not available for other people to use, because someone has already arranged to use it</DEF><THESEXA>I’m sorry – that seat is taken.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m afraid all our rooms are taken at the moment.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7913"><Section><SECHEADING>to avoid a person or place</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>avoid</EXP><DEF>to deliberately not go near a person or place</DEF><THESEXA>Why have you been avoiding me?</THESEXA><THESEXA>We avoided Park Street because of the traffic.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stay away/keep away</EXP><DEF>to avoid a person or place, especially a dangerous one</DEF><THESEXA>She walked along the path, keeping well away from the edge of the cliff.</THESEXA><THESEXA>That man is trouble – I’d stay away from him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>steer clear of sb/sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to avoid a person or place, because there could be problems if you do not</DEF><THESEXA>I travelling around it instead of through the centre</DEF><THESEXA>We had to make a long detour because of the floods.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to avoid doing something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>avoid</EXP><DEF>to find a way of not doing something that you should do</DEF><THESEXA>Some people will do anything to avoid work.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get out of sth</EXP><DEF>to avoid doing something you should do or something you promised to do. <DEFBOLD>Get out of </DEFBOLD> is more common than <DEFBOLD>avoid</DEFBOLD> in everyday spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>We promised we’d go – we can’t get out of it now.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wriggle out of sth</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>wiggle out of sth</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to avoid doing something you should do by making up excuses – used to show legally or morally you should do – used especially about taxes or responsibilities</DEF><THESEXA>It is an offence to evade taxes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The US cannot evade its responsibility for the war.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-78cf"><Section><Exponent><EXP>baby</EXP><DEF>a very young child who has not yet learned to speak or walk</DEF><THESEXA>I sat next to a woman holding a baby.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>toddler</EXP><DEF>a baby who has learned how to walk</DEF><THESEXA>The playground has a special area for toddlers.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>infant</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a baby or a very young child</DEF><THESEXA>The disease is mainly found in infants.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>little one</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a baby, or a small child up to the age of about three</DEF><THESEXA>How old is your little one?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>young</EXP><DEF>the babies of an animal or bird</DEF><THESEXA>Kangaroos carry their young in a pouch.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>litter</EXP><DEF>a group of baby animals that are born at the same time to the same mother</DEF><THESEXA>Our cat has just had a litter of six kittens.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7883"><Section><Exponent><EXP>bad-tempered</EXP><DEF>becoming easily annoyed and talking in an angry unfriendly way to people</DEF><THESEXA>Her husband was a disagreeable, bad-tempered man.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was wondering why she had been so bad-tempered recently.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>moody</EXP><DEF>often becoming annoyed or unhappy, especially when there seems to be no good reason</DEF><THESEXA>a moody teenager</THESEXA><THESEXA>Myra can be a bit moody sometimes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>irritable</EXP><DEF>easily annoyed about small things, especially because you are tired, upset, or worried</DEF><THESEXA>I hadn’t had much sleep and was feeling tired and irritable.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Since Steve quit smoking, he’s been really irritable.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You’re turning into an irritable old man.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grumpy/grouchy</EXP><DEF>easily annoyed and often complaining about things – used especially when talking about people you know well. These words sound much gentler and less critical than <DEFBOLD>bad-tempered</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>There’s no need to be so grumpy!</THESEXA><THESEXA>He woke up in a grouchy mood.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be in a bad mood</EXP><DEF>to be feeling annoyed or unhappy, so that you do not speak in a normal friendly way to people – used especially when this only lasts for a fairly short period of time and is not part of someone’s usual character</DEF><THESEXA>Leave him alone – he’s in a bad mood today.</THESEXA><THESEXA>By the time I got home at 9 o'clock, I was in a really bad mood.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have/have got a short fuse</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be likely to become angry very suddenly, especially in an unreasonable way</DEF><THESEXA>Be careful what you say to the Colonel - he’s got a very short fuse.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cantankerous</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF> bad-tempered and complaining or quarrelling a lot – used especially about old people</DEF><THESEXA> The hotel owner was a cantankerous old man.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In later years, she became quite cantankerous.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7854"><Section><Exponent><EXP>bankrupt</EXP><DEF>without any money and unable to pay your debts – used about a person or business that has to officially admit this and stop trading permanently</DEF><THESEXA>Many small businesses will go bankrupt unless interest rates fall.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was declared bankrupt in the High Court yesterday.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be in financial difficulties</EXP><DEF>to have difficulty paying your bills and debts, often so that you are in danger of becoming bankrupt – used about people and businesses</DEF><THESEXA>The firm has been in financial difficulties for some time.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was in financial difficulties and knew that he would have to sell his home.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go bust/go under</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to become bankrupt and have to stop operating – used about a business</DEF><THESEXA>The supermarket isn’t there anymore – it went bust ages ago.</THESEXA><THESEXA>During the last recession, dozens of businesses were going under every day.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>insolvent</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>bankrupt – used about people and businesses</DEF><THESEXA>The bank was declared insolvent.</THESEXA><THESEXA> Directors of insolvent companies often ignore demands for payment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Individuals becoming insolvent also jumped 9% to 9,470 in the third quarter.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-781f"><Section><SECHEADING /><Exponent><EXP>bar</EXP><DEF>a place where people go to buy and drink alcoholic drinks</DEF><THESEXA>A man tavern.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>watering hole</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a bar, pub etc where people drink alcohol – often used humorously. A <DEFBOLD>watering hole</DEFBOLD> is also the name for a place where wild animals go to drink</DEF><THESEXA>The bar became a popular watering hole for journalists.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What’s your favorite watering hole?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dive</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a bar, club etc that is cheap and dirty</DEF><THESEXA>The place is a bit of dive.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>honky-tonk</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a cheap bar where country music is played</DEF><THESEXA>They played in every honky-tonk in Tennessee.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>saloon</EXP><DEF>a bar in the western United States. Also used in Britain about the part of a pub which has comfortable chairs where you can sit and relax</DEF><THESEXA>I felt like a cowboy walking into a saloon in the Wild West.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you want to stay in the saloon, or would rather go into the other bar?</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>people who work in a bar</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>barman</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>a man who serves drinks in a bar</DEF><THESEXA>A big Irish barman pulled me a pint of beer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>barmaid</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a woman who serves drinks in a bar</DEF><THESEXA>I was working in the evenings as a barmaid.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bartender</EXP><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><DEF>someone who makes, pours, and serves drinks in a bar or restaurant</DEF><THESEXA>The bartender gave him his change.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bar staff</EXP><DEF>the people serving drinks or food in a bar or pub</DEF><THESEXA>The local pub is advertising for bar staff.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>landlord</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a man who owns or manages a pub</DEF><THESEXA>He became violent and the landlord asked him to leave.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-77fa"><Section><Exponent><EXP>basic</EXP><DEF>the basic things or information are the ones that are the most necessary, or the ones that you need to know first</DEF><THESEXA>Visit our website for some basic facts about healthy eating, exercise and weight control.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Water – indeed everything basic to life here must be brought in by truck.</THESEXA><THESEXA>people's basic needs</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fundamental</EXP><DEF>more important than anything else – used especially when you want to emphasize what you are saying</DEF><THESEXA>The fundamental problem is a lack of resources.</THESEXA><THESEXA>one of the fundamental beliefs of Christianity</THESEXA><THESEXA>These values are fundamental to our society.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>essential</EXP><DEF>used when talking about a particular thing or feature, which something must have in order to continue. Also used when talking about the main point, difference etc</DEF><THESEXA>Freedom of the press is essential to our democracy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Change and diversity are essential characteristics of the natural world.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The essential point is this: either we act now, or the whole future of our planet is uncertain. </THESEXA><THESEXA>One of the essential differences between humans and computers is that humans are more likely to make mistakes. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>central</EXP><DEF>very important and having a lot of influence or receiving a lot of attention</DEF><THESEXA>Indonesia occupies a central role in this market.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Education is central to government policy.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-77ec"><Section><Exponent><EXP>basis</EXP><DEF>the facts, ideas, things etc from which something can be developed</DEF><THESEXA>His work will be used as a basis for future research.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>foundation</EXP><DEF>the thing on which something is based, especially something important that continues for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>Their ideas were the foundation for the political system that exists in the UK today.</THESEXA><THESEXA>How can we provide a solid foundation for world peace?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bedrock</EXP><DEF>the most important thing that something depends on in order to be successful</DEF><THESEXA>Honesty is the bedrock of any healthy relationship.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Labour’s traditional bedrock of support is among the working classes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cornerstone</EXP><DEF>the most important thing that something depends on in order to be successful, especially in business and politics</DEF><THESEXA>Confidence is the cornerstone of our business. </THESEXA><THESEXA>NATO remains a cornerstone of defence policy for Europe.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-77d0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>beat</EXP><DEF>to get more points, votes etc than someone. <DEFBOLD>Beat </DEFBOLD> is used especially in spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>We should have beaten them easily.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I always beat my brother at tennis.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>defeat</EXP><DEF>to beat someone. <DEFBOLD>Defeat</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>beat</DEFBOLD> and is used especially in writing</DEF><THESEXA>England were defeated by 2 goals to 1.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Bush defeated Kerry in the election.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trounce</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>traʊns</PRON></PronCodes><DEF> to defeat someone completely in a game</DEF><THESEXA>They were trounced 20–0 by Kuwait.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>thrash</EXP><GEO>BrE </GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><Variant><LEXVAR>cream</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF> to beat someone very easily in a game</DEF><THESEXA>Of course, they totally creamed the other team.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I hope we thrash them!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wipe the floor with sb</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to beat someone completely in a game or argument</DEF><THESEXA>She wiped the floor with her opponent in the debate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They won a £1,000 prize after wiping the floor with the opposition in a bowling competition.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-4a9c" fullpage="yes"><Section><SECHEADING>person</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>beautiful</EXP><DEF>a beautiful woman or child has perfect day!</THESEXA><THESEXA>a lovely voice</THESEXA><THESEXA>a lovely perfume </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pretty</EXP><DEF>pleasant to look at – often used about things that are not big or impressive</DEF><THESEXA>a pretty little pink dress</THESEXA><THESEXA>a pretty village</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>attractive</EXP><DEF>pleasant to look at. <DEFBOLD>Attractive</DEFBOLD> sounds
