<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-77a2"><Section><Exponent><EXP>beauty</EXP><DEF>the quality of being very attractive to look at – used about people, objects, and places</DEF><THESEXA>She was greatly admired for her beauty and intelligence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the natural beauty of the landscape.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The Ancient Greeks created statues of outstanding beauty and dignity.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sb’s good looks</EXP><DEF>someone’s attractive appearance, especially their face and features</DEF><THESEXA>With his dark good looks, Jason could have been a film star.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>glamour</EXP><DEF>an exciting quality which makes someone or something seem attractive and connected with wealth and success</DEF><THESEXA>Men loved her for her beauty and her glamour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Add a black lace top for a touch of glamour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>aesthetic</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>connected with beauty and the study of beauty</DEF><THESEXA>The aesthetic value of their work is easy to appreciate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the declining aesthetic standards of our civilization</THESEXA><THESEXA>The aesthetic qualities in a Buddhist sculpture are similar to those in any other work of art.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7790"><Section><Exponent><EXP>because</EXP><POS>conjunction</POS><DEF>used when giving the reason for something</DEF><THESEXA>I went home because I was tired.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The streets were flooded because of all the rain.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>due to/owing to</EXP><POS>prep</POS><DEF>used to give the reason why something has happened. <DEFBOLD>Due to</DEFBOLD> and <DEFBOLD>owing to</DEFBOLD> are more formal than <DEFBOLD>because</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The delay was due to a problem with the ship’s engines.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The parade had to be cancelled owing to bad weather.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>through</EXP><POS>prep</POS><DEF>because of something. <DEFBOLD>Through </DEFBOLD> is used especially when saying why someone succeeded or failed to do something</DEF><THESEXA>They won the game, more through luck than skill.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You failed that test through carelessness.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>thanks to</EXP><POS>prep</POS><DEF>used when explaining that something good has happened because of someone’s efforts, or because something exists</DEF><THESEXA>Thanks to modern medicine, the disease can now be cured.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>since/as</EXP><POS>conjunction</POS><DEF>used when giving the reason why someone decides to do something or decides that something is true</DEF><THESEXA>We decided to go to the beach since it was a nice day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I thought Kevin was out as his car wasn’t there.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>out of</EXP><POS>prep</POS><DEF>because of a particular feeling or quality</DEF><THESEXA>He started reading the book out of curiosity.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I only asked out of politeness.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-775a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>become</EXP><DEF>to start to have a feeling or quality, or to start to develop into something</DEF><THESEXA>His parents became worried when he was late home from school.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Violent crime is becoming a major problem.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She became more confident when she joined a drama group.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get</EXP><DEF>to start to have a feeling or quality. <DEFBOLD>Get </DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>become</DEFBOLD> and is much more commonly used in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>I was getting very tired.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Make sure you’re home before it gets dark.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go</EXP><DEF>to become something – used about things becoming a different colour, or things or people becoming worse in some way</DEF><THESEXA>Her hair was going grey.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He went blind.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I think I’m going mad.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>turn</EXP><DEF>to become something – used about things becoming a different colour, or the weather or people’s behaviour changing</DEF><THESEXA>The water had turned bright green.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The weather is expected to turn cold again.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He suddenly turned nasty.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grow</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to gradually become something</DEF><THESEXA>People were growing impatient.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The rich grew richer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come undone/loose/apart</EXP><DEF>to become undone, loose, or separated into pieces</DEF><THESEXA>The screws had come loose.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>change into sth</EXP><DEF>to become something completely different</DEF><THESEXA>The caterpillar changed into a beautiful butterfly.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7741"><Section><Exponent><EXP>beer</EXP><DEF>a general word for an alcoholic drink made from <NonDV><REFHWD>malt</REFHWD></NonDV> and <NonDV><REFHWD>hops</REFHWD></NonDV></DEF><THESEXA>a bottle of beer</THESEXA><THESEXA>Let’s go and have a beer in the pub.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lager</EXP><DEF>a light-coloured beer, which often has a lot of bubbles in it</DEF><THESEXA>A pint of lager, please.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ale</EXP><DEF>a type of beer which is usually sold in a bottle or a can</DEF><THESEXA>Beers and fine ales were brewed here for over a hundred years.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Newcastle Brown Ale</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>draught beer</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>draft beer</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>beer that is served from a large container rather than a bottle, especially good quality beer</DEF><THESEXA>The pub serves a range of draught beers.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>real ale</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>beer that has been made in the, traditional way, not in a large factory</DEF><THESEXA>The pub has real ale and live music most nights.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bitter</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a type of dark strong beer that is popular in Britain</DEF><THESEXA>I'll have a pint of bitter.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shandy</EXP><DEF>a drink made of beer mixed with lemonade</DEF><THESEXA>I'd better have a shandy - I'm driving.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cider</EXP><DEF>an alcoholic drink made from apples</DEF><THESEXA>His clothes were old and dirty, and he stank of cider.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7723"><Section><Exponent><EXP>before</EXP><DEF>earlier than something or someone</DEF><THESEXA>She could read before she started school.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you want to have a shower before me?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>prior to</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> before something happens</DEF><THESEXA>Please arrive at the airport two hours prior to departure.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>by</EXP><DEF>no later than a particular time or date</DEF><THESEXA>The children are usually in bed by 9 o'clock.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We have to be out of our hotel room by 10 o'clock.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>earlier</EXP><DEF> before now, or before the time you are talking about</DEF><THESEXA>I saw her earlier.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I had sent the letter earlier in the week.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>previously</EXP><DEF>before now, or before a time in the past</DEF><THESEXA>He previously worked as an electrician.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The show starred a previously unknown actress called Jennifer Aniston.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in advance</EXP><DEF>before something happens – used especially when talking about the arrangements for something</DEF><THESEXA>Let me know in advance if you are going to be late.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The landlord wants three months’ rent in advance.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>beforehand</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>ahead of time</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>before something happens, especially so that you are ready</DEF><THESEXA>I wish we had known about all this beforehand.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s always best to decide beforehand how much you want to pay. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7715"><Section><Exponent><EXP>beginning</EXP><DEF>the first part of something such as a story, event, or period of time</DEF><THESEXA>The beginning of the movie is very violent.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Let’s go back to the beginning.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>start</EXP><DEF>the beginning of something, or the way something begins</DEF><THESEXA>Tomorrow marks the start of the presidential election campaign.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was not a good start to the day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The runners lined up for the start of the race.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>commencement</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the beginning of something – used especially in official contexts</DEF><THESEXA>the commencement of the academic year</THESEXA><THESEXA>the commencement of the contract</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>origin</EXP><DEF>the point from which something starts to exist</DEF><THESEXA>He wrote a book about the origins of the universe.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The tradition has its origins in medieval times.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the onset of sth</EXP><DEF>the time when something bad begins, such as illness, old age, or cold weather</DEF><THESEXA>the onset of winter</THESEXA><THESEXA>An active lifestyle can delay the onset of many diseases common to aging.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dawn</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the beginning of an important period of time in history</DEF><THESEXA>People have worshipped gods since the dawn of civilization.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>birth</EXP><DEF>the beginning of something important that will change many people’s lives</DEF><THESEXA>the birth of democracy in South Africa </THESEXA><THESEXA>the birth of the environmental movement </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-76dd"><Section><Exponent><EXP>behave</EXP><DEF>to do and say things that are good, bad, normal, strange etc</DEF><THESEXA>His teacher said he’d been behaving badly at school.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m not going to talk to her until she starts behaving reasonably.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Oh, be quiet! You’re behaving like a two-year-old.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>act</EXP><DEF>to behave in a particular way, especially in a way that seems unusual, surprising, or annoying to other people</DEF><THESEXA>Tina’s been acting very strangely lately.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What makes grown people act like that?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>treat</EXP><DEF>to behave towards someone or deal with someone in a particular way</DEF><THESEXA>She said that he’d treated her really badly throughout their two-year marriage.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m sick of my parents treating me like a child.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conform</EXP><DEF>to behave in the way that most other people in your group or society behave</DEF><THESEXA>Young people sometimes want to rebel and therefore they refuse to conform.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Society typically brings pressure on individuals and groups to conform to civilised norms.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conduct yourself</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to behave in a particular way, especially in a situation where people will notice and judge the way you behave</DEF><THESEXA>Public figures have a duty to conduct themselves responsibly, even in their private lives.</THESEXA><THESEXA>By the end of the course, you should be able to conduct yourself with confidence in any meeting.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-2d3e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>behaviour</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>behavior</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>the way someone behaves</DEF><THESEXA>Chemicals added to food may be responsible for children’s bad behaviour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Tatsuya apologized for his behaviour towards me.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conduct</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the way someone behaves in public or in their job – used mainly in official or legal contexts</DEF><THESEXA>Bates was arrested and charged with <COLLOINEXA>disorderly conduct</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>noisy and violent</GLOSS>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The committee found him guilty of unsatisfactory <COLLOINEXA>professional conduct</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>manner</EXP><DEF>the way someone behaves when they are talking to or dealing with other people, which is shown in their expression, their voice etc</DEF><THESEXA>She had a pleasant friendly manner.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Suddenly his whole manner changed, and he started shouting.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His initial manner towards her had been rather formal.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>demeanour</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>demeanor</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the way someone looks and behaves, which shows you something about their character or feelings</DEF><THESEXA>She maintained a calm demeanour at all times.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Wenger’s demeanour suggested that he was not happy with the situation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>antics</EXP><DEF>someone’s behaviour – used when you think it is silly, funny, strange, or annoying</DEF><THESEXA>The drunken antics of some English football fans has brought shame on the country.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The actor is becoming known for his increasingly bizarre off-screen antics.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-76ad"><Section><Exponent><EXP>behind</EXP><DEF>at or towards the back of something, and often hidden by it</DEF><THESEXA>The sun went behind a cloud.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I got stuck behind a truck on the way to the airport.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>at the back</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>in (the) back</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>behind something, especially a building</DEF><THESEXA>There’s a small garden at the back of the cottage.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Their house has a pool in the back.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>at/to the rear</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>behind something, especially a building. <DEFBOLD>At/to the rear</DEFBOLD> sounds rather formal and is used especially in written descriptions</DEF><THESEXA>They parked in a small car park at the rear of the hotel.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is a small seating area to the rear.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in the background</EXP><DEF>if someone or something is in the background, it is behind the place where the main activity is happening</DEF><THESEXA>If you look carefully at the painting, you will see several men sitting at a table in the background.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7688"><Section><SECHEADING>to believe something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>believe</EXP><DEF>to be sure that something is true or that <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-766f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>bent</EXP><DEF>no longer straight</DEF><THESEXA>The knife was bent in the middle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a bent nail</THESEXA><THESEXA> She kept her head bent so that Josie couldn’t see her face. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>twisted</EXP><DEF>having a bent shape that turns around – used especially when something has been damaged or is old and has lost its natural straight shape</DEF><THESEXA>a twisted tree trunk</THESEXA><THESEXA>All that was left was a pile of twisted metal.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His knee was all twisted.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a twisted ankle</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>curved</EXP><DEF>bent in the shape of part of a circle, especially naturally or because something has been made that way</DEF><THESEXA>The bird has a long curved bill.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a curved dagger </THESEXA><THESEXA>The plane’s wings are curved.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>warped</EXP><DEF>bent or twisted into the wrong shape because of heat or dryness</DEF><THESEXA>warped floorboards</THESEXA><THESEXA>The windows are warped and won’t open anymore.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>crooked</EXP><DEF>not straight, but bending sharply in one or more places, especially in a way that looks different from normal or does not look right – often used about a part of someone’s body, a path, or a picture</DEF><THESEXA>He had crooked teeth.</THESEXA><THESEXA>her crooked nose and long thin face </THESEXA><THESEXA>The boys at school used to make fun of me because I have a crooked spine. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The picture looks crooked - <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-765a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>better</EXP><DEF>the <NonDV><REFHWD>comparative</REFHWD></NonDV> of good</DEF><THESEXA>She wants a better <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-762c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>big</EXP><THESEXA>a big city</THESEXA><THESEXA>a big guy</THESEXA><THESEXA>a big <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7607"><Section><Exponent><EXP>bill</EXP><DEF>a piece of paper that tells you how much you must pay</DEF><THESEXA>Many families are struggling to pay their bills.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a credit card bill</THESEXA><THESEXA>We got a huge phone bill.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I asked the waiter to bring me the bill.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>check</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF> a bill that tells you how much you must pay in a restaurant</DEF><THESEXA>Can I have the check, please?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>invoice</EXP><DEF>a document that lists the goods that a company has sent, or the services they have provided, and tells you how much you must pay. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-75f8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>bite</EXP><DEF>to use your teeth to cut, crush, or chew something</DEF><THESEXA>The dog bit me!</THESEXA><THESEXA>I sometimes bite my fingernails when I’m nervous.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He bit into the apple.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>chew</EXP><DEF>to keep biting something that is in your mouth</DEF><THESEXA>Helen was chewing a piece of gum.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was chewing on a cigar.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gnaw</EXP><DEF>if an animal gnaws something, it bites it repeatedly</DEF><THESEXA>The dog was in the yard gnawing on a bone.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nip sb/give sb a nip</EXP><DEF>to give someone or something a small sharp bite</DEF><THESEXA>When I took the hamster out of his cage, he nipped me.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nibble</EXP><DEF>to take a lot of small bites from something</DEF><THESEXA>A fish nibbled at the bait.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She sat at her desk, nibbling her sandwich. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sink your teeth into sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to bite someone or something with a lot of force, so that your teeth go right into them</DEF><THESEXA>The dog sank its teeth into my leg.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He sank his teeth into the steak.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>chomp on sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to bite something and chew it in a noisy way</DEF><THESEXA>The donkey was chomping on a carrot.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was chomping away on big slice of toast.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sting</EXP><DEF>if an insect stings you, it makes a very small hole in your skin. You use <DEFBOLD>sting</DEFBOLD> about bees, wasps, and scorpions, and <DEFBOLD>bite</DEFBOLD> about mosquitoes, ants, spiders, and snakes</DEF><THESEXA>She stepped on a wasps’ nest and must have been stung at least 20 times.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-75d3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>bitter</EXP><DEF>having a strong sharp taste that is not sweet, like black coffee without sugar – used especially about chocolate, medicine etc</DEF><THESEXA>The dessert is made with a slightly bitter chocolate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Hops give beer its distinctive bitter taste.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The medicine tasted bitter.</THESEXA><THESEXA>As the lettuce gets older, the leaves become more bitter.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sharp</EXP><DEF>having a taste that makes your tongue sting slightly</DEF><THESEXA>Rhubarb has quite a sharp taste.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The cheese has a pleasing colour and a pleasantly sharp flavour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sour</EXP><DEF>having a usually unpleasant sharp acid taste, like the taste of a lemon, or a fruit that is not ready to be eaten – used especially about fruit, or about liquids that have gone bad</DEF><THESEXA>Some people say that the purpose of the lemon’s sour taste is to stop the fruit being eaten by animals. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Rachel sampled the wine. It was sour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>acidic</EXP><DEF>very sour – used especially about liquids or things made with fruits such as oranges, lemons, or grapes</DEF><THESEXA>Some fruit juices taste a bit acidic.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tangy</EXP><DEF>having a taste that is pleasantly strong or sharp, and that often tastes a little sweet as well</DEF><THESEXA>The ribs are cooked in a tangy barbecue sauce.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tart</EXP><DEF>having a taste that lacks sweetness – used especially about fruit such as apples, which you need to add sugar to</DEF><THESEXA>The pudding had rather a tart flavour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The trees were covered with tart wild plums.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-75b6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>blame</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to say or think that someone or something is responsible for something bad that has happened</DEF><THESEXA>Democrats have blamed Republicans for the failure to reach an agreement.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police blamed bad weather for a series of accidents on the roads.</THESEXA><THESEXA>For many years I blamed myself for her death.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They blamed the failure of the business on the economic downturn.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put/place/lay the blame on sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to say who or what you think is responsible for something bad that has happened, often unfairly or wrongly</DEF><THESEXA>Don’t try to put the blame on me!</THESEXA><THESEXA>Subsequent investigations placed the blame squarely on city officials.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Farmers have laid the blame for their problems entirely on EU policies.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>say it’s sb’s fault</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to say that someone is responsible for something bad that has happened</DEF><THESEXA>Are you saying it’s my fault that we lost the game?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hold sb responsible</EXP><DEF>to say that someone is responsible for something bad that has happened, because it was their duty to prevent it from happening</DEF><THESEXA>He was held personally responsible for the failure of the project.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take the rap</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>carry the can</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>to be blamed and punished for something that you did not do, or that someone else is also responsible for</DEF><THESEXA>He expects his wife to take the rap for him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Alan’s colleagues decided to let him carry the can.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-759c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>boast</EXP><DEF>to talk too proudly about your abilities, achievements, or possessions because you want other people to admire you</DEF><THESEXA>She’s always boasting about how good she is at languages.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>brag</EXP><DEF>to boast in a way that annoys other people. <DEFBOLD>Brag</DEFBOLD> is more informal than <DEFBOLD>boast</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>He was bragging about how many girlfriends he had had.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t think they have anything to brag about.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The rebels have repeatedly bragged that their fighters have been responsible for the mounting attacks on policemen, 226 of whom were killed last year. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blow your own trumpet</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>blow your own horn</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to talk a lot about your achievements – used especially when you want to mention your achievements but do not want to sound as if you are boasting</DEF><THESEXA>I don’t want to blow my own trumpet, but it was me who came up with the idea for the project in the first place.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>crow</EXP><DEF>to boast about something you have achieved, when other people have been less lucky or successful</DEF><THESEXA>Nordstrom and his supporters are still crowing about winning the lawsuit.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gloat</EXP><DEF>to behave in a way that shows that you are proud of your own success and happy about someone else’s failure</DEF><THESEXA>The Australians are still gloating over their victory over England.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The liberals are gloating and celebrating all over town. </THESEXA><THESEXA>I haven’t come to gloat! We all have to lose sometimes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be full of yourself</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to show by your words and behaviour that you are very proud of your abilities and achievements – used when you dislike someone because of this</DEF><THESEXA>‘He’s so full of himself,’ Constance complained. ‘He thinks he can get away with anything.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>After the game she was really full of herself.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7578"><Section><SECHEADING>types of boat</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>yacht</EXP><DEF>a large boat with a sail, used for pleasure or sport</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sailing boat</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>sailboat</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a boat that uses one or more sails</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rowing boat</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>rowboat</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a small boat that you move through the water with oars</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dinghy</EXP><DEF>a small open boat used for pleasure, or for taking people between a ship and the shore</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>catamaran</EXP><DEF>a sailing boat with two separate <NonDV><REFHWD>hull</REFHWD><SUFFIX>s</SUFFIX></NonDV><GLOSS>the part that goes in the water</GLOSS></DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trimaran</EXP><DEF>a sailing boat with three separate <NonDV><REFHWD>hulls</REFHWD><SUFFIX>s</SUFFIX></NonDV></DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>barge</EXP><DEF>a large low boat with a flat bottom, used for carrying heavy goods on a canal or river</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>canal boat</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>narrow boat</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a boat that you use on canals</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>canoe</EXP><DEF>a long light boat that is open at the top and pointed at both ends, which you move along using a <NonDV><REFHWD>paddle</REFHWD></NonDV>. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-74fe"><Section><Exponent><EXP>border</EXP><DEF>the official line that separates two countries, or the area close to this line</DEF><THESEXA>The town lies on the border between Chile and Argentina.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Strasbourg is very close to the German border.</THESEXA><THESEXA>border guards</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>frontier</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>the border</DEF><THESEXA>They crossed the Libyan frontier into Egypt.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>line</EXP><DEF>the official line that separates states and counties in the US</DEF><THESEXA>His family lived across the state line in West Virginia.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>boundary</EXP><DEF>the line that marks the edge of an area of land that someone owns, or one of the parts of a country</DEF><THESEXA>The fence marks the boundary between the two properties.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The Mississippi River forms the boundary between Tennessee and Arkansas.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A road runs along the western boundary of the site.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-74de"><Section><Exponent><EXP>bored</EXP><DEF>feeling that you are not interested in something or that you have nothing interesting to do</DEF><THESEXA>Julia soon got bored with lying on the beach.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I'm bored. Can we go home now?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fed up </EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>feeling very bored and annoyed or unhappy – used especially when something has continued for too long, and you do not want it to continue any longer</DEF><THESEXA>He got fed up with his old job and decided to start looking for a new one.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m fed up with listening to you complain!</THESEXA><THESEXA>You sound a bit fed up. Is everything alright?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be tired of sb/sth</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>be sick of sb/sth</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to feel very annoyed and bored with something that has continued for too long. <DEFBOLD>Be sick of sb/sth</DEFBOLD> sounds stronger and more annoyed than <DEFBOLD>be tired of sb/sth</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>People are tired of hearing politicians make promises that they never keep.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do it yourself – I’m sick of cleaning up after you!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have had enough</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be so bored with something that has continued for a long time that you decide to leave, do something different, or change the situation</DEF><THESEXA>She put up with him for ten years before she finally decided that she had had enough.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I've had enough of all this moaning! Can we try and be more positive? </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

boringnot interesting in any waya boring speechHe found school incredibly boring.not very interestingnot before nounvery ordinary and therefore rather boring. People often use this phrase in everyday English, instead of saying directly that something is boringThe story wasn’t very interesting.dullespecially writtenboringThe conference was usually a dull affair.Life was never dull.tediousˈtiːdiəsvery boring and continuing for a long timeThe process was tedious and slow.Jake began the tedious task of sorting through his papers.monotonousməˈnɒtənəsməˈnɑː-boring and always the sameThe work was monotonous and unchallenging.He was only half listening to the monotonous voice of the teacher.mundanemʌnˈdeɪnrather boring, because it is connected with things you do regularly as part of your daily lifeHe busied himself with the mundane task of cleaning the house.Most arguments are over mundane issues like spending or saving money.humdrumˈhʌmdrʌmusually before nounboring because nothing new or interesting ever happensHe wanted to escape his humdrum life.a humdrum existencedrya subject, piece of writing etc that is dry is boring because it is very serious and does not contain any humourThe students complained that the lecture was dry and uninspiring.a dry academic volume

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-73dc"><Section><Exponent><EXP>breathe</EXP><GRAM>I,T</GRAM><DEF>to take air into your lungs and send it out again</DEF><THESEXA>Mary knew he was asleep because he was breathing deeply.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He breathed the clear spring air.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sigh</EXP><DEF>to breathe out slowly and make a noise that shows you are disappointed, tired, <NonDV><REFHWD>relieved</REFHWD></NonDV> etc</DEF><THESEXA>She just sighed and shook her head.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Never mind,’ he sighed. ‘I’m sure there’ll be other opportunities.’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pant</EXP><DEF>to breathe quickly and noisily through your mouth, because you have been using a lot of effort</DEF><THESEXA>They were panting by the time they got to the finish line.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gasp</EXP><DEF>to breathe very quickly and deeply and with difficulty</DEF><THESEXA>Her eyes were wide, and she was gasping for breath.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Do you think you can walk?’ ‘I’ll try to,’ he gasped.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>snore</EXP><DEF>to breathe noisily while you are sleeping</DEF><THESEXA>I couldn’t sleep because my husband was snoring.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wheeze</EXP><DEF>to breathe with difficulty, making a noise in your throat and chest, because you are ill</DEF><THESEXA>His asthma was making him wheeze.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be out of breath</EXP><DEF>to be unable to breathe easily, especially because you have been running or doing other exercise</DEF><THESEXA>I was out of breath by the time we reached the top of the hill.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be short of breath</EXP><DEF>to be unable to breathe easily, especially because you are unhealthy</DEF><THESEXA>Because she was so overweight, she was often short of breath.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-73ce"><Section><SECHEADING>a bright light</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>bright</EXP><DEF>shining strongly</DEF><THESEXA>The sunshine was very bright.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the bright light of the torch</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>strong</EXP><DEF>very bright</DEF><THESEXA>Photographs fade if they are exposed to strong sunlight.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>brilliant</EXP><DEF>extremely bright, especially in a way that seems good</DEF><THESEXA>The garden was full of brilliant autumn sunshine.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dazzling</EXP><DEF>a dazzling light is so bright that it hurts your eyes and you cannot look at it</DEF><THESEXA>dazzling headlights</THESEXA><THESEXA>the dazzling reflection from the mirror</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blinding</EXP><DEF>a blinding light is very bright and makes you unable to see for a short time after you have looked at it</DEF><THESEXA>There was a sudden blinding light, followed by the sound of a huge explosion.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>harsh</EXP><DEF>a harsh light is bright and unpleasant</DEF><THESEXA>In the harsh light of the kitchen, she looked older than she was.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>good</EXP><DEF>if the light is good in a place where you are working, it is bright enough for you to see what you are doing</DEF><THESEXA>The light wasn’t good enough to read.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>a bright place</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>bright</EXP><DEF>full of light</DEF><THESEXA>I wish the room was a bit brighter.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>light</EXP><DEF>bright because there are big windows</DEF><THESEXA>The dining room was light and airy.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>well-lit</EXP><DEF>bright because there are plenty of electric lights</DEF><THESEXA>Try to park in a well-lit place.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-73b9"><Section><Exponent><EXP>bring</EXP><DEF>to take something or someone to the place where you are now, or the place where you are going</DEF><THESEXA>Have you brought your ticket with you?</THESEXA><THESEXA>He asked his father if he could bring a friend to stay.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take</EXP><DEF>to move something to another place, or help someone go to another place</DEF><THESEXA>I took a book with me to read on the train.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was taken to hospital by ambulance.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>fetch</LEXVAR><GEO>especially BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to go to another place and come back with something or someone</DEF><THESEXA>I went upstairs to get my jacket.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Joseph told me to fetch the doctor, so I ran to the village.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.6735c3fd.11b5f44f24f.-3b26"><Section><Exponent><EXP>broken</EXP><DEF>if something is broken, it does not work properly</DEF><THESEXA>I think my watch is broken.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The camera got broken when we were on holiday.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The birds had gotten into the cabin through a broken window.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In the corner of the room were a broken chair and a rickety old desk.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>out of order</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>if a machine, especially one used by the public, is out of order, it is not working for a temporary period</DEF><THESEXA>Every phone I tried was out of order.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The toilets were all out of order.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>down</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>if a computer system is down, it is not working</DEF><THESEXA>The computer system was down all afternoon, so we went home.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>go down<GLOSS>stop working</GLOSS></THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The network went down at 11:00 and we lost the whole morning’s work.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>on the blink</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>on the fritz</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if a piece of electrical equipment is on the blink, it sometimes works and sometimes does not</DEF><THESEXA>My TV’s on the blink again.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>go on the blink/fritz</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The car’s air conditioning went on the fritz just as we reached Dallas.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>there’s something wrong with sth</EXP><DEF>used when saying that a car, machine etc does not work properly and you do not know why</DEF><THESEXA>There’s something wrong with my car; I think it might be the battery.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There was something wrong with the photocopier, so we called in the service company.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>have something wrong with it</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>If the VCR has something wrong with it, take it back to the store.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sth has had it</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when saying that a car, machine etc is completely broken and cannot be repaired</DEF><THESEXA>I’m afraid the bike’s had it.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7375"><Section><Exponent><EXP>build</EXP><DEF>to make a house, road, wall, bridge etc using bricks, stone, wood, or other materials</DEF><THESEXA>A new stadium will be built for the Olympics.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>construct</EXP><DEF>to build a building, bridge, machine etc. <DEFBOLD>Construct</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>build</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The council plans to construct two new schools.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put up sth</EXP><DEF>to build a wall, fence, or building, or put a statue somewhere. <DEFBOLD>Put up</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>build</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The neighbours have put up a new wooden fence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>erect</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to build a wall, fence, or building, especially a public building, or put a statue somewhere</DEF><THESEXA>A monument to the Canadian soldiers was erected in Green Park.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>throw sth up</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to build something very quickly</DEF><THESEXA>Developers have hastily thrown up family homes in the area.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put together</EXP><DEF>to make something from its parts, for example a piece of furniture</DEF><THESEXA>It took an hour to put the bookcase together.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assemble</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to put all the parts of something such as a machine or a piece of furniture together</DEF><THESEXA>A line of robots assemble the cars.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7366"><Section><Exponent><EXP>building</EXP><DEF>a structure such as a house, church, or factory, that has a roof and walls</DEF><THESEXA>The college needs money to pay for new buildings.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>property</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a building or piece of land, or both together – used especially when talking about buying and selling buildings or land</DEF><THESEXA>The next property they looked at was too small.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company received permission to build six residential properties on the land.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>premises</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the buildings and land that a shop, restaurant, company etc uses</DEF><THESEXA>You are not allowed to drink alcohol on the premises.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The bread is baked on the premises.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>complex</EXP><DEF>a group of buildings, or a large building with many parts, used for a particular purpose</DEF><THESEXA>The town has one of the best leisure complexes in the country.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a luxury apartment complex</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>development</EXP><DEF>a group of new buildings that have all been planned and built together on the same piece of land</DEF><THESEXA>a new housing development </THESEXA><THESEXA>a huge industrial development</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>block</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>a large tall building that contains apartments or offices, or is part of a school, university, or hospital</DEF><THESEXA>an <COLLOINEXA>office block</COLLOINEXA></THESEXA><THESEXA>a <COLLOINEXA>block of flats</COLLOINEXA></THESEXA><THESEXA>a<COLLOINEXA> tower block</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>a very tall building - often used disapprovingly</GLOSS></THESEXA><THESEXA>My next lecture is in the science block.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>facility</EXP><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><DEF>a place or building used for a particular activity or industry</DEF><THESEXA>a research facility on campus</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>edifice</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a large building, especially one that is tall and impressive – a very formal use</DEF><THESEXA>Their head office was an imposing edifice.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>structure</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>something that has been made to stand upright – used especially when talking about buildings</DEF><THESEXA>The stone arch is one of the town’s oldest existing structures.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an immense barn-like structure</THESEXA><THESEXA>Mogul calls this building, designed by Donald and John Parkinson in 1928, ‘the most important structure in Los Angeles of the 20th century.’</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>
