<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-731d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>bus</EXP><DEF>a large vehicle that people pay to travel on</DEF><THESEXA>There were a lot of people on the bus.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>coach</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a bus with comfortable seats used for long journeys</DEF><THESEXA>Taking the coach is cheaper than the train.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>minibus</EXP><DEF>a small bus with seats for six to twelve people</DEF><THESEXA>The school uses a minibus to take teams to matches.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>double-decker</EXP><DEF>a bus with two levels</DEF><THESEXA>the red double-deckers in London</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>articulated bus</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>bendy bus</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a very long bus that has a joint in the middle that allows it to go around corners</DEF><THESEXA>Articulated buses have been used in Europe for many years.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tram</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>streetcar</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><Variant><LEXVAR>trolley/trolley car</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a vehicle for passengers, which travels along metal tracks in the street, and usually gets power from electric lines over the vehicle</DEF><THESEXA>We waited at the stop for the tram.</THESEXA><THESEXA>San Diego has a well-used trolley system.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tram</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>a vehicle with many different parts for people to sit in, and which usually has open sides. A tram runs on wheels and is used to take tourists from place to place within a particular area</DEF><THESEXA>The tram takes visitors around the backlot of Universal Studios, where many famous movies were once made.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7309"><Section><Exponent><EXP>business</EXP><DEF>the activity of making money by producing, buying, or selling goods, or providing services</DEF><THESEXA>Business in Europe has been badly affected by economic conditions in the US.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He works in the advertising business.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trade</EXP><DEF>the buying and selling of goods and services, especially between countries</DEF><THESEXA>Trade between European countries became easier after the introduction of the Euro.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a trade agreement</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>commerce</EXP><DEF>the buying and selling of goods and services. <DEFBOLD>Commerce</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>business</DEFBOLD>, and is used when talking about business activities in general</DEF><THESEXA>One of the roles of the federal government is to regulate commerce.</THESEXA><THESEXA>London became a great <COLLOINEXA>centre of commerce</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the local <COLLOINEXA>chamber of commerce</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>an organization which the companies and shops in an area belong to</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>e-commerce</EXP><DEF>the buying and selling of goods and services on the Internet</DEF><THESEXA>E-commerce is a fast-growing part of the US economy.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-72d3"><Section><SECHEADING>person</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>busy</EXP><DEF>if you are busy, you have a lot of things you need to do</DEF><THESEXA>Sorry I haven’t called you, but I’ve been really busy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a busy housewife</THESEXA><THESEXA>Angela was becoming more and more unhappy, but her husband was too busy to notice.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Not now Stephen, I’m busy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Alex is busy studying for his exams.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rushed/run off your feet</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>very busy and in a hurry, because you have too many things to do</DEF><THESEXA>We’ve been absolutely rushed off our feet getting ready for our son’s birthday party.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>snowed under</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>so busy that you can hardly deal with all the work you have to do</DEF><THESEXA>I can’t stop for lunch today – I’m completely snowed under.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We’ve been snowed under with applications for the job.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>up to your ears/neck in sth</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>extremely busy because you have a lot of work to deal with</DEF><THESEXA>Teachers say they are up to their ears in paperwork and don’t have enough time for teaching.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tied up</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>busy in your job, so that you cannot do anything else</DEF><THESEXA>I’m sorry, but he’s tied up at the moment. Could you call back later?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I can’t see you tomorrow: I’m tied up all day.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have a lot to do</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to have to do a lot of things, so that you need to hurry or work hard</DEF><THESEXA>Let’s get started – we have a lot to do.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have a lot on</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>have a lot going on</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be busy, especially because you have arranged to do a lot of things during a particular period</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve got a lot on this weekend.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He says he’ll try and see you as soon as possible, but he has a lot going on this afternoon.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>time</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>busy</EXP><DEF>use this about times when you have a lot of things you need to do</DEF><THESEXA>We have a busy day ahead of us tomorrow.</THESEXA><THESEXA>July and August are our busiest times.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hectic</EXP><DEF>a hectic time or situation is extremely busy, so that you are always in a hurry and often feel excited or worried</DEF><THESEXA>It was really hectic at work today.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The band had a hectic recording schedule.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the rush hour</EXP><DEF>the time in the morning and evening when a lot of people are travelling to or from work</DEF><THESEXA>The buses are so crowded during the rush hour you never get a seat.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In most British cities the rush hour does not start until about 8 o’clock.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-727d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>but</EXP><POS>conjunction</POS><DEF>used when linking two words or phrases that seem opposite or very different in meaning. Don’t use <DEFBOLD>but</DEFBOLD> at the beginning of a sentence in written English</DEF><THESEXA>The plant’s leaves are big, but its flowers are quite small.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her books are fascinating but often rather disturbing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many French dishes are basically simple, but they can take a long time to prepare.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Most of us value human life, but but some people think of animals as being equally important.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>although</EXP><POS>conjunction</POS><DEF>used when contrasting one clause with another in the same sentence</DEF><THESEXA>Although you are in the middle of the city, you feel as if you are in the countryside.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The windmill is still in good working order, although it has not been used since the 1950s.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I enjoyed German although I wasn’t very good at it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Although lack of sleep causes some problems, it has a relatively small effect on performance at work.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>however</EXP><POS>conjunction</POS><DEF>used when saying something that seems different or surprising after your previous statement. It is usually used in the middle of a sentence, separated from the rest of the sentence by commas. It can also come at the beginning</DEF><THESEXA>Jack and his family managed to escape before the soldiers arrived. Other families in the village, however, were less lucky.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Their economy was incredibly successful in the 1980s. Since then, however, there has been a big rise in unemployment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The town is a long way from the nearest big city. However, there is a good bus service.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He began his academic career as a mathematician. However, his main achievements were in the field of nuclear physics.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nevertheless/nonetheless</EXP><POS>conjunction</POS><DEF>used when saying something that seems different or surprising after your previous statement. It is used at the beginning or end of a sentence</DEF><THESEXA>A series of studies 20 years ago suggested that there was a link between watching violent films and violent behaviour. Nevertheless, the results remain highly controversial.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was certainly a terrible accident. Nevertheless, air travel is still the safest form of transport.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The weather turned bad early in the day, but the festival was a great success nonetheless.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was very hard digging in the dry ground, but the work was satisfying nevertheless.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>while/whereas</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>weərˈæz</PRON></PronCodes><POS>conjunction</POS><DEF>used when making comparisons and saying that something is true of one person, thing, or situation, but it is different for another. They are used when contrasting one clause with another in the same sentence</DEF><THESEXA>Taxes make up 62% of the price of a litre of petrol in France, whereas in Britain, the tax is 75%.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some people visit their doctor once every few weeks, while others may not visit a doctor for several years.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Whereas in most of the world they drive on the right, in the UK and Japan they drive on the left.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>by contrast</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when making comparisons and saying that a person, thing, or situation is very different from the one you have just mentioned. It is used when referring back to the previous sentence</DEF><THESEXA>The surface temperature on Venus is higher than the boiling point of water. Mars, by contrast, is very cold.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A report by the FBI shows that 26% of female murder victims in 1995 were killed by their husbands or boyfriends. By contrast, only 3% of male victims were killed by their wives or girlfriends.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-724c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>buy</EXP><DEF>to pay money for something so that you can own it</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve just bought a new car.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The painting was bought by a museum in New York.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>purchase</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> to buy something, especially something large or expensive, in a business deal or by a legal contract</DEF><THESEXA>They purchased 5,000 acres of land.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>acquire</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> to become the owner of something large or expensive such as property, a company, or a valuable object</DEF><THESEXA>In 2007 the business was acquired by a Dutch company.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Television companies were then allowed to acquire more stations.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF> to buy something, especially ordinary things such as food, clothes, or things for your house</DEF><THESEXA>Did you remember to get some bread?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I never know what to get Dad for his birthday.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>snap sth up</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> to buy something immediately, especially because it is very cheap, or because you want it very much and you are worried that someone else might buy it first</DEF><THESEXA>Real estate in the area is being snapped up by developers.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pick sth up</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> to buy something, especially something ordinary such as food or a newspaper, or something that you have found by chance and are pleased about owning</DEF><THESEXA>Could you pick up some milk on your way home?</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s just a little thing I picked up when I was in Kathmandu.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stock up</EXP><DEF> to buy a lot of something you use regularly, because you may not be able to buy it later, or because you are planning to use more of it than usual</DEF><THESEXA>The supermarkets are full of people stocking up for the New Year’s holiday.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We always stock up on cheap wine when we go to France.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Before the blizzard, we stocked up on food.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>splash out</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><EXP>splurge</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to buy something you would not usually buy, because it is too expensive, in order to celebrate an event or make yourself feel good</DEF><THESEXA>Why don’t you splash out on a new dress for the party?</THESEXA><THESEXA>We splurged on an expensive hotel for the last night of the vacation.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7230"><Section><Exponent><EXP>calculate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to find out an amount, price, or value by adding numbers together</DEF><THESEXA>The students calculated the cost of printing 5000 copies of their book.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>work out</EXP><DEF>to calculate something. <DEFBOLD>Work out</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>calculate</DEFBOLD>, and is more common in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>You need to work out how much you will need to borrow.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>figure out</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>figure</LEXVAR><GEO> AmE</GEO></Variant><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to calculate an amount</DEF><THESEXA>We still haven't figured out how much it's all going to cost.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the method for figuring welfare payments</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>count</EXP><DEF>to find out the total number of things or people in a group by looking at each one and adding them all together</DEF><THESEXA>The teacher counted the children as they got on the bus.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>total</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>total up</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to add a number of things together to get a final number</DEF><THESEXA>Once the scores have been totaled, we will announce the winner.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Okay, now let's total up who had the most points.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>quantify</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to say how much something costs, how much of it there is, how serious or effective it is etc</DEF><THESEXA>I think it's difficult to quantify the cost at the moment, for a variety of reasons.</THESEXA><THESEXA>How do you quantify the benefits of the treatment? </THESEXA><THESEXA> a reliable method for quantifying the amount of calcium in the blood</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assess</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to calculate what the value or cost of something is, or decide how good, bad etc something is</DEF><THESEXA>The value of the paintings was assessed at $20 million.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They are still assessing the damage.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We need to have a better way of assessing students' progress.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>estimate</EXP><DEF>to guess an amount, price, or number as exactly as you can, based on the knowledge you have</DEF><THESEXA>The police department estimates that the number of violent crimes will decrease by 2%.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put a figure on sth</EXP><DEF>to say what you think the exact total amount or value of something is, especially when it is a lot</DEF><THESEXA> It's hard to put a figure on it, but the final cost is likely to be over £225 million.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company has refused to put a figure on its losses. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>project</EXP><DEF>to calculate what an amount will be in the future, using the information you have now</DEF><THESEXA>The company projects sales of $4 million this year.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>different ways of calculating</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>add </EXP><DEF>to put two or more numbers together to find the total</DEF><THESEXA>If you add 7 and 5, you get 12.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>subtract/take away</EXP><DEF>to reduce one number by another number. <DEFBOLD>take sth away</DEFBOLD> sounds more informal than <DEFBOLD>subtract</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>If you subtract 12 from 15, you get 3.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>multiply</EXP><DEF>to add a number to itself a particular number of times</DEF><THESEXA>4 multiplied by 10 is 40.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>divide</EXP><DEF>to calculate how many times one number contains another number</DEF><THESEXA>10 divided by 2 equals 5.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-720e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>calm</EXP><DEF>not getting angry, nervous, or upset, even in a difficult situation</DEF><THESEXA>We’ll talk about this later when you’re feeling calmer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Everyone praised Douglas for the calm way in which he handled the situation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>relaxed</EXP><DEF>not worried about anything, especially so that people feel comfortable</DEF><THESEXA>Looking relaxed and confident, the president answered questions from the press. </THESEXA><THESEXA>There was a relaxed atmosphere.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>chilled-out</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>chilled</LEXVAR></Variant><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>very relaxed and not worried – used especially by young people</DEF><THESEXA>I’m much more chilled-out about the whole thing this year. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>laid-back</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> someone who is laid-back is always relaxed and never seems to get worried or annoyed about anything</DEF><THESEXA>I like his laid-back attitude to life.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My parents are pretty laid-back and don’t mind me staying out late.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mellow</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> relaxed, friendly, and happy, especially after drinking alcohol</DEF><THESEXA>After a few drinks, everyone was pretty mellow.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cool</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> staying calm and not showing your emotions, especially when other people are getting excited or angry</DEF><THESEXA>He is the kind of player who always manages to stay cool, even under pressure.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was as cool as a cucumber<GLOSS>very cool</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>keep your head</EXP><DEF>to manage to stay calm and behave in a sensible way in a difficult or frightening situation</DEF><THESEXA>In this job you need to be good at keeping your head in a crisis.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-71fb" fullpage="yes"><Section><SECHEADING>to be able to do sth</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>can do sth</EXP><DEF>to have the ability, opportunity, time, or equipment that you need in order to do something. <DEFBOLD>Could</DEFBOLD> is used as the past form, and is also used to say what is or may be possible</DEF><THESEXA>‘I don’t think Mike can drive.’ ‘Yes, he can.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>He can run faster than me.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Can you see the TV, or should I move?</THESEXA><THESEXA>This program can translate your e-mail into other languages.</THESEXA><THESEXA>How many hamburgers do you think you can eat?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Adrian could read when he was four.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If we had a boat we could row across to the island.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Why didn’t they ask me? I could have done it for them for half the price.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She could win.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be able to do sth</EXP><DEF>used when talking about ability or success in the past or ability in the future, or when you need to use an <NonDV><REFHWD>infinitive</REFHWD></NonDV>. It is also used instead of <DEFBOLD>can</DEFBOLD> in more formal English</DEF><THESEXA>I was able to get a good job<GLOSS>I got one</GLOSS>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Will you be able to carry those bags on your own?</THESEXA><THESEXA>If you want to join the expedition, you must be able to speak Spanish.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The young birds are now able to fly.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be capable of sth</EXP><DEF>to have the ability, energy, or qualities needed to do something, especially something very difficult or unusual – use this about people or machines</DEF><THESEXA>She’s perfectly capable <GLOSS>completely capable</GLOSS> of dealing with the situation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The car is capable of a top speed of 200 mph.</THESEXA><THESEXA>No one thought he was capable of murder.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have the ability to do sth</EXP><DEF>to be able to do something, especially something that is unusual or that most people cannot do</DEF><THESEXA>She seemed to have the ability to make people do anything she wanted.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I believe the team definitely has the ability to win the championship.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be in a position to do sth</EXP><DEF>to be able to do something because you have enough knowledge, power, money, or equipment to do it</DEF><THESEXA>We will have to run more tests before we are in a position to say whether the document is authentic or not.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They expect be in a position to begin construction within two years.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>manage to do sth</EXP><DEF>to succeed in doing something difficult, especially after trying very hard</DEF><THESEXA>He finally managed to find an apartment near his office.</THESEXA><THESEXA>At least three of the hostages managed to escape.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to be unable to do sth</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>can’t/cannot do sth</EXP><DEF>to be unable to do something because you do not have the ability, opportunity, time, or equipment that you need. <DEFBOLD>Couldn’t/could not</DEFBOLD> is used as the past form</DEF><THESEXA>Louise can’t see anything without her glasses.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He couldn’t remember where he had left the car.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Will you help me move this?’ ‘I’m sorry, I can’t – my back’s still giving me trouble.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>I couldn’t have done it without your help.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I could never have climbed that ridge – I’m too out of shape.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Scientists still cannot explain exactly how the virus reproduces.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sarah could not understand why anyone would want to hurt her.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not be able to do sth</EXP><DEF>used when talking about inability in the past or the future, when you need to use an <NonDV><REFHWD>infinitive</REFHWD></NonDV>, and in more formal English</DEF><THESEXA>Unfortunately, I wasn’t able to help them.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t think I’ll be able to come to the meeting after all.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The doctor told Tina she wouldn’t be able to have children.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve looked all over the house but I haven’t been able to find my keys anywhere.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be unable to do sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to not be able to do something, especially something important that you want to do or need to do</DEF><THESEXA>He was unable to sleep and lay awake all night.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many passengers were unable to reach the lifeboats in time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not be in a position to do sth</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>be in no position to do sth</LEXVAR></Variant><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to not be able to do something because you do not have enough knowledge, power, money, or equipment to do it</DEF><THESEXA>I’m afraid I’m not in a position to answer your questions.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Having committed military force there, America is in no position to repeat this strategy elsewhere.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not be capable of sth</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>be incapable of sth</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to not have the physical strength or mental ability to do something – often used when criticizing someone</DEF><THESEXA>My son seems to be incapable of keeping a job.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She’s not capable of looking after herself anymore.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her father was a man who was utterly incapable of pity or sympathy.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-71cb"><Section><Exponent><EXP>cancel</EXP><DEF>to decide that something that was officially planned will not happen</DEF><THESEXA>The teacher was ill so classes were cancelled for the day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was feeling better so I cancelled my doctor’s appointment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They were forced to cancel the concert when the conductor became ill.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>call off</EXP><DEF>to cancel a meeting, game, or event that you have organized. <DEFBOLD>Call off</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>cancel</DEFBOLD> and is very commonly used in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>Linda decided to call the wedding off.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The game was called off due to heavy rain.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be off</EXP><DEF>if an event or activity is off, it has been cancelled because of a sudden problem or change in someone’s plans</DEF><THESEXA>I’m afraid the party’s off. Nick won’t let us use his apartment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Myers called me yesterday to tell me that the deal was off.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>postpone</EXP><DEF>to decide to do something at a later time, instead of the time that was officially planned</DEF><THESEXA>The show has been postponed until next Saturday.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shelve</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also </LINKWORD><LEXVAR>put sth on ice</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to decide not to continue with a plan, project etc although it may be considered again at some time in the future</DEF><THESEXA>Plans for a new stadium have been shelved for now.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The project had to be put on ice due to lack of funding.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Blears called for the discussions to be put on ice until after the elections.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>annul</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to officially decide that a marriage, result, or agreement has no legal authority and is therefore cancelled</DEF><THESEXA> The election results were annulled by the courts.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A marriage can be annulled if there has been lack of consent. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-718f"><HEADING>Sense 5</HEADING><Section><SECHEADING>carefulness</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>care</EXP><DEF>if you do something with care, you are careful to avoid damage, mistakes etc</DEF><THESEXA>She put the needle in <COLLOINEXA>with great care</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Everyone should <COLLOINEXA>take care</COLLOINEXA> in the sun.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>caution</EXP><DEF>care to avoid danger or risks, or care about trusting information that might not be true</DEF><THESEXA>Her evidence should be treated <COLLOINEXA>with caution</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There was a certain lack of caution among investors.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>prudence</EXP><DEF>a sensible and careful attitude that makes you avoid unnecessary risks – a rather formal use</DEF><THESEXA>Banks should show more prudence in lending money. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>vigilance</EXP><DEF>careful attention to what is happening, so that you will notice any danger or illegal activity</DEF><THESEXA>Governments from across the world have called for greater vigilance against Internet-based crime. </THESEXA><THESEXA>There is a need for constant vigilance to protect vulnerable people.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>regard for sth</EXP><DEF>careful attention and consideration shown to something, to avoid danger or risks – used especially when someone fails to do this</DEF><THESEXA>The court heard that the company had shown no regard for the safety of its employees.</THESEXA><THESEXA>These men are cold-blooded killers and have little regard for human life. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tact</EXP><DEF>care not to say anything that might offend or upset someone</DEF><THESEXA>He handled the matter with a great deal of tact.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>discretion</EXP><DEF>care to deal with situations in a way that does not embarrass, upset or offend people, especially by not telling any of their secrets</DEF><THESEXA>Any confidential information was treated with discretion.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-718a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>take care of sb</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>look after sb</LEXVAR><GEO>especially BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to make sure a child or an old or sick person is safe and has the things they need</DEF><THESEXA>I have to look after my little brother.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Taking care of a baby is hard work. </THESEXA><THESEXA>She is taking care of her grandmother while her grandfather is in hospital. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>care for sb</EXP><DEF>to take care of someone. <DEFBOLD>Care for sb</DEFBOLD> is less common and more formal than <DEFBOLD>take care of/look after sb</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>He was cared for by a team of nurses.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Caring for an elderly relative can be very rewarding.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nurse</EXP><DEF>to look after someone who is ill</DEF><THESEXA>He nursed his wife through a long illness.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The monks <COLLOINEXA>nursed him back to health</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>looked after him until he was well again</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>babysit</EXP><DEF>to look after children in the evening while their parents go out somewhere</DEF><THESEXA>I’ll ask Jane to babysit on Wednesday night.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He used to <COLLOINEXA>babysit for </COLLOINEXA>Mary when she worked nights.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mind</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>to look after a child while their parents are not there, especially for a short time</DEF><THESEXA>Will you mind the baby while I go to the shop?</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7165"><Section><Exponent><EXP>careful</EXP><DEF> trying to avoid mistakes and do everything correctly by paying a lot of attention to details</DEF><THESEXA>Try to be more careful with your punctuation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A careful inspection showed cracks in the foundation of the building.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>methodical</EXP><DEF>always doing things in a careful and well-organized way</DEF><THESEXA>He is very methodical in his work and likes to plan everything in advance.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This time the researchers used a more methodical approach to the problem.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>thorough</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈθʌrə</PRON><AMEVARPRON>ˈθʌroʊ, ˈθʌrə</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>careful to examine or deal with every part of something, so that you do not miss anything important</DEF><THESEXA>There will be a thorough investigation into the circumstances of her death.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Our mechanics will check everything – they’re very thorough.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>meticulous</EXP><DEF>very careful about every small detail in order to make sure everything is done correctly</DEF><THESEXA>She keeps meticulous records of the students’ progress.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was meticulous about keeping the place clean and tidy.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>systematic</EXP><DEF>using a fixed plan in a careful and well-organized way in order to do everything that you should</DEF><THESEXA>a systematic review of the scientific evidence </THESEXA><THESEXA>We need a systematic way to evaluate employees.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>painstaking</EXP><DEF>using a lot of time and effort to do something in a very careful and thorough way</DEF><THESEXA>The book is the result of ten years of painstaking research.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They began the long and painstaking task of translating his work into English.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-714f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>careless</EXP><DEF>not paying enough attention to what you are doing, so that you make mistakes, damage things etc</DEF><THESEXA>I made a few careless mistakes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was careless of you to leave your purse lying about.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He started getting careless about taking his medication.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>clumsy</EXP><DEF>often dropping or breaking things because you move around in a careless way</DEF><THESEXA>I’m so clumsy, I spilt milk all over the floor.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a clumsy child</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sloppy</EXP><DEF>careless and lazy in the way you do your work or in your behaviour generally</DEF><THESEXA>As a student, he was brilliant but sloppy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I will not tolerate sloppy work.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reckless</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>doing dangerous or stupid things without thinking about your own or other people’s safety</DEF><THESEXA>The driver of the car was arrested for reckless driving.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His actions showed a reckless disregard for human life.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>irresponsible</EXP><DEF>careless in a way that might affect other people, especially when you should be taking care of them</DEF><THESEXA>It’s irresponsible for parents to let their children smoke.</THESEXA><THESEXA>his irresponsible attitude to keeping animals</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tactless</EXP><DEF>carelessly saying something that upsets or embarrasses someone, without intending to do this</DEF><THESEXA>He kept making tactless remarks about her appearance. </THESEXA><THESEXA>a tactless question</THESEXA><THESEXA>I thought it would be tactless to ask about her divorce.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>casual</EXP><DEF>not being careful enough about something and treating it as though it is not important</DEF><THESEXA>He seems to have a very casual attitude towards his work.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She disliked the casual way in which he made decisions affecting other people’s lives. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>negligent</EXP><DEF>careless about something that you are responsible for, so that serious mistakes are made – used especially when someone will be officially punished for this</DEF><THESEXA>The doctor was negligent in using the wrong type of needle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They found him guilty of negligent conduct.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-711e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>car park</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a place with spaces for people to park their cars, either outside or in a building</DEF><THESEXA>I parked in the car park next to the hospital.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>parking lot</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>a car park</DEF><THESEXA>The hotel has an underground parking lot.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>multistorey</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>multistorey car park</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>a building containing a car park of several levels</DEF><THESEXA>The car park was a huge concrete multistorey.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>parking garage</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>a multi-storey car park or an indoor, often underground, car park</DEF><THESEXA>I scraped the car leaving the parking garage.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>parking space</EXP><DEF>a space for one car in a car park</DEF><THESEXA>There wasn’t a single parking space available.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>garage</EXP><DEF>an indoor place for parking a car, attached to a house</DEF><THESEXA>He reversed the car out of the garage.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7111"><Section><Exponent><EXP>carry</EXP><DEF>to have something or someone in your hands or arms when you go somewhere</DEF><THESEXA>She was carrying a heavy suitcase.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Mary fainted, and had to be carried inside.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tote</EXP><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> to carry something such as a bag or a gun</DEF><THESEXA>He came out of the office toting a black leather briefcase.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Guards toting machine guns stood inside the airport.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lug</EXP><DEF> to carry something heavy, with difficulty</DEF><THESEXA>They lugged the mail in heavy canvas bags into the building.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cart</EXP><DEF> to carry something large and heavy somewhere, especially when this is annoying or hard work</DEF><THESEXA>We carted all the furniture upstairs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>schlep</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> to carry something heavy</DEF><THESEXA>Marty schlepped the suitcases upstairs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bear</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to carry something – used when talking about what someone has with them when they go somewhere. <DEFBOLD>Bear</DEFBOLD> is commonly used as a participle <DEFBOLD>bearing</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>They arrived bearing gifts.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Anna returned, bearing a large red packet. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-70e1"><HEADING>Sense 2</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>catch</EXP><DEF>to stop someone who is trying to escape, especially by running after them and then holding them</DEF><THESEXA>He raced after her, but he couldn’t catch her.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The police caught the bank robbers after a car chase through the city.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>arrest</EXP><DEF>if the police arrest someone, they take him or her to a police station because they think that person has done something illegal</DEF><THESEXA>Wayne was arrested for dangerous driving.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The police arrested him and charged him with murder.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>apprehend</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if the police apprehend someone they think has done something illegal, they catch him or her</DEF><THESEXA>The two men were later apprehended after they robbed another store.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The killers were never apprehended.</THESEXA><THESEXA>All of the kidnappers were apprehended and convicted. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>capture</EXP><DEF>to catch an enemy or a criminal in order to keep them as a prisoner</DEF><THESEXA>The French king was captured by the English at the Battle of Poitiers in 1356.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The gunmen were finally captured after a shoot-out with the police. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take sb prisoner</EXP><DEF>to catch someone, especially in a war, in order to keep them as a prisoner</DEF><THESEXA>350 soldiers were killed and another 300 taken prisoner.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Ellison was taken prisoner by the Germans during the retreat to Dunkirk.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trap</EXP><DEF>to make someone go to a place from which they cannot escape, especially by using your skill and intelligence</DEF><THESEXA>Police trapped the man inside a bar on the city’s southside.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>corner</EXP><DEF>to force someone into a place from which they cannot escape</DEF><THESEXA>He was cornered outside the school by three gang members.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-70c8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>cause</EXP><DEF>to make something happen, especially something bad</DEF><THESEXA>Bad weather has caused a lot of problems on the roads.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The fault caused the whole computer system to shut down.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make sb/sth do sth</EXP><DEF> to cause someone to do something, or cause something to happen. <DEFBOLD>Make</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>cause</DEFBOLD>, and is the usual word to use in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>What made you decide to become a teacher?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m sorry, I didn’t mean to make you cry.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Gravity is the force which makes the planets move round the Sun.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be responsible for sth</EXP><DEF> if someone or something is responsible for something bad, they caused it to happen</DEF><THESEXA>The excessive heat was responsible for their deaths.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A small militant group was responsible for the bombing.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bring about sth</EXP><DEF>to make something happen – used especially about changes or improvements</DEF><THESEXA>The Internet has brought about enormous changes in society.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s important that we do everything we can to bring about peace.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>result in sth</EXP><DEF> if an action or event results in something, it makes that thing happen</DEF><THESEXA>The fire resulted in the deaths of two children.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The decision is likely to result in a large number of job losses.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lead to sth</EXP><DEF>to cause something to happen eventually after a period of time</DEF><THESEXA>The information led to several arrests.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A poor diet in childhood can lead to health problems later in life.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trigger</EXP><DEF>if one event triggers another, it suddenly makes the second event happen</DEF><THESEXA>The incident triggered a wave of violence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>An earthquake off Java’s southern coast triggered a tsunami.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>precipitate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make a very serious event happen very suddenly, which will affect a lot of people</DEF><THESEXA>The withdrawal of foreign investment would precipitate an economic crisis.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand precipitated World War I.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-70ad"><HEADING>Sense 2</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>certain</EXP><DEF>if something is certain, you are completely sure that it will happen or is true</DEF><THESEXA>Success seems certain.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It is almost certain that there will be a change of government.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Nobody knows exactly who built the manor, but it is certain that an architect called John Sturges supplied the drawings. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>definite</EXP><DEF>if something is definite, it is certain because someone has officially stated that it will happen, is true etc</DEF><THESEXA>I hope you can give me a definite answer soon.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The wedding will be next summer but a definite date has not been arranged yet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve got a good chance of getting the job, but it’s not definite yet.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>inevitable</EXP><DEF>if something, especially something bad, is inevitable, it is certain to happen and you cannot do anything to prevent it</DEF><THESEXA>War now seems inevitable.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was inevitable that he would find out her secret sooner or later.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Facial wrinkles are the inevitable result of aging.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be bound to</EXP><DEF>if something is bound to happen, it is very likely to happen, especially because that is what usually happens in that kind of situation. <DEFBOLD>Be bound to</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>certain</DEFBOLD> and is very common in everyday spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>The kids are bound to be hungry when they get home – they always are.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My car broke down today. It was bound to happen sooner or later.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be assured of sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be certain to get something good, or to be successful</DEF><THESEXA>After the success of its recent single, the band is now assured of a contract with a major record company.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He is is virtually assured of becoming the next prime minister. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Our clients are assured of comfortable accommodation and the attention of our trained staff.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sth is a foregone conclusion</EXP><DEF>if something is a foregone conclusion, its result is certain even though it has not happened yet</DEF><THESEXA>They were winning by such a large margin that victory seemed to be a foregone conclusion.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Party members believe it is a foregone conclusion that he will resign.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-707f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>certainly</EXP><DEF>used when saying that you are sure about something</DEF><THESEXA>Amy’s certainly growing, but she’s not gaining weight.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s certainly not the cheapest way of getting to New York.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Is this his signature?’ ‘Almost certainly.’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>undoubtedly</EXP><DEF>used when saying that you are completely sure about something. <DEFBOLD>Undoubtedly</DEFBOLD> sounds more certain and a little more formal than <DEFBOLD>certainly</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>They are undoubtedly the better team.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Undoubtedly it will be a huge shock for him. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The furniture, although undoubtedly fashionable, was a little too plain for my taste.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>there is no doubt</EXP><DEF>used when you are completely sure about something</DEF><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>There is no doubt that</COLLOINEXA> violence on TV influences children.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s definitely innocent. <COLLOINEXA>There’s no doubt about </COLLOINEXA>that.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>without (a) doubt</EXP><DEF>used when you are completely sure about something</DEF><THESEXA>She was without doubt one of Mexico’s finest artists.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There would be, without a doubt, some perfectly ordinary explanation for his behaviour.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-24d20af9.11ba14e1828.-7dfa"><Section><SECHEADING>to change something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>change</EXP><DEF>to make someone or something different</DEF><THESEXA>Unfortunately, there’s nothing we can do to change the situation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Being at college has changed her – she’s much more confident now.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>alter</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to change something so that it is better or more suitable</DEF><THESEXA>You can alter the colour and size of the image using a remote control. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Can we alter the date of the meeting?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>adapt</EXP><DEF>to change something slightly in order to improve it or make it more suitable</DEF><THESEXA>How much would it cost to adapt the existing equipment?</THESEXA><THESEXA>You can adapt the recipe to suit your own requirements.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>adjust</EXP><DEF>to make small changes in the position or level of something in order to improve it or make it more suitable</DEF><THESEXA>How do you adjust the volume on the television?</THESEXA><THESEXA>He adjusted his tie in the mirror.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>modify</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make small changes to something such as a piece of equipment, a set of ideas, or a way of behaving in order to improve it or use it in a different way</DEF><THESEXA>He’s modified his opinions since then.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a modified version of the original program</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reform</EXP><DEF>to change a law, system, organization etc so that it is fairer or more effective</DEF><THESEXA>plans to reform the tax system</THESEXA><THESEXA>Health care needs to be completely reformed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>revise</EXP><DEF>to change a plan, idea, law etc because of new information and ideas</DEF><THESEXA>In July, China revised the rules for foreign investment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The findings could force the scientists to revise their ideas about climate change. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reorganize</EXP><DEF>to change the way that a system or organization works</DEF><THESEXA>We’ve had to reorganize our database.</THESEXA><THESEXA>During the 1980s, the government reorganized the civil service. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>restructure</EXP><DEF>to make big changes to the way something is organized, especially a large political or economic system or a big company, in order to make it more effective</DEF><THESEXA>The company has been restructured from top to bottom.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to change something completely</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>transform</EXP><DEF>to change something completely, especially so that it is much better</DEF><THESEXA>Well, you’ve certainly transformed this place – it looks great!</THESEXA><THESEXA>Putin transformed the Russian economy.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>revolutionize</EXP><DEF>to completely and permanently change the way people do something or think about something, especially because of a new idea or invention</DEF><THESEXA>Computers have revolutionized the way we work.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This important discovery revolutionized our understanding of the universe.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to change something in order to deceive people</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>distort</EXP><DEF>to explain facts, statements etc in a way that makes them seem different from what they really are</DEF><THESEXA>The judge said that she had deliberately tried to distort the facts.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Don’t try to distort the truth.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>twist</EXP><DEF>to dishonestly change the meaning of a piece of information or of something that someone has said, in order to get an advantage for yourself or to support your own opinion</DEF><THESEXA>He accused reporters of twisting his words.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In her article she twisted the meaning of what I said.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>misrepresent</EXP><DEF>to give people a wrong idea about someone or their opinions, by what you write or say</DEF><THESEXA>I hope I have not misrepresented her opinion.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s taking legal action to stop the film, claiming it grossly misrepresents him.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-7043"><Section><Exponent><EXP>change</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a situation in which someone or something becomes different, or the act of making something different</DEF><THESEXA>There was a sudden change in the weather.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We are living in a period of great change.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was told to make some slight changes to his essay.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>alteration</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a change, especially a small one that happens naturally or gradually, or one that is made in order to improve something</DEF><THESEXA>I noticed a slight alteration in her behaviour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They had to make some alterations to their original theory. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reform</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a change made to a system or law in order to improve it</DEF><THESEXA>He called for a reform of our outdated voting system.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many people opposed the economic reforms.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shift</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a change, especially in people’s attitudes or in the way they do things, or in the position of someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>a shift in public opinion about the war</THESEXA><THESEXA>There has been a noticeable shift in government policy on education. </THESEXA><THESEXA>There needs to be a major shift away from road transport to rail transport.</THESEXA><THESEXA>After he died, there was a dramatic shift in the balance of power.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>swing</EXP><DEF>a big change, especially in someone’s opinions or moods</DEF><THESEXA>There has been a big swing toward the Democrats <GLOSS>many more people are supporting them</GLOSS>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The drug can cause <COLLOINEXA>mood swings</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fluctuation</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a change in something – used when something changes often</DEF><THESEXA>fluctuations in the value of the dollar</THESEXA><THESEXA>temperature fluctuations</THESEXA><THESEXA>fluctuations in his mood</THESEXA><THESEXA>There has been some fluctuation in productivity levels.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>a great change</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>transformation</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a change in which something or someone becomes completely different</DEF><THESEXA>There has been a complete transformation in his attitude since he became a father.</THESEXA><THESEXA>her transformation from shy local girl to famous movie actress</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>revolution</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a complete change in ways of thinking or working</DEF><THESEXA>The 1970s saw the beginnings of a new technological revolution.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Einstein’s General Theory of Relativity started a revolution in scientific thinking.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shake-up</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a situation in which a lot of changes are very quickly made in a system or organization in order to make it more effective</DEF><THESEXA>The department has not performed well and is badly in need of a shake-up.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The Administration is planning a thorough shake-up of the welfare system.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>U-turn</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a complete change in the plans of a government or political party so that it decides to do the opposite of what it originally said it would do</DEF><THESEXA>The government was forced to do a U-turn after angry protests about their taxation policy.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-700d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>characteristic</EXP><DEF>something that is typical of someone or something and makes them easy to recognize</DEF><THESEXA>He had several characteristics which made him different to the rest of his family.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the physical characteristics of the brain</THESEXA><THESEXA>He studied the special characteristics of adult speech addressed to children.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>quality</EXP><DEF>a characteristic of a person, especially a good one such as kindness or intelligence</DEF><THESEXA>Tina has a lot of good qualities.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m not sure about his leadership qualities.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Obedience is a quality that my father </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>property</EXP><GRAM>usually plural</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>technical</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a characteristic of a substance or object, for example hardness or elasticity, or how it behaves – used especially in scientific contexts</DEF><THESEXA>We examined the physical properties of various metals.</THESEXA><THESEXA>changes in the electrical properties of cells</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some plants have healing properties.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>attribute</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a good or useful characteristic</DEF><THESEXA>I suspected I probably did not quite possess all the attributes required to succeed at the highest level.</THESEXA><THESEXA>All living things are able to reproduce their kind, an attribute which no machine possesses..</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>good/bad points</EXP><DEF>good or bad characteristics – used when someone or something has both good and bad characteristics</DEF><THESEXA>The seller will obviously emphasize the car’s good points.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Draw up a list of your partner’s good and bad points.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6ff0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>cheap</EXP><DEF>costing very little money, or less than you expected</DEF><THESEXA>My shoes were really cheap – they only cost £25.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The cheapest way to get to Chicago is to take the bus.</THESEXA><THESEXA>cheap flights</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>low</EXP><DEF>low prices, rents, and fees do not cost a lot of money. Do not use <DEFBOLD>cheap</DEFBOLD> with these words</DEF><THESEXA>Why is the share price so low? </THESEXA><THESEXA>You could get equally good accommodation elsewhere at a lower rent. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>inexpensive</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>not expensive – use this especially about things that are of good quality, even though they do not cost a lot</DEF><THESEXA>The furniture is inexpensive, but well made.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a simple inexpensive meal</THESEXA><THESEXA>a hotel that offers air-conditioned rooms at relatively inexpensive prices</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reasonable</EXP><DEF> a reasonable price seems fair because it is not too high</DEF><THESEXA>The restaurant serves good food at reasonable prices. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Only £25 a night? That sounds reasonable.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>economical</EXP><DEF>cheap because you do not need to use a lot of money or fuel</DEF><THESEXA>an economical car</THESEXA><THESEXA>It is usually more economical to buy in large quantities. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>affordable</EXP><DEF>cheap enough for most people to be able to buy or pay for</DEF><THESEXA>affordable housing </THESEXA><THESEXA>Single mothers often have trouble finding affordable childcare.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The shop sells designer fashions at affordable prices.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>competitive</EXP><DEF>competitive prices and rates are as low as those charged by other shops or companies</DEF><THESEXA>I think you’ll find our prices are extremely competitive.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The hotel offers a high standard of service at very competitive rates.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>budget</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>budget flights, airlines, hotels etc have especially low prices</DEF><THESEXA>You can get a budget flight to Amsterdam for only £19.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a list of budget hotels for under $50 a night</THESEXA><THESEXA>budget accommodation for families with young children</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be good/great value</EXP><DEF>to be worth at least the price you pay for it, so that you feel pleased and think you have spent your money well</DEF><THESEXA>The meals at Charlie’s Pizza are really good value.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The holiday is great value for money.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be a bargain</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be extremely cheap</DEF><THESEXA>I got this shirt when I was in Indonesia. It was a real bargain.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><ThesColloBox><SECHEADING>COLLOCATIONS CHECK</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>low</EXP><COLLORANGE>price/cost/rent/fee/charge</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reasonable</EXP><COLLORANGE>price/cost</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>economical</EXP><COLLORANGE>car/way/method</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>affordable</EXP><COLLORANGE>accommodation/housing/price</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>competitive </EXP><COLLORANGE>price/rate</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>budget</EXP><COLLORANGE>flight/airline/hotel/accommodation</COLLORANGE></Exponent></ThesColloBox></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6fd2"><HEADING>Sense 2</HEADING><Section><SECHEADING>to get money or possessions dishonestly from someone</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>cheat</EXP><DEF>to deceive someone so that they do not get or keep something they have a right to</DEF><THESEXA>He used his charm to cheat the old lady out of everything he could get.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s afraid they’ll cheat him after he hands over the money.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>con</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to get money from someone by telling them lies</DEF><THESEXA>They conned her into spending thousands of pounds on useless equipment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He conned money out of the public by pretending to collect for charity. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>swindle</EXP><DEF>to get money from a person or organization by cheating them in a clever way</DEF><THESEXA>The painting has been stolen and the art gallery has been swindled out of a large sum of money.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A City businessman who swindled investors out of millions of pounds was jailed for four years. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>defraud</EXP><DEF>to commit the crime of getting money from an organization by deceiving them</DEF><THESEXA>He admitted attempting to defraud his former employer of $1 million.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Johnson is accused of conspiring to defraud the taxman of hundreds of thousands of pounds.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>do sb out of sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>to dishonestly stop someone from getting or keeping something, especially something they have a right to have</DEF><THESEXA>They’ve done me out of three weeks wages!</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6fbd"><Section><Exponent><EXP>check</EXP><DEF>to look at something carefully and thoroughly in order to make sure that it is correct, safe, or working properly</DEF><THESEXA>I’ll just check the water level in the battery.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The immigration officer checked their passports.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We need to check the building for structural damage.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>examine</EXP><DEF>to look at something carefully and thoroughly because you want to find out something about it</DEF><THESEXA>Experts who examined the painting believe it is genuine.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The police will examine the weapon for fingerprints.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>inspect</EXP><DEF>to look at something carefully and thoroughly in order to make sure that it is correct, safe, or working properly, especially when it is your job to do this</DEF><THESEXA>The building is regularly inspected by a fire-safety officer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some insurance people have already been here to inspect the damage caused by the storm.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go through sth</EXP><DEF>to examine something such as a document or plan from beginning to end, especially in order to check that it is correct</DEF><THESEXA>You should go through the contract before you sign.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve finished my essay, but I just need to go through it to check for spelling mistakes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>double-check</EXP><DEF>to check something again so that you are completely sure it is correct, safe, or working properly</DEF><THESEXA>I double-checked all my calculations and they seemed fine.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Travellers should double-check flight information before setting off today.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>test</EXP><DEF>to examine or use something in order to find out whether it works or what its qualities are, or in order to check that it is satisfactory</DEF><THESEXA>Test your brakes to check they are working correctly.</THESEXA><THESEXA>These products have not been tested on animals.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>monitor</EXP><DEF>to carefully watch or keep checking someone or something in order to see what happens over a period of time</DEF><THESEXA>Doctors monitored her progress during the night.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Observers have been monitoring the situation in Burma closely. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6fab"><Section><Exponent><EXP>child</EXP><DEF>someone who is not yet an adult. You don’t usually use <DEFBOLD>child</DEFBOLD> to talk about babies or teenagers</DEF><THESEXA>Many children are scared of the dark.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s just a child.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>kid</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a child. <DEFBOLD>Kid</DEFBOLD> is the usual word to use in everyday spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>We left the kids in the car.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>little boy/little girl</EXP><DEF>a young male or female child</DEF><THESEXA>I lived there when I was a little girl.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Little boys love dinosaurs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>teenager</EXP><DEF>someone between the ages of 13 and 19</DEF><THESEXA>There’s not much for teenagers to do around here.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>adolescent</EXP><DEF>a young person who is developing into an adult – used especially when talking about the problems these people have</DEF><THESEXA>He changed from a cheerful child to a confused adolescent.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>youth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially disapproving</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a teenage boy – especially one who is violent and commits crimes</DEF><THESEXA>He was attacked by a gang of youths.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a youth court</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>youngster</EXP><DEF>a child or young person – used especially by old people</DEF><THESEXA>You youngsters have got your whole life ahead of you.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s a bright youngster with a good sense of humour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>minor</EXP><REGISTERLAB>law</REGISTERLAB><DEF>someone who is not yet legally an adult</DEF><THESEXA>It is illegal to sell alcohol to a minor.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6f8b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>choice</EXP><THESPROPFORM>choice of</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The school seems OK, but there isn’t a great choice of courses.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>have a choice<GLOSS>be able to choose from several things</GLOSS></THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>With her high grades and athletic skill, Celeste had her choice of he can give up playing football.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>keep/leave your options open<GLOSS>delay choosing so that you continue to have several things to choose from</GLOSS></THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>I haven’t signed any contracts yet – I want to keep my options open.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>alternative</EXP><DEF>one of two or more ways of doing something</DEF><THESEXA>Did you consider
