id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6f69"><Section><Exponent><EXP>choice</EXP><DEF>something or someone that has been chosen</DEF><THESEXA>Maria was very pleased with her choice.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>first/second/third choice<GLOSS>the thing you wanted most, the thing you wanted most after that etc</GLOSS></THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>Greece was our first choice for a vacation, but all the flights were full.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>somebody’s choice of</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>I don’t like his choice of friends.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>selection</EXP><DEF>a small group of the best things that have been chosen from a larger group</DEF><THESPROPFORM>selection of</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>She showed me a selection of her drawings.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a selection of songs from ‘West Side Story’</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6f56"><Section><Exponent><EXP>choose</EXP><DEF>to decide which one of several things you want</DEF><THESEXA>I chose a black dress.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Which dessert should I choose?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pick</EXP><DEF>to choose something, especially without thinking carefully. <DEFBOLD>Pick</DEFBOLD> is more informal than <DEFBOLD>choose</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Pick any number from one to ten.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>select</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to choose something, especially after thinking carefully</DEF><THESEXA>The committee will meet to select a new chairman.</THESEXA><THESEXA>All our instructors are carefully selected.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>opt for/go for</EXP><DEF>to choose one thing instead of another</DEF><THESEXA>Many car buyers opt for used vehicles.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I think I’ll go for the chocolate cake.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>decide on</EXP><DEF>to choose something from many possible things, especially when the decision has been difficult or taken a long time</DEF><THESEXA>Thomas had decided on a career as a writer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>single out</EXP><DEF>to choose one person or thing from a group because they are better, worse, more important etc than the others</DEF><THESEXA>Why should he be singled out for special treatment?</THESEXA><THESEXA>One student was singled out for special attention.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take your pick</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to choose anything you want, especially when there are many different things available</DEF><THESEXA>You can take your pick from Bodrum’s many bars and restaurants.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6f3f"><HEADING>Sense 3</HEADING><Section><SECHEADING>religious group</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>church</EXP><DEF>one of the different groups within the Christian religion – used especially in names</DEF><THESEXA>The Roman Catholic church is expanding.</THESEXA><THESEXA>members of the Church of England</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>denomination</EXP><DEF> a religious group that has slightly different beliefs from other groups who belong to the same religion</DEF><THESEXA>Our staff come from churches of all denominations.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Several denominations have developed among Jews.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sect</EXP><DEF> a religious group that has separated from a larger group</DEF><THESEXA>He is a leader in the powerful Ansar Sunni Muslim sect.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cult</EXP><DEF> an extreme religious group that is not part of an established religion</DEF><THESEXA>Members of the cult all committed suicide on the same day.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6f30"><Section><Exponent><EXP>citizen</EXP><DEF>someone who lives in a particular town, country, or state</DEF><THESEXA>In order to become a US citizen, you need to have a Permanent Resident card. </THESEXA><THESEXA>All British citizens have the right to live in the UK. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Good citizens understand that they have a responsibility to the community.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>national</EXP><DEF>a citizen of a country who is living in another country</DEF><THESEXA>She insisted that foreign nationals were safe in the country.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Russians nationals were ordered to leave.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her husband is a French national.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>resident</EXP><DEF>someone who lives in a particular street or area</DEF><THESEXA>There have been complaints by local residents about the building work.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was a resident of Chicago for many years.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>native</EXP><DEF>someone who was born in a particular country but moved to another country – used when describing a person or their life</DEF><THESEXA>Picasso was a native of Spain, although he spent much of his life in France.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>subject</EXP><DEF>someone who was born in a country that has a king or queen, and has a right to live there</DEF><THESEXA>Northern Ireland citizens are British subjects.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>alien</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>someone who is not a legal citizen of the country they are living or working in – used in official contexts</DEF><THESEXA>Employers cannot hire illegal aliens.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6f14"><Section><SECHEADING>place with houses, shops, and offices</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>city</EXP><DEF>a large area with houses, shops, offices etc that is often the centre of government for an area. A <DEFBOLD>city</DEFBOLD> is bigger than a <DEFBOLD>town</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The nearest big city is San Francisco.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>town</EXP><DEF>a large area with houses, shops, offices etc. A <DEFBOLD>town</DEFBOLD> is smaller than a <DEFBOLD>city</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>La Coruña is a pretty seaside town. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>capital</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>capital city</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>the city where the government of a country or state is</DEF><THESEXA>We travelled to Budapest, the capital of Hungary.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>metropolis</EXP><DEF>a big busy city that is full of people and activity</DEF><THESEXA>After 1850 Paris grew quickly into a busy metropolis.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>urban</EXP><POS>adj</POS><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>relating to towns and cities</DEF><THESEXA>Air pollution is particularly bad in urban areas.</THESEXA><THESEXA>urban development</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>outside part of city</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>the outskirts</EXP><DEF>the area around the edge of a city or just outside it</DEF><THESEXA>Disneyland is on the outskirts of Paris.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>suburb</EXP><DEF>an area around the edges of a city, where many people live</DEF><THESEXA>He lives in a suburb of London.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>the suburbs</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>More and more people are moving to the suburbs.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6ec2"><Section><Exponent><EXP>claim</EXP><DEF>to say that something is true, even though it has not been proved</DEF><THESEXA>He claims that he didn’t see anything.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She claims to be a descendant of Charles Dickens.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>allege</EXP><DEF>to claim that someone did something wrong or illegal, although you do not give any proof</DEF><THESEXA>It is alleged that he murdered his wife.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Patients allege that the two firms failed to warn doctors about the dangers of taking the drugs over a long period.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>maintain</EXP><DEF>to repeatedly say that something is true, especially when other people do not believe you</DEF><THESEXA>He continued to maintain his innocence, even after he was sent to prison.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My mother always maintains that I learned to talk at six months.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>insist</EXP><DEF>to say very firmly that something is true</DEF><THESEXA>She insists that Tom was there, although he denies it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Turkey insists that there is more than enough water in the two rivers for all three countries. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assert</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to state that something is true – used especially in formal writing when reporting someone’s opinion</DEF><THESEXA>They assert that children work better on their own.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The Environment Minister asserted that one third of the country’s cities had major pollution problems.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>contend</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to claim that something is true, especially when other people disagree with you</DEF><THESEXA>Lawyers contend that his back problems were due to an existing injury, not the accident. </THESEXA><THESEXA>It is possible, as Kennedy contends, that her medical condition is caused by her work. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6e9c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>claim</EXP><DEF>a statement that something is true, even though it has not been proved</DEF><THESEXA>It is difficult to believe some of the manufacturer's claims for its products.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>allegation</EXP><DEF>a statement that someone has done something wrong or illegal, but that has not been proved</DEF><THESEXA>He has strongly denied the allegations of sexual harassment.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assertion</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>something that you say or write that you strongly believe</DEF><THESEXA>the assertion that house prices are falling</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>contention</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a strong opinion that someone expresses</DEF><THESEXA>Her main contention is that doctors should do more to encourage healthy eating.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6e85"><Section><Exponent><EXP>class</EXP><DEF>a group of students or schoolchildren who are taught together</DEF><THESEXA>There are twenty kids in the class.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She gets along well with the other children in her class.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m going out with some friends from my dance class.</THESEXA><THESEXA>'I graduated in 1999.' 'What class were you in?'</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>background</EXP><DEF>the type of home and family you come from, and its social class</DEF><THESEXA>The school takes kids from all sorts of backgrounds.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We come from the same town and share a similar background.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>working-class/middle-class etc background</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The organization helps children from working-class backgrounds to go to university.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>middle-class</EXP><DEF>belonging to the class of people who are usually well educated, fairly rich, and who work in jobs which they have trained to do. For example, doctors, lawyers, and managers are middle-class</DEF><THESEXA>The newspaper’s readers are mostly middle class.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They live in a middle-class neighbourhood on the edge of town.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>white-collar</EXP><DEF>someone who works in an office, not a factory, mine etc</DEF><THESPROPFORM>white-collar worker/job/employee</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The economic recession has put many white-collar workers in danger of losing their jobs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>working-class</EXP><DEF>belonging to the class of people who do not have much money or power, and who have jobs where they do physical work. For example, factory workers, builders, and drivers are  working-class</DEF><THESEXA>Most of the people who live round here are working class.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I come from a working-class family – I’m the first one to graduate from college.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blue-collar</EXP><DEF>someone who does physical work, for example in a factory or a mine, and does not work in an office</DEF><THESPROPFORM>blue-collar worker/job/employee</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>His political support comes mainly from blue-collar workers.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>upper-class</EXP><DEF>belonging to the class of people who originally had most of the money and power, especially families that own a lot of land</DEF><THESEXA>Most senior politicians in the UK are from upper-class families.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He spoke with an upper-class accent.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>underclass</EXP><DEF>the lowest social class, who are very poor and may not have jobs, homes etc</DEF><THESEXA>The government has created an underclass who do not feel they have any rights in society.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6e50"><Section><Exponent><EXP>clean</EXP><DEF>without any dirt or marks</DEF><THESEXA>They need clean water to drink.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t have any clean clothes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pure</EXP><DEF>water or air that is pure does not contain any dirt, pollution, or bacteria</DEF><THESEXA>I breathed in the pure mountain air.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sterile</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈsteraɪl</PRON><AMEVARPRON>-rəl</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>completely clean, with no bacteria, and therefore safe for medical or scientific use</DEF><THESEXA>Place a sterile bandage on the wound.</THESEXA><THESEXA>sterile needles</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spotless</EXP><DEF>completely clean – used mainly about rooms and clothes</DEF><THESEXA>Her kitchen is always spotless.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pristine</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈprɪstiːn</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>completely clean and new-looking</DEF><THESEXA>He wore a pristine white shirt.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>immaculate</EXP><DEF>as clean and tidy as it is possible to be</DEF><THESEXA>The soldiers’ uniforms have to be immaculate.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spick and span</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>clean and tidy, especially after having just been cleaned</DEF><THESEXA>By the end of the day, the whole place was spick and span.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6e40"><Section><Exponent><EXP>clean</EXP><DEF>to remove dirt from something</DEF><THESEXA>I need to clean the car.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Clean the mud off your shoes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wash</EXP><DEF>to clean something with water and usually soap</DEF><THESEXA>She’s washing her hair.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There’s nowhere to wash your clothes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wipe</EXP><DEF>to clean a surface with a cloth, often a wet cloth</DEF><THESEXA>Wipe the worktop when you’ve finished cooking.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scrub</EXP><DEF>to wash something by rubbing it hard, especially with a brush</DEF><THESEXA>They made her scrub the floor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rinse</EXP><DEF>to remove dirt from something using water, especially after washing it with soap</DEF><THESEXA>Rinse your hair thoroughly after shampooing it.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cleanse</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to clean your skin, using water or a special cream</DEF><THESEXA>There are many products available for cleansing your skin.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bathe</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>beɪð</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to clean a wound or a part of your body with water</DEF><THESEXA>Bathe the cut and put a plaster on it.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>do the dishes</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>do the washing-up</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to wash plates and pans after a meal</DEF><THESEXA>Who’s going to help me do the dishes?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>do the laundry</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>do the washing</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to wash clothes</DEF><THESEXA>On Tuesdays, he does the washing.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6dd6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>climb</EXP><DEF>to move up, down, or across something using your hands and feet</DEF><THESEXA>Most kids love climbing trees.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Several fans climbed onto the roof of the arena to get a better view.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She climbed down the ladder.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ascend</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to climb up something</DEF><THESEXA>He began to ascend the narrow winding staircase.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the first man to ascend Mount Everest</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go up</EXP><DEF>to climb up something such as a slope or stairs</DEF><THESEXA>He went up the steps to the platform.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sonia was quiet as they went up the hill.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scale</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to climb to the top of something such as a high wall or <NonDV><REFHWD>fence</REFHWD></NonDV></DEF><THESEXA>Somehow the men had scaled the twenty-foot wall without setting off the alarm.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Protestors scaled the walls of the building and hung banners.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Rescuers had to scale a one-thousand-foot cliff before they could reach the injured climber.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>clamber</EXP><DEF>to climb somewhere with difficulty, using your hands to help you</DEF><THESEXA>At last we saw the two girls clambering down the slope to safety.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Everyone clambered onto the back of the truck.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scramble</EXP><DEF>to climb somewhere quickly and with difficulty, using your hands to help you, especially when you are walking</DEF><THESEXA>They scrambled up the steep rocky bank.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6db9"><Section><Exponent><EXP>close</EXP><DEF>to stop being open, or to make something stop being open. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6d6f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>cloud</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a white or grey mass in the sky that forms from very small drops of water</DEF><THESEXA>The storm was still a way off but black clouds were gathering.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>Thick cloud</COLLOINEXA> obscured the top of the hill.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fog</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>very thick cloud near the ground which is difficult to see through</DEF><THESEXA>The cars crashed into each other in <COLLOINEXA>thick fog</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The fog had lifted <GLOSS>disappeared</GLOSS>slightly.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mist</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>light cloud near the ground that makes it difficult for you to see very far. <DEFBOLD>Mist</DEFBOLD> is usually not as thick as <DEFBOLD>fog</DEFBOLD>. You often get mist near areas of water or mountains</DEF><THESEXA>The morning mist was lifting and the sun was coming up.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A grey mist hung over the water.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The hills were shrouded in mist <GLOSS>surrounded by mist</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>haze</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>singular, U</GRAM><DEF>smoke, dust, or mist in the air which is difficult to see through</DEF><THESEXA>He saw the horses coming towards him through a haze of dust.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The road shimmered in the <COLLOINEXA>heat haze</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A pale blue haze hung over the far-off mountains.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>smog</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>dirty air that looks like a mixture of smoke and fog, caused by smoke from cars and factories in cities</DEF><THESEXA>The city is covered in smog for much of the year.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A smog warning was issued for parts of Southern Oregon.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>thundercloud</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a large dark cloud that you see before or during a storm</DEF><THESEXA>He watched the thunderclouds roll across the valley.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>vapour trail</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>vapor trail</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>the white line that is left in the sky by a plane</DEF><THESEXA>High overhead, a jet left its vapour trail across the blue sky. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6d4c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>cloudy</EXP><DEF>with a lot of clouds</DEF><THESEXA>The weather was cold and cloudy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>cloudy skies</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grey</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>gray</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>grey in colour, because there are dark clouds – used especially in written descriptions</DEF><THESEXA>Mary looked out at the cold grey sky.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>overcast</EXP><DEF>dark and completely covered with clouds</DEF><THESEXA>a chilly overcast day</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>leaden</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a leaden sky is grey and full of dark clouds</DEF><THESEXA>Snow fell from a leaden sky.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gloomy</EXP><DEF>dark and cloudy, in a depressing way</DEF><THESEXA>The gloomy weather shows no sign of improving.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>foggy</EXP><DEF>with thick low cloud that is difficult to see through. You use <DEFBOLD>foggy </DEFBOLD> especially about low-lying places</DEF><THESEXA>a foggy day in London in November</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>misty</EXP><DEF>with light low cloud that is difficult to see through. You use <DEFBOLD>misty</DEFBOLD> especially about places that are next to water or in the mountains</DEF><THESEXA>a cold misty morning</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hazy</EXP><DEF>with air that looks cloudy, because there is smoke, dust, or mist in it</DEF><THESEXA>hazy sunshine</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6d27"><Section><Exponent><EXP>clumsy</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>moving or doing things in a careless way, especially so that you drop things, knock into things etc</DEF><THESEXA>She was very clumsy and was always walking into doors.</THESEXA><THESEXA>With clumsy fingers he took out a pack of cigarettes and tried to light one.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>awkward</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>moving in a way that does not seem relaxed or comfortable</DEF><THESEXA>Her husband always looked a bit awkward when he was dancing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an awkward teenager</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ungainly</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>moving in a way that is not graceful – used especially about people or things that are big</DEF><THESEXA>The ostrich is rather an ungainly bird.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She collapsed into the chair in an ungainly manner.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>uncoordinated</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>not able to control your movements very well, and therefore not very good at physical activities</DEF><THESEXA>When she first starting playing tennis, her movements were slow and uncoordinated.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>accident-prone</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>often having accidents</DEF><THESEXA>I was very accident-prone as a child and was always having to go to hospital.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be all fingers and thumbs</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><Variant><LEXVAR>be all thumbs</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>to be unable to control your fingers very well, so that you cannot do something</DEF><THESEXA>‘Do you want some help unwrapping that?’ ‘Yes please, I’m all fingers and thumbs today.’</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6cac"><Section><Exponent><EXP>colleague</EXP><DEF>someone who you work with in a company or organization, for example someone working in the same office, or someone teaching in the same school</DEF><THESEXA>Friends and former colleagues described him as a kind and caring man.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She discovered that her male colleagues were earning more than she was.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>workmate</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>someone who you work with. <DEFBOLD>Workmate</DEFBOLD> is more informal than <DEFBOLD>colleague</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>He went out for a drink with his workmates.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>coworker</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>someone who you work with</DEF><THESEXA>I was sad to say goodbye to all of my coworkers.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>associate</EXP><DEF>someone who you work with, especially another businessman or businesswoman</DEF><THESEXA>They are close friends and business associates.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>staff</EXP><DEF>all the people who work for an organization</DEF><THESEXA>The company employs a total of 520 staff.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a staff meeting</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6ca0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>collect</EXP><DEF>to get things of the same type from different places and bring them together</DEF><THESEXA>She collects stamps <GLOSS>as a hobby</GLOSS>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They have collected 650 signatures for their petition.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gather</EXP><DEF>to collect information from different places, or to collect crops, flowers, wood etc, especially from the ground</DEF><THESEXA>Computers make it easier to gather information.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The men gathered firewood.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assemble</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to collect something such as information in an organized way</DEF><THESEXA>When all the evidence is assembled, we will write our report.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>build up</EXP><DEF>to gradually collect more things of the same type over time</DEF><THESEXA>He has built up one of the country’s finest collections of art.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>accumulate</EXP><DEF>to gradually get more and more of something such as money, possessions, or knowledge, over time. <DEFBOLD>Accumulate</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>build up</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>He is driven by the desire to accumulate wealth.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>amass</EXP><DEF>to collect a large amount of something such as money, information, or knowledge, over time</DEF><THESEXA>Carnegie amassed a fortune in the steel industry.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>run up a bill/debt/loss</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to allow a debt to increase quickly, especially by spending too much</DEF><THESEXA>He ran up huge gambling debts.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6c8a" fullpage="yes"><Section><Exponent><EXP>colour</EXP><DEF>red, blue, yellow etc</DEF><THESEXA>Blue is my favourite <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6c5f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>comfortable</EXP><DEF>making you feel physically relaxed, and not too hard, hot, cold etc. Also used about </EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>cozy</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>comfortable and warm – used especially about small rooms, houses etc</DEF><THESEXA>There’s a cosy lounge with a real <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6c31"><Section><Exponent><EXP>comment</EXP><DEF>something that you say or write in order to give your opinion</DEF><THESEXA>Does anyone have any comments?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Readers are invited to send in their comments and suggestions.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>remark</EXP><DEF>something that you say</DEF><THESEXA>Just ignore them if they start making rude remarks.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m not sure what he meant by that last remark.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>point</EXP><DEF>something that someone mentions about a subject in a discussion, argument, article etc</DEF><THESEXA>That’s an interesting point, Steve.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He raises <GLOSS>mentions</GLOSS> a number of important points in his paper.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>observation</EXP><DEF>a comment in which you say what you think or have noticed about something</DEF><THESEXA>Karl Marx made the observation that history repeats itself first as tragedy, second as farce.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>aside</EXP><DEF>a comment made in a low voice, that you intend only certain people to hear</DEF><THESEXA>‘Is that true?’ she whispered in an aside to Don.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>quip</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>kwɪp</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>a clever and amusing comment</DEF><THESEXA>She knew she should reply with some light-hearted quip.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dig</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a comment you make to annoy or criticize someone</DEF><THESEXA>I’m tired of her little digs at me.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>taunt</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>tɔːnt</PRON><AMEVARPRON>tɒːnt</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>a comment intended to make someone angry or upset</DEF><THESEXA>The fans made racist taunts throughout the game.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6bff"><Section><Exponent><EXP>common</EXP><DEF>if something is common, there are a lot of them</DEF><THESEXA>Jones is a very common name in Great Britain.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Foxes are common in the area.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Personal computers are nearly as common in American homes as televisions.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>widespread</EXP><DEF>happening in a lot of places or done by a lot of people</DEF><THESEXA>Racism is much more widespread than people imagine.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The report claimed that the problem of police brutality was widespread.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the widespread availability of antibiotics </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>commonplace</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>common in a particular place or time – used especially when saying that this seems surprising or unusual</DEF><THESEXA>Crimes such as robbery are commonplace in big cities.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Expensive foreign cars are commonplace in this Chicago suburb.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>prevalent</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>common in a place or among a group of people – used especially about illnesses, problems, or ideas</DEF><THESEXA>Flu is most prevalent during the winter months.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Depression remains one of the most prevalent health disorders in the US.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This belief is more prevalent among men than women.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rife</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>raɪf</PRON></PronCodes><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>very common – used about illnesses or problems</DEF><THESEXA>AIDS is rife in some parts of the world.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ubiquitous</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>juːˈbɪkwɪtəs</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal </REGISTERLAB><DEF>very common and seen in many different places – often used humorously in written descriptions</DEF><THESEXA>He was carrying the ubiquitous MP3 player. </THESEXA><THESEXA>In Britain, CCTV cameras are ubiquitous.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sth is everywhere</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when saying that you can see something a lot in many different places</DEF><THESEXA>Images of the dictator were everywhere.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Microchips seem to be everywhere these days – even in washing machines.</THESEXA><THESEXA>One of the first things you notice in Amsterdam are the bicycles – they’re everywhere.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6bd9"><Section><Exponent><EXP>communicate</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I,T</GRAM><DEF>to exchange information or have a conversation with someone, by telephone, letter etc, or by seeing them</DEF><THESEXA>Now that we live in different cities, we communicate by e-mail.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He is able to communicate using a special keyboard, which is connected to a computer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This message is not being communicated to staff. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>contact</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to write to, phone, or email someone especially for the first time, in order to give or ask for information</DEF><THESEXA>Neighbours contacted police after seeing a man with a gun.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She refused to comment when contacted by reporters from the local newspaper.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get in touch (with sb)</EXP><DEF>to write to, telephone, or email someone, especially someone who you do not see very often</DEF><THESEXA>I really ought to get in touch with Paula. It's been months since we last spoke.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You've got my phone number if you need to get in touch.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get hold of sb</EXP><DEF>to succeed in contacting someone by telephone after trying several times</DEF><THESEXA>Where have you been? I've been trying to get hold of you all week.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It's no use trying to phone Linda at work – she's impossible to get hold of.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>approach</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to contact someone that you do not know or have not contacted before, in order to offer them something or ask them for something</DEF><THESEXA>He has already been approached by several professional football teams.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company confirmed that it had been approached about a merger.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6bb5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>company</EXP><DEF>an organization that makes or sells something, or provides a service</DEF><THESEXA>big oil companies</THESEXA><THESEXA>telephone companies</THESEXA><THESEXA>He runs a software company.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>firm</EXP><DEF>a company, especially one that provides a service rather than producing goods</DEF><THESEXA>a law firm</THESEXA><THESEXA>a firm of accountants</THESEXA><THESEXA>a security firm</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>business</EXP><DEF> a company – often used when talking about a company that employs only a small number of people</DEF><THESEXA>She set up her own catering business.</THESEXA><THESEXA>small businesses</THESEXA><THESEXA>a family business</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>corporation</EXP><DEF> a large company that often includes several smaller companies</DEF><THESEXA>IBM is one of the biggest corporations in the world.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>multinational</EXP><DEF> a very large company with offices in many different countries</DEF><THESEXA>American multinationals are establishing research and development facilities across the developing world.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conglomerate</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>kənˈɡlɒmərət</PRON><AMEVARPRON>-ˈɡlɑː-</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>a very large company that consists of several different companies which have joined together</DEF><THESEXA>The company was taken over by a German media conglomerate.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>giant</EXP><DEF>a word used mainly by newspapers for a very large company</DEF><THESEXA>Their clients include the retail giant, Wal-Mart.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>subsidiary</EXP><DEF> a company that is owned by a larger company</DEF><THESEXA>The company runs its New York operations through a US subsidiary.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6ba0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>compare</EXP><DEF>to consider two or more things or people, in order to show how they are similar or different</DEF><THESEXA>Scientists compared the results of both experiments.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The woman’s body was identified by comparing her teeth with dental records.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make/draw a comparison</EXP><DEF>to compare two or more things or people and say how they are similar</DEF><THESEXA>In her article, she makes a comparison between people’s lives now and 50 years ago.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It is possible to draw a comparison between  the two poets’ work.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>draw an analogy</EXP><DEF>to say that two situations are similar – a rather formal use</DEF><THESEXA>Some people have attempted to draw an analogy between America’s invasion of Iraq  and the war in Vietnam.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>draw a parallel</EXP><DEF>to say that some features of things are similar, especially things that are actually very different – a rather formal use</DEF><THESEXA>He draws a parallel between football and religion.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Parallels can be drawn between her work and that of Picasso.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>contrast</EXP><DEF>to compare two things, situations etc, in order to show how they are different from each other</DEF><THESEXA>In her novel she contrasts the lives of two families in very different circumstances.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make/draw a distinction between</EXP><DEF>to say that you think two things are very different</DEF><THESEXA>The author draws a distinction between allowing death to occur, and causing it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It is important to make a distinction between people’s fears about crime and the amount of crime that really happens.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>measure sb/sth against sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to compare two people or things, in order to judge which is better, bigger etc</DEF><THESEXA>As a young actress, she was nervous of being measured against her famous father.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Murray’s progress is often measured against that of Nadal.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.6735c3fd.11b5f44f24f.-3732"><Section><Exponent><EXP>competition</EXP><DEF>an organized event in which people or teams compete against each other, especially in order to win a prize</DEF><THESEXA>My sister entered a dance competition.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The winner of the competition will be announced in June.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>contest</EXP><DEF>a competition in which people do an activity, and a group of judges decide the winner</DEF><THESEXA>a beauty contest</THESEXA><THESEXA>a contest to find America’s strongest man</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>championship</EXP><DEF>an important sports competition to find the best player or team in the world or in a particular area</DEF><THESEXA>the European Athletics Championship</THESEXA><THESEXA>Brazil went on to win the world championship.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tournament</EXP><DEF>a competition in a sport or game, in which many players or teams compete against each other until there is one winner</DEF><THESEXA>a golf tournament</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>quiz</EXP><DEF>a competition in which people have to answer questions</DEF><THESEXA>a TV quiz show</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>part of a competition</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>round</EXP><DEF>one of the parts of a competition that you have to finish or win before you can go on to the next part</DEF><THESEXA>Henman lost in the second round of the competition.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>heat</EXP><DEF>one of several races or competitions whose winners then compete against each other</DEF><THESEXA>She came second in her heat, with a time of 23.2 seconds.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6b5d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>complain</EXP><DEF>to say that you are annoyed, unhappy, or not satisfied about something or someone</DEF><THESEXA>Several customers complained about the service they received.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘I wish you’d stop telling me what to do,’ she complained.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make a complaint</EXP><DEF>to formally complain about something to someone in authority</DEF><THESEXA>His parents made a complaint to the head teacher.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>protest</EXP><DEF>to complain about something that you think is wrong, especially publicly</DEF><THESEXA>Demonstrators were protesting against the war.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>object</EXP><DEF>to say that you oppose or disapprove of something</DEF><THESEXA>Local residents have objected to the plan. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Some teachers objected to the scheme.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grumble</EXP><DEF>to keep complaining in a bad-tempered way about something</DEF><THESEXA>Rail travellers have been grumbling about the increase in ticket prices.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What’s he grumbling about now?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>moan/whine</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><Variant><LINKWORD>also </LINKWORD><LEXVAR>whinge</LEXVAR><PronCodes><PRON>wɪndʒ</PRON></PronCodes><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>to keep complaining in an annoying way</DEF><THESEXA>Everyone was moaning about the hotel food.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Stop whingeing and get on with your work!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>kick up/make a fuss</EXP><DEF>to complain or become angry about something, especially something that is not very important</DEF><THESEXA>The soup wasn’t hot enough, but he didn’t want to make a fuss.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He kicked up such a fuss that they were offered another room.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6b40"><Section><Exponent><EXP>complaint</EXP><DEF>something that you say or write when you are complaining, especially to someone in an official position</DEF><THESEXA>If you have any complaints, please contact our customer relations department.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Our main complaint is the poor standard of service.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If you have a complaint, you should write to the manager.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>make a complaint</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>Some employees are worried about what will happen to them if they make a complaint.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>complaint about</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>Keating was dismissed after complaints about the quality of his work.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>formal complaint</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>Mr Kelly has made a formal complaint against the police.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>letters of complaint</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The commission has so far received nearly 10,000 letters of complaint.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>protest</EXP><DEF>when someone complains publicly about something that they think is wrong or unfair and should not be allowed to the team were dismissed, the rest of them walked out in protest.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>protest against</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The ambassador lodged a formal protest against the proposals.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>ignore somebody’s protests</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>Ignoring my protests, he took off his jacket and wrapped it around my refused to promote her.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>grievance against</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>Anyone who has a legitimate grievance against the company can take it to the arbitration committee.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>air a grievance<GLOSS>discuss a grievance publicly</GLOSS></THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The meetings give employees the opportunity to express their views or air grievances.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6b16"><Section><Exponent><EXP>completely</EXP><DEF>to the greatest degree or in every way</DEF><THESEXA>That’s a completely different subject.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Bad weather had completely destroyed the crops.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>totally/absolutely</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>completely</DEF><THESEXA>I’m totally exhausted.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s absolutely right.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>entirely</EXP><DEF>completely – used especially in negative sentences or after ‘almost’</DEF><THESEXA>I’m not entirely sure.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The community is almost entirely dependent on farming.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>utterly</EXP><DEF>completely – used especially to emphasize strongly disapproving words</DEF><THESEXA>It was utterly impossible to work in the house.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The whole thing is utterly absurd.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fully</EXP><DEF>completely – used especially to show that you completely understand something or that you have all the information you need</DEF><THESEXA>He was fully aware of what was happening.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The disease is still not fully understood.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6b00"><Section><Exponent><EXP>complicated</EXP><DEF>consisting of a lot of different parts or details and therefore difficult to understand</DEF><THESEXA>The rules of the game seemed very complicated.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I didn’t realize programming the VCR would be so complicated.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The brain is like a very powerful, very complicated computer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a complicated issue</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>complex</EXP><DEF>a complex process, relationship etc is difficult to understand because it has a lot of parts that are all connected in different ways</DEF><THESEXA>The chemical processes involved are extremely complex.</THESEXA><THESEXA> the complex relationship between government and the media</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>elaborate</EXP><DEF>having a lot of parts or details and very carefully planned, but often more complicated than is necessary</DEF><THESEXA>Mike had worked out an elaborate system for categorizing his collection of DVDs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The plan to kidnap her had become even more elaborate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sociologists have been coming up with increasingly elaborate theories to explain unsafe sexual practices.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>involved</EXP><DEF>very long and complicated – use this especially about something that you think should be made simpler</DEF><THESEXA>The system for choosing candidates is very involved, and I won’t go into it here.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Adopting a child can be a long involved process.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>convoluted</EXP><DEF>too complicated and difficult to understand – used especially about someone’s language or arguments, or about a system</DEF><THESEXA>convoluted sentences</THESEXA><THESEXA>Procedures for government funding have become more convoluted.</THESEXA><THESEXA>James’s books are full of long paragraphs and convoluted sentences, which many people do not find appealing.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>intricate</EXP><DEF>having a lot of small parts or details – used especially about something that is cleverly designed or made</DEF><THESEXA>Lasers are used to cut intricate designs in the metal.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The farmers use an intricate system of drainage canals.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the intricate workings of a watch</THESEXA><THESEXA>intricate patterns of coloured marble</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6ad6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>compromise</EXP><DEF>to reach an agreement with someone in which both of you accept less than you really want</DEF><THESEXA>The employers will have to be ready to compromise if they want to avoid a strike.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The president might be willing to compromise on defense spending.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>meet sb halfway</EXP><DEF>to do part of what someone wants if they do part of what you want, in order to reach an agreement</DEF><THESEXA>Is the president ready to meet these senators halfway?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The asking price is £270,000. If the sellers won’t accept £250,000, maybe they would be prepared to meet us halfway and take £260,000? </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make concessions</EXP><DEF>if one group in a disagreement makes concessions, they let their opponents have something that they are asking for, in order to reach an agreement</DEF><THESEXA>The company has already made significant concessions on pay and conditions.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is a policy of making no concessions to terrorists.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6ac0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>compulsory</EXP><DEF>if something is compulsory, you must do it because of a rule or law</DEF><THESEXA>Maths and English are compulsory for all students.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Compulsory education was introduced in 1870.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Wearing a seat belt is compulsory.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It is now compulsory for anyone claiming state benefit to register with a job centre.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>obligatory</EXP><GRAM>not usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>if something is obligatory, you must do it because of a rule or law. <DEFBOLD>Obligatory</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>compulsory</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>It is now obligatory for all competitors to wear face protectors.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Evening dress is usual, but not obligatory.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Military service is obligatory for all men between 18 and 27.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mandatory</EXP><DEF>if something is mandatory, you must do it because it is the law. <DEFBOLD>Mandatory</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>compulsory</DEFBOLD> and sounds stronger</DEF><THESEXA>Drug smuggling carries a mandatory death sentence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In some countries, wearing helmets is mandatory for all cyclists.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>not compulsory</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>optional</EXP><DEF>if something is optional, you can choose whether to do it or not</DEF><THESEXA>You don’t have to do French – it’s optional.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The holiday price includes optional excursions such as an evening cruise on the lake.</THESEXA><THESEXA>General Studies is optional for sixth-form students.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>voluntary</EXP><DEF>done because you want to do it and not because you have to – used especially about things that you do because you think they will help other people or be useful</DEF><THESEXA>The programme is entirely voluntary, and no one will be forced to join.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The charity is funded by voluntary donations.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6a9b"><Section><SECHEADING>Actions when using a computer</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>start up/boot up </EXP><DEF>to make a computer start computer</DEF><THESEXA>You can download MP3 files.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>upload </EXP><DEF>to move information, pictures, or music from your computer to a different computer across the Internet</DEF><THESEXA>Sites such as YouTube allow you to upload your own videos.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>open </EXP><DEF>to make a file or program ready to use</DEF><THESEXA>Open a new file and type in the information.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scroll up/down</EXP><DEF>to move information on a computer screen up or down so that you can read it</DEF><THESEXA>Scroll down to <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6a58"><Section><Exponent><EXP>conclusion</EXP><DEF>something you decide after considering all the information you have</DEF><THESEXA>Doctors failed to reach a conclusion on the exact cause of death.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The report’s main conclusion was that global warming was a serious threat.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>findings</EXP><DEF>the information that someone has discovered as a result of their study, work etc</DEF><THESEXA>Surveys conducted in other countries reported similar findings.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>result</EXP><DEF>the answers that are provided by a scientific study or test</DEF><THESEXA>Have you had the result of your blood test yet?</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6a46"><HEADING>Sense 4</HEADING><Section><SECHEADING>the condition of something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>condition</EXP><DEF>how something looks and whether it is damaged, working etc or not</DEF><THESEXA>The price of used cars varies according to their condition.</THESEXA><THESEXA>How well your plants will grow depends on the quality and condition of the soil.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The house is in very good condition.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>state</EXP><DEF>the condition of something at a particular time – use this especially when something is in bad condition because it has not been well looked after</DEF><THESEXA>One of the things people complain of most is the state of the sidewalks.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When I got back home, I was horrified to see what a terrible state the kitchen was in.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>in bad condition</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>in (a) bad/terrible/awful condition</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>in a bad state</LEXVAR><GEO>especially BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>if something is in bad condition, it is damaged, dirty, not working properly, etc</DEF><THESEXA>The road was in a very bad condition.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The inspectors said the bridge was in a bad state and potentially dangerous.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shabby</EXP><DEF>used especially about clothes, furniture, or buildings that are in bad condition because they are old and have been used a lot</DEF><THESEXA>His clothes were shabby and ill-fitting.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They lived in a shabby one-room apartment.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tattered</EXP><DEF>used about clothes or recognition.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dilapidated</EXP><DEF>used about a building that is in very bad condition because it has not been looked after</DEF><THESEXA>He shared a dilapidated house with five other people.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The hotel looked slightly dilapidated.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>run-down</EXP><DEF>used about a building or area that is in bad condition, especially because the people who live there do not have enough money to look after it properly</DEF><THESEXA>He found lodgings at a run-down motel.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We lived in a run-down part of the city.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>derelict</EXP><DEF>used about something such as a house or piece of land that is in very bad condition, because it has been empty for a very long time</DEF><THESEXA>In the middle of town is a derelict building that used to be the school.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The land behind the factory is stony and derelict.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>battered</EXP><DEF>used about something that is old and in bad condition because it has been used a lot and treated roughly</DEF><THESEXA>There was nothing in his office except for a few battered chairs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Alex and Lisa used to drive around town in a battered old Fiat Uno.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rickety</EXP><DEF>used about furniture and other structures that are in such bad condition that they look as if they would break if you tried to use them</DEF><THESEXA>The staircase was old and rickety.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They sat around the card table on rickety old chairs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a rickety bamboo fence</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>clapped-out</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><Variant><LEXVAR>beat-up</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>used about a vehicle or machine that is so old that it does not work properly</DEF><THESEXA>She drives an old beat-up Ford.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was using a clapped-out old typewriter.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be falling apart</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if something is falling apart, it is gradually breaking into pieces, because it is old or badly made</DEF><THESEXA>I need some new shoes. These are falling apart.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be on its last legs</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if a vehicle or machine is on its last legs, it has been used so much and is in such bad condition that you will soon not be able to use it anymore</DEF><THESEXA>The washing machine was on its last legs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have seen better days</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if something has seen better days, it is not in as good condition as it was</DEF><THESEXA>The carpets, curtains, and cushions had all seen better days but still looked quite pretty.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She lived in an old Victorian house that had certainly seen better days.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>in good condition</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>in good condition</EXP><DEF>something that is  in good condition  is not broken and has no marks or other things wrong with it</DEF><THESEXA>The car hadn’t been used much, and was in very good condition.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The charity is accepting toys and clothing in good condition.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in good shape</EXP><DEF>in good condition – used especially about a person or part of their body, or about something that has had a lot of use or is rather old</DEF><THESEXA>Doctor Morrissey told her that her leg was healing well, and was now in very good shape.</THESEXA><THESEXA>To avoid accidents, it’s important to check that all your tools are in good shape before starting.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in perfect/mint condition</EXP><DEF>something that is in perfect  or mint condition  looks as good or works as well as when it was new, especially because it has not been used or touched very much</DEF><THESEXA>The book is over 100 years old, but it’s still in perfect condition.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The car had been kept in the garage for 20 years and was in mint condition.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>as good as new</EXP><DEF>something that is as good as new is almost as good as when it was new – used about things that have recently been cleaned or repaired</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve just had the bike <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-69bc"><Section><Exponent><EXP>confidence</EXP><DEF>the feeling that you have the ability to do things well, and to not make mistakes or be nervous in new situations</DEF><THESEXA>You need patience and confidence to be a good teacher.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>have the confidence to do something</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>‘We have the confidence to beat Brazil,’ said Sampson.</THESEXA><THESEXA>After the accident it took a long time before she had the confidence to get back in a car again.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>full of confidence<GLOSS>very confident</GLOSS></THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>I went into the test full of confidence, but it was more difficult than I had imagined.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>self-confidence</EXP><DEF>a strong belief that you can do things well and that other people will like you, which means you behave confidently in most situations</DEF><THESEXA>He’s new in the job but he has plenty of self-confidence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Studies show that girls tend to lose some of their self-confidence in their teenage years.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Students who get some kind of work experience develop greater self-confidence and better communication skills.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>morale</EXP><DEF>the level of confidence, satisfaction, and hope that people feel, especially a group of people who work together</DEF><THESPROPFORM>low/high morale</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>Morale among the soldiers has been low.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>keep up morale<GLOSS>keep it at a high level</GLOSS></THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>They sang songs to keep up their morale until the rescuers arrived.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assurance/self-assurance</EXP><DEF>a feeling of calm confidence in your own abilities, especially because you have a lot of experience</DEF><THESEXA>She envied the older woman’s assurance.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Danby spoke to the committee with the self-assurance of an expert.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>self-esteem</EXP><DEF>the feeling that you are someone who deserves to be liked and respected</DEF><THESEXA>Getting a job did a lot for her self-esteem.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sports should build a child’s self-esteem, not damage it.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6993"><Section><Exponent><EXP>confident</EXP><DEF>sure that you have the ability to do something well or deal with situations successfully</DEF><THESEXA>She seemed confident that she would pass.</THESEXA><THESEXA>his calm and confident manner</THESEXA><THESEXA>George was very confident about his abilities as a writer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Baldwin is confident of victory in this year’s senate race.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>self-confident/self-assured</EXP><DEF>confident, and not shy or nervous in self-confident as a public speaker.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>supremely self-confident</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>She was supremely self-confident, with the gift of being able to talk on any subject whenever the camera was rolling. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>self-possessed</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>confident, calm, and in control of your feelings even in a difficult situation</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assertive</EXP><DEF>confident enough to say what you think and want, so that people take notice of you</DEF><THESEXA>The course helps women learn how to be more assertive in the workplace.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sure of yourself</EXP><DEF>confident that you are right, even when other people do not agree with you</DEF><THESEXA>He sounded so herself.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>extrovert</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>someone who behaves in a confident way in social situations and likes talking and being with other people</DEF><THESEXA>Most actors are natural extroverts.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Jan says her twin babies are completely different: Kelly is a real extrovert while Jessie is quiet and had no friends, and lacked confidence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>While girls lack confidence, boys often overestimate their abilities.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shy</EXP><DEF>not confident about meeting or speaking to people who you do not know</DEF><THESEXA>Jane is a quiet shy person.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He had been <COLLOINEXA>painfully shy</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>very shy</GLOSS> as a and unsure of herself.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was only 21 and still very unsure of himself with girls.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>discouraged</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>dɪsˈkʌrɪdʒd</PRON><AMEVARPRON>-ˈkɜːr-</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>not confident that you can succeed, because you have had problems trying to do something</DEF><THESEXA>Students can become discouraged very easily if they are not given the help they need.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was very discouraged at the time, but I still hoped we could find a solution.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>discouraged by</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>Discouraged by her failed marriages, she gradually withdrew from the world.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>discouraged about</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>Hartman was so discouraged about the way his performing career was going, that he gave up acting for losing a series of games.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a demoralized work force</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many employees became demoralized and cynical when the company announced another round of job cuts.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>utterly/thoroughly demoralized</THESPROPFORM></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6939"><Section><Exponent><EXP>confirm</EXP><DEF>if a piece of new information confirms  an idea or belief that people already have, it shows that it is definitely true</DEF><THESEXA>Police have found new evidence that confirms his story.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The discovery seems to confirm that people lived here over 10,000 years ago.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>prove</EXP><DEF>to show that something is definitely true by providing facts</DEF><THESEXA>DNA tests proved that he was the father of the child.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I think we’ve proved that we are a good team.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>back up</EXP><DEF>to provide additional information to help prove that a statement, belief, or explanation is correct</DEF><THESEXA>When you write a history essay, you should back up all your points with facts.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Wright was accused of pretending to be injured, and this was backed up by video evidence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>support</EXP><DEF>to help to prove that a belief, idea etc is probably true</DEF><THESEXA>Do you have any evidence to support these claims?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The notion that women are worse drivers than men is simply not supported by the facts.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bear sth out</EXP><DEF>if facts or information bear out a claim or opinion, they suggest that something is likely to be true</DEF><THESEXA>Most of the available evidence bears out the view that students learn better in small classes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Marriage is not always easy; a fact that is clearly borne out in divorce statistics.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>corroborate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to provide additional information which supports or agrees with something – used in scientific and legal contexts</DEF><THESEXA>Levine claims that a third car was involved in the accident and witnesses have corroborated this.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Professor Carling’s findings have been corroborated by more recent research.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>substantiate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to provide additional information that helps to prove that a statement is correct, especially if the statement is difficult to believe</DEF><THESEXA>A police investigation failed to substantiate the claim that he had been sexually abused.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>validate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to prove that information or results are correct by using scientific tests or very careful checking – used in scientific or technical contexts</DEF><THESEXA>All the information used in this report has been validated by an independent panel of experts.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-68f8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>confused</EXP><DEF>unable to understand what someone is saying or what is happening</DEF><THESEXA>I was rather confused by his question.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She gave me a confused look.</THESEXA><THESEXA>With the huge range of computers on the market, it’s easy to get confused.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She felt laughed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>baffled</EXP><DEF>very confused and unable to understand something, even though you have tried hard for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>Detectives admit they are watched as the man was arrested.</THESEXA><THESEXA>At first she was bewildered by all the noise and activity of the city.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bemused</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>bɪˈmjuːzd</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>confused and surprised by what has happened, or by what someone has said</DEF><THESEXA>Local think straight</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to have difficulty thinking clearly, for example because you are too tired, too busy, or because a lot of things are happening around you</DEF><THESEXA>I can’t think straight with all this noise going on.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-68cc"><Section><Exponent><EXP>confusing</EXP><DEF>unclear and difficult to understand</DEF><THESEXA>a confusing message</THESEXA><THESEXA>The road signs were very confusing and we ended up getting lost.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>puzzling</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>perplexing</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>confusing, especially because something is different from what you expect</DEF><THESEXA>Jan's decision not to take part in the race was very puzzling.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was the trivial details which he found so perplexing.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>baffling</EXP><DEF>extremely difficult to understand even though you have tried for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>Police are close to solving one of Australia's most baffling murder cases.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The failure was baffling, given the success of his previous businesses.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bewildering</EXP><DEF>very confusing, especially because something is strange or new, or because there are a lot of different things</DEF><THESEXA>Brain diseases can cause many bewildering changes in a person's emotions and behaviour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is a bewildering number of mobile phone deals on offer.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-68ac"><Section><Exponent><EXP>consider</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T not in progressive</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to have passive</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to think about someone or something in a particular way, or as being a particular kind of person or thing</DEF><THESEXA>Historically, nursing has been perceived as a job for women, but things have changed now.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The group were perceived to have little real talent.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do other people perceive us as we perceive <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-2c4e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>consist of/be made up of</EXP><DEF>to be formed from two or more things or people</DEF><THESEXA>Lunch consisted of sandwiches and fruit.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The apartment consisted of three rooms.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The audience was largely made up of families.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be composed of</EXP><DEF>to consist of something – used especially when saying which natural substances something contains, or what kind of people are in a group</DEF><THESEXA>Every chemical element is composed of atoms.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Venus’ atmosphere is mainly composed of carbon dioxide.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The team was composed of leading scientists from around the world.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>comprise</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>kəmˈpraɪz</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to consist of the things mentioned. <DEFBOLD>Comprise</DEFBOLD> can also be used to talk about the people or things which form something</DEF><THESEXA>The event comprises a champagne reception, two-course lunch, and a fashion show.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Men still comprise the majority of people who have the disease.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make up</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>constitute</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>to be the things or people that form something</DEF><THESEXA>Women constitute a significant part of the workforce.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Toys make up about 10% of the company’s sales.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6895"><Section><Exponent><EXP>consult</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to get advice from someone who is trained in a particular profession, for example a lawyer or a doctor</DEF><THESEXA>You should consult your lawyer before exchanging contracts.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>see </EXP><DEF>to go to someone and ask them for for their advice, especially a professional person such as a lawyer or doctor. <DEFBOLD>See</DEFBOLD> is the usual word to use in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>I think he needs to see a doctor.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Why don’t you <COLLOINEXA>go and see</COLLOINEXA> an accountant? </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ask sb’s advice</EXP><DEF>to ask someone to tell you what they think you should do in a particular situation</DEF><THESEXA>I asked my teacher’s advice when it was time to apply for colleges.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>check with sb</EXP><DEF>to ask someone whether something is correct or a good idea</DEF><THESEXA>Check with your doctor before starting on a new exercise program.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>turn to sb</EXP><DEF>to go to someone to ask for help, advice, or sympathy</DEF><THESEXA>Many people who were struggling to repay mortgages turned to the Citizens’ Advice Bureau for help.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pick sb’s brains</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to ask someone who knows a lot about something for their ideas about it</DEF><THESEXA>The conference was a great place to meet other managers and pick their brains about issues I was also facing.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6888"><Section><Exponent><EXP>continual</EXP><DEF>continuing for a long time without stopping, or happening many times in a way that is annoying or causes problems</DEF><THESEXA>There has been a continual improvement in standards.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There were continual interruptions all day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The couple were having continual arguments and they decided that the best thing to do was to split up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>continuous</EXP><DEF>continuing without stopping</DEF><THESEXA>a continuous process of change </THESEXA><THESEXA>I've had six continuous hours of meetings.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The machines have remained in continuous service over the last six years without any problem.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>constant</EXP><DEF>used when saying that something does not stop or always stays the same, or that something keeps happening. <DEFBOLD>Constant</DEFBOLD> is often used about things that are worrying, frightening, or difficult</DEF><THESEXA>There is a constant stream of water coming out of the ground.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The plane was traveling at a constant speed of 650 kilometres an hour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The refugees live in constant fear of attack.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is always the constant threat of war.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His illness makes life a constant struggle for him and his parents. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The photographs are a constant reminder <GLOSS>something that makes you keep remembering something</GLOSS> of what happened in Bosnia. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>uninterrupted</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>continuing for a long time without anything stopping it – used especially when it is important for something to continue</DEF><THESEXA>Women with small babies seldom get more than two or three hours of uninterrupted sleep.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The nation has enjoyed nine years of uninterrupted economic growth.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The system helps to maintain an uninterrupted flow of traffic. </THESEXA><THESEXA>an uninterrupted view of the mountains</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>non-stop</EXP><POS>adj, adv</POS><DEF>continuing for a long time without stopping – used especially about rain, journeys, work, or entertainment</DEF><THESEXA>two days of nonstop rain</THESEXA><THESEXA>a nonstop flight to Tokyo</THESEXA><THESEXA>The concert will be seven hours of non-stop entertainment. </THESEXA><THESEXA>They worked non-stop.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>on-going</EXP><DEF>if work or a situation is on-going, it will continue into the future, though there will be pauses in it</DEF><THESEXA>The police said the investigation is on-going. </THESEXA><THESEXA>ongoing negotiations</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>solid</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>used when emphasizing that you do something all through a period of time, with no breaks at all</DEF><THESEXA>After eight solid hours of driving, I was exhausted.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It took two solid weeks of work to fix the tunnel. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6877"><Section><SECHEADING>to continue doing sth</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>continue</EXP><DEF>to not stop doing something that you are <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6829"><Section><Exponent><EXP>control</EXP><DEF>to have power over a country, place, company etc, and decide what happens city.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be in charge of sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to have control over something, or responsibility for a group of people</DEF><THESEXA>She is in charge of training new employees.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I left him in charge of the children while I was out.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>manage</EXP><DEF>to be in charge of a company, especially one that someone else work.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6810"><Section><Exponent><EXP>controversial</EXP><DEF>causing a lot of disagreement and angry argument, especially in the newspapers, on television etc</DEF><THESEXA>Abortion is a controversial subject.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The film was <COLLOINEXA>highly controversial</COLLOINEXA> at the time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>contentious</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>likely to cause a lot of disagreement and argument </DEF><THESEXA>the <COLLOINEXA>contentious issue</COLLOINEXA> of immigration </THESEXA><THESEXA>These claims are <COLLOINEXA>highly contentious</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>debatable/arguable</EXP><DEF>used when you think that something is not certain and that people have different opinions about it, especially in the phrase <DEFBOLD>it is debatable/arguable whether</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>It is debatable whether this book is as good as her previous novel.</THESEXA><THESEXA> It is arguable whether these measures will reduce the overall cost. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Does the treatment work?
