to cook somethingcookto prepare a meal or food for eating by using heatI offered to cook a meal for her.Cook in a hot oven for 25 minutes.maketo make a meal or a particular dish by cooking it or getting all the parts readyJohn was making dinner.I think I’ll make a salad for lunch.prepareto make a meal or a particular dish by getting all the parts ready. Prepare is more formal than makeThe children helped to prepare the evening meal.The dish takes a long time to prepare.rustle upˈrʌsəlinformalto cook a meal or dish quickly using whatever is availableShe soon rustled up a tasty soup.fixespecially AmEto cook or prepare a meal – used about meals you make quicklyWhy don’t you take a nap while I fix dinner?doBrEinformalto make a particular type of foodI could do you an omelette.I was thinking of doing a fish pie when Michael comes.
ways of cooking somethingbaketo cook things such as bread or cakes in an ovenTom baked a cake for my birthday.roastto cook meat or vegetables in an ovenRoast the potatoes for an hour.fryto cook food in hot oilShe was frying some mushrooms.stir-fryto fry small pieces of food while moving them around continuouslystir-fried tofu and bean sproutssautéˈsəʊteɪsoʊˈteɪto fry vegetables for a short time in a small amount of butter or oilSauté the potatoes in butter.grillto cook food over or under strong heatgrilled fishbroilAmEto cook food under heatbroiled fishboilto cook something in very hot waterHe doesn’t even know how to boil an egg.English people seem to love boiled vegetables.steamto cook vegetables over hot waterSteam the rice for 15 minutes.poachto cook food, especially fish or eggs, slowly in hot waterpoached salmontoastto cook the outside surfaces of breadtoasted muffinsbarbecueto cook food on a metal frame over a fire outdoorsI thought we could barbecue some mackerel. microwaveto cook food in a microwave ovenThe beans can be microwaved.

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-66f9"><Section><Exponent><EXP>courage</EXP><DEF>the quality of being brave when you are facing a difficult or dangerous situation, or when you are very ill</DEF><THESEXA>the courage of the soldiers</THESEXA><THESEXA>She showed great courage throughout her illness.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He finally <COLLOINEXA>plucked up the courage</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>found the courage</GLOSS> to ask her for a date.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bravery</EXP><DEF>courage in a dangerous or frightening situation, especially when you are fighting in a war</DEF><THESEXA>He won a medal for bravery during the Iraq war.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>guts</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the courage and determination to do something difficult or unpleasant</DEF><THESEXA>It must have taken a lot of guts for him to say that.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>heroism</EXP><DEF>very great courage in a dangerous situation</DEF><THESEXA>The president praised the heroism of the firefighters.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-66e9"><Section><SECHEADING>In a court</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>defendant</EXP><DEF>the person who is on trial for a crime</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the defence</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>the defense</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>the lawyers who are working for the defendant</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the prosecution</EXP><DEF>the lawyers who are trying to prove that the defendant is guilty</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>judge</EXP><DEF>the official in charge of a court who decides how criminals should be punished</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>jury</EXP><DEF>a group of people, usually 12 people, who listen to the facts and decide whether the defendant is guilty or not guilty</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>witness</EXP><DEF>someone who describes in a court of law what he or she knows about a crime</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>testimony</EXP><DEF>a formal statement made in a court of law about a particular situation or action</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>verdict</EXP><DEF>the decision of the jury as to whether the defendant is guilty or not guilty</DEF></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-644f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>dark</EXP><DEF>if a place is dark, there is little or no light</DEF><THESEXA>The room was very dark.</THESEXA><THESEXA>No, you can’t play outside, it’s too dark.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a dark night with clouds covering the moon.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dimly-lit</EXP><DEF>a dimly-lit building or place is fairly dark because the lights there are not very bright</DEF><THESEXA>a dimly-lit restaurant</THESEXA><THESEXA>The church was dimly lit.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dim</EXP><DEF>a dim light is fairly dark</DEF><THESEXA>The camera can take good pictures even in dim lighting.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The evening sky grew dim.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>darkened</EXP><DEF>a darkened room or building is darker than usual, especially because its lights have been turned off or the curtains have been drawn</DEF><THESEXA>The prisoner lay in a darkened room.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The play starts with a darkened stage, and the sound of a woman singing softly.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gloomy</EXP><DEF>a gloomy place or room is not at all bright or cheerful</DEF><THESEXA>The bar was gloomy and smelled of stale cigar smoke.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>murky</EXP><DEF>dark and difficult to see through – used especially about water</DEF><THESEXA>the murky waters of the lake</THESEXA><THESEXA>I could hardly see him in the murky light of the bar.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pitch-dark/pitch-black</EXP><DEF>completely dark, so that nothing can be seen</DEF><THESEXA>It was pitch-dark inside the shed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shady</EXP><DEF>a shady place is cooler and darker than the area around it, because the light of the sun cannot reach it</DEF><THESEXA>It was nice and shady under the trees.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They found a shady spot for a picnic.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6432"><Section><Exponent><EXP>dead</EXP><DEF>no longer alive</DEF><THESEXA>the bodies of three dead soldiers</THESEXA><THESEXA>Is her father dead?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lifeless</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF> dead or seeming to be dead</DEF><THESEXA>their lifeless bodies</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>late</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> dead – use this as a polite way of talking about someone who has died, especially recently</DEF><THESEXA>Mrs Lombard’s late husband</THESEXA><THESEXA>a gold Cartier bracelet that once belonged to the late American artist Andy Warhol </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>deceased</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>dead</DEF><THESEXA>Her parents, now deceased, disapproved of her marriage.</THESEXA><THESEXA>her deceased husband</THESEXA><THESEXA>They were friends of <COLLOINEXA>the deceased</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>the person who died</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>departed</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>dead – used in order to be polite and avoid saying the word ‘dead’</DEF><THESEXA>They paid their respects to their departed uncle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>his dear departed wife</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gone</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>dead – used especially when someone was alive not long before</DEF><THESEXA>‘Is she gone?’ ‘I’m afraid so.’</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-5165"><Section><Exponent><EXP>deaf</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>physically unable to hear anything, or unable to hear well</DEF><THESEXA>Deaf people use sign language to communicate.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be hard of hearing</EXP><DEF>to have difficulty hearing things, for example because you are old</DEF><THESEXA>You’ll have to speak up – she’s a bit hard of hearing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>subtitles for the hard of hearing</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hearing-impaired</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><POS>adj</POS><DEF>having a permanent physical condition which makes it difficult for you to hear things</DEF><THESEXA>Not all hearing-impaired people are completely deaf.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-53fb"><Section><Exponent><EXP>deal with sth</EXP><DEF>to take the necessary action, especially in order to solve a problem</DEF><THESEXA>We need to deal with problems like pollution and climate change.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I spend most of my day dealing with customer enquiries.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>handle</EXP><DEF>to deal with a problem or difficult situation by making particular decisions – used especially when talking about how well or badly someone does this</DEF><THESEXA>He handled the situation very well.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Most customers were happy with the way their complaints had been handled.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tackle</EXP><DEF>to start to deal with a problem, especially one that is complicated</DEF><THESEXA>We need to tackle the issue of drugs in schools.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The government is introducing new measures to tackle online crime.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>see to/attend to sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to deal with all the practical details of something that needs to be done or organized. <DEFBOLD>Attend to</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>see to</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>My son saw to all the funeral arrangements.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I have some business to attend to.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take care of sth</EXP><DEF>to do the work or make the arrangements that are necessary for something to happen – used especially when you do this for someone else so that they do not have to worry about it</DEF><THESEXA>My secretary takes care of all the paperwork.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>process</EXP><DEF>if a company or organization processes a letter, form etc, they do the things that are usually done as part of their official system, in order to deal with it</DEF><THESEXA>It will take a minimum of 14 days to process your passport application.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I called their office, and they said that my request was still being processed.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6424"><Section><Exponent><EXP>decay</EXP><DEF>to be slowly destroyed by a natural chemical process – use this especially about natural things such as wood or plants, or about teeth</DEF><THESEXA>The leaves decay and enrich the soil.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He had bad breath and decaying teeth.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The fabric slowly began to decay. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rot</EXP><DEF> to decay. <DEFBOLD>Rot</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>decay</DEFBOLD> and is more common in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>The fruit was left to rot on the ground.</THESEXA><THESEXA>rotting teeth</THESEXA><THESEXA>Most of the wood under the paint had rotted.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the smell of rotting vegetation <GLOSS>decaying leaves and plants</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go off</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>if food goes off, it starts to smell bad and is no longer be safe to eat</DEF><THESEXA>I think the milk’s gone off.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The meat smells as if it's gone off.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spoil</EXP><DEF>if food spoils, it starts to decay, so that it is no longer safe to eat. <DEFBOLD>Spoil</DEFBOLD> is more formal and is less common in everyday British English than <DEFBOLD>go off</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Food left in the sun will quickly start to spoil.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go mouldy</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>moldy</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to begin to have a soft green or black substance growing on the surface of the food, so that it is not good to eat anymore</DEF><THESEXA>Ugh, the cheese has gone mouldy!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>decompose</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> to decay – use this especially about dead plants or flesh</DEF><THESEXA>leaves decomposing on the forest floor</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>putrefy</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to decay and have a very bad smell – use this especially about flesh or plants</DEF><THESEXA>After two days, the body was already beginning to putrefy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>putrefying meat</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>biodegrade</EXP><DEF>to decay naturally into substances that do not harm the environment – use this especially about man-made materials and chemicals</DEF><THESEXA>Unlike many other materials, plastic does not biodegrade.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6419"><Section><Exponent><EXP>deceive</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make someone who trusts you believe something that is not true</DEF><THESEXA>This was a deliberate attempt to deceive the public.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trick</EXP><DEF>to make someone believe something that is not true, in order to get something from them or make them do something</DEF><THESEXA>A man posing as an insurance agent had tricked her out of thousands of dollars.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fool</EXP><DEF>to make someone believe something that is not true by using a clever but simple trick</DEF><THESEXA>His hairpiece doesn’t fool anyone.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mislead</EXP><DEF>to make people believe something that is not true, by deliberately not giving them all the facts, or by saying something that is only partly true</DEF><THESEXA>The company was accused of misleading customers about the nutritional value of the product.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dupe</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to trick or deceive someone, especially so that they become involved in someone else’s dishonest activity without realizing it</DEF><THESEXA>The spies duped government and military officials alike.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>con</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to trick someone, especially by telling them something that is not true</DEF><THESEXA>I’m pretty good at judging people; I didn’t think he was trying to con me.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-63df"><Section><Exponent><EXP>decide</EXP><DEF>to make a choice to do something</DEF><THESEXA>We decided to send our son to a boarding school.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I decided to go home early.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make up your mind</EXP><DEF>to decide something, especially after thinking about it for a long time. <DEFBOLD>Make up your mind</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>decide</DEFBOLD> and is mainly used in spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>Have you made up your mind about where you’ll go on holiday?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>choose to do sth</EXP><DEF>to decide to do something – especially when this is different from what people expect or tell you to do</DEF><THESEXA>She chose to ignore my advice.</THESEXA><THESEXA>More young couples are choosing not to marry.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make a decision</EXP><DEF>to decide after thinking carefully about something, especially about something that is very important</DEF><THESEXA>They made a decision not to have children.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>resolve</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to decide that you will definitely do something, especially because you think it will be better for you, or because of your past experiences</DEF><THESEXA>She resolved to work hard at school.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>determine</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to officially decide what something shall be</DEF><THESEXA>Each hospital can determine its own pay rates.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come down in favour of sth</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>come down in favor of sth</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to decide to support a particular plan, argument etc – used especially about groups of people</DEF><THESEXA> Eight of the ten committee members came down in favour of the changes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come to/reach a decision</EXP><DEF>to officially decide about something important after discussing and carefully considering it – used especially about groups of people</DEF><THESEXA>After two hours of discussion, the committee had still not come to a decision on any of the proposals.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-63c7"><Section><Exponent><EXP>decoration</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C usually plural</GRAM><DEF>something pretty that you put in a place or ornament</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>knick-knacks</EXP><GRAM>plural</GRAM><DEF>small inexpensive objects used to decorate a room</DEF><THESEXA>The shop sold cheap knick-knacks for <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-63be"><Section><Exponent><EXP>decrease</EXP><DEF>to become less in number or amount</DEF><THESEXA>The average rainfall has decreased by around 30 percent.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go down</EXP><DEF>to decrease. <DEFBOLD>Go down</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>decrease</DEFBOLD> and is the usual word to use in conversation</DEF><THESEXA>Unemployment has gone down in the past few months.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>decline</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to decrease – used with numbers or amounts, or about the level or standard of something</DEF><THESEXA>The standard of living has declined.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Support for the government is  steadily declining.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Salaries have declined by around 4.5%.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>diminish</EXP><DEF>to become smaller or less important</DEF><THESEXA>Union membership diminished from 30,000 at its height to just 2,000 today.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fall/drop</EXP><DEF>to decrease, especially by a large amount. <DEFBOLD>Fall</DEFBOLD> and <DEFBOLD>drop</DEFBOLD> are less formal than <DEFBOLD>decrease</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The number of tigers in the wild has fallen to just over 10,000.</THESEXA><THESEXA>At night, the temperature drops to minus 20 degrees.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>plunge</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>plʌndʒ</PRON></PronCodes><EXP>/plummet</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈplʌmət</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to suddenly decrease very quickly and by a very large amount</DEF><THESEXA>Share prices have plummeted 29% in the last four months.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Climate change could cause global temperatures to plummet.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slide</EXP><DEF>if a price or value slides, it gradually decreases in a way that causes problems – used especially in news reports</DEF><THESEXA>The dollar fell in late trading in New York yesterday and slid further this morning.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dwindle</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈdwɪndl</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to gradually decrease until there is very little left of something, especially numbers or amounts, popularity, or importance</DEF><THESEXA>Support for the theory is dwindling.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>taper off</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈteɪpə </PRON><AMEVARPRON> -ər</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>if a number or the amount of an activity that is happening tapers off, it gradually decreases, especially so that it stops completely</DEF><THESEXA>Political violence tapered off after the elections.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-637c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>decrease</EXP><DEF>used when the number, amount, or level of something becomes less than it used to be</DEF><THESEXA>There has been a significant decrease in the number of deaths from lung cancer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a 5% decrease in housing prices</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reduction</EXP><DEF>used when the price, amount, or level of something is made lower</DEF><THESEXA>There will be further price reductions in the sales.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A small reduction in costs can mean a large increase in profits.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cut</EXP><DEF>used when a government or company reduces the price, amount, or level of something</DEF><THESEXA>a 1% cut in interest rates</THESEXA><THESEXA>tax cuts</THESEXA><THESEXA>It is possible that there will be further job cuts.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>drop/fall</EXP><DEF>used when the number, amount, or level of something goes down, especially by a large amount</DEF><THESEXA>The figures showed a sharp fall in industrial output.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There was a dramatic drop in temperature.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>decline</EXP><DEF>used when the number, amount, level, or standard of something goes down, especially gradually</DEF><THESEXA>During the 1970s, there was a gradual decline in the birthrate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a decline in educational standards</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-634a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>defend</EXP><DEF>to say something to support an idea or person when other people are criticizing them</DEF><THESEXA>The mayor defended the action, saying that it was the best option.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stand up for sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to strongly defend someone who is being criticized, or strongly defend your ideas or your rights</DEF><THESEXA>My grandfather would always stand up for what was right.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don't want him fighting, but I do want him to stand up for himself.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stick up for sb</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to strongly defend someone who is being criticized, especially when no one else will defend them</DEF><THESEXA>The other kids tease her, but Sarah often sticks up for her.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come to sb's defence</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>come to sb's defense</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to say something to defend someone who is being criticized</DEF><THESEXA>Aitken's colleagues quickly came to his defence.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-632d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>delay</EXP><DEF>to wait until a later time to do something</DEF><THESEXA>He decided to delay his decision until he had seen the full report.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>postpone</EXP><DEF>to change an event to a later time or date</DEF><THESEXA>The meeting was postponed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put off</EXP><DEF>to delay doing something. <DEFBOLD>Put off</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>delay</DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD>postpone</DEFBOLD>, and is the usual phrase to use in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>I used to put off making difficult decisions.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The game has been put off till next week.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hold off</EXP><DEF>to delay doing something, especially while you are waiting for more information or for something else to happen</DEF><THESEXA>House buyers seem to be holding off until interest rates drop.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>defer</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to delay doing something until a later date, usually because something else needs to happen first</DEF><THESEXA>The decision had been deferred until after a meeting of the directors.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She decided to defer her university application for a year so that she could go travelling.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>procrastinate</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>prəˈkræstəneɪt</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to delay doing something that you ought to do</DEF><THESEXA>Don’t procrastinate – make a start on your assignments as soon as you get them.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-631d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>delete</EXP><DEF>to remove something that has been written down or stored in a computer</DEF><THESEXA>I think you should delete the second paragraph.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You can delete any files that are no longer required.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cut</EXP><DEF>to remove a section from a piece of writing or a film</DEF><THESEXA>You’ll have to cut some of your essay if it’s over 1,500 words.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A couple of scenes had to be cut because they were too violent.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cross sth out</EXP><DEF>to draw a line through a word to show that it was a mistake or that you want to change what you have written</DEF><THESEXA>If you make a mistake in the exam, just cross it out.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve crossed out the names of people who can’t come.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rub sth out</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>erase</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to remove writing or pictures from paper with a rubber, or from a board with a cloth</DEF><THESEXA>Use a pencil so you can rub out your mistakes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I had to rub the whole thing out and start again.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>erase</EXP><DEF>to remove sounds or pictures that have been recorded on a machine</DEF><THESEXA>You can erase the tape or record over it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Somehow all my photographs had been erased.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-62f9"><Section><Exponent><EXP>deliberately</EXP><DEF>if you do something deliberately, you do it because you want to</DEF><THESEXA>He upset her deliberately.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I deliberately kept the letter short.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>on purpose</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF> deliberately, especially in order to annoy someone or get an advantage for yourself</DEF><THESEXA>I didn’t push her on purpose; it was an accident.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>intentionally</EXP><DEF> deliberately, especially in order to have a particular result or effect</DEF><THESEXA>Very few teenagers become pregnant intentionally.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>consciously</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈkɒnʃəsli</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>done after thinking carefully about what you are doing, especially because you know what the results of your actions might be</DEF><THESEXA>Parents pass their values to their children, though not always consciously.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>knowingly</EXP><DEF>if you knowingly do something wrong or illegal, you do it even though you know it is wrong</DEF><THESEXA>FBI agents arrested Dillon for ‘knowingly making a false statement on a passport application’.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-62ee"><Section><Exponent><EXP>delicious</EXP><DEF>something that is delicious  tastes very good, and you enjoy eating or drinking it</DEF><THESEXA>The apple pie is delicious with vanilla ice cream.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tasty</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>food that is tasty  has a strong taste that you like</DEF><THESEXA>She makes a really tasty dish with chicken and rice.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>succulent</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>succulent food contains a lot of juice and tastes good – used especially about fruit, vegetables, or meat</DEF><THESEXA>succulent melons</THESEXA><THESEXA>a succulent steak</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>appetizing</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><ORTHVAR>appetising</ORTHVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>food that looks or smells appetizing  makes you feel that you want to eat it</DEF><THESEXA>The soup didn’t look very appetizing but it tasted delicious.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mouth-watering</EXP><DEF>food that is mouth-watering  smells or looks delicious, especially in a way that persuades you to buy or eat it</DEF><THESEXA>The waiter came round with a tray of mouth-watering cream cakes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>flavourful</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><ORTHVAR>flavorful</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>having a strong pleasant taste – used especially in written descriptions</DEF><THESEXA>a flavourful red wine</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>yummy/scrumptious</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>tasting very good – used about food, not about drinks</DEF><THESEXA>Her homemade brownies are really yummy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a scrumptious meal</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-62c6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>dentist</EXP><DEF>someone whose job is to treat people’s teeth</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve made an appointment with the dentist.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She always dreaded a visit to the dentist.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>oral surgeon</EXP><DEF>a dentist who has special training to operate on people’s teeth</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>orthodontist</EXP><DEF>a dentist who makes teeth straight when they have not been growing correctly</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dental hygienist/hygienist</EXP><DEF>someone who is specially trained to help a dentist by cleaning teeth and giving advice about how to keep teeth healthy</DEF><THESEXA>Ask your dental hygienist for advice on using floss.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dental practitioner</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a dentist</DEF></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-62bb"><Section><Exponent><EXP>depend on sth/sb</EXP><DEF>to need something or someone in order to exist, or be healthy, successful etc</DEF><THESEXA>The island’s economy depends on tourism.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The two industries depend on each other.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many people depend on the sport for their livelihood<GLOSS>they need it in order to make money to live</GLOSS>. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rely on sth/sb</EXP><DEF>to depend on something or someone to do or provide what you need, especially because you have no choice</DEF><THESEXA>In rural areas, many people rely on public transport.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She’d never wanted to rely on a man for money.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>count on sth</EXP><DEF> to depend on something that you expect to happen</DEF><THESEXA>Their government was counting on American support.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m counting on getting a pay rise next year.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lean on sb</EXP><DEF>to depend on someone for support and encouragement, especially at a difficult time</DEF><THESEXA>We all need a good friend that we can lean on.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When her husband died, she leant on Mike for support.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-62ac"><Section><Exponent><EXP>describe</EXP><DEF>to talk or write about a person, place, event etc, in order to show what they are like</DEF><THESEXA>Could you try and describe the man you saw?</THESEXA><THESEXA>In her book, she describes her journey across the Sahara.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police described the attack as particularly violent.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tell sb about sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to describe someone or something to someone. This phrase is more commonly used than <DEFBOLD>describe </DEFBOLD> in everyday spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>So, tell me about your holiday!</THESEXA><THESEXA>My friends have told me all about you!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>depict</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to describe someone or something in a piece of writing</DEF><THESEXA>His stories depict life in Trinidad as seen through the eyes of a young boy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In this new biography she is depicted as a lonely and unhappy woman.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>portray/represent</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to describe someone or something in a particular way</DEF><THESEXA>College teachers are often represented on television shows as slightly eccentric.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The magazine has been criticized for the way it portrays women.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The treatment has been portrayed as a painless way of curing cancer, which is simply not true.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police have represented her as a willing participant in the crimes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>characterize sb/sth as sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to describe someone or something by emphasizing one particular quality or feature about them</DEF><THESEXA>He characterized himself as ‘an average American’.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The successful schools were characterized as innovative and creative.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>paint sb/sth as sth</EXP><DEF>to describe someone or something, especially in a way that makes people believe something that is not true</DEF><THESEXA>Not all young people are as bad as they’re painted in the press.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We won, yet the media is painting it as a victory for our opponents. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The woman was painted as having only a slight grasp of reality.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>paint a picture</EXP><DEF>to describe a situation, so that people can get a general idea of what it is like</DEF><THESEXA>Can you paint a picture of life in Japan for us?</THESEXA><THESEXA>My uncle’s letters generally painted a rosy picture of how things were.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The report painted a bleak picture of the management’s failures.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6284"><Section><Exponent><EXP>destroy</EXP><DEF>to damage something so badly that it no longer exists or cannot be used or repaired</DEF><THESEXA>The earthquake almost completely destroyed the city.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The twin towers were destroyed in a terrorist attack.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>devastate</EXP><DEF>to damage a large area very badly and destroy many things in it</DEF><THESEXA>Allied bombings in 1943 devastated the city.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The country’s economy has been devastated by years of fighting.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>demolish</EXP><DEF>to completely destroy a building, either deliberately or by accident</DEF><THESEXA>The original 15th century house was demolished in Victorian times.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The plane crashed into a suburb of Paris, demolishing several buildings.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>flatten</EXP><DEF>to destroy a building or town by knocking it down, bombing it etc, so that nothing is left standing</DEF><THESEXA>The town centre was flattened by a 500 lb bomb.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wreck</EXP><DEF>to deliberately damage something very badly, especially a room or building</DEF><THESEXA>The toilets had been wrecked by vandals.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They just wrecked the place.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trash</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to deliberately destroy a lot of the things in a room, house etc</DEF><THESEXA>Apparently, he trashed his hotel room while on drugs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>obliterate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to destroy a place so completely that nothing remains</DEF><THESEXA>The nuclear blast obliterated most of Hiroshima.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reduce sth to ruins/rubble/ashes</EXP><DEF>to destroy a building or town completely</DEF><THESEXA>The town was reduced to rubble in the First World War.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ruin</EXP><DEF>to spoil something completely, so that it cannot be used or enjoyed</DEF><THESEXA>Fungus may ruin the crop.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The new houses will ruin the view.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-626d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>deteriorate</EXP><DEF>to become worse</DEF><THESEXA>Air quality is rapidly deteriorating in our cities.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Living conditions here have deteriorated in the past few years.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get worse</EXP><DEF>to become worse. <DEFBOLD>Get worse</DEFBOLD> is less formal and more common than <DEFBOLD>deteriorate</DEFBOLD> in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>My eyesight seems to be getting worse.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s never been well-behaved, but he’s getting even worse.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go down</EXP><DEF>to become gradually worse – used especially about the standard of something</DEF><THESEXA>The hotel’s gone down since its management changed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Nick’s teachers say that his work has gone down recently.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go downhill</EXP><DEF>to become much worse and be in a very bad condition, especially after a particular time or event</DEF><THESEXA>During the recession the business began to go downhill.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her health went downhill rapidly after Christmas.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>decline</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to become gradually worse – used especially about standards of living, education, health etc</DEF><THESEXA>After the war, the standard of living declined.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Over the years, the standard of public transport has declined.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>degenerate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> to become worse, or to become something bad instead of something good</DEF><THESEXA>Relations between the two countries have degenerated.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The debate degenerated into an argument between the two sides.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>escalate</EXP><DEF>to become much worse very quickly – used especially about fighting, violence, or a bad situation</DEF><THESEXA>Further violence could escalate into a full-scale armed conflict.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The political crisis escalated.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spiral out of control</EXP><DEF>to become worse in a way that cannot be controlled and is extremely serious and worrying</DEF><THESEXA>Police say that the violence has spiralled out of control.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Scientists are worried that global warming could soon spiral out of control.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Costs have been spiralling out of control. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-623b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>determination</EXP><DEF>the quality of continuing to try to do something, even when it is difficult</DEF><THESEXA>Bill got where he is today by sheer hard work and determination.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I really admired her determination to live her own life.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>willpower</EXP><DEF>the ability to control your mind and body in order to achieve something you have decided to do</DEF><THESEXA>It takes a lot of willpower to give up smoking.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She made herself get better by sheer willpower, when everyone else had given up hope.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>drive</EXP><DEF>the determination and energy to succeed, especially in business or work</DEF><THESEXA>We’re looking for young people who have drive, initiative, and new ideas.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s clever enough, but he lacks drive.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>resolve</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a strong determination to succeed in doing something, especially because you are sure that it is a good thing to do</DEF><THESEXA>The poverty she saw there strengthened her resolve to do something to help.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The success of a military enterprise depends partly on the resolve of its leaders.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>perseverance</EXP><DEF>the ability to continue trying to achieve something over a long period in a patient way, even when this is difficult</DEF><THESEXA>Reaching this standard of musical skill requires incredible perseverance.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A good teacher should have imagination and perseverance.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ruthlessness</EXP><DEF>the quality of being extremely determined to achieve what you want, and not caring if you have to hurt other people to do it</DEF><THESEXA>He was a man who pursued his aims with complete ruthlessness.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The revolt was suppressed with total ruthlessness.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tenacity</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the determination to never stop trying to do something, especially when other people try to stop you</DEF><THESEXA>He clung to power with a remarkable tenacity.</THESEXA><THESEXA> When fighting cancer, Tsongas showed the same tenacity he displayed in politics. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-620f" fullpage="yes"><Section><Exponent><EXP>determined</EXP><DEF>if you are determined to do something, you have decided that you are definitely going to do it, and you will not let anything stop you. <DEFBOLD>Determined </DEFBOLD> is also used about someone’s character, when they usually behave in this way</DEF><THESEXA>I was determined to be a doctor.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She’s a very determined woman.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stubborn</EXP><DEF>determined not to change what you are doing, especially when other people think you are behaving in an unreasonable way. <DEFBOLD>Stubborn </DEFBOLD> is often used when you disapprove of someone. It is also sometimes used when you admire them</DEF><THESEXA>I wish you would stop being so stubborn!</THESEXA><THESEXA>Churchill’s stubborn refusal to surrender </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>single-minded</EXP><DEF>someone who is single-minded  works very hard in order to achieve one particular thing, and thinks that everything else is much less important</DEF><THESEXA>During a war, a leader must be single-minded and, if necessary, ruthless.</THESEXA><THESEXA>her single-minded pursuit of power</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tough</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>tʌf</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>determined to succeed, even if a situation is difficult or frightening</DEF><THESEXA>In competitive sports, it is as important to be mentally tough as it is to be physically fit.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Gorelick is known as a tough manager.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>firm</EXP><DEF>showing by your behaviour that you are determined not to change your mind, especially when you are telling someone what to do</DEF><THESEXA>What this country needs is firm leadership.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You have to be firm with young children.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>feisty</EXP><DEF>determined and full of energy, and not afraid to say what you think and argue with people – used especially when you admire this person. <DEFBOLD>Feisty</DEFBOLD> is often used about women</DEF><THESEXA>In the film she plays a feisty young woman who is smarter than all the men put together.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a feisty kid with a mind of his own </THESEXA><THESEXA>the city’s feisty mayor</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>headstrong</EXP><DEF>determined to do what you want, without listening to other people’s advice or thinking about the results of your actions – used especially about young people</DEF><THESEXA>Her sister was headstrong and impulsive, and made a point of going out whenever and wherever she liked. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>resolute</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>doing something in a very determined way because you have very strong beliefs, aims etc</DEF><THESEXA>the soldiers’ resolute defence of the town</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tenacious</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>determined and refusing to give up</DEF><THESEXA>McTaggart was seen by many in the environment movement as a tenacious hero.</THESEXA><THESEXA>his tenacious grip on power</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dogged</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>dogged behaviour shows that you are very determined and that you will not give up – used especially in the following phrases</DEF><THESPROPFORM>dogged determination/persistence/resistance/refusal</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The team played with dogged determination.</THESEXA><THESEXA>his dogged refusal to admit defeat</THESEXA><THESEXA>the dogged persistence of the defenders </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>persistent</EXP><DEF>continuing to do something, although this is difficult, or other people warn you not to do it</DEF><THESEXA>If you want to get a job, you have to be persistent. Don’t give up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>strong-willed</EXP><DEF>always very determined to do what you want to do, even if other people think it is not a good idea to do it</DEF><THESEXA>She has always been a strong-willed child.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ruthless</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈruːθləs</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>someone who is ruthless is so determined to get what they want, that they do not care if they harm other people</DEF><THESEXA>a ruthless dictator</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was ruthless in his ambition.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mean business</EXP><DEF>to be determined to do something and show other people that you are determined to do it, even if it involves harming someone</DEF><THESEXA>The one-day strike proved that the union meant business.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-61e1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>diary</EXP><DEF>a book in which you write down the things that happen to you each day, and your private thoughts</DEF><THESEXA>I decided to keep a diary of our trip to Toronto.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You shouldn’t read anyone’s private diary.</THESEXA><THESEXA>On February 3rd, he wrote the last entry in his diary.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>journal</EXP><DEF>a diary, especially one written by a famous or important person</DEF><THESEXA>In the 1837 journal, Darwin gives an account of his voyage to South America. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Her book draws on letters, diaries, journals and historical sources.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>memoirs</EXP><DEF>a written record of the important events and people in your life which you write in order to publish – especially one that is written by a famous or important person such as a politician or a military leader</DEF><THESEXA>A lot of politicians think about writing their memoirs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The book was titled ‘The Memoirs of a President’.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blog</EXP><DEF>a diary that someone puts on a website, which is a record of their activities, experiences, and opinions, with the latest information always at the top. On some blogs, readers can leave comments</DEF><THESEXA>I try to keep a regular online blog.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I came across a great post on a blog I read recently.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>log/log book</EXP><DEF>an official record of events, especially on a journey in a ship or plane</DEF><THESEXA>the ship’s log</THESEXA><THESEXA>The captain had made a few notes in the log book.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-61c4"><Section><Exponent><EXP>die</EXP><DEF>to stop being alive, as a result of old age or illness</DEF><THESEXA>I want to see Ireland again before I die.</THESEXA><THESEXA>No wonder your plants always die – you don’t water them enough.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His son died of liver cancer three years ago.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pass away</EXP><DEF>to die – used when you want to avoid using the word ‘die’, in order to show respect or to avoid upsetting someone</DEF><THESEXA>My wife had just passed away, and I didn’t want to be around people.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pass on</EXP><DEF>to pass away – use this especially when you believe that the soul has a life after the death of the body</DEF><THESEXA>I’m sorry, Emily, but your mother has passed on.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lose your life</EXP><DEF>to be killed in a terrible event</DEF><THESEXA>Hundreds of people lost their lives when the ship overturned in a storm.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>perish</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to die in a terrible event – used especially in <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6198"><Section><Exponent><EXP>difference</EXP><DEF>a detail, fact, or quality that makes one person or thing different from another</DEF><THESEXA>We should think about the similarities between cultures, not the differences.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>difference between</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>Try and spot the differences between these two pictures.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The difference between the two cheeses is that one is made from goat’s milk.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>difference in</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>I don’t think there’s any difference in the way you pronounce these two words.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>know the difference</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>He’s speaking Italian, not Spanish. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6167"><Section><Exponent><EXP>different</EXP><DEF>if something or someone is different, they are not like something or someone else, or they are not like they were before</DEF><THESEXA>You look different. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-611b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>dig</EXP><DEF>to make a hole in the ground using your hands, a tool, or a machine</DEF><THESEXA>Some workmen were digging a trench at the side of the road.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In Africa, the people know where to dig for water.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make a hole</EXP><DEF>to dig a hole in the ground, using your hands or a tool</DEF><THESEXA>Make a hole just big enough for the plant’s roots.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>burrow</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈbʌrəʊ </PRON><AMEVARPRON> ˈbɜːroʊ</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>if an animal burrows, it makes a hole or a passage in the ground by digging the earth with its feet, nose etc</DEF><THESEXA>The rabbits had burrowed a hole under the fence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>plough</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>plow</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><PronCodes><PRON>plaʊ</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to turn over the soil using a machine or a tool pulled by animals, to prepare the soil for planting seeds</DEF><THESEXA>The farmers here still plough their fields using buffaloes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>excavate</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈekskəveɪt</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to dig a large hole in the ground, especially as a preparation for building something</DEF><THESEXA>The men began excavating the hole for the pool.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bore</EXP><DEF>to make a deep round hole in the ground using a special machine, especially in order to look for oil or water</DEF><THESEXA>Companies need a special licence to bore for oil.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-60d0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>disabled</EXP><DEF>not able to use a part of your body properly or not able to learn normally, because of a permanent problem</DEF><THESEXA>Disabled people should have the same rights and choices as everyone else.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Elaine is severely disabled and relies on 24 hour care.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The charity provides opportunities and facilities for disabled people to take part in sport.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>special needs</EXP><DEF>if someone has special needs, they have physical or mental disabilities</DEF><THESEXA>a school for children with special needs</THESEXA><THESEXA>A woman whose five-year-old son has special needs has accused her local education authority of failing to provide him with a decent education.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>learning difficulties/disabilities</EXP><DEF>if someone has learning difficulties, they have difficulty learning basic skills or information</DEF><THESEXA>A child’s behavioural problems may be associated with learning difficulties.</THESEXA><THESEXA>special courses for students with moderate learning difficulties</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>handicapped</EXP><DEF>disabled – this word is old-fashioned and is now considered offensive. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-60bb"><Section><Exponent><EXP>disadvantage</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a bad feature that something has, which makes it less good or less useful than other things</DEF><THESEXA>What do you think are the disadvantages of nuclear energy?</THESEXA><THESEXA>This car uses a lot of fuel, which is a major disadvantage.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>drawback</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a bad feature that something has, although it has advantages that are usually more important</DEF><THESEXA>One of the main drawbacks is the price. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bad point</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a bad feature that something has</DEF><THESEXA>All of these designs have both their good points and bad points.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the downside</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>singular</GRAM><DEF>the disadvantage of a situation that in most other ways seems good or enjoyable</DEF><THESEXA>It’s a great job. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-60b8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>disagree/not agree</EXP><DEF>to have a different opinion from someone else about something</DEF><THESEXA>Scholars disagree about the meaning of the poem.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t agree with a word of what she says.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be divided/split</EXP><DEF>if a group of people is divided or split on something, some of them have one opinion and others have a completely different opinion</DEF><THESEXA>The party is divided on this issue.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>differ</EXP><DEF>if two or more people differ about something, they have different opinions from each other about it</DEF><THESEXA>The two men differed on how to handle the crisis.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not see eye to eye</EXP><DEF>used to say that two people have different opinions and ideas so that it is difficult for them to be friends or work together</DEF><THESEXA>Some of the teachers don’t see eye to eye with the principal of the school.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be mistaken</EXP><DEF>used to say that you disagree with someone’s opinion and that you think they are wrong</DEF><THESEXA>People are mistaken if they think that this problem will go away on its own.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take issue with sb/sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to express strong disagreement with an idea or with what someone has said or done</DEF><THESEXA>A number of people took issue with the mayor’s decision.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dissent</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to say publicly that you disagree with an official opinion or one that most people accept</DEF><THESEXA>Two members of the jury dissented from the majority verdict.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-6a2a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>disappear</EXP><DEF>if something disappears, you cannot see it any longer, or it does not exist any disappointedunhappy because something you hoped for did not happen, or because something was not as good as you expectedHundreds of disappointed fans were unable to get tickets for the game.I was disappointed with the grade I got in my Maths exam.feel let downto feel disappointed because something was not as good as you expected, or someone did not do what you expected them to do for you – a rather informal useI felt let down when I saw the film.Nurses feel badly let down – they were promised a pay increase months ago.disillusionedˌdɪsəˈluːʒənd◂disappointed because you have lost your belief that an idea is right, or that something or someone is goodTheir leaders are deeply disillusioned with the peace process.Disillusioned voters are turning against the government.disenchantedno longer feeling enthusiastic, especially so that you do not think you should be involved in something, or give someone your supportAmericans have grown increasingly disenchanted with politics.a disenchanted workforce be/feel sorryspokenused when telling someone that you feel disappointed and wish that the situation was differentI’m sorry you can’t come to the party.She always felt sorry that she hadn’t got to know him better.
very disappointedguttednot before nounBrEinformalextremely disappointed, especially because you were unsuccessfulI was gutted when we lost the game.crestfallenliterarylooking very disappointed and sad, especially when you suddenly realise that something you want cannot happenGary looked crestfallen as they calculated how much money they would need.a crestfallen look

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-608a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>disaster</EXP><DEF>a sudden event such as an accident, or a natural event such as a flood or storm, which Ethiopia.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hurricane</EXP><DEF>a storm that has very strong fast winds and that moves over water – used about storms in the North Atlantic Ocean</DEF><THESEXA>extreme weather such as hurricanes</THESEXA><THESEXA>Hurricane Andrew left southern Florida in ruins.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>typhoon</EXP><DEF>a violent tropical storm – used about storms in the Western Pacific Ocean</DEF><THESEXA>A typhoon has hit the Philippines, lifting roofs off houses and uprooting trees.</THESEXA><THESEXA /></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tsunami</EXP><DEF>a very large wave, caused by extreme conditions such as an earthquake, which can cause a lot of damage when it reaches land</DEF><THESEXA>Thousands of people were killed in the tsunami.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many Pacific earthquakes have generated tsunamis.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6058"><Section><Exponent><EXP>discussion</EXP><DEF>an occasion when you discuss something, or the process of discussing it</DEF><THESEXA>After a long discussion, they decided to paint the room pale blue.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This led to a discussion of our favourite books.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are several projects <COLLOINEXA>under discussion</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>being discussed</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>debate</EXP><DEF>discussion of a particular subject that often continues for a long time, in which people express different opinions. Also used to mean a formal discussion of a subject, after which people vote for or against something</DEF><THESEXA>There is a lot of debate about the safety of nuclear power.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The student union is holding a debate on immigration.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>talks</EXP><DEF>formal discussions between governments, organizations etc</DEF><THESEXA>the Arab-Israeli peace talks</THESEXA><THESEXA>The next round of trade talks will be held in the spring. </THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>Bilateral talks</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>between two groups or countries</GLOSS>on refugees took place in November.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>negotiations</EXP><DEF>official discussions between two or more groups who are trying to agree on something</DEF><THESEXA>The two sides have begun negotiations aimed at ending the dispute.</THESEXA><THESEXA>International negotiations on reducing sulphur dioxide emissions have ended without agreement.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conference</EXP><DEF>a large formal meeting at which people from many places discuss their work or research, or important matters relating to their organization</DEF><THESEXA>an international conference on nuclear physics</THESEXA><THESEXA>the Labour Party conference</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>consultation</EXP><DEF>a formal discussion in which people who are affected by or involved in something can give their opinions</DEF><THESEXA>The government promised a full public consultation before a decision was made to build the airport. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The revised syllabus was devised after wide consultations with the teaching profession.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6046"><Section><Exponent><EXP>dishonest</EXP><DEF>behaving in a way that is intended to deceive people, for example by lying, cheating, or stealing</DEF><THESEXA>Are you accusing me of being dishonest?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The money was acquired through dishonest means.</THESEXA><THESEXA>People are no longer surprised to find that politicians are dishonest.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>corrupt</EXP><DEF>using your power in a dishonest way for your own advantage – used about people in official positions</DEF><THESEXA>corrupt politicians</THESEXA><THESEXA>Law and order has broken down, and most government officials are corrupt.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>devious</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈdiːviəs</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>good at secretly thinking of clever plans to trick people in order to get what you want</DEF><THESEXA>You have a very devious mind!</THESEXA><THESEXA> They use all kinds of devious methods to find out your personal details.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>underhand</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>underhanded</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>underhand methods involve secretly deceiving people in order to get what you want</DEF><THESEXA>In a series of underhand moves, Browne managed to gain control of the company.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sneaky</EXP><DEF>doing or saying things secretly, in a way that seems wrong because it is slightly dishonest or unfair</DEF><THESEXA> It was pretty sneaky when the bank charged me interest on my account without telling me.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sly</EXP><DEF>deliberately behaving in a way that hides what you are really thinking or doing, in a way that is slightly dishonest</DEF><THESEXA>Lucy decided not to tell him where she was going. She was often a bit sly like that.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s a sly old fox.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unscrupulous</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ʌnˈskruːpjələs</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>using dishonest and unfair methods to get what you want, without caring if you harm other people</DEF><THESEXA>Some unscrupulous companies try to persuade people to borrow huge sums of money.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fraudulent</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈfrɔːdjələnt</PRON><AMEVARPRON> ˈfrɒːdʒə-</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>deliberately deceiving people in an illegal way in order to gain money or power</DEF><THESEXA>You will be prosecuted if you make a fraudulent claim on your insurance policy.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>seeming to be dishonest</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>suspicious</EXP><DEF>if someone or something seems suspicious, they make you think that something dishonest or illegal is happening</DEF><THESEXA>The police are treating the boy’s death as suspicious.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dubious</EXP><DEF>if something seems dubious, you think it may not be completely true, right, or honest</DEF><THESEXA>He has a rather dubious reputation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It all sounds highly dubious to me.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the country’s dubious record on human rights</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shady</EXP><DEF>shady business deals or people seem to be dishonest or connected with secret and illegal activities</DEF><THESEXA>Several senior members of the party had been involved in shady deals.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a shady character</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shifty</EXP><DEF>someone who looks shifty looks as if they are doing or planning something dishonest</DEF><THESEXA>The man on the market stall looked a bit shifty when he gave me my change.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dodgy</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>probably dishonest and not to be trusted – used especially to say that you do not want to be involved with someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>There’s something a bit dodgy about him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>dodgy business deals</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5fe8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>dislike</EXP><DEF>to not like someone or something. <DEFBOLD>Dislike</DEFBOLD> is stronger than <DEFBOLD>not like</DEFBOLD>, and is used especially in written English</DEF><THESEXA>She disliked him as soon as she met him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Chemistry was the only subject he disliked at school.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not like</EXP><THESEXA>Why did you invite Claire? You know I don’t like her.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t really like getting up in the morning when it’s dark.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not be very keen on sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>not be very fond of sth</LEXVAR></Variant><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>used to say that you do not like something, but in a polite or gentle way</DEF><THESEXA>I’m not very keen on Chinese food.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She’s never been very fond of his books.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not think much of sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to not like someone or something because you do not have a good opinion of them</DEF><THESEXA>We’ve tried that restaurant twice and we don’t think much of it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I worked with him for years and I didn’t think much of him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not be sb’s kind of thing</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also </LINKWORD><LEXVAR>not be sb’s cup of tea</LEXVAR></Variant><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to not be the kind of thing you enjoy – used about activities, films, books etc</DEF><THESEXA>Detective stories aren’t really my kind of thing.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go off sb/sth</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to stop liking someone or something that you used to like</DEF><THESEXA>Dan and I went out together for six months and then I just went off him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put sb off sb/sth</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>to make you stop liking someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>I was terrible at sport at school and it totally put me off doing any kind of exercise.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5fbf"><Section><Exponent><EXP>disobey</EXP><DEF>to not obey a person, order, rule, or law</DEF><THESEXA>In the army, it is a crime to disobey a superior officer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He had disobeyed the school rules.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>break a law/rule</EXP><DEF>to not obey a law or rule</DEF><THESEXA>Anyone who breaks the law must expect to be punished.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>defy</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to deliberately refuse to obey a rule or law, or what someone in authority tells you to do</DEF><THESEXA>The police arrested the youth for defying a court order.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>flout</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>flaʊt</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to deliberately disobey a rule or law in a very public way</DEF><THESEXA>Timber companies are continuing to flout environmental laws.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>violate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to disobey a law, or do something that is against an agreement or principle</DEF><THESEXA>Both countries have accused each other of violating the treaty.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Technically he had violated the law. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>contravene</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be against a law, rule, or agreement, or to do something that is against a law, rule, or agreement</DEF><THESEXA>The British government’s actions contravened the European Convention on Human Rights. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5f9e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>divorce</EXP><DEF>to legally end your marriage</DEF><THESEXA>After seven years, they decided to divorce.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She divorced him six months after they were married.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>separate</EXP><DEF> to start to live apart from your husband or wife because of problems in your marriage</DEF><THESEXA>They argued all the time and in the end agreed to separate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She separated from her husband and moved to a flat in London.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>split up/break up</EXP><DEF> to end a marriage or a romantic relationship</DEF><THESEXA>When Andy was nine, his parents split up.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He's just broken up with his girlfriend.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>leave sb</EXP><DEF> to stop living with your husband, wife, or partner, often because you are having a relationship with someone else</DEF><THESEXA>Her husband left her for a younger woman after 27 years of marriage.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Dan's left me.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-773ea7de.1187e6572e0.-6bd9"><Section><Exponent><EXP>do</EXP><DEF>to do something – used in the following phrases</DEF><THESPROPFORM>do your work/homework robbery.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>carry out sth</EXP><DEF>to do something – used in the following phrases</DEF><THESPROPFORM>carry out your work</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The violence is making <DEFBOLD>Perform</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>carry out</DEFBOLD>, and is used in the following phrases</DEF><THESPROPFORM>perform a task/duty</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The job mostly involves
