doctorsomeone who treats people who are ill, who has completed a long course of study at medical schoolIf you have bad chest pains, you should see a doctor.GPBrEa doctor who is trained in general medicine and who treats the people who live in a local areaMy GP told me that I must lose weight.physicianfəˈzɪʃənformalespecially AmE a doctorthe American physician, Dr James Tyler Kentquackinformalsomeone who pretends to be a doctorMy father felt that people practicing alternative medicine were just a bunch of quacks. the medical professiondoctors and nurses considered as a groupThis view is widely accepted among the medical profession.surgeonˈsɜːdʒən ˈsɜːr- a doctor who does operations in a hospitalOne of the world’s top heart surgeons performed the operation.specialist a doctor with special knowledge about a particular illness, part of the body, or type of treatmentThe new drug is being tested by cancer specialists.an eye specialistconsultantBrEa very senior doctor in a hospital, with a lot of knowledge about a particular area of medicineThe consultant said that he did not think it was cancer.paramedicsomeone who has been trained to treat sick or injured people, especially at the scene of an accidentParamedics treated him for shock. vetalsoveterinarianespecially AmEa doctor who treats animalsWe took the cat to the vet.paediatricianBrEpediatricianAmE a doctor who treats children who are sickgynaecologistBrEgynecologistAmEa doctor who treats medical conditions and illnesses that affect women’s bodiesobstetriciana doctor who deals with the birth of childrenradiographersomeone whose job is to take X-rays or to treat people using an X-ray machine
a doctor who treats mental illnesspsychiatristsaɪˈkaɪətrɪst sə- a doctor who is trained to treat people with mental illnessesIn order to become a psychiatrist, you first need a medical degree.psychologistsaɪˈkɒlədʒɪst -ˈkɑː-a scientist who studies and is trained in psychologythe study of the mindMany psychologists believe that aggression is a learned behaviour.shrinkinformala humorous word for a psychiatristtherapista trained person whose job is to help people with their emotional problems, especially by talking to them and asking them to talk about their feelings
someone who is studying to be a doctormedical studenta student who is studying medicine in order to be a doctorJames is a medical student at Edinburgh university.internAmEa student who has almost finished studying to be a doctor, and who is working in a hospital

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5f20"><Section><Exponent><EXP>doubt</EXP><DEF>to think that something may not be true or that it is unlikely</DEF><THESEXA>‘Do you think she really is eighteen?’ ‘I doubt it.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>There was so much noise that I doubt if anyone slept.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be doubtful/dubious</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈdjuːbiəs</PRON><AMEVARPRON>ˈduː- </AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>to doubt that something will happen, is true, or is a good idea</DEF><THESEXA>Economists are doubtful that the situation will improve this year.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘You can eat the whole fish including the head.’ Janey looked dubious.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When my husband suggested emigrating, I was dubious at first.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Mum looked doubtful when I suggested having a party for all my friends.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have reservations</EXP><DEF>to feel that a plan or idea may not be good because you think there may be some problems with it</DEF><THESEXA>Health care professionals had reservations about giving both vaccines together.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have misgivings</EXP><DEF>to feel worried about doing something, because it may have a bad result</DEF><THESEXA>Ralph had serious misgivings about changing his career at the age of 50.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have mixed feelings</EXP><DEF>to be unsure exactly how you feel about something or someone because there are both good and bad things about them</DEF><THESEXA>I have very mixed feelings about moving house – it’s exciting but I’ll miss this area.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have second thoughts</EXP><DEF>to start having doubts about whether a decision you have just made is the right one</DEF><THESEXA>I liked the dress in the shop, but when I got it home I had second thoughts.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5efe"><Section><Exponent><EXP>draw</EXP><DEF>to make a picture, pattern etc using a pen or pencil</DEF><THESEXA>The children were asked to draw a picture of their families.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m going to art classes to learn how to draw.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sketch</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>sketʃ</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to draw a picture of something or someone quickly and without a lot of detail</DEF><THESEXA>Roy took a pencil and sketched the bird quickly, before it moved.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>illustrate</EXP><DEF>to draw the pictures in a book</DEF><THESEXA>It’s a beautiful book, illustrated by Arthur Rackham.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>doodle</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈduːdl</PRON></PronCodes><DEF> to draw shapes or patterns without really thinking about what you are doing</DEF><THESEXA>He was on the phone, doodling on his notepad as he spoke.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scribble</EXP><DEF> to draw shapes or lines without making a definite picture or pattern. Small children do this before they have learned to draw or write</DEF><THESEXA>At the age of two, she loved scribbling with crayons and coloured pencils.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trace</EXP><DEF> to copy a picture by putting a piece of thin paper over it and drawing the lines that you can see through the paper</DEF><THESEXA>First trace the map, and then copy it into your workbooks.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5eeb"><Section><Exponent><EXP>drawing</EXP><DEF>a picture drawn with a pencil, pen, crayon etc</DEF><THESEXA>I did a drawing of the front of the house. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sketch</EXP><DEF>a drawing that is done quickly and has few details</DEF><THESEXA>When she was traveling, she drew quick sketches of the places she’d seen.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>doodle</EXP><DEF>a shape or pattern that you draw when you are bored or thinking about other things</DEF><THESEXA>His schoolbooks were always covered in doodles.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>graffiti</EXP><DEF>drawings or writing that people secretly put on walls or other public places</DEF><THESEXA>Vandals had covered the station in graffiti. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>comic strip</EXP><DEF> a series of pictures drawn inside boxes that tell a story, especially in a newspaper or magazine</DEF><THESEXA>Asterix started off in a comic strip.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cartoon</EXP><DEF> a funny drawing in a newspaper or magazine</DEF><THESEXA>There is a 'New Yorker' cartoon which depicts various inventions for keeping warm.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5ed8"><Section><SECHEADING>when you are sleeping</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>dream</EXP><DEF>the thoughts, images, and feelings that go through your mind while you are asleep</DEF><THESEXA>I had a strange dream last night -- you and I were in some sort of forest.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nightmare</EXP><DEF>a very unpleasant and frightening dream</DEF><THESEXA>She still has terrible nightmares about the accident.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>daydream</EXP><DEF>a series of pleasant thoughts that go through your mind when you are awake, so that you do not notice what is happening around you</DEF><THESEXA>Neil was in a daydream, and didn’t hear the teacher call his name.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reverie</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a state of imagining or thinking about pleasant things, that is like dreaming</DEF><THESEXA>The doorbell rang, shaking her from her reverie.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>something that you want to do</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>dream</EXP><DEF>something very special that you want to do and that you think about a lot, especially something that is not very likely to happen</DEF><THESEXA>As a teenager, his dream was to become a professional footballer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ambition</EXP><DEF>something that you want to achieve and that you work hard to achieve, especially in your work</DEF><THESEXA>My ambition had always been to start my own business.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>aspirations</EXP><DEF>the important things that people want from their lives – used especially about the things a society or a large group of people wants</DEF><THESEXA>It’s important that young people think seriously about their career aspirations.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fantasy</EXP><DEF>something exciting that you imagine happening to you, which is extremely unlikely to happen and often involves sex</DEF><THESEXA>schoolboy fantasies</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pipe dream</EXP><DEF>a dream that is impossible or is extremely unlikely to happen</DEF><THESEXA>Is world peace no more than a pipe dream?</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5e8f"><Section><SECHEADING>to put on clothes</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>get dressed</EXP><DEF>to put on all your clothes</DEF><THESEXA>You’d better get dressed! It’s almost time to leave for school!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dress</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to put on all your clothes. <DEFBOLD>Dress</DEFBOLD> is used especially in literature. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5ded"><Section><Exponent><EXP>dry</EXP><DEF>having very little moisture, or no longer wet</DEF><THESEXA>How do plants survive in hot dry conditions?</THESEXA><THESEXA>My mouth feels dry.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The clothes should be dry.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The ground was <COLLOINEXA>bone dry</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>completely dry</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>parched</EXP><DEF>completely dry – used about land, or about someone’s lips, throat, skin etc</DEF><THESEXA>The earth was so parched that there were huge cracks in it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>parched lips</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>arid</EXP><DEF>extremely dry because of lack or rain and therefore difficult for growing crops</DEF><THESEXA>the arid landscape of the Danakil desert</THESEXA><THESEXA>an arid mountain region </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5dd5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>during</EXP><DEF>at one point in a period of time, or through the whole of a period of time</DEF><THESEXA>Did you hear the storm during the night?</THESEXA><THESEXA>During the summer we spend a lot more time out of doors.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>all through</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>throughout</LEXVAR></Variant><PronCodes><PRON>ɔːl ˈθruː, θruːˈaʊt</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>during all of a period of time</DEF><THESEXA>The cafe’s closed all through the winter and opens again in April.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Throughout her career she has worked hard and achieved the highest standards.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>over</EXP><DEF>at one point or at various different points during a period of time</DEF><THESEXA>I’m going to redecorate my bedroom over the holidays.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She’s been a great help to me over the past year.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in the course of sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>during a particular process or period of time</DEF><THESEXA>In the course of the study we consulted with leading experts in global warming.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many factors control the rise and fall of a species in the course of evolution.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There was huge social change in the course of the 1960s.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>within</EXP><DEF>during a period of time, or before the end of a period – used when you want to emphasize that it is a short or limited period</DEF><THESEXA>There have been five serious accidents within the last few days.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Payment must be made within 30 days.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If we are inviting you for an interview, you will receive a letter within 14 days.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5db4"><Section><Exponent><EXP>early</EXP><DEF>arriving or happening before the usual or expected time</DEF><THESEXA>For once, the train arrived early.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Let’s have an early lunch before we go.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in good time</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>early enough, so that you do not have to rush, or so that you have time to get ready</DEF><THESEXA>Everything was ready for the party in good time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>on time</EXP><DEF>arriving somewhere or happening at the right time</DEF><THESEXA>The bus was on time.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The project was finished on time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ahead of time</EXP><DEF>earlier than the time when you have arranged to do something or than when you need something</DEF><THESEXA>The building work was completed ahead of time.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some of the food can be prepared ahead of time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ahead of schedule</EXP><DEF>earlier than the officially agreed time</DEF><THESEXA>The prime minister called the elections early, five months ahead of schedule.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>with time to spare</EXP><DEF>arriving somewhere or finishing something before the time when you have to arrive or finish</DEF><THESEXA>We got to the airport with plenty of time to spare.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I finished the test with time to spare.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>first thing</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>immediately after you get up, or as soon as you start work</DEF><THESEXA>I’ll telephone her first thing tomorrow.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5d7d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>earn</EXP><DEF>to be paid a particular amount of money for your work. <DEFBOLD> Earn</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>get </DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD> make</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>A newly-qualified teacher can expect to earn about £20,000 a year.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get</EXP><DEF> to earn a particular amount of money every hour, week etc</DEF><THESEXA>How much do you get an hour?</THESEXA><THESEXA>She gets more than I do.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make</EXP><DEF>to earn money, especially a lot of money, or money that is not from regular employment</DEF><THESEXA>You can make a lot of money in banking.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Jo makes a bit of extra money by selling his paintings.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be on sth</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>to earn a particular amount of money each year. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5d6a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>Earth/the Earth</EXP><DEF>the planet we live on – used especially when talking about this in relation to other planets and to space. are furthest away from the centre</DEF><THESEXA>The new station was built on the outskirts of the city.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>perimeter</EXP><DEF>the outside edge around an enclosed area effecta change that is caused by an event, action etcThe people in this area are still suffering from the effects of the famine.The treatment had little or no effect.impactan effect that happens as a result of something important, especially a big and permanent effectChanges in technology have had a massive impact on the way we work.the environmental impact of industrial activityinfluencethe effect that something has on people’s opinions or behaviour, or on how something developsAmerican television has had a big influence on popular culture in the west.His ideas had a lot of influence at the time.side effectan unwanted and unplanned effect that something has – used especially about drugs and medical treatmentCommon side effects of the drug may include headaches and muscle pains.after-effectsBrEaftereffectsAmEbad effects that continue for a long time after the thing that caused themA traumatic experience can have severe psychological after-effects.the after-effects of the warrepercussionsˌriːpəˈkʌʃəns-pər-the effects that happen later as a result of an event or decision, especially a range of effects that continue for a long timeThe scandal could have serious repercussions for her career.The judge’s decision is likely to have important repercussions for future cases of this kind.a knock-on effectBrEused when something has an effect on something, which then has an effect on something elseHigher oil prices have a knock-on effect on other fuels.footprintthe effect that human activities have on the environment, caused by using up its natural resources, pollution, waste etcBusinesses all over the world must attempt to reduce their environmental footprint.The house has a low carbon footprintit uses very little energy from carbon and therefore is good for the environment.

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5cea"><Section><Exponent><EXP>election</EXP><DEF>an occasion when people choose a government or leader by voting</DEF><THESEXA>the American presidential election</THESEXA><THESEXA>South Africa held its first multi-racial elections in 1994.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ballot</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈbælət</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>an occasion when the members of an organization vote by marking what they want on a piece of paper, especially to make sure that it is secret</DEF><THESEXA>The result of the ballot showed that nurses were not in favour of a strike.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>referendum</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˌrefəˈrendəm</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>an occasion when everyone in a country votes on an important political subject</DEF><THESEXA>In the Danish referendum, the people voted ‘no’ to joining the European single currency.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the polls</EXP><DEF>the process of voting in a political election – used especially in news reports</DEF><THESEXA>4,500,000 voters went to the polls in eight provinces to elect six governors.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>show of hands</EXP><DEF>an act of voting informally for something by the people in a group raising their hands</DEF><THESEXA>May I have a show of hands from all those in favour of the proposal?</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5cb2"><Section><Exponent><EXP>electric</EXP><DEF>used about something that works using electricity</DEF><THESEXA>an electric kettle</THESEXA><THESEXA>Is your cooker electric or gas?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>electrical</EXP><DEF>used about things in general that use electricity, or people whose job is to make or repair these things</DEF><THESEXA>a company manufacturing electrical goods</THESEXA><THESEXA>an electrical engineer</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>electronic</EXP><DEF>used about equipment such as computers and televisions that work by using extremely small electrical parts, or about systems that work using computers</DEF><THESEXA>Most kids love electronic games.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Email is the short word for electronic mail.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5ca6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>email</EXP><DEF>a message sent using the email system</DEF><THESEXA>Did you get my email?</THESEXA><THESEXA>There were about 20 emails in my inbox that morning.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>message</EXP><DEF>an email – used when it is obvious that you are talking about a message sent by email, rather than a message sent by some other method</DEF><THESEXA>I deleted your message by mistake.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>attachment</EXP><DEF>a document or file that is sent with an email</DEF><THESEXA>You can email the photo to me as an attachment.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spam</EXP><DEF>unwanted email messages from advertisers</DEF><THESEXA>I was getting so much spam that I changed my email address.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>phishing</EXP><DEF>the activity of sending emails in order to trick someone into giving away information such as their bank account number or their computer password. This information is then used to get money or goods</DEF><THESEXA>Yahoo announced new measures to protect users from phishing. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5c8e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>embarrassed</EXP><DEF>feeling uncomfortable or nervous and worrying about what people think of you, for example because you have made a silly mistake, or because you have to talk or sing in public</DEF><THESEXA>I was really embarrassed when I arrived at the party an hour early.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There’s no need to be embarrassed – you’ve got a lovely voice.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>self-conscious</EXP><DEF>embarrassed about your body or the way you look or talk</DEF><THESEXA>Paul had always been self-conscious about his big feet.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>uncomfortable</EXP><DEF>unable to relax because you are embarrassed and not sure what to say or do</DEF><THESEXA>There was a long silence and everyone at the table looked uncomfortable.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>awkward</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈɔːkwəd </PRON><AMEVARPRON> ˈɒːkwərd</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>feeling embarrassed because you are in a situation in which it is difficult to behave naturally</DEF><THESEXA>Teenagers often feel awkward in formal social situations.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There were some awkward moments when neither of us knew what to say to each other.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sheepish</EXP><DEF>slightly embarrassed because you know that you have done something silly or because you feel a little guilty</DEF><THESEXA>Nigel came in late looking sheepish and apologetic.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>red-faced</EXP><DEF>embarrassed or ashamed – used mainly in newspaper reports</DEF><THESEXA>A judge was left red-faced when his mobile phone rang in court.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mortified</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>extremely embarrassed and ashamed because you realize that you have done something very silly or wrong</DEF><THESEXA>He said he was mortified at the way his comments had been reported in the papers.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5c80"><HEADING>Sense 2</HEADING><Section><SECHEADING>causing strong feelings</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>emotional</EXP><DEF>causing people to have strong feelings – used especially about experiences, speeches, or subjects that people have strong feelings about</DEF><THESEXA>Returning home after a year in hospital was an emotional experience for Katy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He gave an emotional speech at the funeral.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a very emotional moment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Abortion rights is a highly emotional issue.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>emotive</EXP><DEF>used about issues or language that make people have strong feelings</DEF><THESEXA>Fox hunting is a very emotive issue in Britain.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Indoctrination’ is rather an emotive word.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>moving</EXP><DEF>making you feel strong feelings of sadness or sympathy</DEF><THESEXA>Kelly’s book about her illness is deeply moving.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a moving film</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>touching</EXP><DEF>making you feel slightly emotional – used especially when someone does something that shows how much they care about another person</DEF><THESEXA>Your loyalty is very touching.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My son phoned me to wish me good luck, which was a touching gesture. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>poignant</EXP><DEF>making you feel strong feelings of sadness or pity, especially when you remember something in the past</DEF><THESEXA>Her youthful expression is a poignant reminder of the passing of time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sentimental</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>schmaltzy</LEXVAR></Variant><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>dealing with emotions such as love and sadness in a way that seems silly</DEF><THESEXA>He found the film too sentimental.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a schmaltzy comedy</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5c6a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>emphasize</EXP><DEF>to say strongly or show clearly that a fact, idea etc is especially important</DEF><THESEXA>Our company emphasizes the need for good communication between staff.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stress</EXP><DEF>to emphasize something when you are talking about a subject</DEF><THESEXA>Most schools stress the importance of parental involvement in their child’s learning.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He stressed the need for parents to listen to their children. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>highlight</EXP><DEF>to show that something is important, so that people will pay special attention to it</DEF><THESEXA>This case highlights the need for tougher laws on gun ownership. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The report highlights the decline in the numbers of native plants and insects. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>underline/underscore</EXP><DEF>to help to show clearly that a fact is true, especially a fact that is already known</DEF><THESEXA>These attacks underline the fact that the security situation here remains fragile. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The president’s speech repeatedly underscored the progress that has been made.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>accentuate</EXP><DEF> to show something clearly and make it easier to notice</DEF><THESEXA>The recent economic crisis has accentuated the gap between the rich and the poor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>overemphasize</EXP><DEF>to emphasize something too much</DEF><THESEXA>The relation between food and health is often overemphasized in my view.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>play up</EXP><DEF>to emphasize something and make it seem more important than it really is, especially to get advantages for yourself</DEF><THESEXA>The story has been played up by the media.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The Labour party had a great time playing up the Conservatives’ problems.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5c54"><Section><SECHEADING>with nothing in it or on it</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>empty</EXP><DEF>used about something that has nothing inside</DEF><THESEXA>an empty can of hair spray</THESEXA><THESEXA>The fridge is almost empty.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blank</EXP><DEF>used about a computer screen or a piece of paper that has no writing or pictures on it, or a CD, DVD etc with nothing recorded on it</DEF><THESEXA>a blank sheet of paper</THESEXA><THESEXA>He stared at the blank screen for a few minutes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a blank tape</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bare</EXP><DEF>used about a room or cupboard that has very little in it</DEF><THESEXA>His room was bare except for a bed and a wardrobe.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hollow</EXP><DEF>used about something that has an empty space inside</DEF><THESEXA>a hollow tree</THESEXA><THESEXA>The suitcase had a hollow bottom. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>with no people</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>empty</EXP><DEF>used about a place that has no one in it or no one using it</DEF><THESEXA>There were no lights on and the house looked empty.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the empty streets</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>free</EXP><DEF>used about a seat, space, or room that is available to use because no one else is using it</DEF><THESEXA>Is this seat free?</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are never any parking spaces free at this time of day.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>vacant</EXP><DEF>used about a room or building that is available for people to pay to use</DEF><THESEXA>a vacant apartment</THESEXA><THESEXA>The next guesthouse we tried had a couple of rooms vacant.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>deserted</EXP><DEF>used about a place that is quiet because there is no one there, or because the people who used to be there have left</DEF><THESEXA>a deserted village</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was three o'clock in the morning and the streets were deserted.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>uninhabited</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˌʌnɪnˈhæbətəd◂</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>used about a place that has no people living in it, especially permanently</DEF><THESEXA>an uninhabited island</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unoccupied</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ʌnˈɒkjəpaɪd</PRON><AMEVARPRON> -ˈɑːk-</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used about a house, room, or office that no one is living in or using at the moment</DEF><THESEXA>unoccupied buildings</THESEXA><THESEXA>Burglaries frequently happen when people are on holiday and their house is unoccupied.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5c3a"><HEADING>Sense 3</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>end</EXP><DEF>the part of a place or object that is furthest from the centre or the beginning</DEF><THESEXA>the end of the table</THESEXA><THESEXA>the end of the street </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tip</EXP><DEF> the end of something, especially something pointed</DEF><THESEXA>the tip of your nose</THESEXA><THESEXA>an arrow tip</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>point</EXP><DEF> the sharp end of something</DEF><THESEXA>The point of the pencil broke.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5c2f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>end</EXP><DEF>if a event, activity, or story ends, it stops happening</DEF><THESEXA>How does the story end?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The school year ends in June.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>finish</EXP><DEF>to end – use this about an organized event such as a meeting, party, or lesson, especially when saying what time it ends</DEF><THESEXA>The meeting will finish at 5.30.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What time does your Spanish class finish?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be over</EXP><DEF>if an event, activity, or period of time is over, it has ended</DEF><THESEXA>I can’t wait for our exams to be over.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The long summer vacation was almost over.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come to an end</EXP><DEF>to finally end – used about a period of time, a situation, or an activity that has continued for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>The war finally came to an end six years later.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>draw to an end/to a close</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to end gradually over a period of time – used in written descriptions</DEF><THESEXA>These problems still remained as the twentieth century drew to an end.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>time is up</EXP><DEF>if time is up, you are not allowed any more time to do something</DEF><THESEXA>I wasn’t able to finish the test before the time was up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>time runs out</EXP><DEF>if time runs out, there is no more time available to do something, especially something important</DEF><THESEXA>The desperate search for survivors continues, but time is running out.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>expire</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if a ticket, bank card, legal document etc expires, the period of time during which you can use it has ended</DEF><THESEXA>I’m afraid we can’t accept this credit card – it expired last week.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be at an end</EXP><DEF>if something is at an end, it has ended</DEF><THESEXA>We both knew that our marriage was at an end.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The long wait was at an end.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5bfc"><Section><SECHEADING>person</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>enemy</EXP><DEF>someone who does not like you because you have had a big disagreement with them in the past or someone who opposes you in business or politics</DEF><THESEXA>If that’s how he treats his friends, I’d hate to be his enemy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In business, it’s best not to make too many enemies.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>adversary</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an enemy</DEF><THESEXA>When he retired, he was replaced by his old adversary.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He made the fatal mistake of underestimating his adversary.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>foe</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an enemy</DEF><THESEXA>A guard called out ‘Who goes there – friend or foe?’</THESEXA><THESEXA>his former foes</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>arch enemy</EXP><DEF>someone’s main enemy</DEF><THESEXA>In the movie, Batman goes into battle with his arch enemy the Joker.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>opponent</EXP><DEF>someone you are competing against, for example in a sports game, competition, or election</DEF><THESEXA>You win the game if you are left with more cards than your opponent. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He is admired even by his political opponents.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nemesis</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an enemy or opponent that is impossible to defeat – a rather formal use</DEF><THESEXA>In the final, he met his old nemesis, Rafael Nadal.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>country/army</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>enemy</EXP><DEF>a country or army that you are fighting against in a war</DEF><THESEXA>Soldiers who were captured by the enemy suffered terribly.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>foe</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an enemy</DEF><THESEXA>The Russian Army were a formidable foe.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5bdc"><Section><Exponent><EXP>energetic</EXP><DEF>having a lot of energy</DEF><THESEXA>If you’re feeling energetic, we could go out for a run.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>full of energy/bursting with energy</EXP><DEF>energetic and ready to work hard or do a lot of things</DEF><THESEXA>I admire her because she’s so full of energy and enthusiasm.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When she first started at the college she was bursting with energy and full of new ideas. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dynamic</EXP><DEF>very energetic and always wanting to do new things</DEF><THESEXA>What this country needs is a dynamic new leader.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hyperactive</EXP><DEF>having more energy than is normal or good, because you cannot keep still or quiet for very long – used especially about children</DEF><THESEXA>Our youngest daughter was hyperactive, and it had a damaging effect on the whole family.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tireless</EXP><DEF>working with a lot of energy in a determined way, especially to achieve a particular thing</DEF><THESEXA>She was a tireless campaigner against apartheid in South Africa.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>full of beans</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>feeling energetic and happy and showing this in the way you behave</DEF><THESEXA>He’s one of those people who leap out of bed full of beans every morning.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-57c6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>enough</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ɪˈnʌf</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>as much or as many as necessary, or as you want</DEF><THESEXA>My family never had enough money for holidays abroad.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Have you had enough to eat?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sufficient</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>enough for a particular purpose</DEF><THESEXA>The police did not have sufficient evidence to justify a charge.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The accuracy of the older technique was sufficient for our needs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>adequate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> enough in quantity or good enough in quality for a particular purpose</DEF><THESEXA>All staff must be given adequate training in health and safety.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The heating system was barely adequate.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ample</EXP><DEF>more than enough for what is needed</DEF><THESEXA>Local residents will be given ample opportunity to express their views.</THESEXA><THESEXA>People used to think that 1 GB of memory was ample for the average personal computer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>plenty</EXP><DEF> an amount that is enough or more than enough</DEF><THESEXA>Allow yourself plenty of time to get to the airport.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Your daughter won’t need much cash at camp ($20-$25 will be plenty). </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sth will do/sth should do</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used to say that a particular number or amount will be enough for what you need</DEF><THESEXA>‘How many envelopes do you want?’ ‘Ten should do.’</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5bb8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>enter</EXP><DEF>to go or come into a place. <DEFBOLD>Enter</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>go in</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>It appears the burglars entered the house through a back window.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Occupying troops entered the town on 8th April.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go in/into sth</EXP><DEF>to enter a place. <DEFBOLD>Go in/into</DEFBOLD> is the usual phrase to use in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>It was getting cold so we went in.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He went into the cafe and ordered a drink.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come in/into sth</EXP><DEF>to enter a place – used when you are already in that place</DEF><THESEXA>Come in and sit down.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When you come into the village, you’ll see the church on your right.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get in/into sth</EXP><DEF>to succeed in entering a place, especially when it is difficult or it takes a long time</DEF><THESEXA>I’d lost my key so I couldn’t get in.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You can’t get into the club if you are under 18.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>break in/into sth</EXP><DEF>to enter a building using force, for example in order to steal something</DEF><THESEXA>If anyone tries to break in, the alarm will go off.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>burst in/into sth</EXP><DEF>to enter a room or building very suddenly and noisily</DEF><THESEXA>Two men with guns burst in and told us to lie on the floor.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He burst into my office laughing and screaming like a maniac.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>barge in/into sth</EXP><DEF>to suddenly enter a room where you are not wanted because you are interrupting someone or you were not invited</DEF><THESEXA>She just barged into my room without knocking.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You can’t just go barging in.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sneak in/into sth</EXP><DEF>to enter a place quietly and secretly hoping that no one will notice you</DEF><THESEXA>If you’re late, just try and sneak into the back of the class.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slip in/into sth</EXP><DEF>to enter a place quietly and quickly</DEF><THESEXA>Maggie opened the door silently and slipped in.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trespass</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to enter an area of land that belongs to someone else without permission</DEF><THESEXA>The sign said ‘Trespassers will be prosecuted’.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Trespassing on the railway is a criminal offence in the UK.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5b9e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>enthusiastic</EXP><DEF>feeling or showing a lot of interest or excitement about to do something or know about something very soon</DEF><THESEXA>He was eager to hear every detail of her day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was always eager for her turn on the catwalk.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The room was full of eager young faces.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We waited in eager anticipation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>keen</EXP><DEF>eager to work or learn</DEF><THESEXA>She hasn’t much experience but she’s very keen.</THESEXA><THESEXA>John was a keen student who worked very hard.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>zealous</EXP><DEF>doing something in a very enthusiastic way, especially noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when you want to emphasize that someone does something a lot or is very interested in something</DEF><THESEXA>an avid golfer</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was an avid reader all her life.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve become an avid collector of his work.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Henry is an avid fan of the sport. </THESEXA><THESEXA>As a schoolboy he had an avid interest in birds.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fervent</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>believing or feeling something very strongly and sincerely</DEF><THESEXA>Galinsoga was one of Franco’s most fervent supporters.</THESEXA><THESEXA>One of Allen 's fervent admirers was Richard Nixon.</THESEXA><THESEXA>her fervent belief in promoting the work of good women artists</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be mad about sth</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to like something very much and be very interested in it</DEF><THESEXA>My sister’s mad about horses.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Since you’re so mad about clothes, you can help me choose a wedding dress.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>can’t wait to do sth</EXP><DEF>to be very eager to do something</DEF><THESEXA>I can’t wait to get started.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be looking forward to sth</EXP><DEF>to feel pleased and excited because you know that something is going to happen soon</DEF><THESEXA>I’m looking forward to the vacation.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5b75"><Section><Exponent><EXP>the environment</EXP><DEF>the air, water, and land on Earth, which is affected by man’s activities</DEF><THESEXA>We need to do more to protect the environment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>How much does air travel damage the environment?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ecosystem</EXP><REGISTERLAB>technical</REGISTERLAB><DEF>all the animals and plants that exist in a place, considered as a single system with parts that depend on each other</DEF><THESEXA>the island’s fragile ecosystem</THESEXA><THESEXA>The rapid decline in the number of great sharks is disrupting the marine ecosystem.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>habitat</EXP><DEF>the natural home of an animal or plant</DEF><THESEXA>The tree can grow to be 120 feet tall in its natural habitat.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the biosphere</EXP><REGISTERLAB>technical</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the Earth’s surface and atmosphere where animals and plants can live</DEF><THESEXA>Scientists are monitoring changes in the global biosphere.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ecology</EXP><DEF>the scientific study of the way in which the animals, plants, and natural features of a place affect and depend on each other</DEF><THESEXA>She’s studying marine ecology.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>green</EXP><POS>adj</POS><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>relating to the environment or to protecting the environment</DEF><THESEXA>Voters are becoming more concerned about green issues.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The party is keen to demonstrate its green credentials <GLOSS>show that it thinks the environment is important</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>eco-</EXP><POS>prefix</POS><DEF>relating to the environment and protecting the environment</DEF><THESEXA>eco-friendly products<GLOSS>which do not harm the environment</GLOSS></THESEXA><THESEXA>eco-tourism<GLOSS>which does not harm the environment</GLOSS></THESEXA><THESEXA>eco-warrior<GLOSS>someone who protests to try to save the environment</GLOSS></THESEXA><THESEXA>eco-sensitive land<GLOSS>where the environment is easily damaged</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-5dce" fullpage="yes"><Section><Exponent><EXP>environmentally friendly</EXP><DEF>not harmful to the environment</DEF><THESEXA>Cycling is very environmentally friendly.</THESEXA><THESEXA>environmentally friendly holidays </THESEXA><THESEXA>Is there such a thing as an environmentally friendly car?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>eco-friendly</EXP><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>not harmful to the environment – used especially about products</DEF><THESEXA>We always try to use eco-friendly cleaning products.</THESEXA><THESEXA>eco-friendly coffins made from newspapers</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>green</EXP><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>not harmful to the environment – used especially in the following phrases</DEF><THESEXA>green products</THESEXA><THESEXA>green technology</THESEXA><THESEXA>green energy</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was voted the greenest building in Britain.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>clean</EXP><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>clean fuels or forms of energy do not release any harmful substances into the atmosphere</DEF><THESEXA>We need cleaner fuels for cars and other road vehicles.</THESEXA><THESEXA> clean energy sources such as solar panels and wind turbines</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>renewable</EXP><DEF>renewable energy comes from sources that can be easily replaced naturally, so that there is always more available</DEF><THESEXA>The building is heated using renewable energy from the sun.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The government needs to invest more in renewable energy sources. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Wind power is renewable and produces no greenhouse gases during operation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sustainable</EXP><DEF>using the Earth’s resources, without causing damage to the environment – used especially about farming, ways of living, and development</DEF><THESEXA>The flowers are produced to high environmental standards using sustainable farming methods.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many people want to lead more sustainable lifestyles and to conserve the planet’s resources. </THESEXA><THESEXA>sustainable use of the world’s resources</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>carbon-neutral</EXP><DEF>balancing the amount of carbon gases that you put into the Earth’s atmosphere with other activities that will effectively reduce the amount of carbon gases, for example by planting trees</DEF><THESEXA>a carbon-neutral company </THESEXA><THESEXA>Stirling is aiming to become the UK’s first carbon neutral city. </THESEXA><THESEXA>All new homes will be carbon-neutral.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>low-carbon</EXP><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>producing only a small amount of carbon</DEF><THESEXA>Research is being done into the development of low-carbon electricity.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We will need to have a low-carbon economy.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>low-energy</EXP><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>low-energy buildings and lightbulbs use very little energy</DEF><THESEXA>Position is a key factor in the design of a low-energy house.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><ThesColloBox><SECHEADING>COLLOCATIONS <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5b55"><Section><Exponent><EXP>equipment</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>the machines, tools, or objects that you use for doing something</DEF><THESEXA>All the camping equipment is in the garage.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You’ll do a better job if you have the right equipment. </THESEXA><THESEXA>We are raising money for equipment for our playgroup.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gear</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>equipment and clothes for an activity that you do in your spare time</DEF><THESEXA>We loaded all our gear into the boat, and set off to go fishing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve decided to sell my climbing gear.</THESEXA><THESEXA>camping gear</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>kit</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>things for a particular purpose or activity, especially ones kept together in their own container</DEF><THESEXA>You’ll home.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>apparatus</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>tools and machines used for scientific, medical, and technical purposes</DEF><THESEXA>This experiment can be
