apparatus.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stuff</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>equipment that you use to do something</DEF><THESEXA>The builders have left all their stuff round the back of the house.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Where’s the decorating stuff?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>things</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>plural</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>the equipment or clothes that you need for a particular activity</DEF><THESEXA>She went into the shed where her father kept his gardening things.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They got all the Christmas things out of the loft.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>paraphernalia</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a lot of small things that are used for a particular purpose</DEF><THESEXA>All the paraphernalia for making tea and coffee stood on the bedside table.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The police found drug paraphernalia all over the house.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5b23"><Section><Exponent><EXP>escape</EXP><DEF>to leave a place when someone is trying to catch you or stop you, or when there is a dangerous situation</DEF><THESEXA>The thief escaped through an upstairs window.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She managed to escape from her attacker and call the police.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get away</EXP><DEF> to escape from someone who is chasing you, especially when there is no chance that you will be caught. <DEFBOLD>Get away</DEFBOLD> is more informal than <DEFBOLD>escape</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The robbers got away but left plenty of clues at the scene.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Don’t let him get away!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>break free/break away</EXP><DEF> to escape from someone who is holding you</DEF><THESEXA>She broke free and started running.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>flee</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF> to leave somewhere very quickly in order to escape from danger</DEF><THESEXA>Many people were forced to flee the country.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The two men fled before police arrived.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get out</EXP><DEF> to escape from a building or room</DEF><THESEXA>I was locked in the room and couldn’t get out.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>break out</EXP><DEF> to escape from prison</DEF><THESEXA>The jail is so secure that no one has ever broken out of it.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>abscond</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to escape from a prison or institution where you are supposed to stay</DEF><THESEXA>Three prisoners who absconded have still not been found.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He absconded from a psychiatric hospital.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5b03"><Section><Exponent><EXP>especially/particularly</EXP><DEF>used when you want to emphasize that something concerns or affects one person or thing more than others, or is true about a particular situation more than others</DEF><THESEXA>This disease mostly affects women, particularly older women.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Paris is always full of tourists, especially in the summer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s not easy to receive compliments, especially if you’re not used to them. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The surface is very slippery, particularly when it has been raining.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>specially</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>especially</DEF><THESEXA>I like parks, specially when they’ve got lakes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You have to be careful what you say, specially if there are children listening.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in particular</EXP><DEF>especially – used before or after a noun. Often used in the phrases <DEFBOLD>anyone/anything/anywhere in particular</DEFBOLD> and <DEFBOLD>someone/something/somewhere in particular</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Mary loves most classical music, in particular Bach and Vivaldi.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Kids in particular will love this show.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Is there <COLLOINEXA>anything in particular</COLLOINEXA> you would like to eat?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Was there<COLLOINEXA> someone in particular</COLLOINEXA> you were hoping to meet?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>notably</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used for saying someone or something is an important example of what you are talking about</DEF><THESEXA>The use of illegal drugs – notably marijuana – has increased in recent years.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A number of respected philosophers, most notably Leibniz, criticized Newton’s theories.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>above all</EXP><DEF>used for emphasizing that something is more important than all the other things you have mentioned</DEF><THESEXA>Get plenty of sleep, eat lots of good food, and above all try to relax.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>most of all</EXP><DEF>more than anything or anyone else</DEF><THESEXA>Swimming and soccer are fun, but I like dancing most of all.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Out of everybody at school she was the person who helped me most of all.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5ad0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>essay</EXP><DEF>a piece of writing written by a student as part of a course of study</DEF><THESEXA>We had to write an essay about the environment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Have you handed in your essay yet?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>paper</EXP><DEF>an essay written by a someone who has studied a subject as part of their research. <DEFBOLD>Paper</DEFBOLD> is also used about an essay that you have to write for a class at school, especially in American English</DEF><THESEXA>The professor has published over forty scientific papers on diseases of tropical crops</THESEXA><THESEXA>At the beginning of the school year we had to write a paper about our interests.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dissertation</EXP><DEF>a long essay written as part of a university degree</DEF><THESEXA>In the third year of their course, students have to write  a 10,000 word dissertation on a topic that has been approved by their tutor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>thesis</EXP><DEF>a very long essay that is part of an advanced university degree such as a master’s degree or a doctorate</DEF><THESEXA>He wrote his doctoral thesis on  the literature of the English romantic movement.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5aab"><Section><Exponent><EXP>essential</EXP><DEF>extremely important and necessary</DEF><THESEXA>Water is essential for life.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The right to protest is an essential part of any democracy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I missed out an essential ingredient.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They learn skills that are essential for success in later life.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>vital</EXP><DEF>extremely important and necessary. <DEFBOLD>Vital</DEFBOLD> sounds stronger than <DEFBOLD>essential</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>His evidence was vital to the defence case.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Nurses and police officers provide vital services.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It is vital that leaking gas pipes are fixed immediately.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>crucial</EXP><DEF>extremely important and making the difference between success and failure</DEF><THESEXA>Advertising is crucial to successful book selling.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The headteacher has played a crucial role in transforming this school.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>indispensable</EXP><DEF>if someone or something is indispensable, you cannot do something without them and they are extremely useful</DEF><THESEXA>She soon made herself indispensable in the office.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Mobile phones have become an indispensable part of our daily lives.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sth is a must</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when recommending something to someone and saying that they should definitely have it, do it, visit it etc</DEF><THESEXA>Virus checkers are a must for computer users concerned with security.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If you’re in Austria, a visit to Salzburg is a must.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5a94"><Section><Exponent><EXP>establish</EXP><DEF>to start a company or organization, especially one that exists for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>The company was established in 1899.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He established a new research centre in Dublin.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Most of the money will be used to establish local industries and mobilize the work-force.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>set up</EXP><DEF>to start a new company or organization. <DEFBOLD>Set up</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>establish</DEFBOLD>, and is the usual phrase to use in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>Kate and her partner are setting up their own printing business.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Dad set up as a builder in 1990 and now he employs over twenty men.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>open</EXP><DEF>to start a business that provides services to the public, such as a shop, restaurant, or hotel</DEF><THESEXA>He opened his first restaurant in 1995.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They just opened a new supermarket on Van Nuys Boulevard.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>found</EXP><DEF>to start a company or an organization such as a school or a hospital, especially by providing the money for it – used about something that was started a long time ago</DEF><THESEXA>Who originally founded the college?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The bank was founded 60 years ago in Munich.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>inaugurate</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ɪˈnɔːɡjəreɪt</PRON><AMEVARPRON> -ˈnɒː-</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to start an organization with an official ceremony</DEF><THESEXA>Twenty years after the airport was inaugurated, it introduced its first transatlantic flights.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-503b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>etc.</EXP><DEF>used when you want to say that there are many other examples of things of the same kind. The full form is <DEFBOLD>et cetera</DEFBOLD>, but this is rarely used. Don’t use <DEFBOLD>etc.</DEFBOLD> in essays or formal writing</DEF><THESEXA>They asked me about my past experience, qualifications etc.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Precise details of times, dates etc. should be recorded.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>such as</EXP><DEF>used before one or more nouns to say that there are many other examples of the same kind. It is better to use <DEFBOLD>such as</DEFBOLD> rather than <DEFBOLD>etc.</DEFBOLD> in essays and formal writing</DEF><THESEXA>It is best to avoid drinks such as tea, coffee or alcohol.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a distressing event such as the break-up of a marriage</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>to name (but) a few</EXP><DEF>used after examples of something when saying that there are many more you could have mentioned</DEF><THESEXA>Our daily activities include tennis, swimming, darts, snooker to name but a few.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>and so on</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used in spoken English when you want to say that there are other examples of things of the same kind. Don’t use <DEFBOLD>and so on</DEFBOLD> in essays or formal writing – it sounds too vague</DEF><THESEXA>You can do things to look after yourself in the way of diet, exercise, not smoking and so on.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>and many others</EXP><DEF>and many other people or things</DEF><THESEXA>The book includes poems by Christina Rossetti, William Blake and many others.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The college offers courses in maths, physics, computer science and many others. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5a6f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>event</EXP><DEF>something that happens, especially something important, interesting, or unusual</DEF><THESEXA>He spoke of the tragic event in which more than 100 people died.</THESEXA><THESEXA>recent political events</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>occurrence</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>əˈkʌrəns </PRON><AMEVARPRON> əˈkʌrəns</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>something that happens – used especially when saying how often something happens</DEF><THESEXA>Divorce is a common occurrence these days.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Storms like this one are fortunately a rare occurrence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Accidents are almost a daily occurrence on this road.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>incident</EXP><DEF>something that happens, especially something that is unusual or unpleasant, or something that is one of several events</DEF><THESEXA>He died after a violent incident outside a nightclub.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This latest incident could put an end to his career.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>occasion</EXP><DEF>an important social event or celebration</DEF><THESEXA>She only wore the dress for special occasions.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was his 100th birthday, and friends and family gathered to mark the occasion.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>affair</EXP><GRAM>usually singular</GRAM><DEF>something that happens, especially something shocking in political or public life which involves several people and events</DEF><THESEXA>The affair has caused people to lose confidence in their government.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>phenomenon</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>fɪˈnɒmənən</PRON><AMEVARPRON>fɪˈnɑːmənɑːn, -nən</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>something that happens or exists in society, science, or nature, especially something that is studied because it is difficult to understand</DEF><THESEXA>natural phenomena such as earthquakes</THESEXA><THESEXA>Homelessness is not a new phenomenon.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5a5c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>everywhere</EXP><POS>adv</POS><DEF>in or to every country or area of the world, or in or to every part of a place</DEF><THESEXA>Poverty affects children everywhere.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I've been looking everywhere for my keys.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>everyplace</EXP><POS>adv</POS><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF> everywhere</DEF><THESEXA>There you are. I've been looking for you everyplace.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>all over</EXP><POS>prep, adv</POS><DEF> everywhere on a surface or in a place</DEF><THESEXA>Jack's clothes were all over the floor.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Teams from all over the world will be taking part.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Fry the potatoes until they are brown all over.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>worldwide</EXP><POS>adv, adj</POS><DEF> everywhere in the world</DEF><THESEXA>The Olympic Games are watched by people worldwide.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The show has a worldwide audience of 50 million viewers.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nationwide</EXP><POS>adv, adj</POS><DEF> in all parts of a particular country</DEF><THESEXA>a nationwide study of adolescents</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company has over 200 stores nationwide.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>throughout</EXP><POS>adv, prep</POS><DEF>in every part of an area or place</DEF><THESEXA>The house is carpeted throughout.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He travelled throughout Scotland. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5a49"><Section><Exponent><EXP>exact</EXP><DEF>an exact  number, amount, or time is completely correct and is no more and no less than it should be</DEF><THESEXA>The exact weight of the baby at birth was 3.2 kg.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The exact time is 8:16 a.m.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>precise</EXP><DEF>precise  information is based on clear and exact figures or measurements, especially when it is important that no mistakes are made</DEF><THESEXA>We need to know your precise location.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s difficult to be precise about the number of deaths caused by smoking.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>accurate</EXP><DEF>completely correct because all the details are true</DEF><THESEXA>The witness tried to give an accurate description of what she had seen.</THESEXA><THESEXA>accurate financial forecasts</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5a2c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>exactly</EXP><DEF>used when emphasizing that something is no more and no less than a number or amount, or is completely correct in every detail</DEF><THESEXA>The bill came to exactly $1,000.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police are still trying to find out exactly how the accident happened.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>precisely</EXP><DEF>exactly – used when it is important to be sure that something is completely correct in every detail</DEF><THESEXA>We need to know precisely how much this is going to cost.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Can you tell us precisely where he is?</THESEXA><THESEXA>What precisely do you mean by ‘relativity’?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>just</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>exactly – used especially when saying that things are exactly right, exactly the same, or exactly in a particular position</DEF><THESEXA>The frame is just the right size for the picture.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He and his brother are just the same.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The hotel is just next to the station.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A new handbag! That’s just what I wanted.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>right</EXP><DEF>exactly in a particular position or direction</DEF><THESEXA>The ball hit me right in the eye!</THESEXA><THESEXA>There’s the house, right in front of you.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I got a mosquito bite right on the end of my nose. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He sat down right beside her.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>directly</EXP><DEF>exactly in a particular position or direction <DEFBOLD>Directly</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>right</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Amy was sitting directly opposite me.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>on the dot</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>at exactly a particular time, and no earlier or later than that time</DEF><THESEXA>She always leaves the office at 5.30 p.m. on the dot.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bang</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>exactly – used especially in the following very informal expressions</DEF><THESEXA>The train was <COLLOINEXA>bang on time</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The shot was <COLLOINEXA>bang on target</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Cockatoo Island is right <COLLOINEXA>bang in the middle of</COLLOINEXA> Sydney harbour.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5a00"><Section><Exponent><EXP>exaggerate</EXP><DEF>to say that something is much bigger, better, worse, more important etc than it really is</DEF><THESEXA>He exaggerated his own achievements.</THESEXA><THESEXA>These claims are wildly exaggerated. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The grass in the garden was about three feet high – I’m not exaggerating.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blow sth (up) out of all proportion</EXP><DEF>to say a situation or event is a lot worse or much more serious than it really is</DEF><THESEXA>The problem really has been blown up out of all proportion.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make too much of sth</EXP><DEF>to treat something that has happened as though it were more important or serious than it really is – used especially when saying that you should not do this</DEF><THESEXA>She loved the fact that he’d sent her flowers, but she didn’t want to make too much of it in case it meant nothing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It's probably nothing – children often get these strange ideas. You shouldn’t make too much of them</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>overemphasize</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>overemphasise</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE </GEO></Variant><DEF>to say that a part of something is more important and has a bigger effect than is really true, especially in relation to other things</DEF><THESEXA>The importance of cleanliness in the preparation of food cannot be overemphasized.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Historians have tended to overemphasize the role of the monarchy in British history.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>overstate</EXP><DEF>to describe something in a way that makes it sound more important or serious than it really is</DEF><THESEXA>Sometimes she <COLLOINEXA>overstates her case</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The importance of advertising can hardly be overstated.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>overplay</EXP><DEF>to make something seem more important than it is, in the way that you talk about it or treat it</DEF><THESEXA>The media has greatly overplayed this issue.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>glorify</EXP><DEF>to make someone or something seem much better, less harmful etc than they really are – used when you think it is wrong to do this</DEF><THESEXA>His films glorify violence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My father never glorified war. </THESEXA><THESEXA>These images were produced in order to glorify the empire.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>talk sth up</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to talk about something in a way that makes it appear more important, interesting, or successful than it really is, especially to get someone’s support or interest</DEF><THESEXA>At the interview, he talked up his experience of working in sales.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The government tried to talk up the threat posed by these countries, in order to justify an increase in military spending. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>overrated</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>if you think someone or something is overrated, you think they are not nearly as good as people claim</DEF><THESEXA>I think her books are overrated.</THESEXA><THESEXA>As a player he’s overrated.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>idealized</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>an idealized image, view, picture etc of someone or something shows them as being much better than they really are</DEF><THESEXA>His books give an idealized picture of English rural life.</THESEXA><THESEXA>her friend’s idealized view of marriage </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-59de"><Section><Exponent><EXP>examine</EXP><DEF>to look at something carefully and thoroughly because you want to find out more about it</DEF><THESEXA>Experts who examined the painting believe it is genuine.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her new book examines the causes of social discontent.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A team of divers was sent down to examine the wreckage.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The police will examine the weapon for fingerprints.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have a look at/take a look at</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to quickly examine something to find out what is wrong with it or to find out more about it. <DEFBOLD>Have a look at</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>examine</DEFBOLD>, and is the usual phrase to use in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>I’ll have a look at your car if you like.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>inspect</EXP><DEF>to examine something carefully to make sure that it is correct, safe, or working properly, especially when it is your job to do this</DEF><THESEXA>The building is regularly inspected by a fire-safety officer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some insurance people have already been here to inspect the damage caused by the storm.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Russian and American teams will have the right to inspect each other’s missile sites.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>analyze</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><ORTHVAR> analyse</ORTHVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to examine something carefully, especially detailed information about something, so that you can understand it</DEF><THESEXA>Researchers analyzed the results of the survey.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We’re still analysing all the data.</THESEXA><THESEXA>One of the problems in analyzing the situation is that we do not have all the information yet.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>study</EXP><DEF>to spend a lot of time examining something very carefully, for example a problem or situation</DEF><THESEXA>I won’t comment till I’ve had time to study the proposals.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A team of scientists has been studying the effects of global warming on Antarctica.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>review</EXP><DEF>to examine something such as a situation or process to see if any changes are necessary</DEF><THESEXA>The bank will review its security procedures after last week’s attack.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scrutinize</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><ORTHVAR>scrutinise</ORTHVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to examine something very carefully to find out if there is anything wrong with it</DEF><THESEXA>Congress is currently scrutinizing the deal.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-59b3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>example</EXP><DEF>something that you mention because it is typical of the kind of thing that you are talking about</DEF><THESEXA>The hall is a perfect example of medieval architecture.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are many ways in which technology has changed our lives. The car is an obvious example.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This painting is a typical example of Picasso’s work in his Blue Period.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>case</EXP><DEF>an example of something that happens, especially something bad</DEF><THESEXA>There have been some cases of women employees being fired because they are pregnant.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In one case a man was charged $2,000 for a simple medical check-up.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Recent government spending on schools is a classic case of too little, too late.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>instance</EXP><DEF>an example of a particular kind of situation or event, especially something bad. <DEFBOLD>Instance</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>case</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Some users of Ecstasy have actually died, but such instances are very rare.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The report describes instances of discrimination.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In a few rare instances, the disease can cause death.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>illustration</EXP><DEF>something, especially a story or situation, that you use as an example</DEF><THESEXA> As an illustration of this, consider the following example. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He mentioned the recent events in the Middle East <COLLOINEXA>by way of illustration</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>as an example</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>what you say when giving examples</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>for example</EXP><DEF>used when giving an example</DEF><THESEXA>Prices have risen sharply. The price of gasoline, for example, has risen by over 50%.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Nepal has many attractions for visitors. For example, you can go trekking in the Himalayas, or see tigers in Chitwan National Park.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>for instance</EXP><DEF>used when giving an example. <DEFBOLD>For instance</DEFBOLD> is slightly less formal than <DEFBOLD>for example</DEFBOLD> and is used more in spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>There were many unanswered questions. For instance, where was the money going to come from?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some people are really good at languages. <COLLOINEXA>Take</COLLOINEXA> Katie, <COLLOINEXA>for instance</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>eg/e.g.</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when giving an example or a list of examples. Don’t use <DEFBOLD>eg</DEFBOLD> in formal writing – use the full phrase <DEFBOLD>for example</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Make sure you eat foods that contain protein, e.g. meat, fish, eggs, milk, or cheese.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>such as</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when giving one or two typical examples when there are many others</DEF><THESEXA>It is difficult to get even basic foods such as bread and sugar.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when giving a particular example as a way of proving that what you are saying is correct</DEF><THESEXA>Take John – he has a good job but he didn’t go to university.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It is possible to recover from some types of cancer. <COLLOINEXA>Take</COLLOINEXA> skin cancer, <COLLOINEXA>for example</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be a case in point</EXP><DEF>used when emphasizing that someone or something is a good or typical example of what you have just mentioned</DEF><THESEXA>Some birds have returned to Britain after once being extinct here. The return of the osprey is a case in point.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5967"><Section><Exponent><EXP>excellent</EXP><DEF>extremely good or of very high quality</DEF><THESEXA>His English was excellent.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an excellent piece of work</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>great</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>extremely good. <DEFBOLD> Great</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>excellent</DEFBOLD>, and is the usual word to use in everyday spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>‘Did you have a good holiday?’ ‘It was great!’</THESEXA><THESEXA>He did a great job as captain.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fantastic/terrific/wonderful</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>extremely good. These words sound even more excited than <DEFBOLD>great</DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD>excellent</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>‘I passed my exam.’ ‘That’s fantastic!’</THESEXA><THESEXA> Last year Jess had a terrific season, averaging eleven points a game. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The trip will be a wonderful opportunity for the children.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>amazing/incredible</EXP><DEF>extremely good in a surprising and exciting way</DEF><THESEXA>an amazing view of Tokyo</THESEXA><THESEXA>The trip was an incredible experience.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>superb</EXP><DEF>extremely good – used especially when you are very impressed by something</DEF><THESEXA>The acting was superb.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is a superb range of cheeses. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>first-class</EXP><DEF>of a high quality and much better than most others</DEF><THESEXA>The service is first-class.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a first-class restaurant </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>outstanding</EXP><DEF>extremely good – used when saying how well someone does something</DEF><THESEXA>He’s an outstanding player.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was an outstanding performance by a talented young actor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>exceptional</EXP><DEF>unusually good – used when saying that someone or something is much better than any others</DEF><THESEXA>She has an exceptional talent.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He writes good essays, but I wouldn’t say that his work is particularly exceptional.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Merits are given as an honour for exceptional achievement.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>out of this world</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>spoken informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when saying that something is so good that it is better than anything you could have imagined</DEF><THESEXA>The food is out of this world.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>awesome</EXP><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><DEF>very good</DEF><THESEXA>It was an awesome concert.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-593d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>except</EXP><DEF>used when saying that a statement does not include a particular person or thing. At the beginning of a sentence, you must use <DEFBOLD>except for</DEFBOLD>, not just <DEFBOLD>except</DEFBOLD>, before a noun</DEF><THESEXA>The office is open every day except  Sundays.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Except for a man walking his dog, the park was empty.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Most of the critics liked the play, except for one critic on the ‘Los Angeles Times’.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Except for a few years in the early sixties and seventies, inflation has been a continuing feature of American life since World War II.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The area looks very much like the state of Iowa, except that  it is surrounded by beautiful snow-covered mountains.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Except in an emergency, these doors must remain closed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>apart from/aside from</EXP><DEF>used when mentioning one or two things that do not fit the main thing that you are saying</DEF><THESEXA>Aside from one or two minor errors, this is an excellent piece of research.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The films were all made in Hollywood, apart from one, which was made in the UK.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The weather was not very good in the first week. Apart from that, it was a good holiday.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>excluding/not including</EXP><DEF>used when saying that something, especially a total number or amount, does not include a particular thing or person. <DEFBOLD>Excluding</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>not including</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The software costs $49.95, not including tax.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Excluding  students, the total number of unemployed rose from 2 million to 2.3 million.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>with the exception of</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>except for one particular person or thing</DEF><THESEXA>Denmark has more wind turbines than any other place in the world, with the exception of California.</THESEXA><THESEXA>With the exception of skin cancer, breast cancer is the most common cancer among women.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>but</EXP><DEF>used especially after words such as <DEFBOLD>nothing, all, any, anyone, everything</DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD>everyone</DEFBOLD> when saying that something is the only thing, or someone is the only person</DEF><THESEXA>There is nothing but trees, for mile after mile.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The garment covers everything but the eyes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>All but a few of her family died of the disease.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>save</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used for mentioning the only person or thing which is not included in what you are saying</DEF><THESEXA>Every man she had ever loved, save her father, was now dead.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5906"><Section><Exponent><EXP>exchange</EXP><DEF>to give something to someone, and receive a similar thing from them at the same time. <DEFBOLD>Exchange</DEFBOLD> is often used about people telling each other about their ideas, phone numbers, addresses etc</DEF><THESEXA>They exchanged photographs before they met.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a place where people can exchange ideas</THESEXA><THESEXA>We exchanged email addresses.</THESEXA><THESEXA>if you are unhappy with the jacket, you can always take it back and exchange it for another one.</THESEXA><THESEXA>These coupons can be exchanged for meals and accommodation. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>change</EXP><DEF>to exchange something, especially money. Also used in British English about exchanging something you have bought for something different</DEF><THESEXA>I need to change some dollars. </THESEXA><THESEXA>She changed all her money into euros.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We thought it was time we changed our car for something more modern. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>swap</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>do a swap</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>BrE</REGISTERLAB></Variant><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> to give something to someone, who gives you something similar</DEF><THESEXA>The two schools use the Internet to swap pictures, stories, and jokes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I like your room better – do you want to do a swap? </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trade</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>do a trade</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF> to exchange something that you have for something that someone else has</DEF><THESEXA>The stolen phones are being traded for drugs. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The boys trade sports cards on the playground.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We've got lots of plants we don't need – do you want to do a trade?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>switch</EXP><DEF>to change the places of two or more people or things, so that each one is in the place the other was before</DEF><THESEXA>Can I switch seats with you?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reciprocate</EXP><DEF>to do or give something, because someone has done or given something similar to you – a rather formal use</DEF><THESEXA>They invited us to dinner a while ago, and I'd like to reciprocate.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in exchange/return (for sth)</EXP><DEF> if you give something in exchange or in return for something else, you give it in order to get something else back</DEF><THESEXA>Williams will plead guilty in exchange for a reduced sentence.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-58e4"><Section><Exponent><EXP>excited</EXP><DEF>feeling happy, especially about something good that has happened or is going to happen</DEF><THESEXA>He’s excited about his new job.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The kids always get excited on their birthday.</THESEXA><THESEXA>An excited crowd watched their team win 3–0.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When we get home, the dogs are always excited to see us.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Doctors are very excited by the discovery.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>thrilled</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>θrɪld</PRON></PronCodes><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>very excited and pleased</DEF><THESEXA>She was thrilled to hear that you were in London.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was thrilled when they told me that I’d got a place on the course.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>exhilarated</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ɪɡˈzɪləreɪtɪd</PRON></PronCodes><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>very excited and full of energy, especially because you are experiencing something new or something that involves risks</DEF><THESEXA>She felt exhilarated by her new freedom.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The climb left him feeling exhilarated.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>look forward to sth</EXP><DEF>to feel excited about something good that is going to happen and to think about it a lot</DEF><THESEXA>The kids are looking forward to their vacation – they’ve never been to California before.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She’s really looking forward to meeting him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>can’t wait to do sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be very excited about something good that is going to happen</DEF><THESEXA>I can’t wait to see him again.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>on the edge of your seat</EXP><DEF>extremely excited and interested when you are watching a film, game etc, because you do not know what is going to happen next</DEF><THESEXA>I was on the edge of my seat throughout the movie.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The game kept fans on the edge of their seats.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>on tenterhooks</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈtentəhʊks </PRON><AMEVARPRON> -ər-</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>very excited and nervous because you are waiting to find out what has happened</DEF><THESEXA>Don’t keep us on tenterhooks! Did you pass your test?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rapturous</EXP><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>rapturous behaviour shows that a large group of people are extremely excited and pleased</DEF><THESEXA>They received a <COLLOINEXA>rapturous welcome</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He came on the stage to <COLLOINEXA>rapturous applause</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>everyone was clapping and cheering</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-58b8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>thrilling</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈθrɪlɪŋ</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>very exciting</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gripping</EXP><DEF>a gripping film, story etc is very exciting and interesting</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dramatic</EXP><DEF>used about something that is exciting to watch or hear about as it happens</DEF><THESEXA>the dramatic events of the past week</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>exhilarating</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ɪɡˈzɪləreɪtɪŋ</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>making you feel happy, excited, and full of energy</DEF><THESEXA>an exhilarating ride</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nail-biting</EXP><DEF>very exciting, especially because you do not know what is going to happen next</DEF><THESEXA>a nail-biting finish</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-58a3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>exclude</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to deliberately not include someone or something, especially in a way that seems wrong or unfair</DEF><THESEXA>The new law protects most workers, but excludes those on part-time contracts.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>omit</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to not include something, especially a piece of information, either deliberately or because you forget</DEF><THESEXA>Sara’s name had been omitted from the list of employees.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>leave out</EXP><DEF>to not include someone or something, either deliberately or accidentally. <DEFBOLD>Leave out</DEFBOLD> is more common in everyday English than <DEFBOLD>exclude</DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD>omit</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Fans were shocked that Giggs had been left out of the team.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You must have left out one of the numbers. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>miss out</EXP><GEO>British</GEO><DEF>to not include someone or something that should be included, often by mistake</DEF><THESEXA>You missed out several important facts.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They’ve missed out the last letter of his name.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>drop</EXP><DEF>to decide not to include someone or something – used especially about not including someone in a team</DEF><THESEXA>He was dropped from the team because of injury.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company decided to drop the word ‘healthy’ from its advertising.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be exempt(ed) from sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when saying that a rule, law, agreement etc does not affect someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>People with bad eyesight were exempt from military service.</THESEXA><THESEXA>High technology equipment would be exempted from any trade agreement.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5875"><Section><Exponent><EXP>exercise</EXP><DEF>to walk, do sports etc in order to stay healthy and become stronger</DEF><THESEXA>To lose weight, exercise regularly and eat less.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>do some exercise/a lot of exercise etc</EXP><DEF>this phrase is much more common than the verb <DEFBOLD>exercise</DEFBOLD>, and means the same thing</DEF><THESEXA>Her doctor said that she needed to do more exercise.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My son does very little exercise – I don’t know how he stays so slim. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Dogs need lots of exercise.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stay/keep/get in shape</EXP><DEF>to stay or to become physically healthy and strong – used especially when you consider exercise as a way to keep a nice-looking body</DEF><THESEXA>Try jogging with a friend who also wants to get in shape.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>keep fit</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>to exercise regularly in order to stay healthy and strong</DEF><THESEXA>The class encourages older people to keep fit.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>work out</EXP><DEF>to do exercise in order to be healthy and strong, especially to exercise regularly in a gym or exercise class</DEF><THESEXA>He works out three times a week.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tone up</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>firm up</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to exercise in order to make your body or part of your body firmer</DEF><THESEXA>I need to tone up my stomach and legs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>warm up</EXP><DEF>to do gentle exercises to prepare your body for more active exercise</DEF><THESEXA>It’s important to warm up before you begin to play.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stretch</EXP><DEF>to reach your arms, legs, or body out to full length, in order to make your muscles as long as possible, so that you do not injure them when you exercise</DEF><THESEXA>Jog for five minutes, then stretch before starting on your run.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>limber up</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>loosen up</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to do gentle exercises so that your muscles are warm and not tight before you begin a more active exercise</DEF><THESEXA>The footballers were limbering up before a training session.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>train</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>to prepare for a sporting event by exercising in a particular way</DEF><THESEXA>She’s training to do the London Marathon.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>practise</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>practice</LEXVAR></Variant><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>to do a sports activity regularly, in order to get better and prepare for competition</DEF><THESEXA>The team practices on Wednesdays and Saturdays.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5863"><Section><Exponent><EXP>exhibition</EXP><DEF>an event at which a collection of paintings, interesting objects etc are shown to the public for a period of time</DEF><THESEXA>All the photographs in the exhibition are for sale.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The Great Outdoors Exhibition is a great place to find out about new outdoors activities.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>show</EXP><DEF>an exhibition. <DEFBOLD>Show</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>exhibition</DEFBOLD>. It can be used about small events as well as big public ones</DEF><THESEXA>The artist is preparing a show at the Museum of Modern Art.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He is putting on a one-man show in a local gallery. </THESEXA><THESEXA>a trade show</THESEXA><THESEXA>the London Motor Show at Earl’s Court</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>exhibit</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>an exhibition</DEF><THESEXA>an exhibit of Western paintings and sculptures</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fair</EXP><DEF>an event at which people or businesses show or sell their products, which is often not open to the public</DEF><THESEXA>the Frankfurt Book Fair for publishers and the media</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>exposition</EXP><DEF>a large and important public event at which people or businesses show or sell their products</DEF><THESEXA>177 galleries from nineteen countries will take part in the Chicago International Art Exposition. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

expensivecosting a lot of moneyan expensive carApartments in the city are very expensive.An underground train system is expensive to build.highcosting a lot of money. You use high about rents/fees/prices/costs. Don’t use expensive with these wordsRents are very high in this area.Lawyers charge high fees.the high cost of living in Japandearnot before nounBrEspokenexpensive compared to the usual price£3.50 seems rather dear for a cup of coffee.priceyˈpraɪsiinformalexpensiveThe clothes are beautiful but pricey.costlyexpensive in a way that wastes moneyUpgrading the system would be very costly.They were anxious to avoid a costly legal battle.cost a fortuneinformalto be very expensiveThe necklace must have cost a fortune!exorbitantɪɡˈzɔːbətənt -ɔːr-much too expensiveSome accountants charge exorbitant fees.astronomicalastronomical prices, costs, and fees are extremely highthe astronomical cost of developing a new spacecraft the astronomical prices which some people had paid for their seats The cost of living is astronomical. overpricedtoo expensive and not worth the priceThe DVDs were vastly overpriced.sb can’t afford sthsomeone does not have enough money to buy or do somethingMost people can’t afford to send their children to private schools.

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-582d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>expert</EXP><DEF>someone who has a lot of knowledge about something or skill at doing something</DEF><THESEXA>a computer expert</THESEXA><THESEXA>an expert on beetles</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s best to ask an expert.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>specialist</EXP><DEF>an expert who has studied a particular medical or technical subject for a long time and knows much more about it than other people</DEF><THESEXA>She is a specialist in corporate finance.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My doctor sent me to see a heart specialist.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>authority</EXP><DEF>an expert whose knowledge and opinions are greatly respected</DEF><THESEXA>The professor is one of the world’s leading authorities on African art.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>connoisseur</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˌkɒnəˈsɜː </PRON><AMEVARPRON> ˌkɑːnəˈsɜːr</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>an expert on food, art, literature, or design, who has had a lot of experience and knows when something is of very good quality</DEF><THESEXA>He was a connoisseur of fine wines.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His works are popular among connoisseurs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>buff</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>bʌf</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>someone who is very interested in a subject and knows a lot about it</DEF><THESEXA>a wine buff</THESEXA><THESEXA>Jazz buffs will be familiar with the band’s first album.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>virtuoso</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˌvɜːtʃuˈəʊsəʊ</PRON><AMEVARPRON>ˌvɜːrtʃuˈoʊsoʊ</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>an expert player or performer</DEF><THESEXA>The piece was played by violin virtuoso Pavel Sporcl.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a virtuoso pianist</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-581a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>explain</EXP><DEF>to give someone the information they need to understand something</DEF><THESEXA>She explained how to use the software.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Doctors usually explain the risks of the treatment to patients.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tell</EXP><DEF> to explain something. People often use <DEFBOLD>tell</DEFBOLD> instead of <DEFBOLD>explain</DEFBOLD> in everyday conversation</DEF><THESEXA>He told me how to get to his house. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The book tells you how to improve your health through diet. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>show</EXP><DEF> to explain to someone how to do something by doing it while they watch you</DEF><THESEXA>Ellen showed me how to work the coffee maker.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>demonstrate</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈdemənstreɪt</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to explain to someone how to do something by doing it while they watch you, especially when it is part of your job</DEF><THESEXA>The cabin crew demonstrated the safety procedures to the passengers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A qualified instructor will demonstrate how to use the equipment properly.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go through sth</EXP><DEF> to explain something carefully, especially one step at a time</DEF><THESEXA>Mrs. Riddell went through the homework assignment.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>set out sth</EXP><DEF>to explain a series of ideas, reasons or opinions in an organized way in writing or in a speech</DEF><THESEXA>In his speech, he intended to set out the party’s policies on education.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-57f8"><Section><SECHEADING>to explode</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>explode</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>if a bomb explodes, it bursts suddenly and violently with a loud noise</DEF><THESEXA>A bomb exploded in a crowded metro station this morning, killing five people.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go off</EXP><POS>phr v</POS><DEF>if a bomb goes off, it explodes. <DEFBOLD>Go off</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>explode</DEFBOLD> and is the usual phrase to use in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>Luckily the station was empty when the bomb went off.</THESEXA><THESEXA>As many as ten bombs went off across the city, most of them car bombs. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blow up</EXP><POS>phr v</POS><DEF>if a building, car, plane etc blows up, it bursts suddenly and violently into pieces, causing a lot of damage</DEF><THESEXA>The plane blew up in mid-air, killing all the passengers and crew.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In early 1986, a US space shuttle blew up shortly after launch.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>erupt</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ɪˈrʌpt</PRON></PronCodes><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>if a <NonDV><REFHWD>volcano </REFHWD></NonDV>erupts, it explodes and sends smoke and rock into the sky</DEF><THESEXA>The volcano has erupted at least fifteen times since 1883.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>burst</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>if something that has air or liquid inside it bursts, it explodes and the air or liquid comes out</DEF><THESEXA>One of the water pipes had burst.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The plane caught fire after its tyre burst on landing.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to make something explode</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>explode</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to make a bomb burst suddenly and violently with a loud noise</DEF><THESEXA>The terrorists planned to explode a car bomb outside the US embassy.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>set off</EXP><POS>phr v</POS><DEF>to make a bomb explode, either deliberately or accidentally. <DEFBOLD>Set off</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>explode</DEFBOLD> and is the usual phrase to use in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>The group set off a bomb outside a crowded cafe in Izmir last September.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police say radio signals were probably used to set the bomb off.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>detonate</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈdetəneɪt</PRON></PronCodes><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to make a bomb explode, especially by using special equipment. <DEFBOLD>Detonate</DEFBOLD> is a more technical word than <DEFBOLD>set off</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Army experts detonated the bomb safely in a nearby field.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The 200 kg bomb was detonated by terrorists using a remote-control device.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>let off</EXP><POS>phr v</POS><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>to deliberately make a bomb explode</DEF><THESEXA>Terrorists let off a bomb in the city centre.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The bomb was let off shortly before 3 pm.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blow up</EXP><POS>phr v</POS><DEF>to make a building, car, plane etc explode</DEF><THESEXA>He was involved in a plot to blow up a passenger jet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He struck a match and blew the whole place up.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-57e0"><HEADING>Sense 2</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>expression</EXP><DEF>a look on someone’s face that shows what they are thinking or feeling</DEF><THESEXA>His expression became more serious as he listened to her story.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She had a contented expression.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He has a very different expression in the next picture.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>look</EXP><DEF>an expression – used especially with adjectives that describe the expression. <DEFBOLD>Look</DEFBOLD> sounds less formal than <DEFBOLD>expression</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>She had a sad look on her face.</THESEXA><THESEXA>With a look of relief, he handed her the baby.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What’s that look for?</THESEXA><THESEXA>She gave me a dirty look <GLOSS>a look that showed she was angry</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>face</EXP><DEF>used when talking about someone’s expression, especially in the following phrases</DEF><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>You should have seen his face</COLLOINEXA>!</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>Look at my face</COLLOINEXA>. Am I bothered?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The boys were <COLLOINEXA>making faces</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>making strange, silly, or rude expressions which show that you dislike someone</GLOSS>through the window.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>different types of expression</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>frown</EXP><DEF>the expression on your face when you move your eyebrows together because you are angry, unhappy, or confused</DEF><THESEXA>With a frown, she asked, ‘So what’s wrong with that?’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>smile</EXP><DEF>an expression in which your mouth curves upwards, when you are being friendly or are happy or amused</DEF><THESEXA>She gave him a quick smile.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scowl</EXP><DEF>an angry or disapproving expression</DEF><THESEXA>There was a scowl of irritation on his face.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>glare</EXP><DEF>a long angry look</DEF><THESEXA>He gave her a furious glare, but said nothing.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grimace</EXP><DEF>an expression you make by twisting your face because you do not like something or because you are feeling pain</DEF><THESEXA>His face twisted into a grimace of anguish.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sneer</EXP><DEF>an expression that shows you have no respect for something or someone</DEF><THESEXA>‘That’s what you said last time,’ she said with a sneer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>smirk</EXP><DEF>an expression in which you smile in an unpleasant way that shows you are pleased by someone else’s bad luck or that you think you are better than other people</DEF><THESEXA>He had a self-satisfied smirk on his face.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pout</EXP><DEF>an expression in which you push out your lower lip because you are unhappy that you did not get what you want</DEF><THESEXA>‘You’re going away?’ she said with a pout.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-57d1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>extra</EXP><DEF>in addition to the usual or standard cost, time, amount etc</DEF><THESEXA>They let the kids stay up an extra hour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some stores charge extra for delivery.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Postage is extra.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>additional</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>more than the basic amount or the amount that you expected or agreed. <DEFBOLD>Additional </DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>extra</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>An evening job would provide additional income.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There may be an additional charge for paying bills by credit card.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>added</EXP><GRAM>only before noun </GRAM><DEF>extra – used when talking about something that makes something better or more effective</DEF><THESEXA>an added benefit</THESEXA><THESEXA>an added precaution</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a beautiful place, and the good weather was an <COLLOINEXA>added bonus</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Use a sunscreen for added protection.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spare</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>kept in addition to the one you usually use, so that it is available if the one you usually use breaks, gets lost etc</DEF><THESEXA>a spare key</THESEXA><THESEXA>a spare tyre </THESEXA><THESEXA>a spare set of clothes</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-57ac"><Section><Exponent><EXP>factory</EXP><DEF>a building or group of buildings in which goods are produced in large quantities, using machines</DEF><THESEXA>She works in a chocolate factory.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a clothing factory</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>plant</EXP><DEF>a large factory where cars, chemicals, or energy is produced</DEF><THESEXA>Local residents are protesting about the new nuclear power plant.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a car plant</THESEXA><THESEXA>a nuclear power plant</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>facility</EXP><DEF>a factory. <DEFBOLD>Facility</DEFBOLD> is often used instead of <DEFBOLD>factory </DEFBOLD> in business English</DEF><THESEXA>The new production facility is one of the most up-to-date in the area.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>works</EXP><DEF>used in the following compounds to describe a factory that produces a particular thing</DEF><THESPROPFORM>a steelworks/ironworks/brickworks/a chemical/cement works/a printing works</THESPROPFORM></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mill</EXP><DEF>a factory that produces paper, cotton, or cloth</DEF><THESEXA>a paper mill</THESEXA><THESEXA>The textile mill has been converted into luxury flats.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shipyard</EXP><DEF>a place where ships are built or repaired</DEF><THESEXA>The vessel was built in the Kobe shipyard. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>foundry</EXP><DEF>a factory where metal is made into things using <NonDV><REFHWD>mould</REFHWD><SUFFIX>s</SUFFIX></NonDV></DEF><THESEXA>Mandela’s statue was cast here in a local foundry.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sweatshop</EXP><REGISTERLAB>disapproving</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a small factory where people work hard in bad conditions for very little money</DEF><THESEXA>The company was fined for selling goods produced in sweatshops.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

failto not succeed – used about people, plans, methods etcThe plan failed.They failed to persuade her to change her mind.This method never fails.go wrongif something you do goes wrong, it fails after starting wellThe experiment went wrong when the chemicals combined to form a poisonous gas.not workif something does not work, it does not do what you want it to doThe drugs don’t work.I tried to fix it with glue, but that didn’t work.be unsuccessfulˌʌnsəkˈsesfəl◂to not have the result you wantedHis first attempt to get a teaching job was unsuccessful.The search was unsuccessful.be a failureto be unsuccessful, with the result that you have wasted your effortsThe government’s 5-year plan to modernize the economy was a complete failure.backfireif a plan or action backfires, it does the opposite of what it was intended to doHis plan to get attention backfired, and instead of being promoted he lost his job.in vainif you try to do something in vain, you fail to do itThey tried in vain to save him.All her efforts had been in vain.

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-574e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>failure</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>someone or something that is not successful</DEF><THESEXA>The book was a complete failure. </THESEXA><THESEXA>I felt a complete failure.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>flop</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>something that is not successful because people do not like it – used especially about a film, play, product, or performance</DEF><THESEXA>Despite the hype, the movie was a flop at the box office.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Their next computer was a flop.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>disaster</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>used when saying that something is extremely unsuccessful</DEF><THESEXA>Our first date was a disaster.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Their marriage was a total disaster.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fiasco</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C usually singular</GRAM><DEF>something that is completely unsuccessful and goes very badly wrong – used especially about things that have been officially planned, which go very wrong</DEF><THESEXA>The baggage system broke down on the first day the airport was open. It was a complete fiasco.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The fiasco came close to ending de Gaulle's political career. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>debacle</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C usually singular</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an event or situation that is a complete failure, because it does not happen in the way that it was officially planned</DEF><THESEXA>the banking debacle that has put our economy at risk</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shambles</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>singular</GRAM><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>if a situation or event is a shambles, it is completely unsuccessful because it has been very badly planned or organized, and no one seems to know what to do</DEF><THESEXA>The first few shows were a shambles, but things soon got better.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>washout</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>singular</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a failure – used when something is so bad that it would be better if it had not happened</DEF><THESEXA>The play wasn’t a complete washout; the acting was okay.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His most recent and ambitious project, a big-budget Hollywood film, was a washout with both critics and audiences alike.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>turkey</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>something that is so bad and unsuccessful that you think the people involved should be embarrassed about it – a very informal use</DEF><THESEXA>At the time most people thought the car was a complete turkey.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Since then he has appeared in a string of turkeys.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

fairtreating people equally or in the way that is rightIt’s not fair that she gets paid more than me.Everyone has the right to a fair trial.justformalmorally right and faira just punishmenta just causea just societyDo you think it was a just war?reasonable fair and sensible according to most people’s standardsa reasonable requestLateness, without a reasonable excuse, will not be tolerated.balancedgiving fair and equal treatment to all sides of an argument or subjectBalanced reporting of the news is essential.even-handed giving fair and equal treatment to everyone, especially when it would be easy to favour one particular groupThe drama takes an even-handed look at the consequences of violent crime, both on attackers and their victims.The film is even-handed and does not try to make you support either side.equitableˈekwətəbəlformalgiving equal treatment to everyone involvedWe need an equitable solution to this problem.a more equitable distribution of wealth

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5703"><Section><Exponent><EXP>fall</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>fall over</LEXVAR><LEXVAR>fall down</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to suddenly go down onto the floor when standing, walking, or running</DEF><THESEXA>She fell on the stairs and broke her ankle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Children are always falling over.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trip on/over sth</EXP><DEF>to fall or almost fall when you hit your foot against something</DEF><THESEXA>Someone might trip over those toys.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I tripped on a piece of wood.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slip</EXP><DEF>to fall or almost fall when you are walking on a wet or very smooth surface</DEF><THESEXA>She slipped and hurt her ankle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was scared I would slip on the highly polished floor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stumble</EXP><DEF>to almost fall when you put your foot down in an awkward way</DEF><THESEXA>He stumbled and almost fell.</THESEXA><THESEXA>One of our porters stumbled on the rough ground. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>collapse</EXP><DEF>to fall suddenly and heavily to the ground, especially when you become unconscious</DEF><THESEXA>One of the runners collapsed halfway through the race.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lose your balance</EXP><DEF>to become unsteady so that you start to fall over</DEF><THESEXA>She lost her balance on the first step and fell down the stairs. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Have something to hold onto, in case you lose your balance.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fall flat on your face</EXP><DEF>to fall forwards so you are lying on your front on the ground</DEF><THESEXA>She fell flat on her face getting out of the car.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-2c371bee.11a8d8e2f61.-7c9a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>be familiar with sth</EXP><DEF>to have a good knowledge or understanding of something, because you have used it, read about it etc before</DEF><THESEXA>Before the interview, make sure you are familiar with the company’s main products.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It will take a little while to become familiar with the new software.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be used to sth</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR> be accustomed to sth</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>to have experienced something before, so that it no longer seems surprising, difficult, strange etc</DEF><THESEXA>I’m used to eating spicy food.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Jay found the job hard to begin with, but he soon got used to it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Consumers have <COLLOINEXA>grown accustomed</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>become accustomed</GLOSS>to having a lot of choice.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Our staff are accustomed to dealing with the needs of foreign clients.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be at home with sth</EXP><DEF>to feel happy and confident about doing or using something, especially because you have had a lot of practice</DEF><THESEXA>Tom’s lived in Moscow for 20 years and he’s completely at home with the language.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It takes a few weeks for people to feel at home with the system.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be comfortable with sth/sb</EXP><DEF>to feel relaxed and not worried about doing something or being with someone</DEF><THESEXA>We’re all becoming increasingly comfortable with buying things online.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The activities are intended to help new students feel more comfortable with each other..</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5553"><HEADING>Sense 3</HEADING><Section><SECHEADING>something wrong</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>fault</EXP><DEF>a problem in a machine, system, design etc that causes damage or makes it not work properly</DEF><THESEXA>The fire was caused by an electrical fault.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a fault in the engine </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>defect</EXP><DEF>a fault in something such as a product or machine, resulting from the way it was made or designed</DEF><THESEXA>Cars are tested for defects before they leave the factory.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>weakness</EXP><DEF>a part of a plan, system, or argument that is not as good as the other parts, and makes it likely to fail</DEF><THESEXA>What are the strengths and weaknesses of each method?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>flaw</EXP><DEF>a fault in a plan, system, argument etc, especially one that makes it useless or not effective</DEF><THESEXA>Your argument has a fundamental flaw.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There was one major flaw in his suggestion – we didn’t have enough money.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bug</EXP><DEF>a fault in a computer program</DEF><THESEXA>A bug in the system was quickly fixed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>glitch</EXP><DEF>a small fault in the way something works, that can usually be easily corrected</DEF><THESEXA>I noticed a small glitch when installing the software.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mistake</EXP><DEF>something that is wrong in someone’s spelling, grammar, calculations etc</DEF><THESEXA>The article was full of spelling mistakes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>there’s something wrong with sth</EXP><DEF>used when saying that there is a problem in a machine, car etc, but you do not know what it is</DEF><THESEXA>There’s something wrong with the computer – it won’t close down.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-553f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>fear</EXP><DEF>a feeling of being frightened</DEF><THESEXA>He was trembling with fear.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Fear of failure should not stop you trying.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>terror</EXP><DEF>a feeling of great fear, because you think that something terrible is about to happen</DEF><THESEXA>She let out a scream of pure terror.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fright</EXP><DEF>a sudden feeling of fear, or a situation that makes you feel this</DEF><THESEXA>My body was shaking with fright.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You gave me a fright!</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s had a bit of fright, that’s all.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>panic</EXP><DEF>a sudden feeling of fear or nervousness that makes you unable to think clearly or behave sensibly</DEF><THESEXA>She was in such a panic that she hardly knew what she was doing!</THESEXA><THESEXA>There were scenes of sheer panic immediately following the bomb blast.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>alarm</EXP><DEF>a feeling of fear or worry which shows in your voice or behaviour, because you think something bad might happen</DEF><THESEXA>When I mentioned her name, he looked up at me in alarm.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The streets were calm and there was no sign of alarm.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>foreboding</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>fɔːˈbəʊdɪŋ </PRON><AMEVARPRON> fɔːrˈboʊ-</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>a feeling that something bad or unpleasant might happen although there is no obvious reason why it should</DEF><THESEXA>She felt the same sense of foreboding she had before her father died.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>phobia</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈfəʊbiə </PRON><AMEVARPRON> ˈfoʊ-</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>a permanent strong unreasonable fear of something</DEF><THESEXA>I had a phobia about going to the dentist.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-54e3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>fill</EXP><DEF>to put enough of something into a container to make it full</DEF><THESEXA>Jenny filled the kettle and put it on to boil.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Party balloons can be filled with helium.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fill up</EXP><DEF>to fill something completely – used especially about putting petrol in the tank of a car</DEF><THESEXA>I need to fill up the car.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The waiter filled up everyone’s glasses.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If the oil tank is less than half full, tell them to fill it up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>load/load up</EXP><DEF>to fill a vehicle with goods, furniture etc</DEF><THESEXA>Two men were loading a truck with boxes of melons.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stuff/cram</EXP><DEF>to quickly fill something such as a bag or pocket by pushing things into it tightly</DEF><THESEXA>She hurriedly stuffed some things into an overnight bag and left.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>refill</EXP><DEF>to fill a container again, after what was in it has been used</DEF><THESEXA>I’m just going to refill this bottle from the tap.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>top up</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>top off</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to fill a glass or cup that still has some liquid in it</DEF><THESEXA>Can I top up your glass of wine?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>replenish</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make something full again, especially with a supply of something such as water or food</DEF><THESEXA>The lake is fed by springs that are eternally replenished by the rain.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-54b2"><Section><Exponent><EXP>finally/eventually/in the end</EXP><DEF>after a long time, especially when there have been difficulties or delays. <DEFBOLD>In the end</DEFBOLD> is used especially in spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>After a lot of questioning, James finally admitted he had taken the car.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The plane eventually arrived at 6:30 – over three hours late.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In the end, I decided that the best thing to do was to ask Billy for help.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>at last</EXP><DEF>used when something good happens after you have waited for it for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>I’m really glad that Ken’s found a job at last.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>At long last </COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>after a very long time</GLOSS>he was able to see his family again.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>after much ado</EXP><REGISTERLAB>often humorous</REGISTERLAB><DEF>after a long time and when there have been a lot of worries, problems, or delays</DEF><THESEXA>After much ado, we moved into our new house last week.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-24d20af9.11ba14e1828.-7dad"><Section><Exponent><EXP>finished</EXP><DEF>if something is finished, you have done all of it</DEF><THESEXA>She showed him the finished drawing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was very pleased with the finished result.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>done</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>finished – used especially in everyday English instead of <DEFBOLD>finished</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>I can’t come out till my essay’s done.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They promised the work would be done by April.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>complete</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF> completely finished – used especially to emphasize that there is no more work to do</DEF><THESEXA>Six months later the job was complete.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The first stage of the project is now complete.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>over</EXP><DEF>finished – used about an event, activity, or period of time</DEF><THESEXA>Football practice is over at 4:30. fireflames that burn in an uncontrolled way and destroy or damage thingsIn April, a fire at the school destroyed the science block.a forest fireflamesthe bright parts of a fire that you see burning in the airThe flames from the burning building were lighting up the night sky.blazewrittena large and dangerous fire – used especially in news reportsFiremen fought to keep the blaze under control.infernowrittenan extremely large and dangerous fire which is out of control – used especially in news reportsThe entire building was on fire and hundreds of people were trapped in the inferno.conflagrationˌkɒnfləˈɡreɪʃən ˌkɑːn-formala very large fire that destroys a lot of buildings, trees etcThe conflagration spread rapidly through the old town.

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-53ea"><Section><Exponent><EXP>first/firstly</EXP><DEF>used when mentioning the first in a list of reasons, arguments, or questions to consider</DEF><THESEXA>I want you to consider these three points in your essay: first, what is the writer’s attitude to the war in this poem ...</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are several reasons for this conclusion.
