<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-53d4"><Section><Exponent><EXP>flat</EXP><DEF>on one level, without any holes or raised areas, and not sloping or curving</DEF><THESEXA>a flat roof</THESEXA><THESEXA>a flat screen</THESEXA><THESEXA>Before you lay the tiles, make sure that the ground is completely flat.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>level</EXP><DEF>not sloping in any direction, so that every part is at the same height</DEF><THESEXA>Is the top of this picture level?</THESEXA><THESEXA>After four hours coming down the mountain, I was glad to be back on level ground.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>smooth</EXP><DEF>without any holes or raised areas – used especially when saying how something feels when you touch it</DEF><THESEXA>her lovely smooth skin</THESEXA><THESEXA>I ran my hand across the animal’s smooth fur.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>even</EXP><DEF>without any holes or raised areas</DEF><THESEXA>Apply the paint to an even surface.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Be careful – the path is not very even here.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>horizontal</EXP><DEF>going straight across and not sloping</DEF><THESEXA>a horizontal line</THESEXA><THESEXA>Raise both arms to a horizontal position.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-53c2"><Section><Exponent><EXP>floor</EXP><DEF>one of the levels in a building</DEF><THESEXA>She lives in an apartment on the eighteenth floor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>storey</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>story</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>used when saying how many levels a building has</DEF><THESEXA>a five-storey car park</THESEXA><THESEXA>The school is a single storey building.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the ground floor</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>the first floor</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>the floor of a building that is at ground level</DEF><THESEXA>There is a shop on the ground floor.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The emergency room is on the first floor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the first floor</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>the second floor</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>the floor of a building above the one at ground level</DEF><THESEXA>She lives on the first floor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>deck</EXP><DEF>one of the levels on a ship, bus, or plane</DEF><THESEXA>The Horizon Lounge is on the top deck of the ship.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5343"><Section><Exponent><EXP>forbid</EXP><DEF>to tell someone in a very strong way that they must not do something or that something is <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-773ea7de.1187e6572e0.-6dcf"><Section><Exponent><EXP>force</EXP><DEF>to make someone do something they do not want to do. Used when people or situations make you do something</DEF><THESEXA>They were beaten and forced to confess to crimes they had not committed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The drought forced millions of farmers to sell their cattle.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make</EXP><DEF>to force someone to do something by using pressure, threats, or violence. <DEFBOLD>Make sb do sth </DEFBOLD> is more common than <DEFBOLD>force sb to do sth</DEFBOLD> in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>Her parents disapproved of Alex and they made her stop seeing him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Two men with guns made the staff hand over the money.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pressure</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>pressurize</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to try to force someone to do something by making them feel that they should do it</DEF><THESEXA>Some employers pressure their staff into working very long hours.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She felt they were trying to pressurize her into getting married.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blackmail</EXP><DEF>to force someone to give you money or do what you want by threatening to tell embarrassing secrets about them</DEF><THESEXA>She tried to blackmail him with photographs of them together at the hotel.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>compel</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to force someone to do something using official power or authority. Also used when someone has to do something because of their situation</DEF><THESEXA>The town was surrounded and compelled to surrender.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I felt compelled to offer them some kind of explanation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You are compelled by law to carry an ID card.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>coerce</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>kəʊˈɜːs </PRON><AMEVARPRON> ˈkoʊɜːrs</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to force someone to do something by threatening them</DEF><THESEXA>Local people were coerced into joining the rebel army.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be obliged to do sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if someone is obliged to do something, they must do it because it is the law or the rule, or because of the situation they are in</DEF><THESEXA>You are not obliged to say anything which may harm your defence in court. </THESEXA><THESEXA>They were obliged to sell the land.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-52c9"><Section><Exponent><EXP>forget</EXP><DEF>to not remember something or someone</DEF><THESEXA>I’m sorry, I’ve forgotten your name.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was an experience she would never forget.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>don’t remember/can’t remember</EXP><DEF>used when saying that you have forgotten something</DEF><THESEXA>I know I needed something at the shops, but I can’t remember what it was.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have no recollection of sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to not remember anything about something that happened in the past</DEF><THESEXA>He told the jury that he had no recollection of the accident.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slip your mind</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if something that you must do slips your mind, you forget to do it because you are busy thinking about other things</DEF><THESEXA>I’m sorry I didn’t call. There was so much going on that it completely slipped my mind.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>it goes in one ear and out the other</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used to say that someone forgets what you tell them very quickly because they are not interested or do not listen properly</DEF><THESEXA>With kids you have to say everything twice. It all goes in one ear and out the other.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>my mind goes blank</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used to say that you are suddenly unable to remember something at a time when you need it</DEF><THESEXA>I was so nervous that my mind went blank as soon as they asked me a question.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to try to forget something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>forget</EXP><DEF>to deliberately try not to think about something sad or unpleasant</DEF><THESEXA>After the divorce came through, I just wanted to forget about it all.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put sth out of your mind</EXP><DEF>to make yourself stop thinking about something that stops you concentrating or makes you angry, sad, or nervous</DEF><THESEXA>When I’m competing I put everything out of my mind and concentrate on winning.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put sth behind you</EXP><DEF>to stop thinking about something sad or unpleasant that happened to you in the past, so that you can continue with your life and be happy</DEF><THESEXA>It can take a long time to put a traumatic experience like that behind you.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take/keep your mind off sth</EXP><DEF>to do something that helps you stop thinking about a problem for a short time</DEF><THESEXA>Sara went out for a walk to it</DEF><THESEXA> He started drinking heavily in an effort to blot out the thought of what he had done.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I wanted to blot out the events of the previous day.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shut sb/sth out</EXP><DEF>to deliberately forget someone or something and not let them be part of your thoughts and feelings</DEF><THESEXA>The marriage was a disaster, and her husband shut her out of his life completely. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5298"><Section><Exponent><EXP>fragile</EXP><DEF>easily broken or damaged</DEF><THESEXA>The documents are old and very fragile. </THESEXA><THESEXA>a fragile glass case </THESEXA><THESEXA>The seventeenth century wall hangings are extremely fragile.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>delicate</EXP><DEF>easily damaged – used especially about things that are made from thin material and look attractive</DEF><THESEXA>a delicate gold necklace</THESEXA><THESEXA>The plant has delicate blue flowers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>delicate fabrics </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>brittle</EXP><DEF>brittle hair, nails, bones etc have a hard surface, but they break easily, especially because they are not in good condition</DEF><THESEXA>As you get older, your bones become more brittle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a special shampoo for dry and brittle hair </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>breakable</EXP><DEF>breakable objects must be handled carefully because they will break easily</DEF><THESEXA>Put breakable objects out of the reach of children.</THESEXA><THESEXA>breakable ornaments</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>flimsy</EXP><DEF>made of thin material that tears easily, or badly-made and likely to break easily</DEF><THESEXA>a flimsy cotton shirt</THESEXA><THESEXA> a flimsy wooden table</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>frail</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>not strong and therefore easy to break, damage, or hurt</DEF><THESEXA>The young trees are frail and need to be protected from the wind.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a frail little fishing boat </THESEXA><THESEXA>a frail old lady</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-527d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>free</EXP><DEF>something that is free does not cost you any money</DEF><THESEXA>Parking is free after 6 pm.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘How much is it to get into the concert?’ ‘Oh, I think it’s free.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>They were giving away free tickets for the concert.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m saving these tokens to get a free poster.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>for nothing/for free</EXP><DEF>without having to pay for something that you would normally have to pay for</DEF><THESEXA>He offered to fix the car for nothing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Fans were allowed into the stadium for nothing as a way of celebrating.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Children under five can see the show for free.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>at no extra cost</EXP><DEF>if a shop or company provides an additional service at no extra cost, they do it without asking you for any more money</DEF><THESEXA>The shop will install the cooker for you at no extra cost.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Tours of the city are available from the hotel at no extra cost.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>complimentary</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>free – use this about things that a company, theatre, hotel etc gives people</DEF><THESEXA>Karen’s sister works at the New York Ballet and she’s managed to get us some complimentary tickets.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Honeymooners receive a complimentary bottle of champagne  in their hotel room.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be on the house</EXP><DEF>if food or drinks are on the house, the owner of a bar or restaurant says you do not have to pay for them</DEF><THESEXA>These drinks are on the house.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gratis</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>without having to pay for something</DEF><THESEXA>Users will be able to listen to tracks <GLOSS>songs</GLOSS>gratis.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

friendsomeone who you know and like very much and enjoy spending time withDad, this is my friend Steve.She’s going to Palm Springs with some friends.I got a letter from a friend from college.Amy’s a close friend of mine.John was a really good friend to me when I had all those problems last year.acquaintanceəˈkweɪntənssomeone who you know and see sometimes, but who is not one of your close friendsWe borrowed the money from one of Paul’s business acquaintances.mateBrEinformala friend – used especially about boys or menHe always goes to the pub with his mates on Friday night.Terry’s an old mate of mine.buddyAmEinformala friend – used especially about men or young peopleHe’s out playing basketball with some of his high school buddies.palinformala friend – pal sounds rather old-fashionedThey met at school and have remained close pals.cronyusually pluraldisapprovinga friend – used about powerful people who will help each other even if it is slightly dishonestHe’s one of the president’s cronies.companionwrittensomeone who spends time with you, doing the same things as you – used about animals as well as peopletravelling companionsHis dog was his constant companion.the perfect companionthe girlsinformala woman’s female friendsWe’re having a girls’ night out.the ladsBrEinformala man’s male friendsa night out with the lads

friendlybehaving towards someone in a way that shows you like them and are ready to talk to them or help thema friendly smileThe local people are very friendly.warmusually before nounfriendly and caring about other people, in a way that makes people like you and feel comfortableHe was such a warm caring person and everyone loved him.We received a very warm welcome.niceespecially spokenfriendly and kindChris is a nice guy. I’m sure you’ll get on with him.He wasn’t very nice to the other children.amiableˈeɪmiəbəlformalfriendly and easy to likeTom was an amiable young man.welcomingfriendly to someone who has just arrived somewherea welcoming smileThe group are very welcoming to new members.hospitablefriendly and eager to make visitors comfortableI found Japanese people to be very hospitable.genialformalbehaving in a cheerful and friendly waya genial hostShe was in a genial mood.cordialformalfriendly and polite but formalThe two nations have always maintained cordial relations.approachablefriendly and easy to talk to – used especially about people in important positionsThe head teacher is very approachable.

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5227"><Section><Exponent><EXP>frighten</EXP><DEF>to make someone feel afraid</DEF><THESEXA>The thought of being in court frightened him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scare</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to frighten someone. <DEFBOLD>Scare</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>frighten</DEFBOLD>, and is the usual word to use in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>He was driving fast just to scare us.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It scared him to think that his mother might never recover.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>terrify</EXP><DEF>to make someone feel extremely frightened</DEF><THESEXA>The idea of going down into the caves terrified her.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Robbers terrified bank staff by threatening them at gunpoint.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give sb a fright</EXP><DEF>to make someone suddenly feel frightened in a way that makes their heart beat more quickly</DEF><THESEXA>It gave me a terrible fright when I found him unconscious on the floor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give sb the creeps</EXP><DEF>if a person or place gives you the creeps, they make you feel slightly frightened because they are strange</DEF><THESEXA>This house gives me the creeps.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>startle</EXP><DEF>to frighten someone. Used when you suddenly see someone and did not know they were there, or when you suddenly hear something</DEF><THESEXA>I’m sorry. I didn’t mean to startle you.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The noise startled him, and he dropped his glass on the floor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>alarm</EXP><DEF>to make someone feel frightened and worried that something bad might happen</DEF><THESEXA>I didn’t want to alarm her by calling in the middle of the night.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>intimidate</EXP><DEF>to deliberately frighten someone, especially so that they will do what you want</DEF><THESEXA>Many of the gangs were using dogs to intimidate people.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-51eb"><Section><Exponent><EXP>frightened</EXP><DEF>feeling worried because you might get hurt or because something bad might <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-51dc"><Section><Exponent><EXP>frightening</EXP><DEF>making you feel frightened</DEF><THESEXA>Being held at gunpoint had been the most frightening moment of his life.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The experience was very frightening.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scary</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>frightening. <DEFBOLD>Scary</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>frightening</DEFBOLD> and is very common in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>The movie was really scary.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There were some scary moments.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>chilling</EXP><DEF>frightening, especially because violence, cruelty, or danger is involved</DEF><THESEXA>a chilling tale of revenge, murder and madness</THESEXA><THESEXA>The court heard chilling details about the attack.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spooky</EXP><DEF>frightening and strange, especially because something involves ghosts or powers that people do not understand</DEF><THESEXA>The forest is really spooky in the dark.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a spooky coincidence</THESEXA><THESEXA>spooky stories</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>creepy</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>frightening in a way that makes you feel nervous, especially when you are not sure exactly why – used especially about places, people, and feelings</DEF><THESEXA> This place is really creepy. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-773ea7de.1187e6572e0.-6d1a"><Section><SECHEADING>the time after now</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>the future</EXP><DEF>the time after now</DEF><THESEXA>What </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>destiny</EXP><DEF>what will happen to someone in their life, especially something important</DEF><THESEXA>Sartre believed that everyone is in charge of their own <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-6530"><Section><Exponent><EXP>in general</EXP><DEF>used when saying that something is usually true in most situations, or about most satisfaction.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>generally</EXP><DEF>another way of saying ‘in general’, which is often used before a verb. <DEFBOLD>Generally</DEFBOLD> can also be used to say that most people have a particular opinion</DEF><THESEXA>Women generally live longer than men.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Newton is generally regarded as the father of modern science.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>generally speaking/as a rule</EXP><DEF>other ways of saying ‘in general’</DEF><THESEXA>Generally speaking, large breeds of dog are becoming less popular.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s a singer who doesn’t do interviews, as a rule.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The graduates are, generally speaking, a confident and articulate group of young people. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mostly/mainly/largely</EXP><DEF>used when saying that something is true about most people or things, or about most of something. <DEFBOLD>Largely</DEFBOLD> is slightly more formal than <DEFBOLD>mostly</DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD>mainly</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The disease mainly affects women.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Their attempts were largely that something is true in most cases, but not in every case</DEF><THESEXA>These problems have for the most part been resolved.</THESEXA><THESEXA>For the most part, the gangs were made up of boys aged between 11 and 16.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>by and large/on the whole</EXP><DEF>used for saying that something is true in most ways or in most cases</DEF><THESEXA>The project was, by and large, a success.</THESEXA><THESEXA>On the whole, people were very friendly.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-504b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>ghost</EXP><DEF>the spirit of a dead person that some people think they can feel or see in a place</DEF><THESEXA>His ghost is believed to haunt the house.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spirit</EXP><DEF> a creature without a physical body, such as an angel or ghost</DEF><THESEXA>evil spirits</THESEXA><THESEXA>the spirit world</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>apparition</EXP><DEF>an image of a dead person that someone sees suddenly for a short time</DEF><THESEXA>He claimed to have seen an apparition in the church.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>poltergeist</EXP><DEF>a ghost that people cannot see, which throws things or moves things around</DEF><THESEXA>The house was haunted by a poltergeist that makes things move around all by themselves, sometimes quite big things like beds or wardrobes. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spook</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a ghost</DEF><THESEXA>I’m not scared of spooks.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>phantom</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF> a frightening and unclear image of a dead person</DEF><THESEXA>They had seen phantoms gliding on the surface of the water.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spectre</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>specter</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a ghost, especially a frightening one</DEF><THESEXA>She had looked like a spectre.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The following night, the spectre appeared again.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4f9c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>goodbye</EXP><DEF>used when you are leaving or when someone is leaving you. <DEFBOLD>Goodbye</DEFBOLD> sounds rather formal and is used especially when talking to people you do not know well. In everyday English people usually just say <DEFBOLD>bye</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>‘Goodbye, Mrs Moore.’ ‘Goodbye, Dr Aziz.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>Thank you for calling. Goodbye.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bye</EXP><DEF>goodbye</DEF><THESEXA>‘Bye, Annie.’ ‘Bye, Mom,’ she said, kissing her mother on the cheek.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Bye everyone!</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>Bye for now </COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>used especially on the phone when you will speak to someone soon</GLOSS> - call me if you need anything.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>see you</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when saying goodbye to a friend who you will see again soon</DEF><THESEXA>See you, Darren.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘I’m going now.’ ‘Okay. <COLLOINEXA>See you tomorrow</COLLOINEXA>.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>Safe trip back guys and we’ll <COLLOINEXA>see you soon</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>later/catch you later</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when saying goodbye to a friend you will see again soon. These expressions sound very informal and are used especially by young people</DEF><THESEXA>Catch you later, Matt. </THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Later, Mike.’ ‘Later, Steve.’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>see you around</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when saying goodbye to someone and you are not sure when you will see them again</DEF><THESEXA>He spent the night with me and then just said ‘See you around’ and left.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>so long</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>used when saying goodbye to someone who you will not see again for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>‘So long,’ he said. ‘Don’t forget to write.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>She grabbed Nick by the shoulders and hugged him affectionately. ‘So long, Nick.’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have a good weekend/a great trip etc</EXP><DEF>used when saying goodbye to someone to wish them a good weekend, an enjoyable trip etc</DEF><THESEXA>Have a nice weekend.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Have a great time at the concert, guys!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have a nice day</EXP><DEF>used by people working in shops when saying goodbye to customers</DEF><THESEXA>Here’s your change. Have a nice day.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take care</EXP><DEF>used when saying goodbye to someone, especially someone you like every much, to show that you care about them</DEF><THESEXA>‘Bye love ! Take care.’ ‘You too! <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4ebf"><Section><Exponent><EXP>guilt</EXP><DEF>the feeling you have when you have done something you know is wrong</DEF><THESEXA>Divorce often leaves people with feelings of guilt.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shame</EXP><DEF> the feeling of being guilty or embarrassed that you have after doing something that is wrong, when you feel you have lost people’s respect</DEF><THESEXA>I was too scared to help him, and I was filled with shame.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>regret</EXP><DEF>a feeling of sadness about something, especially because you wish it had not happened</DEF><THESEXA>Kate watched her go with a pang of regret.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>remorse</EXP><DEF> a strong feeling of being sorry for doing something very bad</DEF><THESEXA>a murderer who showed no remorse</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>contrition</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a feeling of being guilty and sorry for something wrong that you have done</DEF><THESEXA>The company CEO expressed contrition for the errors that led to the crash.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He sounded full of contrition.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They wanted to perform some kind of <COLLOINEXA>act of contrition</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>do something that shows you feel sorry for something </GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>penitence</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a feeling of being sorry for something that you have done wrong, when you do not intend to do it again</DEF><THESEXA>He expressed genuine penitence at the harm he had done her.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a period of reflection and penitence</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conscience</EXP><DEF> the part of your mind that tells you whether what you are doing is morally right or wrong</DEF><THESEXA>My conscience has been troubling me ever since.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

Sense 2
guiltyif someone is guilty of a crime or doing something wrong, they did it, and they should be punished for itShe was found guilty of murder.He was guilty of serious misconduct.The two guilty men were ordered to pay damages to their victims.responsiblenot before nounused when saying who should be blamed for something bad that has happenedPolice believe a local gang is responsible for the burglaries.As manager, he is ultimately responsible for the failure of the project.The other driver was responsible for the accident.be to blameif someone is to blame for a bad situation, they are responsibleThe government is partly to blame for the crisis.If anything goes wrong, it’s never him that is to blame.culpableˈkʌlpəbəlformalresponsible for something bad or illegal, so that you deserve to be blamed or punishedThe people who helped the terrorists are equally culpable for what happened on July 7th.He pleaded guilty to culpable homicidebeing guilty of causing someone’s death. negligentˈneɡlɪdʒəntnot usually before nounresponsible for something bad that has happened, because you did not take enough care, or you did not try to stop it from happeningThe court decided that the railway company was negligent.be in the wrongto be responsible for an accident, mistake etc – used when deciding which person, group etc should be blamedThe other driver was clearly in the wrong. She always thinks it’s me who is in the wrong.incriminatingused about things which seem to show that someone is guilty of a crimeincriminating evidenceincriminating documentsHe didn’t want to say anything incriminating.

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4ea7"><Section><Exponent><EXP>habit</EXP><DEF>something you do regularly, often without thinking about it</DEF><THESEXA>Biting your nails is a bad habit.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I always go to the same supermarket, out of habit.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mannerism</EXP><DEF>a way of speaking or a small movement of your face or body that is part of your usual behaviour</DEF><THESEXA>Even her mannerisms are the same as her sister’s.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>custom</EXP><DEF>something that people in a particular society do because it is traditional or the accepted thing to do</DEF><THESEXA>In Japan, it is the custom to take off your shoes when you enter a house.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tradition</EXP><DEF>a belief, custom, or way of doing something that has existed for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>The tradition of giving Easter eggs goes back hundreds of years.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In many countries, it’s a tradition for the bride to wear white.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a family tradition to go for a walk on Christmas Day.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>practice</EXP><DEF>something that people often do, especially as part of their work or daily life</DEF><THESEXA>The hotel has ended the practice of leaving chocolates in guests’ rooms.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-773ea7de.1187e6572e0.-6f74"><Section><Exponent><EXP>harm</EXP><DEF>to have a bad effect on someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>Many working mothers said having children harmed their careers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A little hard work never harmed anyone.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>damage</EXP><DEF>to harm something badly. <DEFBOLD>Damage</DEFBOLD> is more serious than <DEFBOLD>harm</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>His reputation was damaged and his career was in ruins.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The affair has damaged people’s confidence in the government.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be bad for sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to be likely to harm someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>Too much fatty food is bad for you.</THESEXA><THESEXA>All this rain is bad for business.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be detrimental to sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be bad for something</DEF><THESEXA>The new housing development will be detrimental to the character of this small town.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>impair</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to harm something, especially someone’s ability to do something or the correct working of a system</DEF><THESEXA>Any amount of alcohol that you drink will impair your ability to drive.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His vision was impaired.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>prejudice</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈpredʒədɪs</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to have a bad effect on the future success of something</DEF><THESEXA>Don’t do anything to prejudice our chances of winning.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4d56"><Section><Exponent><EXP>hello</EXP><DEF>used when you meet someone, or when you start a telephone conversation. How’s it going?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>good morning/good afternoon/good evening</EXP><DEF>used when meeting someone at a particular time of day. In everyday English, people usually just say <DEFBOLD>Morning!</DEFBOLD>, <DEFBOLD>Afternoon!</DEFBOLD> etc</DEF><THESEXA>Good morning, class!</THESEXA><THESEXA>Morning everyone. Sorry I’m late.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Good afternoon, Mr Smith.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>how are you?</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>how are you doing?/how’s it going?</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>used when you are greeting someone and starting a conversation</DEF><THESEXA>How’s it going Tom? I haven’t seen you for ages.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Hi Helen. How are you doing? Is the new job going well?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>how do you do?</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when you meet someone for the first time, especially after you have just been told their name. <DEFBOLD>How do you do</DEFBOLD> sounds very formal. These days in everyday English, people often say <DEFBOLD>hi</DEFBOLD> when meeting someone for the first time</DEF><THESEXA>‘John, I’d like you to meet our new project manager, Nisha Patel.’ ‘How do you do?’ </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pleased/good/nice to meet you</EXP><DEF>used when you meet someone for the first time and have just been told their name</DEF><THESEXA>‘Richard, this is my brother Ronnie.’ ‘Nice to meet you, Ronnie.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘My name is Lena Curtis.’ ‘Pleased to meet you, I’m David Bennet.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>Mrs Parrish, it’s good to meet you after hearing Lynn talk so much about you.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4d33"><Section><Exponent><EXP>help</EXP><DEF>to make it easier for someone to do something, by doing something for them or giving them something they need</DEF><THESEXA>Is there anything I can do to help?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Dad, I can’t do my homework. Will you help me?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assist</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal </REGISTERLAB><DEF>to help someone</DEF><THESEXA> He was employed to assist the manager in his duties.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some of the guests assisted with the preparation of the food.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>aid</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal </REGISTERLAB><DEF>to help someone to do something – used especially when saying that something helps your body to do something</DEF><THESEXA>Coffee can aid concentration. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Fennel aids the digestion.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are plenty of materials to aid the teacher.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>help out</EXP><DEF>to help someone, especially because there are not enough people to do all the work, or they need someone to give them something</DEF><THESEXA>Organizing the school trip will be a lot of work, so I need some volunteers to help out.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My parents have helped us out on several occasions by sending us money.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give sb a hand</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to help someone to do something, especially by carrying or lifting things</DEF><THESEXA>Can you give me a hand moving these boxes?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Dave wants to paint the kitchen and I promised I’d give him a hand.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lend a hand</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to help someone, especially when there are not enough people to do something</DEF><THESEXA>Scott is moving on Saturday and we promised to lend a hand.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I went over to see if I could lend a hand.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-4817"><Section><Exponent><EXP>help</EXP><DEF>the things you do to make it easier or possible for someone to do something</DEF><THESEXA>Now that I’m working, the kids need to give me more help with the housework.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The book would never have been finished without his help.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assistance</EXP><DEF>help. <DEFBOLD>Assistance</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>help</DEFBOLD>, and is used especially about people giving official help</DEF><THESEXA>The police are asking the public for their assistance.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The project received financial assistance from the government.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Thank you very much for your assistance, sir.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He set up the business <COLLOINEXA>with the assistance of</COLLOINEXA> his two sons.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>aid</EXP><DEF>help. <DEFBOLD>Aid </DEFBOLD>is more formal than <DEFBOLD>help</DEFBOLD>, and is used especially about money, food, medicine etc. that is given to countries or people that are in a very bad situation</DEF><THESEXA>Aid is being sent to areas affected by the earthquake.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The US spends billions of dollars on aid to developing countries.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Another driver stopped and <COLLOINEXA>came to his aid</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>support</EXP><DEF>help and encouragement</DEF><THESEXA>Thanks to everyone who gave us their support.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We could not have won the case without your support.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cooperation</EXP><DEF> help – used especially when people, organizations, or countries work together to get things done, and show that they are willing to do this</DEF><THESEXA>a spirit of international cooperation</THESEXA><THESEXA>The cooperation of landowners was needed for the plan to succeed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>backup</EXP><DEF>extra people, equipment etc that can be used in case people need help</DEF><THESEXA>The officer waited for backup to arrive before making any arrests.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4d19"><Section><Exponent><EXP>hide</EXP><DEF>to make something difficult to see or find, or to not show your true feelings</DEF><THESEXA>He hid the gun in his pocket.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She tried to hide her anger.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The actress put up a hand to hide her face from the cameras.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conceal</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to hide something, especially by carefully putting it somewhere. Also used when talking about hiding your feelings, especially in negative sentences</DEF><THESEXA>Several kilos of drugs were concealed in the back of the truck.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He could not conceal his feelings any longer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The girl quickly concealed the photograph she had been gazing at.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cover up</EXP><DEF>to put something over another thing that you do not want people to see, in order to hide it completely</DEF><THESEXA>People cover up cracks with wallpaper or tiles.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I used some make-up to cover up the spots.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was wearing a thin shawl to cover up the bruises on her arm.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>disguise</EXP><DEF>to make someone or something seem like a different person or thing, so that other people cannot recognize them</DEF><THESEXA>She managed to get into the camp by disguising herself as a soldier.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The men had disguised the vessel as fishing boat.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>camouflage</EXP><DEF>to hide something by covering it with materials that make it look like the things around it</DEF><THESEXA>We camouflaged the plane by covering it with leaves.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The troops used charcoal to camouflage their faces.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Soldiers had camouflaged the trucks with branches and dirt.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>obscure</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make it difficult to see something clearly</DEF><THESEXA>The view was obscured by mist.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His body was found, partially obscured by bushes, at the bottom of a shallow canyon.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mask</EXP><DEF>to make something less noticeable, for example a taste, a smell, a sound, or a feeling</DEF><THESEXA>The lemon helps to mask the taste of the fish.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Helen had turned on the radio to mask the noise of the traffic.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He did little to mask his contempt.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4d06"><Section><SECHEADING>buildings/mountains etc</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>high</EXP><DEF>measuring a long distance from the bottom to the top – used about mountains, walls, and buildings</DEF><THESEXA>the highest mountain in the world</THESEXA><THESEXA>The castle was surrounded by high walls.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a high cliff</THESEXA><THESEXA>The council told the architects the tower was too high.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tall</EXP><DEF>high – used about people, trees, plants, and buildings. <DEFBOLD>Tall</DEFBOLD> is used especially about things that are high and narrow</DEF><THESEXA> tall marble columns </THESEXA><THESEXA>A cat was hiding in the tall grass.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a tall modern building</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>majestic</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>very impressive because of being very big and tall – used about mountains, buildings, trees, and animals</DEF><THESEXA>the majestic mountains of the Himalayas</THESEXA><THESEXA>The abbey is noted for its majestic arches, fine doorways and elegant windows.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The cathedral looked majestic in the evening light. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>soaring</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used about a building or mountain that looks extremely tall and impressive</DEF><THESEXA>a soaring skyscraper</THESEXA><THESEXA>the soaring towers of the palace</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>towering</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>extremely high, in a way that seems impressive but also often rather frightening</DEF><THESEXA>The sky was shut out by the towering walls of the prison. </THESEXA><THESEXA>towering trees</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lofty</EXP><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>very high and impressive – used in literature</DEF><THESEXA>the lofty peaks in the far distance</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>high-rise</EXP><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>a high-rise building is a tall modern building with a lot of floors containing apartments or offices</DEF><THESEXA>a high-rise apartment block</THESEXA><THESEXA>He works in a high-rise office in New York.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><ThesColloBox><SECHEADING>COLLOCATIONS CHECK</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>high</EXP><COLLORANGE>mountain/building/wall/fence</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tall </EXP><COLLORANGE>person/tree/plant/building/tower/statue</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>majestic</EXP><COLLORANGE>mountain/building/tree/animal</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>soaring</EXP><COLLORANGE>building/tower/mountain</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>towering</EXP><COLLORANGE>wall/tree/mountain/building</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lofty</EXP><COLLORANGE>building/mountain/tree/heights</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>high-rise</EXP><COLLORANGE>building</COLLORANGE></Exponent></ThesColloBox><Section><SECHEADING>sounds</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>high-pitched</EXP><DEF>higher than most sounds or voices</DEF><THESEXA>He has a rather high-pitched voice.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Bats make high-pitched squeaks. </THESEXA><THESEXA>the high-pitched whine of a dentist's drill</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shrill</EXP><DEF>high and unpleasant</DEF><THESEXA>Her voice became more shrill.</THESEXA><THESEXA> The bird has rather a shrill cry. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>piercing</EXP><DEF>extremely high and loud, in a way that is unpleasant</DEF><THESEXA>a piercing scream</THESEXA><THESEXA>Suddenly I heard a piercing whistle.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>squeaky</EXP><DEF>making very high noises that are not loud</DEF><THESEXA>a squeaky gate</THESEXA><THESEXA>squeaky floorboards</THESEXA><THESEXA>a squeaky little voice</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4cf4" fullpage="yes"><Section><SECHEADING>to hit someone</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>hit</EXP><DEF>to hit someone quickly and hard with your hand, a stick etc</DEF><THESEXA>He hit him hard in the stomach.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t like to see people hitting a dog.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>beat</EXP><DEF>to hit someone deliberately many times, especially very hard</DEF><THESEXA>The girl had been beaten to death.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was beating the donkey with a stick.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>strike</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to hit someone with your hand or a weapon. <DEFBOLD>Strike </DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>hit</DEFBOLD> and is mainly used in written English</DEF><THESEXA>Her husband struck her twice across the face.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police say that the man had been struck on the head.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>punch</EXP><DEF>to hit someone hard with your closed hand, especially in a fight</DEF><THESEXA>I punched him on the nose.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was screaming and punching him with her fists.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>thump</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>θʌmp</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to punch someone very hard</DEF><THESEXA>Sometimes I just want to thump him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>beat sb up</EXP><DEF>to hurt someone badly in a violent attack, by hitting them many times</DEF><THESEXA>If I tell the police, they'll beat me up.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He had been beaten up and tortured with lighted cigarettes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slap</EXP><DEF>to hit someone with your open hand, especially because you are angry with them</DEF><THESEXA>They had a big row and she ended up slapping him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spank</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>smack</LEXVAR><GEO>especially BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to hit someone, especially a child, with your open hand in order to punish them</DEF><THESEXA>Should a parent ever smack a child?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t agree with smacking.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In those days, children were spanked if they behaved badly.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to hit something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>hit</EXP><THESEXA>Jack hit the ball and it flew over the fence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>knock</EXP><DEF>to hit a door or window with your closed hand in order to attract the attention of the people inside</DEF><THESEXA>Someone was knocking on the door.</THESEXA><THESEXA> I knocked loudly but no one came.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>strike</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to hit a surface. <DEFBOLD>Strike</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>hit</DEFBOLD> and is mainly used in written English</DEF><THESEXA>The ball struck the side of the goal.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>whack</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>wæk</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to hit something very hard</DEF><THESEXA>Edmonds whacked the ball into the air.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bash</EXP><DEF>to hit something hard, especially in a way that causes damage</DEF><THESEXA>The police had to bash the door down to get in.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tap</EXP><DEF>to gently hit something with your fingers, often in order to attract someone’s attention</DEF><THESEXA>I tapped him on the shoulder.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I heard someone tapping on the window.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rap</EXP><DEF>to knock quickly or hit something several times</DEF><THESEXA>He rapped the table with his pen to bring the meeting to order.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Two police officers rapped on the door at 7 o'clock in the morning.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bang</EXP><DEF>to suddenly hit something hard, in a way that makes a loud noise</DEF><THESEXA>Her father banged his fist down on the table angrily.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The door suddenly banged shut.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pound</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to hit something many times with a lot of force</DEF><THESEXA>I could hear the sea pounding on the rocks.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She pounded on the door and shouted wildly.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hammer</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to hit something quickly many times making a loud continuous noise</DEF><THESEXA>The rain was hammering on the roof.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A crowd of people were outside hammering on the door angrily.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to hit something accidentally</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>hit</EXP><THESEXA>I’ve got a bad bruise where I hit my leg against the table.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The car hit a tree.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bump</EXP><DEF>to hit a part of your body against something, especially because you do not see or notice it</DEF><THESEXA>Careful you don’t bump your head – the ceiling’s very low.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bang/bash</EXP><DEF>to hit something hard, so that you hurt yourself or damage something</DEF><THESEXA>He banged into the car in front.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I bashed my knee climbing over a gate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She fell and bashed her chin on the ground.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stub</EXP><DEF>to hit your toe against something and hurt it</DEF><THESEXA>I stubbed my toe on the piano leg.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4cba"><Section><Exponent><EXP>hobby</EXP><DEF>an activity that you enjoy, and that you like to spend time doing when you are not working</DEF><THESEXA>Stamp collecting has been a hobby of mine since I was a child.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We had to write an essay about our hobbies.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>interest</EXP><DEF>something that you are interested in and like doing or talking about</DEF><THESEXA>Her interests were the same as most young girls – pop music, boys and clothes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pastime</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>something that you do because it is enjoyable or interesting</DEF><THESEXA>Gardening was her mother's favourite pastime.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In England talking about the weather is a <COLLOINEXA>national pastime</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>something that a lot of people in a country do</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>passion</EXP><DEF>something that you feel extremely interested in and care a lot about</DEF><THESEXA>Football was his passion.</THESEXA><THESEXA>For the French, food is a passion.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pursuit</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an activity that you spend time doing – used especially in the following phrases</DEF><THESEXA>The hills and lakes are used for a variety of <COLLOINEXA>outdoor pursuits</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her son wasn't really interested in <COLLOINEXA>academic pursuits</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Mountaineering, golfing, and fishing were among his <COLLOINEXA>leisure pursuits</COLLOINEXA></THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4ca1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>hold</EXP><DEF>to have something in your hand, hands, or arms</DEF><THESEXA>Maria came in holding a letter.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Can I hold the baby?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grip</EXP><DEF>to hold something very tightly and not let it go</DEF><THESEXA>He gripped her arm so she couldn’t walk or lose it</DEF><THESEXA>A businessman hurried past, clutching his briefcase.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The little girl clutched onto his hand.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>clasp</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to hold someone or something tightly, closing your fingers or arms around them</DEF><THESEXA>She was clasping a bunch of small summer flowers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He clasped her in his arms and kissed her.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get/take hold of sth</EXP><DEF>to take something in your hand or hands and hold it</DEF><THESEXA>I took hold of the handle and pulled as hard as I could.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Quickly – try and get hold of that frog!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grasp</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to take hold of something firmly, especially in a determined way</DEF><THESEXA>She grasped the lowest branch and pulled herself up into the tree.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grab</EXP><DEF>to take hold of something suddenly and often violently</DEF><THESEXA>He grabbed my bag and ran off with it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The other man <COLLOINEXA>grabbed hold of</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>suddenly took hold of </GLOSS> my arms and threatened me with a knife.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>seize</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>siːz</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to take hold of something suddenly and often violently</DEF><THESEXA>A police officer ran after him and seized the gun.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hang on (to sth)</EXP><DEF>to hold on to something or someone tightly to support yourself</DEF><THESEXA>He hung on to the rail at the back of the motorbike.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Hang on tight!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>keep hold of sth</EXP><DEF>to continue to hold something</DEF><THESEXA>Greg was struggling to keep hold of the dog.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She tried to take her hand away but he kept hold of it.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4c8b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>hole</EXP><DEF>an empty space in the surface of something, which sometimes goes all the way through it</DEF><THESEXA>A fox had dug a hole under our fence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Rain was coming in through a hole in the roof.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>space</EXP><DEF>an empty area between two things, into which you can put something</DEF><THESEXA>Are there any empty spaces on the bookshelf?</THESEXA><THESEXA>a parking space</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gap</EXP><DEF>an empty area between two things or two parts of something, especially one that should not be there</DEF><THESEXA>He has a gap between his two front teeth.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I squeezed through a gap in the hedge.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>opening</EXP><DEF>a hole that something can pass through or that you can see through, especially at the entrance of something</DEF><THESEXA>The train disappeared into the dark opening of the tunnel.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I looked through the narrow opening in the wall.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>leak</EXP><DEF>a small hole where something has been damaged or broken that lets liquid or gas flow in or out</DEF><THESEXA>a leak in the pipe</THESEXA><THESEXA>The plumber’s coming to repair the leak.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>puncture</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>a small hole in a tyre through which air escapes</DEF><THESEXA>My bike’s got a puncture.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>crack</EXP><DEF>a very narrow space between two things or two parts of something</DEF><THESEXA> The snake slid into a crack in the rock.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was peering through the crack in the curtains.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slot</EXP><DEF>a straight narrow hole that you put a particular type of object into</DEF><THESEXA>You have to put a coin in the slot before you dial the number.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A small disk fits into a slot in the camera.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>crater</EXP><DEF>a round hole in the ground made by an explosion or by a large object hitting it hard</DEF><THESEXA>a volcanic crater</THESEXA><THESEXA>The meteor left a crater over five miles wide.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the craters on the Moon</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to make a hole in something </SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>make a hole in sth</EXP><DEF> to cause a hole to appear in something</DEF><THESEXA>Make a hole in the bottom of the can using a hammer and nail.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pierce</EXP><DEF>to make a small hole in or through something, using a pointed object</DEF><THESEXA>The dog’s teeth had pierced her skin.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Shelley wanted to have her ears pierced<GLOSS>for earrings</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>prick</EXP><DEF>to make a very small hole in the surface of something, using a pointed object</DEF><THESEXA>Prick the potatoes before baking them.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My finger was bleeding where the needle had pricked it.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>punch</EXP><DEF>to make a hole through paper or flat material using a metal tool or other sharp object</DEF><THESEXA>I bought one of those things for punching holes in paper.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You have to get your ticket punched before you get on the <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4b62"><Section><Exponent><EXP>human/human being</EXP><DEF>a person</DEF><THESEXA>The monkeys show no fear of humans.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was thought that the disease could not be passed to human beings.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the human race</EXP><DEF>all the people in the world, considered as one group</DEF><THESEXA>War and aggression threaten the future of the whole human race.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>humanity</EXP><DEF>people in general – used especially when you are talking about people’s rights, or their suffering etc</DEF><THESEXA>The general was accused of committing crimes against humanity.</THESEXA><THESEXA>30% of humanity live in conditions of terrible poverty.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mankind/humankind</EXP><DEF>people in general – used especially when talking about their history and development. Some people prefer not to use <DEFBOLD>mankind</DEFBOLD> because it sounds like it does not include women</DEF><THESEXA>Travelling into space was a great advance for mankind.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The greatest threat to humankind is poverty.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>man</EXP><DEF>people in general – used especially when you are talking about people compared to nature. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4b11"><Section><Exponent><EXP>hurry</EXP><DEF>to go somewhere or do something more quickly than usual, for example because you are late or you must finish something soon</DEF><THESEXA>If you don’t hurry, you’ll miss the bus.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We have plenty of time. There’s no need to hurry.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rush</EXP><DEF>to go somewhere very quickly, or to do something too quickly and without thinking carefully enough</DEF><THESEXA>Everyone rushed out into the street to see what was happening.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Try to answer the questions calmly, without rushing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A police car rushed past.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dash</EXP><DEF>to go somewhere very quickly, especially because there is something important or urgent you must do</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve got to dash to the shops to get some more milk.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She had to dash off and get the kids from school.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in a hurry/in a rush</EXP><DEF>doing something quickly because you do not have much time, usually with the result that you make mistakes</DEF><THESEXA>She had left in a hurry, and forgotten her passport.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I had to choose a present for her in a rush.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get a move on/get moving</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to start to do something or go somewhere more quickly than before</DEF><THESEXA>Get a move on – it’s already 8 o'clock!</THESEXA><THESEXA>I think we’d better get moving, it’s only five minutes to boarding time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get cracking</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to start working quickly</DEF><THESEXA>It’s time you got cracking with your homework.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When Alfie arrives we’ll get cracking moving the furniture.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4a86"><Section><Exponent><EXP>ice</EXP><DEF>water that has frozen into a solid state</DEF><THESEXA>ice cubes in her Coke</THESEXA><THESEXA>the ice on the lake</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>frost</EXP><DEF>a thin coating of white powder-like ice that forms on the ground and plants, or the weather conditions in which this powder appears</DEF><THESEXA>There was frost on the ground.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Even in May we can sometimes get a late frost.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>black ice</EXP><DEF>an area of ice that is very difficult to see on a road</DEF><THESEXA>Driving conditions are dangerous, with black ice in many areas.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>icicle</EXP><DEF>a long thin pointed piece of ice that hangs from a roof or other surface</DEF><THESEXA>There were icicles hanging down from the side of the house.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hailstones</EXP><DEF>frozen balls of ice which fall like rain from the sky</DEF><THESEXA>Hailstones as big as marbles flattened the crops.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>glacier</EXP><DEF>a large mass of ice which moves slowly down a mountain valley</DEF><THESEXA>The high mountain glaciers of South America and Asia are melting at an alarming rate. </THESEXA><THESEXA>the Kangshung glacier</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>iceberg</EXP><DEF>a very large mass of ice floating in the sea, most of which is under the surface of the water</DEF><THESEXA>The ship sank after hitting an iceberg in the North Atlantic.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ice cap</EXP><DEF>an area of thick ice that permanently covers the North and South Poles</DEF><THESEXA>We all know that the polar ice caps are melting because of global warming.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-68b49d38.11be7425eae.-44be"><Section><Exponent><EXP>idea</EXP><DEF>something that you think of, especially something that you could do or suggest</DEF><THESEXA>I think that’s an excellent idea.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Let me know if you have any good ideas.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>thought</EXP><DEF>something that comes into your mind</DEF><THESEXA>The thought had entered my mind that he might be lying.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a worrying thought.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was lost in her thoughts.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>impression</EXP><DEF>the idea that you have in your mind about what someone or something is like</DEF><THESEXA>What was your impression of him?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>inspiration</EXP><DEF>a good and original idea, which makes you think of doing or creating something</DEF><THESEXA>Where did you get your inspiration from for the book?</THESEXA><THESEXA>He suddenly had a flash of inspiration.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The design for the house was entirely the inspiration of the architect.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>brainwave</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>brainstorm</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a sudden new and clever idea, especially one that solves a problem</DEF><THESEXA>I thought I’d have to sell the house, but then I had a brainwave.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>concept</EXP><DEF>an idea of how something is, or how something should be done</DEF><THESEXA>Concepts of beauty are different in different cultures.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the traditional concept of marriage</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>notion</EXP><DEF>an idea about life or society, especially one that is a little silly or old-fashioned</DEF><THESEXA> There is no evidence to support the notion that poverty is caused by laziness.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>
