future</DEF><THESEXA>Anna visualized meeting Greg again at the airport.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The finished house may be hard to visualize.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>picture</EXP><DEF>to form a clear picture of something or someone in your mind</DEF><THESEXA>I can still picture my father, even though he died a long time ago.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The town was just how she had pictured it from his description.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>envisage</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ɪnˈvɪzɪdʒ</PRON></PronCodes><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>envision</ORTHVAR></Variant><DEF>to imagine something as possible or likely to happen in the future</DEF><THESEXA>How do you envisage your career developing over the next ten years?</THESEXA><THESEXA>They had envisioned the creation of a single armed force, small but efficient.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conceive of sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to imagine a situation, especially one that is difficult to imagine</DEF><THESEXA>For many people, music is so important that they cannot conceive of life without it.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fantasize</EXP><DEF>to imagine something exciting that you would like to happen, but that is very unlikely to happen</DEF><THESEXA>I used to fantasize about becoming a film star.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>daydream</EXP><DEF>to imagine pleasant things, so that you forget where you are and what you should be doing</DEF><THESEXA>Mark began to daydream, and didn’t even hear the teacher’s question.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hallucinate</EXP><DEF>to imagine that you are seeing things that are not really there, especially because you are ill or have taken drugs</DEF><THESEXA>The drug can cause some people to hallucinate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When I saw the walls moving, I thought I must be hallucinating.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-49af"><Section><Exponent><EXP>immediately</EXP><DEF>very soon after something else happens, and without any delay</DEF><THESEXA>Sam immediately offered to help.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>at once</EXP><DEF>immediately – used especially for emphasis</DEF><THESEXA>Remove the pie from the oven and serve at once.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He came home at once when he heard his wife was ill.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>right away</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>straightaway</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>immediately, especially because something is urgent. <DEFBOLD>Right away</DEFBOLD> and <DEFBOLD>straightaway</DEFBOLD> are less formal than <DEFBOLD>immediately</DEFBOLD>, and are very common in everyday spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>If this happens, let us know right away.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I apologized straightaway.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>instantly</EXP><DEF>if something happens instantly, it happens immediately after something else, with almost no time between</DEF><THESEXA>The boy was killed instantly.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The message is sent instantly from your computer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>right now/this minute</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if someone orders you to do something right now, they want you to do it immediately, especially because they are annoyed with you</DEF><THESEXA>The head teacher wants to see you right now.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>without delay</EXP><DEF>immediately, because it is important that you do something as soon as possible</DEF><THESEXA>If you lose your passport, you should contact the embassy without delay.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-498c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>importance</EXP><DEF>the quality of being important</DEF><THESEXA>the importance of cleanliness in preventing infections</THESEXA><THESEXA>He may have been an evil tyrant, but you cannot deny his importance in world history.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>significance</EXP><DEF>the importance of an event, action etc, especially because of the effects or influence it will have in the future</DEF><THESEXA>The significance of the discovery was not understood until years later.</THESEXA><THESEXA>9/11 was an event of global significance.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>value</EXP><DEF>the importance and usefulness or something</DEF><THESEXA>The athletes talked to the students about the value of a college education.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Such methods are of little value.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>prominence</EXP><DEF>the fact of being important and well-known</DEF><THESEXA>He first <COLLOINEXA>came to prominence</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>became well-known </GLOSS>in the 1990s.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4979"><Section><Exponent><EXP>important</EXP><DEF>having a big effect on people’s lives or on events in the future</DEF><THESEXA>an important decision</THESEXA><THESEXA>Good qualifications are increasingly important.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>big</EXP><DEF>important or serious</DEF><THESEXA>It’s a big decision.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a big problem</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>significant</EXP><DEF>important enough to be noticeable or have a big effect</DEF><THESEXA>There is no significant difference between the performance of male and female students.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The Internet has brought about significant changes in people’s lives. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>noteworthy</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>important or interesting enough to deserve your attention</DEF><THESEXA>The castle is the island’s most noteworthy feature.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The jury’s verdict was noteworthy for a number of reasons.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>very important</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>essential</EXP><DEF>very important, especially for the success, health, or safety of someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>It is essential to speak the local language.</THESEXA><THESEXA>essential supplies of food and clothing</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>vital/crucial</EXP><DEF>extremely important, because without it there could be serious problems</DEF><THESEXA>His evidence was vital to the case.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The US plays a crucial role in the region.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The media are a vital part of the political system.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>key</EXP><DEF>extremely important and having a big effect</DEF><THESEXA>Wheeler had a key role in the development of the atom bomb.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Timing is key.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>of great/considerable importance</EXP><DEF>very important</DEF><THESEXA>In the construction industry, health and safety are of considerable importance.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>momentous</EXP><DEF>very important because it has a very great effect on the future</DEF><THESEXA>Momentous events were taking place in Russia.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a momentous decision</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>most important</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>main/chief/principal</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>most important</DEF><THESEXA>What was your main aim?</THESEXA><THESEXA> the principal reason for their decision</THESEXA><THESEXA>the chief cause of the recession</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>major</EXP><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>one of the most important or serious things</DEF><THESEXA>Smoking is a major cause of heart disease.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Street crime is a major problem.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>central/primary</EXP><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>most important – used especially when talking about the main thing that people are discussing, worried about, or trying to do</DEF><THESEXA>Education will be the central issue in the election.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Our primary concern is passenger safety.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>paramount</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈpærəmaʊnt</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>more important than anything else, so that you must consider it when deciding what to do</DEF><THESEXA>Airport security is of paramount importance.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The needs of the students are paramount.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4952"><Section><Exponent><EXP>impossible</EXP><DEF>something that is impossible cannot happen or be done</DEF><THESEXA>It’s impossible to get a taxi around here.</THESEXA><THESEXA>At times, finding a job felt like an impossible task.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>impractical</EXP><DEF>something that is impractical is too expensive, takes too much time, is too difficult etc and therefore it is not sensible to try to do it</DEF><THESEXA>It’s a nice idea, but it’s totally impractical.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We cannot all just stop using our cars - that would be completely impractical.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an impractical suggestion</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>out of the question</EXP><DEF>used when saying that something is completely impossible, especially because it is not allowed</DEF><THESEXA>It’s out of the question for you to go alone.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>there’s no way </EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when saying that you think something is completely impossible</DEF><THESEXA>There’s no way we can get to the airport in less than an hour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>inconceivable</EXP><DEF>impossible to imagine or believe</DEF><THESEXA>It seemed inconceivable that there could be an end to the fighting in Northern Ireland.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unthinkable</EXP><DEF>impossible to imagine or believe – used especially about something that seems very shocking to people</DEF><THESEXA>In those days it was unthinkable for a lady to work outside the home.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unattainable</EXP><DEF>impossible to achieve</DEF><THESEXA>an unattainable goal</THESEXA><THESEXA>Victory seemed unattainable.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>it can’t be done</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when saying very definitely that something is impossible</DEF><THESEXA>I’m afraid it can’t be done.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4918"><Section><Exponent><EXP>impressive</EXP><DEF>used about something that people admire because it is very good, large, important etc</DEF><THESEXA>The cathedral is very impressive.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The drug has shown some impressive results.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dazzling</EXP><DEF>extremely impressive – used especially when someone does something very well or looks very beautiful</DEF><THESEXA>a dazzling performance</THESEXA><THESEXA>a dazzling smile</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>breathtaking</EXP><DEF>extremely impressive – used especially about beautiful views, or the fast speed of something</DEF><THESEXA>The views of the Rocky Mountains are simply breathtaking.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The economy is growing at a breathtaking pace.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spectacular</EXP><DEF>very impressive and exciting to look at or watch</DEF><THESEXA>a spectacular fireworks display</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>awe-inspiring</EXP><DEF>so impressive that you feel great respect and admiration, or you feel rather frightened</DEF><THESEXA>Michelangelo’s masterpiece is awe-inspiring.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The volcano was an awe-inspiring sight.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>majestic</EXP><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>very impressive because of being big and beautiful</DEF><THESEXA>the majestic mountain scenery</THESEXA><THESEXA>a majestic animal</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>imposing</EXP><GRAM>usually before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used about buildings and people that are large and impressive</DEF><THESEXA>the imposing entrance hall </THESEXA><THESEXA>The bishop was an imposing figure.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-48dc"><Section><SECHEADING>to increase</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>increase</EXP><DEF>to become larger in number, amount, or degree</DEF><THESEXA>Sales increased by 25%.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The level of violence has increased.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go up</EXP><DEF>to increase. <DEFBOLD>Go up</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>increase</DEFBOLD>, and is the usual verb to use in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>The price of coffee has gone up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rise</EXP><DEF>to increase. <DEFBOLD>Rise</DEFBOLD> sounds a little formal and is often used when talking about the level of something increasing</DEF><THESEXA>The demand for oil has been rising steadily.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Living standards have risen dramatically.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grow</EXP><DEF>to increase, especially gradually over a period of time – used about numbers or amounts</DEF><THESEXA>Since 1990, US imports of foreign goods have grown at a rate of 7.7% per year.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The number of people working from home has grown substantially.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>escalate</EXP><DEF>to increase to a high level – used about things that you do not want to increase such as costs, crimes, or violence</DEF><THESEXA>Fuel prices are escalating.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The fighting has escalated.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>double/triple</EXP><DEF> to become twice as much or three times as much</DEF><THESEXA>Since 1950, the number of people dying from cancer has almost doubled.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company’s profits tripled last quarter.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>expand</EXP><DEF>to become larger in size, or to include a wider range of activities</DEF><THESEXA>The business has expanded at a rate of 15% per year.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We are hoping to expand into mobile phone services.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>soar</EXP><DEF>to increase and reach a very high level – used about numbers and amounts, or about feelings</DEF><THESEXA>The temperature soared to 36.6 degrees Centigrade.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His confidence soared.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The singer’s popularity has soared.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shoot up</EXP><DEF> to increase very quickly and suddenly – used about prices, numbers, or temperatures</DEF><THESEXA>Share prices shot up 30% over the last week.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to make something increase</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>increase</EXP><DEF>to make something larger in number, amount, or degree</DEF><THESEXA>Being overweight increases the risk of having a heart attack.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We need to increase the number of police officers on the streets.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put sth up</EXP><DEF>to increase prices, taxes etc. <DEFBOLD>Put sth up</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>increase</DEFBOLD>, and is the usual verb to use in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>The landlord has put the rent up again.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They’re always putting up gas prices.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>raise</EXP><DEF>to increase something such as prices or taxes, or levels or standards</DEF><THESEXA>The bank has raised interest rates for the third time this year.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The school aims to raise students’ levels of achievement.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>double/triple</EXP><DEF>to increase the amount of something so that it is twice or three times as large</DEF><THESEXA>The airline plans to double the number of passengers it carries by 2015.</THESEXA><THESEXA>High blood pressure triples the risk of strokes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>boost</EXP><DEF>to increase sales, profits, production etc, especially when they have been lower than you want them to be</DEF><THESEXA>Growing affluence has boosted sales.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Oil exports boosted the economy.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>expand</EXP><DEF>to increase something so that it contains a wider range of things, or to increase the size of a business</DEF><THESEXA>The company plans to expand its retail operations.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Supermarkets have expanded their ranges to include non-food items.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>extend</EXP><DEF>to increase something such as your power or influence, or the number of things you are involved in</DEF><THESEXA>We are hoping to extend the range of services that we offer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company plans to extend its dominance of the world car market.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>step up sth</EXP><DEF>to increase your efforts or activities, especially to change a situation</DEF><THESEXA>Security has been stepped up following the bombing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Local people have stepped up their campaign to prevent a prison being built in their neighbourhood.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>heighten</EXP><DEF>to increase a feeling or effect</DEF><THESEXA>The attack has heightened concerns about racism in schools.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Hunger can heighten the effect that alcohol has on you.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>maximize</EXP><DEF>to increase something as much as possible</DEF><THESEXA>Businesses try to maximize efficiency and cut costs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>To maximize the chances of a successful pregnancy, you should make sure that you eat well.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-48c5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>increase</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>an occasion when the amount or number of something becomes contexts, or when talking about an increase in someone’s weight</DEF><THESEXA>sales gains</THESEXA><THESEXA>The December job figures show a <COLLOINEXA>net gain</COLLOINEXA> of 81,000 hikes</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>a very big increase</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>explosion</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a sudden very large increase in the amount or caused an explosion of interest in Renaissance Italy.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>boom</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>singular</GRAM><DEF>a sudden large increase in trade, profits or sales, with the <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4885"><Section><Exponent><EXP>injury</EXP><DEF>damage to part of your body caused by an accident or an attack</DEF><THESEXA>The passengers were taken to hospital with minor injuries.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wound</EXP><DEF>an injury, especially a deep cut in your skin made by a knife, bullet, or bomb</DEF><THESEXA>He died of a gunshot wound to the head.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cut</EXP><DEF>a small injury made when a sharp object cuts your skin</DEF><THESEXA>Blood was running from a cut on his chin.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bruise</EXP><DEF>a dark mark on your skin that you get when you fall or get hit</DEF><THESEXA>Jack often comes home from playing rugby covered in bruises.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>graze/scrape</EXP><DEF>a small injury that marks your skin or breaks the surface slightly</DEF><THESEXA>She fell off her bike and got a few grazes on her legs and knees.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gash</EXP><DEF>a long deep cut</DEF><THESEXA>He had a deep gash across his forehead.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bump</EXP><DEF>an area of skin that is swollen because you have hit it against something</DEF><THESEXA>How did you get that bump on your head?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sprain</EXP><DEF>an injury to your <NonDV><REFHWD>ankle</REFHWD></NonDV>, <NonDV><REFHWD>wrist</REFHWD></NonDV>, knee etc, caused by suddenly twisting it</DEF><THESEXA>It’s a slight sprain – you should rest your ankle for a week.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>strain</EXP><DEF>an injury to a muscle caused by stretching it or using it too much</DEF><THESEXA>a muscle strain in his neck</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fracture</EXP><DEF>a crack or broken part in a bone</DEF><THESEXA>a hip fracture</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4869"><Section><Exponent><EXP>insect</EXP><DEF>a small creature such as a fly or ant, that has six legs, and sometimes wings</DEF><THESEXA>Head lice are tiny insects that live in the hair.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bug</EXP><DEF>a small insect</DEF><THESEXA>What are those little bugs in my roses?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>creepy-crawly</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an insect, especially one that you are frightened of</DEF><THESEXA>The mattress was covered in creepy-crawlies.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>aphid</EXP><REGISTERLAB>technical</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a small insect that lives on the juice of plants and destroys them</DEF><THESEXA>The leaves have been attacked by aphids.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4858"><Section><Exponent><EXP>insist</EXP><DEF>to say firmly that someone should do something or that something should happen</DEF><THESEXA>She insisted that it was her turn to drive.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>demand</EXP><DEF>to say very strongly and sometimes angrily that you want something or that something must happen</DEF><THESEXA>I wrote a letter to the company, demanding an apology and a refund.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The guards demanded to see her ID.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>require</EXP><GRAM>usually passive</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if you are required to do something, a rule or law says that you must do it</DEF><THESEXA>The successful applicant will be required to sign a two-year contract.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be adamant</EXP><DEF>to say very firmly that something must happen or is right, and refuse to change your mind when other people try to persuade you</DEF><THESEXA>The actress has always been adamant about keeping her private life private.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>won’t take no for an answer</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to insist that someone must do what you say or ask</DEF><THESEXA>You’re coming home with me – I won’t take no for an answer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put your foot down</EXP><DEF>to say very firmly that someone must not do something</DEF><THESEXA>Ed was talking about dropping out of school, but Mom and Dad put their foot down.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-482d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>install</EXP><DEF>to put a piece of equipment somewhere and connect it so that it is ready to be used</DEF><THESEXA>The company is installing a new computer system.</THESEXA><THESEXA>How much does it cost to install central heating?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put in</EXP><DEF> to install something. <DEFBOLD>Put in </DEFBOLD> is more common in everyday English than <DEFBOLD>install</DEFBOLD> and is used especially about things that are not very complicated to install</DEF><THESEXA>The workmen are coming to put the new windows in today.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They removed the bath and put in a shower instead.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fit</EXP><DEF>to put a new part or piece of equipment into or onto something</DEF><THESEXA>I had to fit new locks after the burglary.</THESEXA><THESEXA>All vehicles must have seat belts fitted.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lay</EXP><DEF>to put cables or a <NonDV><REFHWD>carpet</REFHWD></NonDV> in the correct place on the ground</DEF><THESEXA>Work on laying the telephone cables has not yet begun.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Two workmen were laying carpet tiles in the kitchen.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4817"><Section><Exponent><EXP>instead</EXP><DEF>used when saying that a particular thing is used or happens and not another thing, or that someone does something and not another person</DEF><THESEXA>There were no single rooms, so I booked a double instead.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Chris couldn't go to the meeting, so I said that I would go instead.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Can I have soup <COLLOINEXA>instead of </COLLOINEXA>salad?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I really wished it had been me <COLLOINEXA>instead of</COLLOINEXA> him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rather than</EXP><DEF>instead of something or someone – often used when someone does something instead of doing another thing, because it seems better or more suitable</DEF><THESEXA>Rather than driving around all day looking for somewhere to park, why don’t you take a bus into town?</THESEXA><THESEXA>A lot of people are choosing to rent rather than buy their own homes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’d say it was warm rather than hot. </THESEXA><THESEXA>It might be better if you asked him rather than me.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in sb/sth’s place</EXP><DEF>having the position or role that something or someone else once had</DEF><THESEXA>The factory was demolished and flats were built in its place.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If I refused to go, they would send someone else in my place.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>on behalf of sb/on sb’s behalf</EXP><DEF>instead of someone or as their representative</DEF><THESEXA>She asked the doctor to speak to her parents on her behalf.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He collected the award on behalf of his sister.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in favour of</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>in favor of</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>used when something is not chosen or used, because something else seems much better</DEF><THESEXA>The original plan was abandoned in favour of a new proposal.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was rejected for the job in favor of a more experienced teacher.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in preference to</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when something is chosen or used because it seems much better than something else</DEF><THESEXA>What makes customers choose one brand in preference to another?</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-47f0"><HEADING>Sense 1</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>intelligent</EXP><DEF>having a high level of mental ability, and good at thinking clearly and understanding ideas</DEF><THESEXA>The top universities aim to select the most intelligent students.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>clever</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>smart</LEXVAR></Variant><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><DEF>intelligent, so that you can think and learn quickly and find ways to solve problems</DEF><THESEXA>That was very clever of you. How did you do that?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I wasn’t smart enough to be a lawyer. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bright</EXP><DEF>intelligent – used especially about children and young people</DEF><THESEXA>He’s a very bright kid.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the brightest student in the class</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>brilliant</EXP><DEF>extremely intelligent and good at the work you do</DEF><THESEXA>a brilliant scientist</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gifted</EXP><DEF>a gifted child is much more intelligent than most other children</DEF><THESEXA>a special school for gifted children</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wise</EXP><DEF>able to make good decisions and give sensible advice, especially because you have a lot of experience</DEF><THESEXA>a wise old man</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cunning/crafty</EXP><DEF>good at using your intelligence to get what you want, often by making secret plans or tricking people</DEF><THESEXA>She was cunning enough to keep this latest piece of information secret.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s a crafty old devil!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>brainy</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>intelligent and good at studying</DEF><THESEXA>My sister is the brainy one in our family.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-47d5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>intend to do sth</EXP><DEF>to have decided that you want to do something at some time in the future</DEF><THESEXA>He intends to appeal against the decision.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be going to do sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to intend to do something – used when you have made definite arrangements to do it</DEF><THESEXA>We’re going to have a meeting about it next week.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m going to start karate lessons.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mean to do sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to intend to do something – used especially when you forget to do something or when something does not happen in the way you intended</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve been meaning to call you for ages.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sorry, I didn’t mean to scare you.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>plan to do sth</EXP><DEF>to intend to do something – used especially when you have thought carefully about how and when you will do it</DEF><THESEXA>The airline plans to start flights to Thailand in July.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Jane and Rob are planning to get married next year.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>set out to do sth</EXP><DEF>to intend to do something – used when someone is very determined and knows clearly what they want to do</DEF><THESEXA>He set out to make a movie that would challenge people’s prejudices.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>aim to do sth</EXP><DEF>to intend to do something – used when saying what someone hopes to achieve</DEF><THESEXA>We aim to finish the work by next week.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The film aims to explain global warming and what people can do about it.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>propose to do sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to intend to do something – used when saying what someone suggests doing</DEF><THESEXA>How do you propose to deal with the situation? </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have sb/sth in mind</EXP><DEF>to imagine that something is the kind of thing that you want to happen, or that someone is the person you want to choose</DEF><THESEXA>‘How about going out for a pizza?’ ‘That wasn’t exactly what I had in mind.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>Who do you have in mind?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have no intention of doing sth</EXP><DEF>to have decided that you will definitely not do something</DEF><THESEXA>Tom has no intention of retiring just yet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The government has no intention of lifting the ban.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-47c7"><Section><Exponent><EXP>interested</EXP><GRAM>not usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>giving your attention to something because you want to know more about it or you enjoy it</DEF><THESEXA>Recently I’ve got very interested in photography.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP> fascinated</EXP><GRAM>not usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>very interested by something you see, read, or hear about</DEF><THESEXA>She watched, fascinated, as the bird came closer until she could almost touch it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The more I read about the place, the more fascinated I became.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>curious</EXP><DEF>wanting to find out more information about something</DEF><THESEXA>Small children are naturally curious.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was curious to find out the reasons for his sudden departure.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>intrigued</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>interested in something because it seems strange or mysterious</DEF><THESEXA>He was intrigued by her story.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be into sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when talking about the kind of things you are interested in and enjoy doing</DEF><THESEXA>Are you into classical music?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I got into <GLOSS>became interested in</GLOSS> yoga when I was at college.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>so interested that you give something all your attention</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>absorbed/engrossed in sth</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>very interested in something, so that you give it all your attention and do not notice anything else</DEF><THESEXA>Jane was lying on the sofa engrossed in a novel.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was so absorbed in her own thoughts that she didn’t hear me call.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gripped/riveted</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>very interested, especially in a story you are reading, a film you are watching etc, so that you are eager to find out what happens next</DEF><THESEXA>It was a brilliant book and I was gripped from beginning to end.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was so riveted by the film that I forgot the time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>enthralled</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>very interested and enjoying something very much, so that you want to see or hear more</DEF><THESEXA>From the opening line of the play, the audience was completely enthralled.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spellbound</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>extremely interested in something very strange or wonderful, so that you are unable to move or think of anything else</DEF><THESEXA>He could <COLLOINEXA>hold</COLLOINEXA> audiences <COLLOINEXA>spellbound</COLLOINEXA> with the power of his voice.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They all listened, spellbound.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rapt</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>showing by your expression that you are very interested in something and are giving all your attention to it</DEF><THESEXA>He spoke before a rapt audience.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The congregation listened with rapt attention. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be all ears</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be extremely interested in what someone is telling you</DEF><THESEXA>Tell me exactly what happened. I’m all ears.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>not interested</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>not interested</EXP><DEF>not wanting to know about something or give it your attention</DEF><THESEXA>I just wasn’t at all interested in science at school.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>uninterested</EXP><DEF>not interested in wanting to know about something, especially something you are told</DEF><THESEXA>When I tried to tell her about my holiday, she seemed completely uninterested.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>apathetic</EXP><DEF>not interested in a particular problem or situation, so that you are not willing to make an effort to change and improve things</DEF><THESEXA>Voters have become increasingly apathetic.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>indifferent</EXP><DEF>having no interest in someone or something, and often not caring about them</DEF><THESEXA>Sometimes the government seems indifferent to the problems of the poor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sb couldn’t care less</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when saying that someone does not care at all about something and is not interested in it</DEF><THESEXA>I couldn’t care less what other people think.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-47b4"><Section><Exponent><EXP>interesting</EXP><DEF>keeping your attention and making you want to know more</DEF><THESEXA>There’s an cell.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>absorbing/engrossing</EXP><DEF>interesting in a way that keeps your attention completely or for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>Growing your own vegetables can be an <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-479f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>interfere</EXP><DEF>to try to get involved in a situation where you are not wanted or needed</DEF><THESEXA>She tried not to interfere in her children’s lives.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s not your problem, so don’t interfere.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>meddle</EXP><DEF>to interfere in someone else’s affairs in a way that is annoying for them. <DEFBOLD>Meddle</DEFBOLD> is more informal than <DEFBOLD>interfere</DEFBOLD>, and has more of a feeling of being annoyed</DEF><THESEXA> I did not want my parents meddling in my private affairs. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He warned diplomats against meddling in Indonesia’s affairs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>intrude</EXP><DEF>to interfere by being somewhere where you are not wanted, or getting involved in a situation that is private – used especially when saying that you want to avoid doing this</DEF><THESEXA>Sorry, I didn’t mean to intrude while you were on the phone.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When someone dies, people are often worried about intruding.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>butt in</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to interfere by trying to become involved in a private situation or conversation that does not concern you</DEF><THESEXA>Stop butting in, will you!</THESEXA><THESEXA>I didn’t want to give them any advice in case they thought I was butting in.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pry</EXP><DEF>to try to find out what someone else is doing in their private life, by asking questions or secretly checking what they are doing, in a way that seems annoying or rude</DEF><THESEXA>Journalists like to pry into the lives of the rich and famous.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I didn’t mean to pry – I just wanted to know if I could help.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>poke/stick your nose into sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to ask questions about someone else’s private life or give them advice they do not want, in a way that annoys them</DEF><THESEXA>She’s one of those people who’s always poking her nose into other people’s business.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-477b"><Section><SECHEADING>things you do when using the Internet</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>surf the net</EXP><POS>v</POS><DEF>to look quickly through information on the Internet, looking for anything that interests you</DEF><THESEXA>He spends his evenings surfing the net.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>search</EXP><DEF>to look for a specific piece of information on the Internet, for example by entering text into a box</DEF><THESEXA>Try searching for his name on Google.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shop/book online</EXP><DEF>to buy things or reserve tickets using the Internet</DEF><THESEXA>More and more people are preferring to shop online.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s easier to book online.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>visit a website</EXP><DEF>to look at a particular Internet site</DEF><THESEXA>You can check to see how many people visit your website.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>download</EXP><DEF>to get files, programs, music, films etc from the Internet and put them on your own computer</DEF><THESEXA>I downloaded the software for free from the Internet.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>chat</EXP><DEF>to communicate with several people in a <NonDV><REFHWD>chat room</REFHWD></NonDV> on the Internet</DEF><THESEXA>Using the Internet, you can chat to people from around the world.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4759"><Section><SECHEADING>when someone is speakng</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>interrupt</EXP><GRAM>I,T</GRAM><DEF>to stop someone from continuing speaking</DEF><THESEXA>She interrupted him to ask exactly how he had broken his ankle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Oliver began his story but was soon interrupted by the arrival of Mr Gosling.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I wish you wouldn’t interrupt all the time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>butt in</EXP><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>to rudely start speaking when someone is already speaking</DEF><THESEXA>Will you please stop butting in!</THESEXA><THESEXA>Steve kept butting in with silly comments.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cut sb off/cut sb short</EXP><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>to prevent someone from finishing what they are saying</DEF><THESEXA>He slammed down the phone and cut her off in mid-sentence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Bob began to speak but Donna cut him short.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>heckle</EXP><GRAM>I,T</GRAM><DEF>to deliberately interrupt a speaker or performer by shouting, especially to show that you do not agree with what they are saying</DEF><THESEXA>Comedians are used to dealing with members of the audience who heckle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The speaker was heckled by a group of protesters.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>chip in</EXP><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>to interrupt a conversation or discussion by adding comments, especially helpful or useful ones</DEF><THESEXA>Feel free to chip in if you have any comments to make. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>when someone is doing something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>interrupt</EXP><GRAM>I,T</GRAM><DEF>to stop someone from continuing to do something</DEF><THESEXA>Miss Danziger apologised for interrupting their enjoyment of their meal.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My thoughts were interrupted by the sound of the doorbell. </THESEXA><THESEXA> His interview was interrupted by a call from the president.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>disturb</EXP><DEF>to interrupt someone when they are trying to work, sleep etc</DEF><THESEXA>The sign on the door said ‘Do not disturb. Meeting in progress’.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I hope I’m not disturbing you. Do you want me to come back later? </THESEXA><THESEXA>Her sleep was disturbed by a violent hammering on the door. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bother</EXP><DEF>to interrupt someone, especially by asking questions when they are trying to do something else</DEF><THESEXA>‘I’m very sorry to bother you,’ Jackson said smoothly, ‘but I’d like a few minutes of your time.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>You mustn’t bother him - he’s working on his essay.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4740"><Section><Exponent><EXP>interview</EXP><DEF>a meeting in which someone is asked questions, to find out if they are suitable for a job, or to help the police find out about a crime. Also used about someone being asked questions on TV, in a newspaper, in a magazine etc</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve got another job interview tomorrow. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Since the police interview, she had changed her statement.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an interview with Keith Richards</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>interrogation</EXP><DEF>an occasion when someone is asked a lot of questions for a long time in order to get information, sometimes using threats, usually by the police or the army</DEF><THESEXA>He claims he was tortured during his interrogation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police interrogation methods have been questioned.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cross-examination</EXP><DEF>an occasion when someone is asked questions about what they have just said, in order to see if they are telling the truth, especially in a court of law</DEF><THESEXA>Under cross-examination, the only witness said she could not be sure about what she saw.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>consultation</EXP><DEF>a meeting with a doctor or an expert to discuss treatment or to get advice</DEF><THESEXA>The therapist charges $100 for a half hour consultation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Would you like to come back for another consultation?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>audience</EXP><DEF>a formal meeting with a very important person</DEF><THESEXA>He was granted an audience with the Pope.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-472a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>introduction</EXP><DEF>a written or spoken statement at the beginning of a book, speech, or meeting, giving a general idea of what it is about</DEF><THESEXA>After a brief introduction by the chairman, the meeting began.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The author outlines his methods of research in the introduction.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>preface</EXP><DEF>a short piece of writing at the beginning of a book that says what the book is about or the reason for writing it</DEF><THESEXA> In the preface, he explains his motives for returning to the subject of Middle Eastern politics. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>foreword</EXP><DEF>a short introduction to a book or report, usually written by someone who is not the author</DEF><THESEXA>Greene wrote the foreword to Suzmann’s book.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>prologue</EXP><DEF>an introduction to a piece of writing, especially a play or a long poem</DEF><THESEXA>the prologue to Shakespeare’s ‘Henry V’</THESEXA><THESEXA>The poem begins with a brief prologue.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>preamble</EXP><DEF>a statement at the beginning of something, especially an official document, which explains what it is about</DEF><THESEXA>the Preamble to the US Constitution</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4715"><Section><Exponent><EXP>invent</EXP><DEF>to think of an idea for a new product, machine etc, and design it or make it</DEF><THESEXA>The telephone was invented by Alexander Graham Bell.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Who invented the Internet?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>create</EXP><DEF>to make or design something new of sth/think up sth</EXP><DEF>to produce a new idea, plan, method, excuse etc by thinking</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve thought of an idea.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They’re always trying to think up new ways to improve <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-46e3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>investigate</EXP><DEF>to try to find out the truth about something such as a crime, accident, or <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-46c1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>investigation</EXP><DEF>a process in which the police or another organization try to find out the truth <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-45da"><Section><Exponent><EXP>jump</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I,T</GRAM><DEF>to push yourself up into the air, over something etc, using your legs</DEF><THESEXA>The cat jumped up onto the table.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He jumped over the stream.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His horse jumped the fence successfully.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>skip</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>to move forwards with little jumps between your steps, especially because you are feeling happy</DEF><THESEXA>The little girl was skipping down the street.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hop</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>to jump or move around on one leg</DEF><THESEXA>He was hopping around because he’d injured his foot.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>leap</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I,T</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to suddenly jump up high or a long way</DEF><THESEXA>The deer leapt over the fence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Tina leapt onto the boat as it was moving away.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Fish were leaping out of the water.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bounce</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>to jump up and down several times, especially on something that has springs in it</DEF><THESEXA>Children love bouncing on beds.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dive</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>to jump into water with your head and arms first</DEF><THESEXA>Zoë dived into the swimming pool.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>vault</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>vɔːlt </PRON><AMEVARPRON> vɒːlt</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I,T</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to jump over something in one movement, using your hands or a pole to help you</DEF><THESEXA>He vaulted the ticket barrier and ran for the exit.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Ben tried to vault over the bar.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-45c8"><HEADING>Sense 5</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>keep</EXP><DEF>to leave something in one particular place so that you can find it easily</DEF><THESEXA>Where do you keep the scissors?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The keys are kept in my office.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>store</EXP><DEF>to put things away and keep them until you need them</DEF><THESEXA>Villagers have begun storing wood for the winter.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>save</EXP><DEF>to keep something so that you can use or enjoy it in the future</DEF><THESEXA>He had been saving the bottle of champagne for a special occasion.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We can save the rest of the pie for later.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>file</EXP><DEF>to store papers or information in a particular order or a particular place</DEF><THESEXA>All the contracts are filed alphabetically.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>collect</EXP><DEF>to get and keep objects of the same type because you think they are attractive or interesting</DEF><THESEXA>Kate collects old postcards.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hold</EXP><DEF>to keep something to be used when it is needed, especially something that many different people may need to use</DEF><THESEXA>Medical records are now usually held on computers.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reserve</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to keep part of something for use at a later time during a process such as cooking</DEF><THESEXA>Reserve some of the chocolate so that you can use it for decorating the cake.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hoard</EXP><DEF>to keep large amounts of food, money etc because you think you may not be able to get them in the future – used when you do not approve of people doing this because it is not necessary or not fair to other people</DEF><THESEXA>People have been hoarding food and fuel in case there is another attack.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Rationing of basic food products was introduced to prevent hoarding.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-45b2"><Section><Exponent><EXP>kill</EXP><DEF>to make someone die</DEF><THESEXA>The driver and his passenger were killed in the informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to kill someone</DEF><THESEXA>He was so irritating I felt like bumping him off myself.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>do away with sb</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to kill someone</DEF><THESEXA>The settlers in Jamestown had been done away with, but no one knew how.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to kill yourself</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>commit suicide</EXP><DEF>to kill yourself</DEF><THESEXA>He committed suicide after the death of his </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>exterminate</EXP><DEF>to kill large numbers of a particular group, so that they no longer exist</DEF><THESEXA>Hitler’s goal was to exterminate the <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-459e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>kind</EXP><DEF>someone who is kind tries to help people and make them happy or comfortable, and shows that they care about them</DEF><THESEXA>They were very kind to us and let us stay in their house as long as we liked.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a kind old lady</THESEXA><THESEXA>a kind thing to say</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nice</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>friendly and kind. <DEFBOLD>Nice</DEFBOLD> is very common in everyday spoken English and is often used instead of <DEFBOLD>kind</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Everyone has been so nice to me.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s nice of you to invite me here.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He seems such a nice man.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>generous</EXP><DEF>kind because you give people money, presents etc</DEF><THESEXA>‘I’ll pay for the meal.’ ‘That’s very generous of you.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>a guests.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>thoughtful</EXP><DEF>thinking of things you can do to make other people happy or feel good – used especially when someone does something such as giving office.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>caring</EXP><DEF>kind and wanting to help and look after people</DEF><THESEXA>She’s lucky to have such a loving and caring husband.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The British are well-known for their caring attitude toward animals.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sympathetic</EXP><DEF>saying kind things to someone who has problems and behaving in a way that smile.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>good</EXP><DEF>kind and showing that you want to help – used especially in the following phrases</DEF><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>It was good of you to</COLLOINEXA> come and see me. </THESEXA><THESEXA>She’s always been very <COLLOINEXA>good to</COLLOINEXA> us.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sweet</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>very kind – used especially when you like someone very much, or you are very pleased because of something they have done</DEF><THESEXA>I was given the flowers by a sweet little old lady who lived next door. </THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s sweet of you to ask.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a sweet thing to say</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>kind-hearted/warm-hearted</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>having a kind and friendly character, which makes other people like you</DEF><THESEXA>He was a wonderful father, kind-hearted and always laughing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The town is full of warm-hearted, helpful people. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>benevolent</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>kind and wanting to help people – often used about someone who is important or who people respect</DEF><THESEXA>a benevolent ruler</THESEXA><THESEXA>They believe in the existence of a benevolent God who will save mankind. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He listened politely, like some benevolent uncle. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4586"><Section><Exponent><EXP>king</EXP><DEF>the male ruler of a country, who comes from a royal family</DEF><THESEXA>George III was the <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4573"><Section><Exponent><EXP>knowledge</EXP><DEF>the facts and information that you have learned, and the understanding you have gained</DEF><THESEXA>Our knowledge of other cultures and societies has improved.</THESEXA><THESEXA>scientific knowledge</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>expertise</EXP><DEF>special knowledge about how to do something, that you get through experience, training, or study</DEF><THESEXA>The technical expertise was provided by a Japanese company.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They need people with medical expertise.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>know-how</EXP><DEF>practical knowledge about how to do something</DEF><THESEXA> Business leaders often lack the local know-how to tackle problems in specific countries. </THESEXA><THESEXA>financial know-how</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wisdom</EXP><DEF>good sense and judgment, based on knowledge and experience</DEF><THESEXA>the wisdom of the older family members</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s a matter of common wisdom that newspapers cannot be trusted.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grasp</EXP><DEF>how much you know about a situation or subject, and how well you understand it</DEF><THESEXA>He’s been praised for his grasp of the country’s economic problems.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She has a good grasp of the language.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4559"><Section><Exponent><EXP>lake</EXP><DEF>a large area of water surrounded by land</DEF><THESEXA>Lake Michigan</THESEXA><THESEXA>We went for a swim in the lake.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lagoon</EXP><DEF>an area of water that is separated from the sea by rocks, sand, or <NonDV><REFHWD>coral</REFHWD></NonDV></DEF><THESEXA>a tropical lagoon</THESEXA><THESEXA>coastal lagoons</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>loch</EXP><DEF>in Scotland, a lake or an area of sea water that is almost completely surrounded by land</DEF><THESEXA>Loch Ness</THESEXA><THESEXA>a sea loch</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reservoir</EXP><DEF>a lake, especially an artificial one, where water is stored before it is supplied to people’s houses</DEF><THESEXA>The reservoirs supply water to Greater Manchester.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pond</EXP><DEF>a small area of fresh water that is smaller than a lake, which is either natural or artificially made</DEF><THESEXA>There were several ducks on the village pond.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pool</EXP><DEF>a small area of still water in a hollow place</DEF><THESEXA>a pool of water near the summit of the mountain</THESEXA><THESEXA>a <COLLOINEXA>rockpool</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>a pool in some rocks near the sea</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>puddle</EXP><DEF>a very small area of water on the ground, especially after it has been raining</DEF><THESEXA>She turned quickly to avoid stepping in a puddle.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>waterhole</EXP><DEF>a small area of water in a dry country, where wild animals go to drink</DEF><THESEXA>The waterhole is used by elephants.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4540"><Section><Exponent><EXP>land</EXP><DEF>an area that is owned by someone or that can be used for farming or building houses</DEF><THESEXA>This is private land.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They moved to the country and bought some land.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>farmland</EXP><DEF>land that is used for farming</DEF><THESEXA>The area is one of gently rolling hills and farmland.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>territory</EXP><DEF>land that belongs to a country or that is controlled by a country during a war</DEF><THESEXA>His plane was forced to make an emergency landing in Chinese territory.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The army was advancing into enemy territory.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the grounds</EXP><DEF>the gardens and land around a big building such as a castle, school, or hospital</DEF><THESEXA>The grounds of the castle are open to visitors every weekend.</THESEXA><THESEXA> the school grounds</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>estate</EXP><DEF>a large area of land in the country, usually with one large house on it and one owner</DEF><THESEXA>The film is set on an English country estate.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4524"><Section><SECHEADING>different kinds of language</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>dialect</EXP><DEF>a form of a language that is spoken in one area of a country, with different words, grammar, or pronunciation from other areas</DEF><THESEXA>Cantonese is only one of many Chinese dialects.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the local dialect</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>accent</EXP><DEF>the way that someone pronounces words, because of where they were born or live, or their social class</DEF><THESEXA>Karen has a strong New Jersey accent.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an upper class accent</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slang</EXP><DEF> very informal spoken language, used especially by people who belong to a particular group, for example young people or criminals</DEF><THESEXA>Teenage slang changes all the time.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Dosh’ is slang for ‘money’.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>terminology</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> the technical words or expressions that are used in a particular subject</DEF><THESEXA>musical terminology</THESEXA><THESEXA>Patients are often unfamiliar with medical terminology. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>jargon</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially disapproving</REGISTERLAB><DEF>words and phrases used in a particular profession or subject and which are difficult for other people to understand</DEF><THESEXA>The instructions were written in complicated technical jargon.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Outsourcing’ is business jargon for sending work to people outside a company to do. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The letter was full of legal jargon.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>techniques used in language</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>metaphor</EXP><DEF>a way of describing something by referring to it as something different and suggesting that it has similar qualities to that thing</DEF><THESEXA>The beehive is a metaphor for human society.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>simile</EXP><DEF>an expression that describes something by comparing it with something else, using the words <DEFBOLD>as</DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD>like</DEFBOLD>, for example ‘as white as snow’</DEF><THESEXA>The poet uses the simile ‘soft like clay’.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>irony</EXP><DEF>the use of words that are the opposite of what you really mean, often in order to be amusing</DEF><THESEXA>‘I’m so happy to hear that,’ he said, with more than a trace of irony in his voice.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bathos</EXP><DEF>a sudden change from a subject that is beautiful, moral, or serious to something that is ordinary, silly, or not important</DEF><THESEXA>The play is too sentimental and full of bathos.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hyperbole</EXP><DEF>a way of describing something by saying that it is much bigger, smaller, worse etc than it actually is – used especially to excite people’s feelings</DEF><THESEXA>In his speeches, he used a lot of hyperbole.</THESEXA><THESEXA>journalistic hyperbole</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>alliteration</EXP><DEF>the use of several words together that all begin with the same sound, in order to make a special effect, especially in poetry</DEF><THESEXA>the alliteration of the ‘s’ sound in ‘sweet birds sang softly’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>imagery</EXP><DEF>the use of words to describe ideas or actions in a way that makes the reader connect the ideas with pictures in their mind</DEF><THESEXA>the use of water imagery in Fitzgerald’s novel ‘The Great Gatsby’</THESEXA><THESEXA>She uses the imagery of a bird’s song to represent eternal hope.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rhetorical question</EXP><DEF>a question that you ask as a way of making a statement, without expecting an answer</DEF><THESEXA>When he said ‘how can these attitudes still exist in a civilized society?’, he was asking a rhetorical question.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4509"><Section><SECHEADING>the one before this one</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>last</EXP><DEF>most recent or nearest to the present time</DEF><THESEXA>His last film was much better.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It rained all day last Saturday.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The last time I saw her was two years ago.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>previous</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>before this one, or before the one that you are talking about</DEF><THESEXA>See the diagram in the previous chapter.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His previous records had all been jazz records.</THESEXA><THESEXA>How much were you earning in your previous job?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>former</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>existing or having a particular position in the past, but not now</DEF><THESEXA>the former Soviet Union</THESEXA><THESEXA>the former US president</THESEXA><THESEXA>Interest rates are unlikely to return to their former level.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the former Chief Executive</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>old</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>used about a person or thing that existed in the past, but has been replaced by a newer one</DEF><THESEXA>an old boyfriend</THESEXA><THESEXA>The old model was much slower.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>the one that comes at the end</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>last</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>happening or existing at the end, with no others after</DEF><THESEXA>What time does the last train leave?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Our house is the last one on the right.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>final</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>last in a series of actions, events, parts of a story etc</DEF><THESEXA>It’s the final game of the championship tomorrow.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the final scene of the film</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>closing</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>used about the last part of a long period of time, or of an event, book etc that has been exciting or interesting</DEF><THESEXA>the closing years of the twentieth century</THESEXA><THESEXA>Barnes scored the winning goal in the closing minutes of the game.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>concluding</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>used about the last part of a piece of writing, a speech, or an organized event, that ends it in a definite way</DEF><THESEXA>the concluding section of the report</THESEXA><THESEXA>the judge’s concluding remarks</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>penultimate</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>peˈnʌltəmət, pə-</PRON></PronCodes><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>the one before the last one</DEF><THESEXA>the penultimate chapter</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-44e5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>late</EXP><DEF>arriving or happening after the time that was expected or arranged</DEF><THESEXA>Sorry I’m late.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The bus was late.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Spring seems to be very late this year.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not on time</EXP><DEF>not arriving or doing something at the time flight.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>overdue</EXP><DEF>not done or happening by the expected time – used especially about payments that are late or library books that should have been returned</DEF><THESEXA>Your rent is three weeks overdue.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I had to pay a £3 fine on some overdue library books.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be behind with <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-44c6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>later</EXP><DEF>happening or existing at some time in the future after something or someone else</DEF><THESEXA>We will discuss this at a later time.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Later historians have cast doubt on the truth of his story. </THESEXA><THESEXA>They say that they may need to review the case at a later stage.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>following</EXP><DEF> happening or coming immediately after something – used about periods of time, or parts of a piece of writing</DEF><THESEXA>He resigned the following year.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The following day he was back to normal.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Gandhi 's attitude to religion is dealt with in the following chapter.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>future</EXP><DEF>likely to happen or exist at some time in the future</DEF><THESEXA>What influence will this have on future developments?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>subsequent</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>happening or coming at some time after something else</DEF><THESEXA>a subsequent decision by the Supreme Court</THESEXA><THESEXA>Subsequent events proved him wrong. </THESEXA><THESEXA>In subsequent years, the number of patients became smaller and smaller. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>succeeding</EXP><DEF>coming after someone or something else – used about a series of groups of people, periods of time, or parts of a book</DEF><THESEXA>His work was admired by succeeding generations.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Over the succeeding months, the stitches were carefully removed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>These problems are further discussed in the succeeding chapters.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-44aa"><Section><Exponent><EXP>laugh</EXP><DEF>to make sounds with your voice and move your face, because you think that something is over a magazine</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>roar with laughter</EXP><DEF>to laugh very loudly, especially with a deep voice</DEF><THESEXA>I could hear my father roaring with laughter at <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-448d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>lawyer</EXP><DEF>someone whose job is to advise people about laws, write formal agreements, or represent people in court</DEF><THESEXA>His lawyer told him to plead guilty.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>attorney</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>a lawyer. <DEFBOLD>Attorney</DEFBOLD> sounds more formal than <DEFBOLD>lawyer</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>‘The United States sees intellectual property rights as sacred,’ said Thomas Klitgaard, an attorney specializing in international law.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Acting on the advice of his attorney, he remained silent throughout the questioning. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>solicitor</EXP><DEF>a type of lawyer in Britain who gives legal advice, prepares the documents when property is bought or sold, and defends people, especially in the lower courts of law</DEF><THESEXA>She works as a commercial property solicitor at Nabarro Nathanson in London. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He went to the family solicitor to make a will.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>barrister</EXP><DEF>a lawyer in Britain who can argue cases in the higher law courts</DEF><THESEXA>McWalter's barrister, Hugh Vass, stressed his client’s previous good character. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>advocate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a formal word for a lawyer in American English, or a barrister in Scotland</DEF><THESEXA>The committee can put questions to the defendant or his advocate.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>brief</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the lawyer who represents someone in a court case</DEF><THESEXA>His brief asked for a fine rather than a prison sentence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>counsel</EXP><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>the lawyer or group of lawyers who are representing someone in court</DEF><THESEXA>counsel for the defence/prosecution</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-447d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>lazy</EXP><DEF>not liking work or physical activity, or not making any effort to do anything</DEF><THESEXA>a lazy student</THESEXA><THESEXA>You make your own breakfast! <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4468"><Section><Exponent><EXP>lead</EXP><DEF>to take a person or animal somewhere by going in front of them while they follow, or by <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-444b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>leader</EXP><DEF>the person who directs or controls a group, organization, country etc</DEF><THESEXA>Party members will vote next week to elect a new leader.</THESEXA><THESEXA>leaders of developing nations from around the world</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ruler</EXP><DEF>someone, such as a king or queen or a military leader, who has official power over a country or area</DEF><THESEXA>Sheik Mohammed, ruler of Dubai</THESEXA><THESEXA>Argentina’s former military rulers</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>head of state</EXP><DEF>someone who leads a country or state</DEF><THESEXA>Twenty-one heads of state will meet at the annual World Trade summit. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>figurehead</EXP><DEF>someone who is seen as the leader of a country or organization, although he or she does not have any real power</DEF><THESEXA>the Queen’s role as a national figurehead</THESEXA><THESEXA> There are advantages in having a respected personality as a figurehead for the organisation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ringleader</EXP><DEF>someone who leads a group that is doing something illegal or wrong</DEF><THESEXA>The alleged ringleaders of the gang have been arrested.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the ringleaders of the airplane bomb plot</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-440d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>learn</EXP><DEF>to gain knowledge of a subject or skill, especially by being taught or trained</DEF><THESEXA>How long have you been learning Italian?</THESEXA><THESEXA>At what age can you learn to drive in America?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>study</EXP><DEF>to learn about a subject by reading books, going to classes etc, especially at school or university</DEF><THESEXA>She’s studying music at Berkeley College in California.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>train</EXP><DEF>to learn the skills and get the experience that you need in order to do a particular job</DEF><THESEXA>Julie’s training to be a nurse.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pick sth up</EXP><DEF>to learn something without much effort, by watching or listening to other people</DEF><THESEXA>It’s easy to pick up a language when you’re living in a country.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The rules of the game are easy – you’ll soon pick them up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get the hang of sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to learn how to do or use something that is fairly complicated, especially with practice</DEF><THESEXA>It took me a while to get the hang of all the features on my new camera.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>revise</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>review</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to study facts again, especially on your own, in order to learn them before an examination</DEF><THESEXA> Jenny’s upstairs revising for her Maths exam tomorrow.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>master</EXP><DEF>to learn something so well that you have no difficulty with it, especially a skill or a language</DEF><THESEXA>She gave me a book called ‘Mastering the Art of French Cooking’.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I learnt Spanish for years but I never really mastered it.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4387"><Section><Exponent><EXP>lend</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>loan</LEXVAR><GEO>especially AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to let someone borrow money or something that belongs to you for a short time</DEF><THESEXA>Can you lend me $20?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Did you lend that book to Mike?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The documents were loaned by the local library.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>let sb use sth/let sb have sth</EXP><DEF>to let someone use something that belongs to you for a short time, especially a room, a house, or something big and expensive</DEF><THESEXA>Some friends are letting us use their house while they are on vacation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Dad said he’d let me have his car for the weekend.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be on loan</EXP><DEF>if something is on loan, it has been lent to a person or organization in an official way – often used about a library book or a work of art</DEF><THESEXA>The museum has an exhibition of paintings on loan from the Louvre.</THESEXA><THESEXA>According to the computer, this book is still out on loan.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-78ef4d03.118e0988fe1.31f0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>like</EXP><DEF>to think that someone or something is nice</DEF><THESEXA>I like your dress – it’s a beautiful colour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you like spaghetti?</THESEXA><THESEXA>What did you like about the movie?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I like travelling by train.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I like to see the children enjoying themselves.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Everybody liked Mr Schofield.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be fond of sb/sth</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>to like someone or something, especially something that you have liked for a long time or someone who you have known for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>Connie had always been fond of animals.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Over the years, I’ve become quite fond of him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He had always been fond of drinking at lunchtime, perhaps too fond.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be keen on sb/sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to like someone or something – often used in negative sentences</DEF><THESEXA>I like Maria but I’m not keen on her husband.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Our English teacher was very keen on Shakespeare, but I couldn’t stand him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was keen on all sports at school.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I know he’s keen on opera. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-42b4"><Section><Exponent><EXP>limit</EXP><DEF>the highest number, speed, temperature etc that is allowed by a law or rule</DEF><THESEXA>He borrowed money up to the limit that the bank allowed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The speed limit is 65 m.p.h.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There’s no limit on the amount of money that may be brought into the US.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Pollution levels are over the official limit.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some families set limits on how much they spend on each other’s Christmas present.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>restrictions</EXP><DEF>rules or laws that strictly control what you are allowed to do</DEF><THESEXA>Travel restrictions might reduce the spread of the disease.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Unions are pressing for restrictions on steel imports from Japan.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Because of restrictions on reporting, newspapers were not allowed to cover the story.</THESEXA><THESEXA>New restrictions have been imposed on immigration.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>limitations</EXP><DEF>limits on what a person or thing is able to do – used especially
