</THESEXA><THESEXA>Hikers should know their physical limitations and not take unnecessary risks.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>constraints</EXP><DEF>facts or conditions that limit what you can do, for example not having enough time, money etc</DEF><THESEXA> Financial constraints are forcing many people in their twenties to live with their parents.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The last part of the show had to be cut because of time constraints.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The constraints of prison life are sometimes too much for people to bear.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>maximum</EXP><DEF>the largest number or amount that is possible, normal, or allowed</DEF><THESEXA>Classes have a maximum of twenty students.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What’s the maximum you can earn before you have to pay tax?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>minimum</EXP><DEF>the smallest number or amount that is possible or allowed</DEF><THESEXA>He was sentenced to a minimum of 25 years in prison.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Our aim is to reduce the number of accidents to an <COLLOINEXA>absolute minimum</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ceiling</EXP><DEF>the largest number or amount of something that is officially allowed</DEF><THESEXA>There is a ceiling on the amount of foreign investment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Import quotas may rise from the present ceiling of 18.5 million to 20 million.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Congress was once again considering raising the federal debt ceiling.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4271"><Section><SECHEADING>on the ground/on the surface of something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>line</EXP><DEF>a long thin mark on a piece of paper, the ground, or another surface</DEF><THESEXA>The teacher put a red line through the first sentence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If the ball goes over the line, it’s out of play.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>groove</EXP><DEF>a thin line that has been cut into a surface</DEF><THESEXA>Deep grooves had been cut into the stone to channel the water.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Lyle ran his fingernail along the groove in the table.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rut</EXP><DEF>a deep line in the ground which has been made by the wheels of vehicles</DEF><THESEXA>The deep ruts made by the trucks were full of water.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The tractor’s wheels caught a rut in the field and jolted him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>crease/wrinkle</EXP><DEF>a line on clothes, material, or paper where it has been folded or crushed</DEF><THESEXA>She was trying to smooth out the creases in her dress.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She had wrinkles in her skirt where she had sat.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>on someone’s face</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>line</EXP><DEF>a line on the skin of someone’s face</DEF><THESEXA>The deep lines on his forehead showed that he was a worried man.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wrinkle</EXP><DEF>a deep line on someone’s face caused by becoming old</DEF><THESEXA>The manufacturers claim that the cream will reduce wrinkles.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Even with a few wrinkles, she still looks great.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4213"><Section><Exponent><EXP>listen</EXP><DEF>to pay attention to what someone is saying or to a sound that you hear</DEF><THESEXA>I didn’t hear the answer, because I wasn’t listening when she read it out.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He listened carefully to every word I said.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pay attention</EXP><DEF>to listen carefully to what someone is saying</DEF><THESEXA>I nodded to show I was paying attention.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was tired and wasn’t paying attention to what he was saying.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>eavesdrop</EXP><DEF>to secretly listen to someone else’s conversation by standing near them, hiding behind a door etc</DEF><THESEXA>I caught him eavesdropping on our conversation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They spoke in quiet voices which made it hard to eavesdrop.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>overhear</EXP><DEF>to hear someone say something, especially accidentally</DEF><THESEXA>I overheard him say something about wanting to move house. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Excuse me! <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-416e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>lonely</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>lonesome</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>unhappy because you are alone or do not have any friends</DEF><THESEXA>Tammy felt very lonely when she first arrived in New York.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Our neighbor George is a very lonely man.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I get so lonesome here with no one to talk to.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>isolated</EXP><DEF>lonely because your situation makes it difficult for you to meet people</DEF><THESEXA>People caring for sick relatives often feel very isolated.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Children of very rich parents can grow up isolated from the rest of society.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>alienated</EXP><DEF>feeling that you do not belong in a particular place or group</DEF><THESEXA>She felt very alienated as the only woman in the company.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In high school she felt somehow different and alienated from other students.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>homesick</EXP><DEF>unhappy because you are a long way from your home and the people who live there</DEF><THESEXA>When I first went to Germany, I was very homesick.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>miss sb</EXP><DEF>used when saying that you feel unhappy because someone is not there with you</DEF><THESEXA>I miss you.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She misses her friends.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

students develop their communication skills.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Security is our primary concern.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The primary function of the university was considered to be the teaching of ‘the great <NonDV><REFHWD>target</REFHWD></NonDV> or <NonDV><REFHWD>suspect</REFHWD></NonDV>.<DEFBOLD> Prime </DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>main</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Their prime objective is to <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3f6f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>maker</EXP><DEF>a company or person whose business is to make things to be sold, either by hand or using machines</DEF><THESEXA>the country’s largest car maker</THESEXA><THESEXA>We got a <COLLOINEXA>cabinet maker</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>someone who makes furniture from wood</GLOSS> to redo our kitchen for us.</THESEXA><THESEXA /></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>producer</EXP><DEF>a company or country that makes large quantities of something to be sold – often used about raw materials such as oil, coffee, or steel</DEF><THESEXA>OPEC is an organization of 13 major oil producers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Gold producers' shares went up by 50%. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>manufacturer</EXP><DEF>a company that makes things in large quantities, especially in a factory</DEF><THESEXA>Clothing manufacturers have been feeling the effects of the recession.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a plane manufacturer</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shipbuilder/boatbuilder</EXP><DEF>a person or company that makes ships or boats</DEF><THESEXA>a Japanese shipbuilder, Mitsubishi Heavy Industries</THESEXA><THESEXA>He started out as a boatbuilder.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3f51"><Section><Exponent><EXP>make-up</EXP><DEF>coloured substances that are put on your face to improve or change your appearance</DEF><THESEXA>I don’t usually wear much make-up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cosmetics</EXP><DEF>creams, powders etc that you use on your face and body in order to look more attractive</DEF><THESEXA>a range of cosmetics and toiletries</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lipstick</EXP><DEF>a substance you use for adding colour to your lips, in the shape of a small stick</DEF><THESEXA>She was wearing bright red lipstick.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>eyeshadow</EXP><DEF>coloured cream or powder that you put on your eyelids</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>eyeliner</EXP><DEF>something you use for adding a line of colour at the edges of your eyelids to make your eyes look bigger or more noticeable</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mascara</EXP><DEF>a dark substance you use to colour your eyelashes and make them look thicker</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blusher</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>blush</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO><LEXVAR>rouge</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>old-fashioned</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>red or pink cream or powder used for making your cheeks look slightly more pink</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>foundation</EXP><DEF>a cream the same colour as your skin that you put on your face before the rest of your make-up</DEF></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3f26"><Section><Exponent><EXP>man</EXP><DEF>an adult male human</DEF><THESEXA>a young man</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sir Edmund Hillary was the first man to climb Mount Everest.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>guy</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>bloke/chap</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a man</DEF><THESEXA>She’d arranged to meet a guy in the bar.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Alex is a really nice bloke.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gentleman</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a man – used as a very polite way of talking about a man</DEF><THESEXA>an elderly gentleman</THESEXA><THESEXA>Please could you serve this gentleman?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>boy</EXP><DEF>a young male person, usually a child or a teenager</DEF><THESEXA>a teenage boy</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lad</EXP><REGISTERLAB>old-fashioned informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a boy or young man</DEF><THESEXA>When I was a young lad, I wanted to join the army.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>youth</EXP><DEF>a teenage boy or young man – used especially in news reports to show disapproval</DEF><THESEXA>Gangs of youths roam the streets.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>male</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a man – used especially by the police or in science and research contexts. The adjective <DEFBOLD>male </DEFBOLD> is much more common than the noun</DEF><THESEXA>We are investigating the death of an unidentified male.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The condition is usually found only in males.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dude</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a man – a very informal use</DEF><THESEXA>You could tell there was something creepy going on with that dude.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>relating to men</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>male</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>a male teacher, singer etc is a man. Male jobs are the kind of jobs that men typically do</DEF><THESEXA>Most science teachers are male.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a male nurse</THESEXA><THESEXA>We are trying to recruit more women to do traditionally male jobs such as engineering.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>masculine</EXP><DEF>considered to be more typical of a man than of a woman</DEF><THESEXA>He had a very masculine face.</THESEXA><THESEXA>masculine aggression</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>manly</EXP><DEF>having the qualities that people expect and admire in a man, such as being brave and strong</DEF><THESEXA>He took off his shirt, revealing his manly chest.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It isn’t considered manly to cry.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In the portrait, the King looked manly and in control.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was bronzed and athletic, with manly features and a steady gaze.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>macho</EXP><DEF>behaving in a way that is traditionally typical of men, for example by being strong and tough and not showing your feelings – used especially either humorously or to show disapproval</DEF><THESEXA>On the outside he may seem to be very macho but inside he’s very sensitive.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Stallone always plays macho men.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s far too macho to drink mineral water.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3f01"><Section><Exponent><EXP>mankind/humankind</EXP><DEF>people in general. Some people think that the word <DEFBOLD>mankind</DEFBOLD> seems to make women seem unimportant, and prefer to use <DEFBOLD>humankind</DEFBOLD> instead</DEF><THESEXA>a scientific advance that has benefited mankind</THESEXA><THESEXA>He once said that the invention of television was the ‘greatest single disaster in the history of mankind’. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the human race</EXP><DEF>all the people in the world, considered as one group</DEF><THESEXA>The entire human race could be wiped out by nuclear war.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The majority of the human race will soon be living in big cities. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>humanity</EXP><DEF>people in general – used especially when you are talking about caring for people and respecting their rights and their desire for happiness</DEF><THESEXA>The General was accused of committing crimes against humanity.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They hope to achieve a better future for all humanity.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>man</EXP><DEF>people in general – use this when you are comparing humans with other living things. Some people prefer not to not use <DEFBOLD>man</DEFBOLD> because they think it makes women seem unimportant</DEF><THESEXA>Jericho is the oldest continuously inhabited city known to man.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The chimpanzee is man’s closest relative.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

manya large number of people or things – used in everyday English in questions and negative sentences, and after ‘too’ and ‘so’. In formal or written English, you can also use it in other sentencesThere weren’t many people at the meeting.Did you get many birthday presents?Many people voted against the proposal.a lotmany. A lot is less formal than many and is the usual phrase to use in everyday EnglishA lot of tourists visit Venice in the summer.The club has a lot more members now.dozens/hundreds/thousands/millionsmany – used when you cannot be exact but the number is two dozen or more, two hundred or more etcAt least five people died and dozens more were injured in a gas explosion.They’ve wasted thousands of pounds on the project.a large number ofwrittena lot of a particular type of person or thingChina plans to build a large number of nuclear power plants.numerousformalmany – used especially when saying that something has happened many timesWe’ve contacted him on numerous occasions.Numerous studies have shown a link between smoking and lung cancer.countless/innumerableɪˈnjuːmərəbəl ɪˈnuː-only before nounmany – used when it is impossible to count or imagine how many. Innumerable is more formal than countlessHe spent countless hours in the gym.They had been given innumerable warnings.a host ofmany – used especially when something seems surprising or impressiveAge is the biggest risk factor in a host of diseases.People leave jobs for a whole host of reasons.a raft ofmany – used especially when talking about ideas, suggestions, changes in business or politicsThe report made a raft of recommendations.The new government is planning a whole raft of changes.quite a fewespecially spokena fairly large number of people or thingsWe’ve had quite a few problems with the software.I’ve met quite a few of his friends.lotsinformalmanyI’ve invited lots of people.‘How many cats has she got?’ ‘Lots!’tons/loadsinformalmany – a very informal useI’ve got tons of books.Have a strawberry – there are loads here.

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3de6"><Section><SECHEADING>Sense 2</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>meaning</EXP><DEF>the thoughts or ideas that someone wants you to such as an article or a speech</DEF><THESEXA>Read the story quickly first, just to get the gist of it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I think I understand the gist of what you’re saying. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the drift/your drift</EXP><DEF>the main set of ideas involved in something such as an explanation, argument, or account of something – often used when the ideas are not stated directly</DEF><THESEXA>The drift of his letter is that he wants to come back.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t quite follow your drift. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the essence</EXP><DEF>the most important meaning of something such as an argument or piece of writing</DEF><THESEXA>The essence of what he is saying is that human character is formed by <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3d38"><Section><Exponent><EXP>mention</EXP><DEF>to talk or write about something or someone, usually quickly and without saying very <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3d17"><Section><Exponent><EXP>method</EXP><DEF>a way of doing something, especially one that is well known and often <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3cc4"><Section><Exponent><EXP>mind</EXP><DEF>what you use to think and imagine things</DEF><THESEXA>My mind was full of big ideas.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I never know <COLLOINEXA>what’s going on in her mind</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>At the back of my mind </COLLOINEXA>I had the funny feeling that I’d met her know <COLLOINEXA>what’s going on inside her head</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>what she is thinking</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>subconscious</EXP><DEF>the part of your mind that influences the way you think or behave, even though you may not realize this is happening, and which makes you have dreams</DEF><THESEXA>She suddenly remembered a traumatic incident that had been <COLLOINEXA>buried deep in her subconscious</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>During the daytime our conscious minds are active, but during the night <COLLOINEXA>the subconscious</COLLOINEXA> takes over. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>psyche</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>someone’s mind, especially their feelings and attitudes, and the way these influence their character – used especially when talking about people’s minds in general</DEF><THESEXA>The need for love is deeply buried in our psyche.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Freud has provided an account of <COLLOINEXA>the human psyche</COLLOINEXA>’s stages of development. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The child is simply searching his psyche to find some past event that relates to his new experiences. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mentality</EXP><DEF>a particular way of thinking that a group of people have, especially one that you think is wrong or bad</DEF><THESEXA>I just don’t understand them.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the ego</EXP><REGISTERLAB>technical</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the part of your mind that gives you your sense of who you are – used especially in Freudian psychology</DEF><THESEXA>the rational world of the ego and the irrational world of the unconscious</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3c8f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>mistake</EXP><DEF>something incorrect that you accidentally do, say, or write</DEF><THESEXA>a spelling mistake</THESEXA><THESEXA>I made a mistake – it should say £230, not £320.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>error</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a mistake</DEF><THESEXA>an error in the report</THESEXA><THESEXA>grammatical errors</THESEXA><THESEXA>He had made a serious error on his tax form.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>misprint</EXP><DEF>a small mistake in something that is printed</DEF><THESEXA>There was a misprint in the article, and instead of ‘pleasant’ it said ‘pheasant’.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>typo</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a mistake in something that has been typed or printed</DEF><THESEXA>I spotted a couple of typos in the letter.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>inaccuracy</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a piece of information that is not completely correct</DEF><THESEXA>The report contained several inaccuracies.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mix-up</EXP><DEF>a careless mistake in which one name, time, address etc has been confused with another, so that the details of something are wrong</DEF><THESEXA>There was a mix-up over the train times and I missed my train.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slip-up</EXP><DEF>a careless mistake when you are doing something</DEF><THESEXA>The other team took advantage of the goalie’s slip-up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>oversight</EXP><DEF>a mistake in which you forget something or do not notice something</DEF><THESEXA>Through some oversight, the brochures were not ready by the right date.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>a slip of the tongue</EXP><DEF>a mistake in which you accidentally say a similar sounding word</DEF><THESEXA>When I said Thursday, I meant Tuesday. It was a slip of the tongue.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>faux pas</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˌfəʊ ˈpɑː, ˈfəʊ pɑː</PRON><AMEVARPRON>ˌfoʊ ˈpɑː</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an embarrassing mistake in a social situation, when you do or say something that you shouldn’t</DEF><THESEXA>Harris, trying to be funny, addressed the waiter as ‘boy’. A deathly silence followed this faux pas.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>a stupid mistake</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>blunder</EXP><DEF>a stupid mistake caused by not thinking carefully enough about what you are saying or doing, which could have serious results</DEF><THESEXA>In a serious blunder by the hospital, two babies were sent home with the wrong parents.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gaffe</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ɡæf</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>an embarrassing and stupid mistake made in a social situation or in public</DEF><THESEXA>a serious gaffe in her speech about immigration</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>howler</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a very bad mistake, especially one that shows you do not know something, and that often makes other people laugh</DEF><THESEXA>Photographers should be careful of making classic howlers, such as having a tree grow out of your subject’s head.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cock-up</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a silly mistake when you are doing something – a very informal use</DEF><THESEXA>They made a cock-up with the bill.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The government is anxious to avoid any more cock-ups.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3c7a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>misunderstand</EXP><DEF>to think that someone means one thing, when in fact they mean something else</DEF><THESEXA>I think you’ve misunderstood what I’m saying.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some companies appear to have misunderstood the new rules.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Don’t misunderstand me - I have nothing against these people.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get sb/sth wrong</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to misunderstand someone or something – used especially in everyday spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>Looks like you’ve got it all wrong. </THESEXA><THESEXA>You’ve got me all wrong - that’s not what I meant. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Tell me if I’ve got it wrong.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mistake</EXP><DEF>to misunderstand someone’s intentions, and react in the wrong way</DEF><THESEXA>He was a very private man, and some people mistook this for unfriendliness.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I thought she wanted us to leave her alone, but I may been mistaken.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>misread/misjudge</EXP><DEF>to wrongly believe that someone’s actions show that they have a particular opinion or feeling, or that a situation means that you should behave in particular way</DEF><THESEXA>The party completely misread the mood of the voters at the last election.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Eddie wondered if he should be scared, too. Maybe he had misjudged the situation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>misinterpret</EXP><DEF>to not understand the true meaning of someone’s actions or words, so that you believe something that is not in fact true</DEF><THESEXA>A lot of people misinterpreted what I was saying, and have called me a racist.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Struggling with an unfamiliar language, the simplest conversations were misinterpreted.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>misconstrue</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to misunderstand something that someone has said or done</DEF><THESEXA>She claimed that members of the press had misconstrued her comments.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>miss the point</EXP><DEF>to not understand the main part or meaning of what someone is saying or what something is intended to do</DEF><THESEXA>I think you’re missing the whole point of the film.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If he thinks it’s all about how much profit he can make, then he’s missing the point.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get the wrong end of the stick</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make a mistake about one part of something that you are told, so that you understand the rest of it in completely the wrong way</DEF><THESEXA>Maybe I got the wrong end of the stick. I thought she was leaving him, not the other way round.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3c59"><Section><SECHEADING>to mix foods, liquids etc</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>mix</EXP><DEF>to put different substances or liquids together so that they can no longer be separated</DEF><THESEXA>Mix yellow and blue paint to make green.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This cake is really easy – you just mix everything together in the bowl.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Concrete is made by mixing gravel with sand, cement, and water.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>combine</EXP><DEF> to mix things together so that they form a single substance. <DEFBOLD>Combine</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>mix</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Combine the flour and the eggs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Steel is produced by combining iron with carbon.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stir</EXP><DEF>to move a spoon or stick around in a liquid, a pan etc, especially when you are mixing things together</DEF><THESEXA>Keep stirring until the sauce becomes thicker. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Stir the sugar into the warm milk.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Stir the paint before you use it.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blend</EXP><DEF> to mix together soft or liquid substances to form a single smooth substance</DEF><THESEXA>Blend the yogurt with fresh fruit for a great drink.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>beat</EXP><DEF> to mix food together quickly and thoroughly using a fork or kitchen tool – used especially about eggs</DEF><THESEXA>Beat the eggs and add them to the milk and flour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>whisk</EXP><DEF>to mix foods that are soft or liquid very quickly so that air is mixed in, using a fork or special tool</DEF><THESEXA>Whisk the egg whites until they form soft peaks.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dilute</EXP><DEF>to mix a liquid with water in order to make it weaker</DEF><THESEXA>Dilute the bleach with two parts water to one part bleach.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to mix styles, ideas, or other things </SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>mix</EXP><DEF>to put different styles, ideas, or other things together</DEF><THESEXA>His music mixes jazz and classical styles.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The different categories of books were all mixed together.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>combine</EXP><DEF>to mix different styles, ideas, or other things, so that they work together or become a single thing</DEF><THESEXA>Diets are most effective when they are combined with exercise.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He combines Greek philosophy with Christian teachings.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blend</EXP><DEF>to combine parts of different things together, especially in a successful and effective way</DEF><THESEXA>The teaching course blends theory and practice in the classroom.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fuse</EXP><DEF>to combine different styles in order to form a new style</DEF><THESEXA>The band fuses African rhythms with traditional Celtic music.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>jumble</EXP><DEF>to mix things together in an untidy way, so that they are not in any order</DEF><THESEXA>The jigsaw pieces were all jumbled together in the box.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3c43"><Section><Exponent><EXP>mixture</EXP><DEF>several different substances, ideas, qualities etc that have been put together, especially so that they form one thing</DEF><THESEXA>Pour the mixture into the cake pan and bake for 50 minutes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He looked at her with a mixture of admiration and curiosity.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>combination</EXP><DEF>two or more different things, substances etc that are used together or work together</DEF><THESEXA>Doctors use a combination of drugs to combat the disease.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The business failed due to a combination of bad management and a lack of experience.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blend</EXP><DEF>a mixture of two or more things, qualities, or characteristics, especially ones that combine successfully or in a pleasant way</DEF><THESEXA>The England team is a good side, with a nice blend of experience and youthful energy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The sauce uses a blend of different ingredients.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>a cross between sth and sth</EXP><DEF>a mixture of very different things – used when you are describing what something looks or sounds like</DEF><THESEXA>The building looked like a cross between a museum and a spaceship.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her music sounds like a cross between the Rolling Stones and Amy Winehouse. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hybrid</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈhaɪbrəd</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>something that is produced by combining two or more things, especially using advanced scientific methods</DEF><THESEXA>Scientists are combining human and animal embryos to create genetic hybrids.</THESEXA><THESEXA>These industries use a hybrid of different technologies. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The plant is a hybrid of wheat and rye.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>amalgam</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>əˈmælɡəm</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a mixture of different things, in which you can still recognize the original features</DEF><THESEXA>The record is an amalgam of hard rock, jazz, and blues.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>synthesis</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>something that has been made by combining different things, especially information or ideas</DEF><THESEXA>The essay should be a synthesis of the information from various sources.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>an untidy mixture</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>jumble</EXP><DEF>a lot of different things mixed together in an untidy way</DEF><THESEXA>Rae looked through the jumble of old record albums and tapes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mishmash</EXP><EXP>/hodgepodge</EXP><REGISTERLAB> informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a mixture of a lot of different things, styles etc that do not seem right together</DEF><THESEXA>If you look closely at the individual buildings they are a real hodgepodge of styles.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The story is a bit of a mishmash.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>a mxture of chemical substances</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>compound</EXP><DEF>a chemical substance that contains atoms of two or more <NonDV><REFHWD>element</REFHWD><SUFFIX>s</SUFFIX></NonDV></DEF><THESEXA>common chemical compounds such as sodium chloride</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>solution</EXP><DEF>a liquid mixed with a solid or a gas</DEF><THESEXA>a weak sugar solution</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3c30"><Section><Exponent><EXP>mock</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to laugh at and say unkind things about a person, institution, belief etc, to show that you do not have a high opinion of them. <DEFBOLD>Mock</DEFBOLD> is a formal word – in everyday English people usually say <DEFBOLD>make fun of</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The press mocked his attempts to appeal to young voters.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was mocked by other pupils in her class. </THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>You shouldn’t mock the afflicted</COLLOINEXA>! <GLOSS>you should not make fun of people who cannot help having problems – used especially <NonDV><REFHWD>ironically</REFHWD></NonDV>, when really you think it is funny too</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make fun of sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to make someone or something seem stupid by making unkind jokes about them</DEF><THESEXA>Peter didn’t seem to realize that they were making fun of him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It used to be fashionable to make fun of the European Parliament. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>laugh at sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to make unkind or funny remarks about someone or something, because they seem stupid or strange</DEF><THESEXA>I don’t want the other kids to laugh at me.</THESEXA><THESEXA>People would laugh at the idea nowadays.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>poke fun at sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to make someone or something seem silly by making jokes about them, especially in a way that is funny but not really cruel</DEF><THESEXA>a TV series that regularly poked fun at the government</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s in no position to poke fun at other people’s use of English!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ridicule</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make unkind remarks that make someone or something seem stupid</DEF><THESEXA>Catesby ridiculed his suggestion.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His ideas were widely ridiculed at the time.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Scientists ridiculed him for doubting the existence of the greenhouse effect.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>deride</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make remarks that show you think that something is stupid or useless – often used when you think that the people who do this are wrong</DEF><THESEXA>Some forms of alternative medicine – much derided by doctors – have been shown to help patients.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the system that Marxists previously derided as ‘bourgeois democracy’ </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3c19"><Section><Exponent><EXP>modern</EXP><DEF>something that is modern uses the most recent designs or methods</DEF><THESEXA>The house <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3bfc"><Section><Exponent><EXP>modest</EXP><DEF>not wanting to talk about your abilities or achievements and to say that you are good at something, even when you are – used to show approval</DEF><THESEXA>Miller, a quiet, modest man, gave credit to Asher for the initial discovery.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was surprisingly modest about her own achievements as a player.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>self-effacing</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>not wanting to talk about yourself or to attract attention</DEF><THESEXA>Her husband was a quiet, self-effacing man who spent much of his time in his study.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unassuming</EXP><DEF>not wanting to be noticed and not expecting to be treated in a special way</DEF><THESEXA>By now Chapman was famous, but he remained as unassuming as ever.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He then began, in typically unassuming fashion, to establish the first modern dance company.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>humble</EXP><DEF>believing that you are not more important, better, or cleverer than other people, and therefore not expecting to be treated in a special way</DEF><THESEXA>A good leader is humble enough to get advice from experts.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unpretentious</EXP><DEF>not trying to seem better than other people, even if you are rich, famous, clever etc</DEF><THESEXA>President Eisenhower was a friendly and unpretentious man who seemed to embody American virtues.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It's his unpretentious boyish charm that ensures Jackie Chan's position as one of the giants of international cinema. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>self-deprecating</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>behaving or talking about your own abilities or achievements in a way that makes them seem unimportant</DEF><THESEXA>Despite his intellect, he had an appealing self-deprecating humour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The nice thing about him is his self-deprecating manner. His intellectual ability was never pushed at you, yet he obviously had great potential. </THESEXA><THESEXA>a self-deprecating smile</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3bdb"><Section><Exponent><EXP>money</EXP><DEF>what you use to buy things, in the form of notes or coins</DEF><THESEXA>He spent all his money on computer equipment.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cash</EXP><DEF>money in the form of coins and notes</DEF><THESEXA>I didn’t have any cash with me.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>currency</EXP><DEF>the money used in a particular country</DEF><THESEXA>The dollar gained in value against other currencies.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a single European currency</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>change</EXP><DEF>money in the form of coins of low value</DEF><THESEXA>Do you have any small change?</THESEXA><THESEXA>a pocketful of loose change</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>note</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>bill</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a piece of paper money</DEF><THESEXA>a £20 note</THESEXA><THESEXA>a $5 bill</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>coin</EXP><DEF>a flat round piece of metal used as money</DEF><THESEXA>She put some coins in the parking meter.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He took a coin out of his pocket.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>a ten-pence/50-cent etc piece</EXP><DEF>a coin worth a particular amount</DEF></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3ba7"><Section><Exponent><EXP>morals</EXP><DEF>all of the basic ideas that a person or group of people has about what is morally good violence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>morality</EXP><DEF>ideas about what is right and wrong, and what is acceptable behaviour – used when talking about whether it is right to do a particular principles</COLLOINEXA> to kill any living thing.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>values</EXP><DEF>your ideas about what is important in life</DEF><THESEXA>During the 1960s, many young people prevent you from doing bad things</DEF><THESEXA>She had no scruples about listening to their private conversation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My scruples would not allow me to interfere in their relationship.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-5417"><Section><Exponent><EXP>more</EXP><DEF>in addition to an amount or number</DEF><THESEXA>Can I have some more coffee?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I have one more question. </THESEXA><THESEXA>It only costs a few dollars more.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>further</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>as well as the ones that you have already mentioned</DEF><THESEXA>She will remain in hospital for further tests.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They waited for a further two hours.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>supplementary</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>in addition to the main part of something</DEF><THESEXA>Supplementary information is available on request.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a supplementary question</THESEXA><THESEXA>supplementary income</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>extra</EXP><DEF>in addition to the usual or standard cost, time, amount etc</DEF><THESEXA>They let the kids stay up an extra hour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some stores charge extra for delivery.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Postage is extra.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>additional</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>more than the basic amount or the amount that you expected or agreed. <DEFBOLD>Additional </DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>extra</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>An evening job would provide additional income.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There may be an additional charge for paying bills by credit card.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3b92"><Section><Exponent><EXP>mother</EXP><DEF>a female parent</DEF><THESEXA>My mother and father are both teachers.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mum</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><Variant><LEXVAR>mom</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>used when talking to your father, or about someone’s father</DEF><THESEXA>My mum and dad won’t mind if you want to stay the night.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Mom, where’s my sweater?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mummy</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>mommy</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a name for mother, which is used especially by young children or when you are talking to young children</DEF><THESEXA>Where’s Mummy, Abbie?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ma</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>old-fashioned</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when talking to your mother, or about someone’s mother</DEF><THESEXA>Ma stirred the soup on the stove.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mama</EXP><REGISTERLAB>old-fashioned</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when talking to your mother, or about someone’s mother</DEF><THESEXA>Mama seldom disagreed with Papa.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stepmother</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>stepmum</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><LEXVAR>stepmom</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>a woman who is married to your father, who is not your mother but often acts as your parent</DEF><THESEXA>the wicked stepmother in fairy stories</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3b74"><Section><Exponent><EXP>mountain</EXP><DEF>a very high hill</DEF><THESEXA>the highest mountain in Austria</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hill</EXP><DEF>an area of land that is higher than the land around it, which is like a mountain but smaller and usually has a rounded top</DEF><THESEXA>We went for a walk in the hills.</THESEXA><THESEXA> The house is surrounded by woods, farmland and gentle hills.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>Mount</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><ORTHVAR>Mt</ORTHVAR><REGISTERLAB>written abbreviation</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>used in the names of mountains. Don’t say ‘Fuji Mountain’ – say <DEFBOLD>‘Mount Fuji’</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Mount Everest</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cliff</EXP><DEF>the steep side of an area of land, often next to the sea</DEF><THESEXA>the white cliffs of Dover</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>precipice</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a very steep and dangerous cliff</DEF><THESEXA>They were standing on the edge of a precipice. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>crag</EXP><DEF>a high steep rock or mountain</DEF><THESEXA>An eagle sailed over the high crags. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ridge</EXP><DEF>a long narrow area of high ground, especially at the top of a mountain</DEF><THESEXA>I could see a group of climbers high up on a ridge.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>knoll</EXP><DEF>a small round hill</DEF><THESEXA>a grassy knoll</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>volcano</EXP><DEF>a mountain with a large hole at the top, through which <NonDV><REFHWD>lava</REFHWD></NonDV><GLOSS>hot liquid rock</GLOSS> is sometimes forced out</DEF><THESEXA>the eruption of a volcano</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>summit</EXP><DEF>the very highest point of a mountain</DEF><THESEXA>the summit of Mt Everest</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>peak</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the top of a mountain</DEF><THESEXA>the snow-covered peaks of the Himalayas</THESEXA><THESEXA>a distant peak</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>range/chain</EXP><DEF>a group of mountains or hills arranged in a line</DEF><THESEXA>the mountain range that is part of the border between Norway and Sweden</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>foothills</EXP><DEF>a group of smaller hills below a range of high mountains</DEF><THESEXA>the Sierra foothills</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3b56"><Section><Exponent><EXP>move</EXP><DEF>to go to a different place, or change the position of your body</DEF><THESEXA>Sarah moved away from the window.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Every time I move I get a pain in my left shoulder.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sway</EXP><DEF>to move slowly from one side to the other</DEF><THESEXA>The branches swayed in the wind.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Donny swayed drunkenly as he walked back to his car.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rock</EXP><DEF>to move repeatedly from waves.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wobble</EXP><DEF>to move unsteadily from side to side</DEF><THESEXA>The bike wobbled a bit, but she soon got it under control.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fidget</EXP><DEF>to keep moving or playing with your fingers, hands, feet etc, because you are bored or nervous</DEF><THESEXA>Diana fidgeted nervously with her pencil.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>squirm</EXP><DEF>to make very small movements from side to side with your body, especially because you feel uncomfortable</DEF><THESEXA>By the end of the hour, most of the children were squirming in their seats.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wriggle</EXP><DEF>to make small movements from side to side, especially in order to get into or out of something</DEF><THESEXA>The dog wriggled under the fence and escaped into the street.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She managed to wriggle into the dress, but it was much too tight.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>twitch</EXP><DEF>if part of your body twitches, it makes small movements that you cannot control</DEF><THESEXA>A muscle on Yang’s face twitched.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stir</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make a movement – used especially when describing a situation in which no one moves, or someone wakes up</DEF><THESEXA>In the village a dog barked but <COLLOINEXA>no one stirred</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The sleeping child stirred and opened her eyes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>budge</EXP><DEF>to move – used when you are trying hard to make something move, often without success</DEF><THESEXA>The piano wouldn’t budge.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to move to a different house, office etc</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>move</EXP><DEF>to move to a different house, office etc</DEF><THESEXA>They’ve moved back to Santiago.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My brother’s helping us <COLLOINEXA>move house</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>relocate</EXP><DEF>to move to a different place – used about companies, organizations, and people who work for them</DEF><THESEXA>NATO’s main headquarters relocated to Brussels.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Klein is relocating to London to head up the investment banking team. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>not moving</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>still</EXP><DEF>not moving – use this especially about people who are not moving, or about places where there is no wind</DEF><THESEXA>There was no wind and the trees were completely still.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Keep still while I tie your shoes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stationary</EXP><DEF>not moving – use this about cars, trains, or objects</DEF><THESEXA>The truck swerved and hit a stationary vehicle.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>immobile</EXP><DEF>not moving or not able to move, especially because of fear or tiredness</DEF><THESEXA>As the disease progressed, she became increasingly immobile.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>motionless</EXP><DEF>completely still – used especially in literature</DEF><THESEXA>Kemp sat motionless as the verdict was <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3aa5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>music</EXP><DEF>the sounds made by musical instruments or people singing</DEF><THESEXA>The music was really loud.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tune</EXP><DEF>the main series of musical notes in a piece of music</DEF><THESEXA>a folk song with a pretty tune</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>melody</EXP><DEF>the main series of notes in a piece of music that has many notes being played at the same time, especially in classical music</DEF><THESEXA>The soprano sang the melody.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>harmony</EXP><DEF>the chords or notes in a piece of music that support the melody</DEF><THESEXA>the rich harmonies in the symphony</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>piece</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>piece of music</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>an arrangement of musical notes – use this about music without words</DEF><THESEXA>It’s a difficult piece to play.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>composition</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a piece of music that someone has written</DEF><THESEXA>This is one of his own compositions.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>work</EXP><DEF>a piece of music, especially classical music</DEF><THESEXA>one of Mozart’s best-known works</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>track</EXP><DEF>one of the songs or pieces of music on a CD</DEF><THESEXA>the album’s title track</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>number</EXP><DEF>a piece of popular music that forms part of a concert or show</DEF><THESEXA>the show’s first number</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3a82"><Section><Exponent><EXP>must do sth</EXP><DEF>used when saying that it is very important that someone does something, because someone in authority or a rule says this, or because of the situation</DEF><THESEXA>You must be home by midnight.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He must not smoke in here.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I must remember to call her.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have to do sth</EXP><DEF>to need to do something because it is necessary or important</DEF><THESEXA>I have to go home early.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She has to talk to him first.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be obliged to do sth</EXP><DEF>to have to do something, because of a rule or law, or because of the situation you are in</DEF><THESEXA>Members of parliament are obliged to declare all their financial interests.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was obliged to admit defeat.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be required to do sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to have to do something because of a rule or law – used especially in written notices and official documents</DEF><THESEXA>New students are required to register with a doctor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have an obligation to do sth</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>be under an obligation to do sth</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to have to do something because it is the duty of someone in your position to do it, or because you have officially agreed to do it</DEF><THESEXA>The landlord is under an obligation to keep the building in good repair.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be forced/compelled to do sth</EXP><DEF>to have to do something that you do not want to do, because you are in a situation that makes it impossible to avoid</DEF><THESEXA>She was forced to retire early due to ill health.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3a78"><Section><Exponent><EXP>mysterious</EXP><DEF>difficult to explain or understand, and often strange or slightly understand, but also often attractive or interesting – used especially about people, their expressions, or things they say</DEF><THESEXA>She gave him an enigmatic smile.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an enigmatic remark</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cryptic</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>having a meaning that is difficult to understand, and is not expressed in a clear direct way – used <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3a5e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>naked</EXP><DEF>not wearing any clothes or not covered by clothes – used especially when this seems rather shocking</DEF><THESEXA>a naked man</THESEXA><THESEXA>He got out of bed naked, and answered the telephone.</THESEXA><THESEXA>his naked chest</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have nothing on/not have anything on</EXP><DEF>to not be wearing any clothes. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-39c1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>nervous</EXP><DEF>worried or a little frightened about something and unable to relax</DEF><THESEXA>Kelly <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3982"><Section><Exponent><EXP>never</EXP><DEF>not at any time, or not once</DEF><THESEXA>Ali had never seen snow before.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ll never forget that day.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>never ever</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF> used to emphasize that you mean never</DEF><THESEXA>Do you promise never ever to tell anyone else about this?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not once</EXP><DEF> used to emphasize that you are surprised or annoyed because someone never did something</DEF><THESEXA>She’s never said thank you – not once.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Jo hasn’t emailed me once in six months.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not/never for a moment</EXP><DEF>used to emphasize that you never had a particular thought or idea</DEF><THESEXA>‘Did you ever suspect he was cheating on you?’ ‘No, not for a moment.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>Not for one moment did she think it was a trap.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not/never in a million years</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF> used to say that it is completely impossible that something could ever happen</DEF><THESEXA>Dad will never agree to that – not in a million years!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>at no time</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used to emphasize that something never happened</DEF><THESEXA>At no time did anyone suggest that the drug was dangerous.</THESEXA><THESEXA>At no time were the prisoners mistreated.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-38ad"><Section><Exponent><EXP>noise</EXP><DEF>a loud sound, especially an unpleasant one</DEF><THESEXA>Traffic noise is a problem in <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3884"><Section><Exponent><EXP>normal</EXP><DEF>usual, typical, and as you would expect it to be</DEF><THESEXA>Is this cold weather </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conventional</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>of the kind that is usually used – used when comparing this with a different or special type</DEF><THESEXA>The <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-386d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>notice</EXP><DEF>to realize that something is there or that something is happening, because you can see, hear, or feel it</DEF><THESEXA>I noticed that he was rather quiet during dinner.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Children don’t seem to notice the cold.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Julie’s home.’ ‘Yes, I noticed her bicycle outside.’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>detect</EXP><DEF>to notice something that is difficult to see, hear etc because it is very small, faint, or unclear. <DEFBOLD>Detect</DEFBOLD> is used about people and machines, and is more formal than <DEFBOLD>notice</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>X-ray telescopes can now detect virtually every type of astronomical object.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He thought he detected a flicker of interest in her eyes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spot</EXP><DEF>to notice something, especially something that is difficult to see or that you see for a short time only. <DEFBOLD>Spot</DEFBOLD> is more informal than <DEFBOLD>notice</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Can you spot the difference between these two pictures?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m glad you spotted the mistake before it was too late.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>become aware/conscious</EXP><DEF>to gradually begin to notice that something is happening or is true</DEF><THESEXA>I became aware that Mum was getting a lot older.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Tessa became conscious of a feeling of guilt. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>can tell</EXP><DEF>to be able to notice something because you can see, smell, hear it etc</DEF><THESEXA>I could tell that she had been drinking.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Can you tell I’ve lost weight?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sth/sb catches your eye</EXP><DEF>used to say that you suddenly notice something or someone because they are interesting, attractive, or good at something</DEF><THESEXA>I saw this shirt in the shop window and it just caught my eye.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Clarke caught the eye of boss Bryan Robson when he scored for Newry last week.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>observe</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to notice something as a result of watching or studying it closely</DEF><THESEXA>Psychologists observed that the mice became more aggressive in smaller cages.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>perceive</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to notice something, especially that something is happening, or is true, or needs to be done</DEF><THESEXA>The company quickly perceived the need for change.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Doctors perceive that they do not have adequate time to spend with their patients.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>witness</EXP><DEF>to see something happen because you are there at the time</DEF><THESEXA>People have witnessed some great economic changes during the last ten years.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>miss</EXP><DEF>to not notice something because it is difficult to see</DEF><THESEXA>It’s easy to miss the entrance – the sign is hidden behind a tree.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-381d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>now</EXP><DEF>at this time</DEF><THESEXA>Where are you living now?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The population is much larger now than it used to be.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>currently</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>now – used when describing what a situation is like</DEF><THESEXA>The firm currently employs 113 people.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Currently, the starting salary is around £20,000.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>at the moment</EXP><DEF>now – used when talking about a short period of time, after which the situation is likely to change</DEF><THESEXA>I think she’s at lunch at the moment – can I ask her to call you back?</THESEXA><THESEXA>At the moment I’m working in a restaurant, but I’m hoping to go to college.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>at present/at the present time</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>presently</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>now – used when you do not expect something that is true now to be permanent</DEF><THESEXA>Many areas are inaccessible at present due to heavy snow.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The official currency is the crown, presently about 30 to the dollar.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>for the time being</EXP><DEF>now – used when a situation is likely to change, especially because an arrangement is only temporary</DEF><THESEXA>You can stay here for the time being, until you find a flat.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-380e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>obey</EXP><DEF>to do what someone in authority tells you to do, or what a law or rule says you must do</DEF><THESEXA>You must obey a senior officer at all times.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If everyone obeys the rules of the road, safety is much improved.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>do what sb says</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to do what someone has advised or ordered you to do. In informal and everyday situations, people usually say <DEFBOLD>do what sb says</DEFBOLD> rather than <DEFBOLD>obey</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>I did what you said but the car still hasn’t started.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My husband never does what I say.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>do what you are told/do as you are told</EXP><DEF>to do what your parent or teacher says you must do – used especially about children</DEF><THESEXA>At school, we expect the pupils to do what they are told.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do as you’re told and sit down.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>follow sb’s orders/instructions/advice</EXP><DEF>to do what someone says you should do, or advises you to do</DEF><THESEXA>You must follow your doctor’s orders.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I followed the manufacturer’s instructions.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I hope she’ll follow my advice.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>abide by sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to accept and obey a rule, law, agreement etc</DEF><THESEXA>Players have to abide by the rules of the game.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>respect</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to obey the law or customs of a place, even when you do not agree with them, because this is a necessary part of living in a society</DEF><THESEXA> Smokers should respect the law, and only smoke in the privacy of their own homes.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3802" fullpage="yes"><Section><Exponent><EXP>obvious</EXP><DEF>something that is obvious is very easy to notice or understand – used especially when you are surprised that other people cannot notice it</DEF><THESEXA>There is an obvious connection between the two murders.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was obvious that something was wrong.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>clear</EXP><DEF>easy to notice that something is true, so that you feel sure about it and have no doubts</DEF><THESEXA>It was clear to me that my father was dying.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are clear signs of an economic recovery.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>noticeable</EXP><DEF>very easy to notice, especially because you can see, hear, smell, or feel something</DEF><THESEXA>Steroid drugs cause a noticeable change in someone’s behaviour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Road noise tends to be more noticeable in certain weather conditions.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conspicuous</EXP><DEF>very easy to notice, because of being different from things around them</DEF><THESEXA>a conspicuous white spot on the bird’s wings</THESEXA><THESEXA>She tried to make herself look less conspicuous.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Don’t leave your valuables in a conspicuous place.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unmistakable</EXP><DEF>extremely obvious, so that you cannot possibly confuse something with something else</DEF><THESEXA>the unmistakable sound of gunfire</THESEXA><THESEXA>The flower’s scent is unmistakable. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>self-evident</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>facts, ideas etc that are self-evident  are obvious and true, although some people may not accept them or know about them</DEF><THESEXA>The facts in this case are self-evident and cannot be denied.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We hold these truths to be self-evident <GLOSS>we believe that they are obvious and true – from the American Declaration of Independence</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blatant</EXP><DEF>use this about something that someone does which is clearly bad, but which they do not seem to be ashamed of</DEF><THESEXA>a blatant lie</THESEXA><THESEXA>The bill is a blatant attempt to limit our right to free speech. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>can tell</EXP><DEF>to know that something must be true because you can see signs that show this</DEF><THESEXA>Even though it was dark, she could tell it was him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>How can you tell if you’ve broken your arm? </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-37bc"><Section><Exponent><EXP>often</EXP><DEF>used when saying that something happens many times</DEF><THESEXA>Gary was often bad-tempered in the morning.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We often meet up for a drink after work.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>a lot</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>often</DEF><THESEXA>I forget to take their medicine.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>regularly</EXP><DEF>often, especially at regular intervals</DEF><THESEXA>Buses run regularly every ten it</DEF><THESEXA>Dave tends to arrive late so don’t worry.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Girls tend to be better at languages than boys.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have a tendency to do sth</EXP><DEF>to often do something, especially something that is not good, and be more likely to do it than other people or things</DEF><THESEXA>Some people have a tendency to put on weight.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This type of cloth has a tendency to shrink.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be inclined to do sth</EXP><DEF>to do something fairly often or be fairly likely to do it, especially because this is part of your character</DEF><THESEXA>Teenage boys are inclined to take risks.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was inclined to giggle in class.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have a habit of doing sth</EXP><DEF>used when saying that something has happened many times before and is likely to happen again</DEF><THESEXA>Ben had a habit of coming home drunk.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Life has a habit of surprising people.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be prone to sth</EXP><DEF>to be more likely to have problems</DEF><THESEXA>Some older computer systems were prone to go wrong.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The fitter <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3780"><Section><Exponent><EXP>open</EXP><DEF>used about a door, window, container, package, letter, your eyes, or your mouth</DEF><THESEXA>I opened the door quietly.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was nervous about opening the letter.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Open your mouth wide.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unlock</EXP><DEF>to open a door, drawer, box etc with a key</DEF><THESEXA>You need a key to unlock the safe.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unscrew</EXP><DEF>to open a lid on a bottle, container etc by turning it</DEF><THESEXA>I carefully unscrewed the lid of the jar.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>force open</EXP><DEF>to open a drawer, window, cupboard etc using force</DEF><THESEXA>The door was locked so we had to force it open.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unwrap</EXP><DEF>to open a package by removing the paper that covers it</DEF><THESEXA>The children were busy unwrapping their Christmas presents.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unfasten/undo</EXP><DEF>to make something no longer fastened or tied, for example a seat belt or a piece of clothing</DEF><THESEXA>He unfastened the top button of his shirt.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was so full I had to undo my belt.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3771"><Section><Exponent><EXP>operation</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>the process of cutting into someone’s body to repair or remove a part that is damaged</DEF><THESEXA>He had an operation to remove a blood clot from his brain.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>procedure</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>an operation performed in a particular way on a particular part of the body. <DEFBOLD>Procedure</DEFBOLD> is used especially in medical English, for example by doctors and people who work in hospitals. In everyday English people usually say <DEFBOLD>operation</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>a common surgical procedure </THESEXA><THESEXA>The procedure normally only takes a few minutes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>transplant</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>an operation to remove an organ from one person’s body and put it into another person’s body</DEF><THESEXA>There is a shortage of donors for heart transplants.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She had to have a kidney transplant. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>surgery</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>medical treatment involving an operation</DEF><THESEXA>She required surgery on her right knee.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was taken in for surgery.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>plastic surgery</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>surgery to improve someone’s appearance</DEF><THESEXA>The singer has just had plastic surgery on her nose.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bypass</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>an operation to make someone’s blood flow past a part of their heart that is blocked or damaged, often using a <NonDV><REFHWD>vein</REFHWD></NonDV> that has been removed from another part of their body</DEF><THESEXA>He went into hospital for a triple heart bypass.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>skin graft</EXP><DEF>an operation to repair an area of skin that has been badly damaged, especially using skin from another part of someone’s body</DEF><THESEXA>Her leg was severely burned and may require a skin graft. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-375b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>opinion</EXP><DEF>what you think about something or someone</DEF><THESEXA>People didn’t usually ask his opinion about anything.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She has rather a low opinion of young people.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>view</EXP><DEF>your opinion about a serious or important issue</DEF><THESEXA>She has strong views about education.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>In my view</COLLOINEXA>, footballers are paid too much.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>point of view</EXP><DEF>your opinion, especially when this is influenced by the situation you are in</DEF><THESEXA>From a farmer’s point of view, foxes are a nuisance.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It all depends on your point of view.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>position</EXP><DEF>the official opinion of a government, political party, or someone in authority</DEF><THESEXA>The prime minister has made his position perfectly clear.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The party has changed its position on nuclear weapons.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>attitude</EXP><DEF>your opinions and feelings about something or someone, especially when this shows in your behaviour</DEF><THESEXA>My parents and I have very different attitudes to life.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was his attitude to women that shocked me. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>school of thought</EXP><DEF>an opinion that one group of people have about a subject, especially when this is different from that of another group</DEF><THESEXA>There is one school of thought that says that coffee is addictive and is therefore a bad thing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are two schools of thought on this.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3748"><Section><Exponent><EXP>oppose</EXP><DEF>to think that a plan or idea is wrong, and to try to prevent it from happening or succeeding</DEF><THESEXA>Hundreds of people wrote to oppose the scheme.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The other parties opposed the bill.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be against sth/be opposed to sth</EXP><DEF>to think that something is wrong and should not be allowed</DEF><THESEXA>I used to be against the death penalty.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Most people are opposed to the privatization of the city’s public transportation system.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not agree with sth</EXP><DEF>to think that something is wrong</DEF><THESEXA>I don’t agree with training animals for entertainment.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>object</EXP><DEF>to say or feel that something is wrong</DEF><THESEXA>No one objected to the proposals.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She strongly objects to being told what to do.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>resist</EXP><DEF>to try to prevent a change from happening, or prevent yourself from being forced to do something</DEF><THESEXA>People often resist change, when they have been used to doing things in a certain way for a long time.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She resisted all attempts to teach her how to cook.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>anti-</EXP><DEF>opposed to something – used in adjectives</DEF><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>anti-war </COLLOINEXA>protesters</THESEXA><THESEXA>an <COLLOINEXA>anti-slavery </COLLOINEXA>movement</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3726"><Section><Exponent><EXP>opposition</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>strong disagreement with or protest against something</DEF><THESEXA>Opposition to the proposed scheme was widespread.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The plan met with stiff opposition <GLOSS>strong opposition</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>objection</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF> a reason you give for opposing an idea or plan</DEF><THESEXA>My main objection is that it will cost too much money.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A number of objections were raised.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>antagonism</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>a strong feeling of opposition to something, or dislike for someone, which is shown in your behaviour, and has often existed for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>his own antagonism to any form of authority</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is no antagonism towards tourists on the island.</THESEXA><THESEXA>people’s antagonism to communism</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hostility</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>angry remarks or behaviour that show someone opposes something very strongly, or dislikes someone very much</DEF><THESEXA>The announcement was greeted with hostility from some employees.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is a certain amount of hostility towards the police among local people.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>antipathy</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a strong feeling of opposition and dislike for someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>his fundamental antipathy to capitalism</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her long-standing antipathy to Herr Kohl was well-known.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Darwin shared Lyell's antipathy to the idea that the same species could appear independently in different areas.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3716"><Section><Exponent><EXP>optimistic</EXP><DEF>believing that good things will happen</DEF><THESEXA>I’m optimistic about our chances <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-36fa"><Section><Exponent><EXP>or</EXP><POS>conjunction</POS><DEF>used between two possibilities or choices, or before the last one in a list of possibilities or choices</DEF><THESEXA>Don’t get the chemicals on your hands or your clothes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Payment can be made by cash, cheque  or  credit card.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>alternatively</EXP><POS>adv</POS><DEF>used when giving another choice apart from the one you have already mentioned</DEF><THESEXA>Use a pair of scissors or, alternatively, a very sharp knife.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You can go up into the mountains. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP> the birthplace of sth</EXP><DEF>the place where something first started to exist</DEF><THESEXA>New Orleans is the birthplace of jazz.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Ethiopia,
