Ages.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3644"><Section><Exponent><EXP>outside</EXP><DEF>not inside a building, but usually close to it</DEF><THESEXA>Why don’t you go outside and play?</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was standing outside, smoking a cigarette.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>out</EXP><DEF>outside – used mainly before the following prepositions or adverbs</DEF><THESEXA>We slept <COLLOINEXA>out under </COLLOINEXA> the stars.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Don’t stand <COLLOINEXA>out in</COLLOINEXA> the rain – come inside.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I can hear somebody <COLLOINEXA>out there</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>outdoors/out of doors</EXP><DEF>away from buildings and in the open air – used especially when talking about pleasant or healthy things you do outside</DEF><THESEXA>In the summer, we like to eat outdoors.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Kids should spend as much time out of doors as possible.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in the open air</EXP><DEF>outside where the air is fresh</DEF><THESEXA>It’s good to exercise in the open air.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Leave the wood to dry slowly in the open air.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>al fresco</EXP><DEF>outside – used when talking about eating outside</DEF><THESEXA>We prefer to dine al fresco.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-362e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>owe</EXP><DEF>to need to pay someone for something they have sold to you or because they have lent you money – used especially when talking about particular amounts of money</DEF><THESEXA>You owe me $50. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The football club still owes £2.3 million.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be in debt</EXP><DEF>to owe money, especially to several different companies – used when talking about a person’s financial situation</DEF><THESEXA>She hates being in debt.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We started getting deeper and deeper into debt.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be overdrawn</EXP><DEF>to owe an amount of money to your bank because you have taken more money out of an account than you have put in</DEF><THESEXA>He received a letter saying he was overdrawn.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The bank charged me even though I was only a few pence overdrawn.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have an overdraft</EXP><DEF>to be overdrawn, with the agreement of your bank</DEF><THESEXA>When I finished college I had a big overdraft.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be in the red</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to have spent more money than you have</DEF><THESEXA>The firm is £190,000,000 in the red.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-361f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>own</EXP><DEF>if you own something, it legally belongs to you</DEF><THESEXA>They live in a flat but they don’t own it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The land is owned by farmers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a privately owned plane</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have</EXP><GRAM>not in passive</GRAM><DEF>to own something – used when focussing on the fact that someone has the use of something, rather than the fact that they legally own it</DEF><THESEXA>How many students have a cell phone?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I wish I had a sports car.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>possess</EXP><GRAM>not in passive</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> to own something</DEF><THESEXA>It is illegal to possess a firearm in Britain.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t even possess a smart suit!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>belong to sb/sth</EXP><GRAM>not in passive</GRAM><DEF> if something belongs to you, you own it</DEF><THESEXA>The ring belonged to my grandmother.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hold</EXP><DEF>to own shares in a company</DEF><THESEXA>One man holds a third of the company’s shares.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be the property of sb/sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be owned by someone – written on signs, labels etc</DEF><THESEXA>This camera is the property of the BBC.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3610"><Section><Exponent><EXP>pain</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>the feeling when part of your body hurts</DEF><THESEXA>A broken leg can cause a lot of pain.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He felt a sharp pain in his chest.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ache</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a continuous pain, especially one that is not very bad. Most commonly used in compounds such as <DEFBOLD>headache</DEFBOLD>, <DEFBOLD>toothache</DEFBOLD>, and <DEFBOLD>backache</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>I felt an ache in my back after decorating all day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Driving gives me a <COLLOINEXA>headache</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve got <COLLOINEXA>stomach ache</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you have <COLLOINEXA>earache</COLLOINEXA>?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>twinge</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a sudden slight pain that comes and then disappears quickly</DEF><THESEXA>When I bent down I felt a twinge in my back.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>discomfort</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an uncomfortable feeling in your body, or a slight pain</DEF><THESEXA>The procedure takes five minutes and only causes slight discomfort.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>agony</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>a feeling of great pain, or a situation in which you feel a lot of pain</DEF><THESEXA>the agony of childbirth</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was <COLLOINEXA>in agony</COLLOINEXA> by the time I got to the hospital.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>It was agony</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>very painful</GLOSS>getting up out of bed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>suffering</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>continuous physical or mental pain, which makes someone very unhappy</DEF><THESEXA>I just wanted someone to put an end to my suffering.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the suffering of the earthquake victims</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-35de"><Section><SECHEADING>when a part of your body hurts </SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>painful</EXP><DEF>used for describing a part of the body that hurts</DEF><THESEXA>Her leg is still painful where she broke it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>painful joints</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was suffering from painful stomach cramps.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tender</EXP><DEF>painful when touched</DEF><THESEXA>The skin around the wound is very tender.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stiff</EXP><DEF>painful and difficult to move</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve got a stiff neck.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her body was stiff from sitting in the car for so long.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sore</EXP><DEF>painful as a result of a wound, infection, or too much exercise</DEF><THESEXA>sore muscles</THESEXA><THESEXA>a sore throat</THESEXA><THESEXA>The wound was sore and red.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-35ce"><Section><Exponent><EXP>pale</EXP><DEF>a pale colour is very light</DEF><THESEXA>He has very pale blue eyes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a flower with pale green leaves</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>light</EXP><DEF>a light colour is not dark</DEF><THESEXA>His T-shirt was light green.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a light blue sweater</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pastel</EXP><DEF>pastel colours have a lot of white in them</DEF><THESEXA>The girls wore pastel pink sundresses.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>faded</EXP><DEF>light in colour because of being washed many times or affected by the sun</DEF><THESEXA>The curtains were old and faded.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a pair of faded jeans</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-35b1"><Section><SECHEADING>places where you can park a car</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>parking space/place</EXP><DEF>a space in a street, car park etc where a car can be left</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>garage</EXP><DEF>a building for keeping a car in, usually next to or attached to a house</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>parking garage</EXP><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><DEF>a building near or under a public place where cars can be parked</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>car park</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><AmEVariant><LEXVAR>parking lot</LEXVAR></AmEVariant><DEF>an area where cars can be parked</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>multistorey car park</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a building with many levels where cars can be parked</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>carport</EXP><DEF>a shelter for a car which has a roof but no sides</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>drive</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><AmEVariant><LEXVAR>driveway</LEXVAR></AmEVariant><DEF>the hard area between your house or garage and the street. on which you can leave a car</DEF></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3593"><Section><Exponent><EXP>part</EXP><DEF>something that together with other things forms a whole</DEF><THESEXA>It looks like part of a car engine.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The best part of the movie was the end.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The hardest part is getting started.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bit</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a small part of something</DEF><THESEXA>Some bits of the book are really funny.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I didn’t read the bottom bit.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>piece</EXP><DEF>one of several different parts that you join together to make something</DEF><THESEXA>One of the pieces of the jigsaw puzzle was missing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The furniture comes in several pieces.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>component</EXP><DEF>a part of a machine or process</DEF><THESEXA>The company supplies engine components for trucks.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Education is a major component in a child’s growth and development.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>section</EXP><DEF>a part of something that is clearly different and separate from other parts</DEF><THESEXA>The test is divided into two sections.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the reference section of the library</THESEXA><THESEXA>the string section of the orchestra</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>chapter</EXP><DEF>one of the numbered parts that a book is divided into</DEF><THESEXA>The opening chapter of the book sets the scene.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scene</EXP><DEF>one of the parts that a film or play is divided into</DEF><THESEXA>Some scenes had to be cut because they were too violent.</THESEXA><THESEXA>act 1, scene 2 of the play</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>episode</EXP><DEF>a part of a story on the television or the radio, which is told in separate parts</DEF><THESEXA>I missed last week’s episode.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3578"><Section><Exponent><EXP>participate </EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to do an activity together with other people</DEF><THESEXA>He always participates in classroom activities. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The people should have the right to participate in discussions about their future.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are many ways people can participate in sports.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Who will be participating?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take part</EXP><DEF>to participate in something. <DEFBOLD>Take part</DEFBOLD> is less formal that <DEFBOLD>participate</DEFBOLD> and is more common in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>Nearly 500 teams took part in the competition.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was asked to take part in a TV debate on drugs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Anyone who is over 18 can take part.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>play a part/role</EXP><DEF>to take part in something in a useful way</DEF><THESEXA>Everyone can play a part in improving the security of their neighborhood.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Schneider played a key role in getting the organization started.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be involved</EXP><DEF>to take part in an activity in some way</DEF><THESEXA>He has denied being involved in the murder.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a big project and many people were involved.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be active in sth</EXP><DEF>to actively take part in the work of an organization such as a political group or church</DEF><THESEXA>He is very active in the church’s work with homeless people.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She is no longer active in politics.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>compete</EXP><DEF>to take part in a competition or race</DEF><THESEXA>Athletes from all over the world will be competing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She’s hoping to compete in the Olympic Games.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3541" fullpage="yes"><Section><Exponent><EXP>party</EXP><DEF>a social event when a lot of people meet together to enjoy themselves by party.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get-together</EXP><DEF>an informal party</DEF><THESEXA>Christmas is the perfect time for a family for a woman who is getting married and her female friends</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stag night</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>bachelor party</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a social <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.46254e.11c6fa7611a.-45a5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>in the past</EXP><DEF>at a time before now, especially a long time ago</DEF><THESEXA>In the past, most children didn’t go to school at all.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We had a big argument, but it’s all in the past now.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in those days/in the old days</EXP><DEF>a long time ago in your life, or in your parents’ or grandparents’ lives, when things were different</DEF><THESEXA>£5 was a lot of money in those days.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In the old days, only very rich people had cars.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>at one time</EXP><DEF>used for saying that something was true in the past, but is not now</DEF><THESEXA>At one time there were six schools in the village.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I would have agreed with you at one time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>back in the day</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when you are talking about a time in the past, especially one that you remember as being very good</DEF><THESEXA>I loved Blondie back in the day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Back in the day, everyone would meet at the boys’ club.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-34ff"><Section><Exponent><EXP>pattern</EXP><DEF>a regularly repeated arrangement of shapes, colours, or lines on a surface</DEF><THESEXA>Some of his pictures use patterns of dots.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The lines formed a regular pattern.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>design</EXP><DEF> a pattern used for decorating something, especially cloth or paper</DEF><THESEXA>curtains with a floral design <GLOSS>based on flowers</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>markings</EXP><DEF> the coloured patterns and shapes on an animal’s fur, feathers, or skin</DEF><THESEXA>the tiger’s black and orange markings</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>motif</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> a single shape that is regularly repeated to form a pattern which decorates something</DEF><THESEXA>A triangle within a square is a very common motif in Muslim art.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The shield motif in the frescoes at Knossos is a religious, not a military, symbol.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-34f0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>pause</EXP><DEF>to stop speaking or stop doing something for a very short time before starting again. <DEFBOLD>Pause</DEFBOLD> is used especially in written descriptions. In everyday spoken English, people usually just say <DEFBOLD>stop</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>She paused at the bottom of the stairs and looked up at the clock.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He paused, waiting for Larry to say something.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hesitate</EXP><DEF>to stop for a moment and wait before doing something, because you feel unsure or nervous about it</DEF><THESEXA>She hesitated for a moment before replying.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have/take a break</EXP><DEF>to stop working for a short time in order to rest, eat etc</DEF><THESEXA>We’re all getting tired. Let’s take a break for ten minutes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>adjourn</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if a meeting or court adjourns or is adjourned, it stops for a short time</DEF><THESEXA>If there are no more questions, the committee will adjourn until tomorrow morning.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The trial was adjourned because one of the defendants was ill.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take five</EXP><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to stop for a short time in order to rest</DEF><THESEXA>Let’s take five and get some coffee.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>break off</EXP><DEF>to suddenly stop speaking, especially because you see, hear, or think of something</DEF><THESEXA>He broke off his conversation when he saw Mary running towards him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She broke off and looked embarrassed, then said, ‘I’ll explain later.’ </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-34c0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>pay</EXP><DEF>to give someone money for something you are buying from them, or a service they are providing</DEF><THESEXA>I paid a lot of money for that computer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You have to pay to park your car.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>meet the cost of sth</EXP><DEF>to pay for something for someone else, or to provide the money needed to do something</DEF><THESEXA>We will meet the cost of your travelling expenses.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The cost will be met from public donations.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>foot the bill</EXP><DEF>to pay for something for someone else, especially when you do not want to, or do not think that you should</DEF><THESEXA>As usual, the taxpayer will have to foot the bill.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pick up the tab</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to pay for something</DEF><THESEXA>My company will pick up the tab for all moving costs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fork out/shell out</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to pay a lot of money for something because you have to and not because you want to</DEF><THESEXA>He had to fork out £500 to get his car fixed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Fans are having to shell out roughly $65 per seat for football games.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>settle the bill</EXP><DEF>to pay the bill after eating a meal, staying in a hotel etc</DEF><THESEXA>She went down to the hotel lobby to settle the bill.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to pay a particular amount of money for something – used especially when saying how much you are willing to pay</DEF><THESEXA>How much will you give me for the car?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ll give you $50 for the lot <GLOSS>for everything</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sth is on sb</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when saying that someone else will pay for your meal, drinks etc</DEF><THESEXA>Order whatever you like – this is on me!</THESEXA><THESEXA>The drinks<COLLOINEXA> are on the house</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>the bar, restaurant etc will let you have them for free</GLOSS>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Put your money away – the drinks are on us.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3493"><Section><Exponent><EXP>income</EXP><DEF>money that you receive from working, investments etc</DEF><THESEXA>families on a low income</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>salary</EXP><DEF> the pay that professional people such as teachers or lawyers earn every year</DEF><THESEXA>a salary of $65,000 a year</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wages</EXP><DEF> the pay that someone earns every hour or every week</DEF><THESEXA>Her wages barely cover the rent.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bonus</EXP><DEF>money added to someone’s pay, as a reward for good work or as a reward when the company does well</DEF><THESEXA>The company pays an attendance bonus – if you go 30 days without being late or absent, you get the bonus.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>earnings</EXP><DEF> all the money that you earn by working</DEF><THESEXA>In a good year, a bonus can double an executive’s earnings.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-347c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>payment</EXP><DEF>an amount of money that you pay for something, especially when it is only one part of the total amount you have to pay</DEF><THESEXA>They have a monthly car payment of £220.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>instalment</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><AmEVariant><LEXVAR>installment</LEXVAR></AmEVariant><DEF>a regular payment you make to pay back money that you have borrowed or to pay for things that you have already received</DEF><THESEXA>I borrowed $2,000, which was to be paid back in monthly installments of $250.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>deposit</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>down payment</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>part of the cost of something that you pay before you get it, so that it will not be sold to anyone else</DEF><THESEXA>They used the money they inherited as a down payment on a house.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The hotel asks for a $20 deposit to reserve a room.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>subscription</EXP><DEF>an amount of money you pay, usually once a year, to receive copies of a newspaper or magazine</DEF><THESEXA>A subscription to the magazine is $52 a year.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tip</EXP><DEF>a small amount of money that you give someone, for example a waitress or taxi driver, in addition to paying for a service they have given you</DEF><THESEXA>I usually leave a 10% tip.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>premium</EXP><DEF>the amount you pay for insurance each year</DEF><THESEXA>your monthly life insurance premiums</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3456"><Section><Exponent><EXP>peace</EXP><DEF>a situation in which there is no war or fighting</DEF><THESEXA>There has been peace in the region for six years now.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>truce</EXP><DEF>an agreement to stop fighting or arguing with each other for a limited period of time</DEF><THESEXA>The rebels have ended a 17-month-old truce.</THESEXA><THESEXA> He called for a truce between Israeli and Palestinian forces. </THESEXA><THESEXA>There was an uneasy truce between Lily and Stephen at dinner.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ceasefire</EXP><DEF>an agreement to stop fighting for a limited period of time, especially in order to discuss making peace</DEF><THESEXA>The two sides agreed to a ceasefire which would come into force immediately. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>armistice</EXP><DEF>a formal agreement to stop fighting a war and to discuss making peace</DEF><THESEXA>The Korean armistice was signed in July 1953.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>calm</EXP><DEF>a situation in which there is no violence, argument, or protest</DEF><THESEXA>Attempts by the police to restore calm in the area failed.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-342b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>people</EXP><DEF>people in general</DEF><THESEXA>Most people know that smoking causes lung cancer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>People are the same everywhere.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>folk</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>people</DEF><THESEXA>Louisa's parents were country folk and believed very much in herbal remedies. </THESEXA><THESEXA>They looked like two ordinary folk.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are still folk around here who remember the old days.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the public</EXP><DEF>ordinary people, not people who belong to the government or are members of a particular company or organization</DEF><THESEXA>This information should be made available to the public. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>population</EXP><DEF>all the people who live in a particular area</DEF><THESEXA>The majority of the population were farmers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The city has a population of 11 million.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the human race</EXP><DEF>all the people in the world, considered as a group</DEF><THESEXA>the origins of the human race</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mankind</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>humankind</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>people in general – used especially when talking about their history or development, or how something affects their existence. Some people think that the word <DEFBOLD>mankind</DEFBOLD> seems to make women seem unimportant, and prefer to use <DEFBOLD>humankind</DEFBOLD> instead</DEF><THESEXA>Travelling into space was a great advance for mankind.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>populace</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the people who live in a country – a very formal use</DEF><THESEXA>It is a country where 80% of the populace live in poverty.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3416"><Section><SECHEADING>without anything wrong</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>perfect</EXP><DEF>used about something that is very good in every way, and could not be better</DEF><THESEXA>We had a wonderful vacation - the weather was perfect.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The meal was absolutely perfect.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She has perfect teeth.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>flawless/faultless</EXP><DEF>without any mistakes or faults</DEF><THESEXA>His English was flawless.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a faultless performance</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>impeccable</EXP><DEF>so good that you cannot find anything wrong with it – used especially about someone’s behaviour, taste, or experience</DEF><THESEXA>The food was excellent and the service impeccable.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a man of impeccable manners, charm, and sensibility</THESEXA><THESEXA>her mother's impeccable taste in clothing</THESEXA><THESEXA>The commission was composed of economists with impeccable credentials <GLOSS>very impressive qualifications and experience, which are impossible to criticize</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unspoiled</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>unspoilt</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>an unspoiled area has not been changed or had ugly buildings, roads etc built on it</DEF><THESEXA>a beautiful area of unspoiled countryside </THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>most suitable</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>perfect</EXP><DEF>completely suitable for something or someone</DEF><THESEXA>It’s the perfect place to relax after a hard day at work.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a perfect day for going to the beach.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I think she would be perfect for him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ideal</EXP><DEF> very suitable and exactly what you want – often used about someone or something that you imagine, but which does not really exist</DEF><THESEXA>What would be your ideal job?</THESEXA><THESEXA>She still hasn’t found her ideal man.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It is the ideal place for a vacation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>just right</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF> very good or suitable in every way</DEF><THESEXA>The weather was just right for a day at the beach.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The dress is just right for you.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be just the thing/person</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-340b"><Section><SECHEADING>a period in history</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>period</EXP><DEF>a particular time in history, especially one studied as a subject</DEF><THESEXA>the late Victorian period</THESEXA><THESEXA>the interwar period</THESEXA><THESEXA>During that period many people moved from the countryside to the towns.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>time</EXP><DEF>a period of years, months, days etc</DEF><THESEXA>The 1960s were a time of great social change.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the biggest earthquake in modern times</THESEXA><THESEXA>Verdun was an important city in Roman times.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>age</EXP><DEF>a long period, especially one that represents a particular stage in the development of civilization or technology</DEF><THESEXA>the industrial age</THESEXA><THESEXA>We are now in the age of the Internet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the Stone Age<GLOSS>when people used tools made of stone</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>era</EXP><DEF>a long period that has a particular character or that is marked by particular events</DEF><THESEXA>We live in an era of breathtaking change.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the post-war era</THESEXA><THESEXA>De Gaulle’s death marked <COLLOINEXA>the end of an era</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>epoch</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈiːpɒk </PRON><AMEVARPRON>ˈepək</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>means the same as <DEFBOLD>era</DEFBOLD>, but sounds more formal and important</DEF><THESEXA>We are now entering a new epoch in human history.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the colonial epoch</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was the <COLLOINEXA>end of an epoch</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-33e5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>permanent</EXP><DEF>continuing forever, for a very long time, or for the rest of your life</DEF><THESEXA>She has permanent damage to her eyesight.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the search for a permanent solution to the problem</THESEXA><THESEXA>They’ve offered her the job on a permanent basis.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lasting</EXP><DEF>continuing for a very long time – used especially when something continues to affect someone or something for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>The experience left a lasting impression on him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Things that happen in early childhood can have a lasting effect on your life. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Shelley was to have a lasting influence on him. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Is there any hope for a lasting peace in the Middle East?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>never-ending</EXP><DEF>continuing so long that you think it will never end – used especially when something needs a lot of effort</DEF><THESEXA>Keeping the house clean is a never-ending battle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a never-ending task.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The search was never-ending.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>perpetual</EXP><DEF>a perpetual state or feeling seems to be there all the time – used especially about something that is very annoying, worrying, or tiring</DEF><THESEXA>For many working mothers, balancing the demands of children and job is a perpetual challenge.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The people live in a perpetual state of fear.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>everlasting</EXP><DEF>continuing forever – used especially in the following phrases</DEF><THESEXA>Gold is the symbol of <COLLOINEXA>everlasting love</COLLOINEXA>. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He promised them <COLLOINEXA>everlasting life</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>eternal</EXP><DEF>continuing forever – used especially in the following phrases</DEF><THESEXA>Do you believe in <COLLOINEXA>eternal life</COLLOINEXA>? </THESEXA><THESEXA>the secret of <COLLOINEXA>eternal youth</COLLOINEXA></THESEXA><THESEXA>She has my <COLLOINEXA>eternal gratitude</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>I will always be grateful to her</GLOSS>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You’re always the <COLLOINEXA>eternal optimist</COLLOINEXA>. <GLOSS>you always think that good things will happen</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-33c3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>persuade</EXP><DEF>to make someone decide to do something, especially by giving them reasons why they should do it, or asking them many times to do it</DEF><THESEXA>I persuaded her to change her mind.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you think you can persuade him to lend us the money?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>talk sb into (doing) sth</EXP><DEF>to persuade someone to do something, especially something they do not really want to do</DEF><THESEXA>Why did I let you talk me into this?</THESEXA><THESEXA>He finally talked her into going on a date with him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get sb to do sth</EXP><DEF>to make someone do something by persuading or asking them</DEF><THESEXA>If we can’t get a taxi I’ll get Joe to pick us up.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I know how to get you to kiss me.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>convince</EXP><DEF>to persuade someone that they should do something, because it is the best or the right thing to do. Some British speakers think this use is incorrect, and prefer to use <DEFBOLD>persuade</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>It would be difficult to convince him to move.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>encourage</EXP><DEF>to try to persuade someone to do something, especially because you think it will be good for them</DEF><THESEXA>Children should be encouraged to read all kinds of books.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>influence</EXP><DEF>to have an effect on what someone decides to do</DEF><THESEXA>What influences you to buy clothes?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>coax</EXP><DEF>to persuade someone to do something by talking gently and kindly</DEF><THESEXA>I tried to coax him to eat a little.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cajole</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>kəˈdʒəʊl </PRON><AMEVARPRON> -ˈdʒoʊl</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>to persuade someone to do something by praising them or making promises to them</DEF><THESEXA>He hoped to cajole her into selling her house.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put sb up to sth</EXP><DEF>to persuade or encourage someone to do something wrong or stupid</DEF><THESEXA>Who put you up to this?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dissuade</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to persuade someone not to do something</DEF><THESEXA>How do you dissuade young people from experimenting with drugs?</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-33a6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>pessimistic</EXP><DEF>expecting that bad things will happen, or that someone will be unsuccessful</DEF><THESEXA>He was pessimistic about the team’s chances of winning the championship.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a pessimistic view of human nature</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>downbeat</EXP><DEF>having an attitude that is not hopeful and not expecting success, or not expecting the situation to improve, especially the economic or political situation</DEF><THESEXA>The overall mood in the stock market is decidedly downbeat.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The chairman made some downbeat remarks about the company’s sales performance.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His assessment of the UK’s economic prospects is generally downbeat.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gloomy</EXP><DEF>not having much hope for the future</DEF><THESEXA>Environmental groups are gloomy about the future of our planet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The article painted a gloomy picture of the human rights situation in Burma.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>negative</EXP><DEF>considering only the bad qualities of a situation, person etc, and not the good ones</DEF><THESEXA>His negative attitude towards work was affecting his colleagues.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Why are you always so negative? </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fear the worst</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to expect a situation to have the worst possible result, because you know how bad the situation could be</DEF><THESEXA>I hadn’t heard any news from her for over a week, and I was starting to fear the worst.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sb’s glass is half empty</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used about people who only see the bad qualities of a situation, even when other people might see better qualities in the same situation</DEF><THESEXA>He’s one of those people whose glass is always half empty.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-1ee3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>phone</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>telephone</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><THESEXA>My wife was talking to someone on the phone.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What’s your home phone number?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The nearest telephone was in the school secretary’s office.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mobile phone</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>mobile</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>a telephone that you can carry with you, that works by using a network of radio stations to pass on signals</DEF><THESEXA>Even children as young as eight have mobile phones.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She always has her mobile switched off.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cell phone</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>cell</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>a mobile phone</DEF><THESEXA>You can reach me on my cell phone.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>voice mail</EXP><DEF>a system that records messages so that you can listen to them on your phone</DEF><THESEXA>Let me check my voice mail.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>text message</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>text</LEXVAR><LEXVAR>SMS</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>a message from someone that you can read on your mobile phone</DEF><THESEXA>I got a text from Paul.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>landline</EXP><DEF>a telephone that uses wires – used when comparing this with a mobile phone</DEF><THESEXA>Calls cost 25p from a landline, more from a mobile phone.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>receiver</EXP><DEF>the part of a telephone that you pick up to listen and talk</DEF><THESEXA>She put down the receiver and started crying.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3387"><Section><Exponent><EXP>phone</EXP><DEF>to speak to someone by telephone. <DEFBOLD>Phone </DEFBOLD>is more common in British English than American English</DEF><THESEXA>I’ll phone you tomorrow.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>call</EXP><DEF>to phone someone. <DEFBOLD>Call</DEFBOLD> is used in both British and American English</DEF><THESEXA>One of the neighbors called the police.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Call me later.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ring</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to phone someone. <DEFBOLD> Ring</DEFBOLD> is more informal than <DEFBOLD>phone</DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD>call</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>I can ring her at the office tomorrow.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give sb a call</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also </LINKWORD><LEXVAR>give sb a ring</LEXVAR></Variant><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to phone someone</DEF><THESEXA>If you ever come to Seattle, give me a call.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ll give the hospital a ring and see how he is.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>telephone</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to phone someone</DEF><THESEXA>Angry listeners telephoned the BBC to complain. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>Skype</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>skaɪp</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>trademark</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make a telephone call using special software that allows you to make calls over the Internet</DEF><THESEXA>I Skyped her last night and we spoke for hours.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3352"><Section><Exponent><EXP>photograph</EXP><DEF>a picture taken using a camera</DEF><THESEXA>Visitors are not allowed to <COLLOINEXA>take photographs</COLLOINEXA> inside the museum.</THESEXA><THESEXA>our wedding photographs</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>photo</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a photograph</DEF><THESEXA>a way of displaying your digital photos</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you want me to <COLLOINEXA>take your photo</COLLOINEXA>?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>picture</EXP><DEF>a photograph of someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>I saw her picture in the paper the other day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This is a really good picture of Sarah.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Can I <COLLOINEXA>take your picture</COLLOINEXA>?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>snap</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><Variant><LEXVAR>snapshot</LEXVAR><GEO>especially AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a photograph that you take quickly and without thinking carefully about how it will look, for example when you are on holiday</DEF><THESEXA>Patrick showed me his holiday snaps.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She showed me a snapshot of her three children.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shot</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a photograph – used especially by people who often take photographs</DEF><THESEXA>I got some great shots of Mount Fuji.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It's a lovely shot.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>print</EXP><DEF>a photograph that has been printed on photographic paper</DEF><THESEXA>a set of 4 by 6 inch prints</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-331f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>phrase</EXP><DEF>a group of words that have a particular meaning when used together, or which someone uses on a particular occasion</DEF><THESEXA>What was the phrase he used to describe her?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve never heard of the phrase before.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The president often used the phrase ‘War common English expression</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was absolutely knackered, <COLLOINEXA>if you’ll pardon the expression</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>used when you think someone might be offended by the words you have used</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>idiom</EXP><DEF>a group of words that has a special meaning which you cannot guess from the meanings of each separate word</DEF><THESEXA>‘Under <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3311"><Section><Exponent><EXP>picture</EXP><DEF>shapes, lines etc painted or drawn on a surface, especially as a piece of art, and often justice.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>realistic</EXP><DEF>if something is realistic, it seems sensible to think that it can be done or achieved</DEF><THESEXA>a realistic target</THESEXA><THESEXA>Their expectations didn’t seem very realistic.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It is important to set yourself realistic goals.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3062"><Section><Exponent><EXP>postpone</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to change the date or time of a planned event or action to a later one</DEF><THESEXA>The game was postponed because of heavy snow.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We had to postpone the trip because of my father’s illness.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The trial has been postponed until November.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put sth off</EXP><POS>phr v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to decide to do something later than you planned to do it or later than you should do it, especially because there is a problem or because you do not want to do it now</DEF><THESEXA>I really should go to the dentist, but I keep putting it off.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The concert’s been put off till next week.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The committee decided to put off making any decision until the new year.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>delay</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to not do something until something else has happened or until a more suitable time</DEF><THESEXA>He decided to delay his departure until after he’d seen the Director.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police delayed making any announcement until the girl’s relatives had been contacted.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>procrastinate</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to delay doing something that you ought to do, usually because you do not want to do it – used especially to show disapproval</DEF><THESEXA>Kerry procrastinated for as long as possible before firing anyone.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He had been procrastinating over starting the work.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be pushed/moved/put back</EXP><DEF>if an event is pushed back, someone arranges for it to be held at a later time or date than originally planned</DEF><THESEXA>Their meeting has been put back to next Thursday.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The museum’s opening date was pushed back so that safety checks could be carried out.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The game on April 1 has been moved back to April 5.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shelve</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to decide not to continue with a plan, project etc now although it may be considered again at some time in the future</DEF><THESEXA>Plans for a new stadium have been shelved for now.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The city shelved the project due to lack of funding.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put sth on ice/put sth on the back burner</EXP><DEF>to decide not to continue with a plan, project etc until a later time. These expressions are rather informal and are often used in business English</DEF><THESEXA>The project has had to be put on ice due to lack of funding.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Blears called for the discussions to be put on ice until after the elections.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The plan seems to have been put on the back burner.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3033"><Section><Exponent><EXP>pour</EXP><DEF>to make a liquid or other substance flow out of or into a container by holding it at an angle</DEF><THESEXA>Jessica was pouring more wine into her glass.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He poured me a drink.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Raj poured some water from the jug.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>drizzle</EXP><DEF>to pour a liquid onto food in small drops or in a small stream – often used in cooking instructions</DEF><THESEXA>Drizzle a little olive oil onto the bread.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Drizzle the lemon juice over the cake.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tip</EXP><DEF>to pour something out of a container by turning it upside down</DEF><THESEXA>He tipped the cup of milk into the pan.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She weighed out the flour and tipped it into the bowl.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spill</EXP><DEF>to accidentally make a liquid or other substance come out of a container</DEF><THESEXA>Someone had spilled coffee all over the carpet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The tanker was leaking, and spilled oil into the sea.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>splash</EXP><DEF>to pour a liquid quickly in an irregular stream</DEF><THESEXA>Tony hurriedly splashed some cream in his coffee.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Someone had splashed petrol over the steps and set light to them.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She splashed some perfume onto her wrists.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>decant</EXP><DEF>to pour liquid from one container into another container – a rather formal use</DEF><THESEXA>Rachel decanted the shampoo into small bottles for travelling.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He often decanted cheap whisky into bottles of more expensive brands.</THESEXA><THESEXA /></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3011"><Section><Exponent><EXP>powerful</EXP><DEF>a powerful person, organization, group etc is able to control and influence events and other people’s actions</DEF><THESEXA>the world’s most powerful nation</THESEXA><THESEXA>Parliament had become more powerful than the King.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a powerful political family</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>influential</EXP><DEF> having a lot of power to influence what happens, because people pay attention to what you say</DEF><THESEXA>a highly influential fashion designer</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s one of the most influential figures in international politics.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>strong</EXP><DEF>powerful – used about people or groups in politics who have a lot of supporters</DEF><THESEXA>The communists were particularly strong in the big industrial cities.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He is a strong voice in the state assembly.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dominant</EXP><DEF>more powerful than other people, groups, countries etc</DEF><THESEXA>She was the dominant force in women’s tennis for many years.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company has a dominant position in the market.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3002"><Section><Exponent><EXP>practise</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>practice</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I,T</GRAM><DEF>to do an activity many times in order to improve your skill or to prepare for a test</DEF><THESEXA>The course will give you a chance to practise your language skills.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was practising his golf swing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You need to practise regularly if you're going to be a good piano player.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>train</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>to practise physical movements or activities in preparation for a race or game</DEF><THESEXA>He's training for the Olympics.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rehearse</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I,T</GRAM><DEF>to practise a play, speech, or music in preparation for a public performance</DEF><THESEXA>She's in New York where she's rehearsing her new play.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The band are currently rehearsing for their world tour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>work on sth</EXP><DEF>to practise a particular skill so that your general performance improves</DEF><THESEXA>You need to work on your listening comprehension.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go/run through sth</EXP><DEF>to practise something such as a speech, play, or piece of music by reading or playing it from the beginning to the end</DEF><THESEXA>I'll just run through the speech one more time.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2ffa"><Section><Exponent><EXP>praise</EXP><DEF>to say that you admire and approve of someone or something, especially publicly</DEF><THESEXA>The film was praised by the critics when it first came out.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The report praises staff in both schools.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s important to praise children.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>congratulate</EXP><DEF>to tell someone that you think it is good that they have achieved something</DEF><THESEXA>I congratulated him on his you like something they have done</DEF><THESEXA>She complimented me on my new hairstyle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He complimented my cooking.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>flatter</EXP><DEF>to praise someone in order to please them or get something from them, even though you do not mean it</DEF><THESEXA>He had persuaded her to buy it by flattering her and being charming.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You’re just flattering me!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rave about sth</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also </LINKWORD><LEXVAR>enthuse about sth </LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>to talk motherhood.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>applaud</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to publicly praise a decision, action, idea etc</DEF><THESEXA>Business leaders applauded the decision.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A spokesperson applauded the way the festival had been run.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>commend</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to praise someone or something, especially officially</DEF><THESEXA>After the battle, Andrew Jackson commended him for ‘his courage and fidelity’.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The officers should be commended for their prompt action.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hail sb/sth as sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to describe someone or something in a way that shows you have a very good opinion of them, especially in newspapers, on television reports etc</DEF><THESEXA>The book was hailed as a masterpiece.</THESEXA><THESEXA> Journalists and music writers hailed the band as ‘the next big thing’.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He is being hailed as the new James Dean.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2fe8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>predict</EXP><DEF>to say that something will happen, before it happens</DEF><THESEXA>In the future, it may <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2fad"><Section><SECHEADING>to prepare for an event</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>prepare</EXP><DEF>to make plans or arrangements for <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2fa0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>present</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>happening or existing now</DEF><THESEXA>The present situation could get much worse.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the present century</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are no plans to build more houses here at the present time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>current</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>present – used especially about something that is not expected to stay the same for long</DEF><THESEXA>current trends in fashion</THESEXA><THESEXA>the current state of the UK economy</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>existing</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>existing or being used now – used about things or situations that you think may be changed in the future</DEF><THESEXA>The existing offices are too small.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The proposal will strengthen existing immigration laws.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>contemporary</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>used about the art, writing, ideas, society etc that belong to the present time</DEF><THESEXA>the impact of computer-generated imagery on contemporary art and design </THESEXA><THESEXA>contemporary music in Russia</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>today’s/of today</EXP><DEF>used about conditions and attitudes that exist now, when you are comparing them with those that existed in the past</DEF><THESEXA>People struggle to keep up with the pace of life in today’s world of instant communications.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the liberal ideology of today</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2f6a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>press</EXP><DEF>to push something down or against a surface with your fingers or foot</DEF><THESEXA>The doctor gently pressed her stomach.</THESEXA><THESEXA>To move forward, press the accelerator.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I pressed ‘delete’ and started again.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>squeeze</EXP><DEF>to press something inwards from both sides</DEF><THESEXA>It’s one of those balls that make a funny noise when you squeeze it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Squeeze the lemon and add the juice to the sauce.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>squash</EXP><DEF>to press something against a surface accidentally and damage it by making it flat</DEF><THESEXA>Don’t squash the tomatoes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He sat on my hat and squashed it.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>crush</EXP><DEF>to press something very hard so that it breaks into very small pieces, or is very badly damaged</DEF><THESEXA>Crush two cloves of garlic.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The front of the car was completely crushed in the crash.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mash</EXP><DEF>to press cooked vegetables or fruit until they are soft and smooth</DEF><THESEXA>Mash the potatoes while they are warm.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Babies love mashed bananas.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grind</EXP><DEF>to press something solid until it becomes a powder, using a machine or tool</DEF><THESEXA>the machine that grinds the corn</THESEXA><THESEXA>freshly ground coffee</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2f58"><Section><Exponent><EXP>pretend</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>prɪˈtend</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to deliberately behave as though something is true when it is not, either for fun or to deceive someone</DEF><THESEXA>Mark closed his eyes and pretended to be asleep.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She shouted but he pretended that he hadn’t heard <COLLOINEXA>acting the fool</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>pretending to be stupid</GLOSS> and couldn’t care less about his studies.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The boy acted all innocent and said that he knew nothing about the money. </THESEXA><THESEXA>I tried to act the good housewife when I got married.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make out sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to pretend that something is true, in order to avoid doing something or to deceive someone</DEF><THESEXA>I didn’t want to go, so I made out I was busy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She always makes out that she doesn’t have any etc</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to pretend that you are interested, surprised etc</DEF><THESEXA>‘Oh really!’ he said, trying to feign interest.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sometimes it’s best you have suffered some kind of trouble or loss</DEF><THESEXA>Although we were poor, our family always tried to keep up appearances.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She did her best to keep up appearances after her husband left her.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to pretend to be someone or something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>impersonate</EXP><DEF>to copy the way that a famous person speaks and behaves, in order to entertain people, or to pretend to have an official job, in order to trick people</DEF><THESEXA>He’s brilliant at impersonating the president.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s illegal to impersonate a police officer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pose as sb</EXP><DEF>to pretend to be someone else, especially someone in an official position, so that it is easier for you to do someone or something is not who they claim to be</DEF><THESEXA>Their advertisements are always full of people in white coats masquerading as scientists.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s not real news – it’s <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2f28"><Section><Exponent><EXP>previous</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>used when talking about a person, thing, time etc before temperature is higher than in previous years.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>last</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>used when talking about the one that you had just before now, or the one that existed just before now</DEF><THESEXA>The last apartment we lived in was much smaller than this one.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Ben’s last girlfriend was a teacher.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Beth broke up with her last boyfriend because he drank too much.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>old</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>used when talking about people you knew or things you had in the past, or about things that existed in the past and have been replaced by newer things</DEF><THESEXA>I never liked my old boss.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I saw Phil with one of my old girlfriends.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The new stadium is much bigger than the old one.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was my old maths teacher.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>preceding</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>coming just before the time or thing you have just mentioned, or before the part of a book where you are now</DEF><THESEXA>There were fewer crimes compared to the preceding year.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The author dealt with this subject in the preceding chapters of the book. </THESEXA><THESEXA>In the preceding section of the poem, Whitman is talking about how important it is to live in the present.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>former</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used to describe someone or something that used to have a particular job or position but does not anymore</DEF><THESEXA>her former husband </THESEXA><THESEXA>the former US president</THESEXA><THESEXA>Kyoto was the former capital of Japan.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ex-wife/boyfriend/soldier etc</EXP><DEF>someone who used to be someone’s wife, used to be a soldier etc, but is not anymore</DEF><THESEXA>Her dad’s an ex-policeman.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Lydia is still friends with her ex-husband.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the one before</EXP><DEF>the person or thing that existed before the one you have just mentioned</DEF><THESEXA>I didn’t enjoy Spielberg’s last film but I thought the one before was all right.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Each year, the convention is a little larger than the one <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2ef7"><Section><Exponent><EXP>price</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>the amount of money that you have to pay for something</DEF><THESEXA>The prices in that shop are rather high.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You can have a two-course meal for a special price of £9.95.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cost</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>the amount of money that you have to pay for services, activities, or things you need such as food and electricity</DEF><THESEXA>The cost of the two-day course is $1,295.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many banks are raising their borrowing costs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a sudden increase in energy costs</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>value</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>the amount of money that something is worth and that people are willing to pay if it is sold</DEF><THESEXA>The value of the painting was estimated at £500,000.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Fine wines may increase in value.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The shares have gone down in value.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2e9f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>prison</EXP><DEF>a large building where people are kept as a punishment for a crime or while they are waiting to go to court for their trial</DEF><THESEXA>He was sentenced to five years in prison.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Wandsworth Prison</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>jail</EXP><DEF>a prison, or a similar smaller building where prisoners are kept for a short time</DEF><THESEXA>This old building is the jail that Butch Cassidy escaped from in 1887.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was taken to a cell in the Los Angeles County Jail.</THESEXA><THESEXA>58% of prisoners are in jail for non-violent crimes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The strikers were harassed, beaten and put in jail for trespassing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Grover got caught for not paying his taxes and was sent to jail.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gaol</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>dʒeɪl</PRON></PronCodes><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>another way of spelling <DEFBOLD>jail</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>He spent the night in gaol.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>penitentiary</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˌpenəˈtenʃəri</PRON></PronCodes><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>a large prison for people who are guilty of serious crimes</DEF><THESEXA>the Ohio State Penitentiary</THESEXA><THESEXA>The murderer served 10 years at the  penitentiary in Stillwater.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the abandoned federal penitentiary on Alcatraz Island</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>correctional facility</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an official word for a prison</DEF><THESEXA>1,000 prisoners rioted at the North County Correctional Facility.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>detention centre</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>detention center</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a place where young people who have done something illegal are kept, because they are too young to go to prison. Also used about a place where people who have entered a country illegally are kept</DEF><THESEXA>Kevin, who had been abandoned by his mother, had been in and out of detention centres all his life.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a juvenile detention center</THESEXA><THESEXA>Harmondsworth detention centre, near Heathrow airport</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>open prison</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a prison in which prisoners have more freedom than in an ordinary prison, usually because their crimes were less serious</DEF><THESEXA>In some open prisons, prisoners are allowed to go home at weekends.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cell</EXP><DEF>a small room in a prison or police station, where someone is kept as a punishment</DEF><THESEXA>a prison cell</THESEXA><THESEXA>Conditions were poor, and there were several prisoners to one cell.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2e62"><Section><Exponent><EXP>prisoner</EXP><DEF>someone who is kept in a prison as a punishment for a crime, or while they are waiting for their <NonDV><REFHWD>trial</REFHWD></NonDV></DEF><THESEXA>Prisoners may be locked in their cells for twenty-two hours a day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a prisoner serving a life sentence for murder</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>convict</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>someone who has been found guilty of a crime and sent to a prison. <DEFBOLD>Convict</DEFBOLD> is used especially about someone who is sent to prison for a long time. It is more commonly used in historical descriptions, or in the phrase <DEFBOLD>an escaped convict</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The convicts were sent from England to Australia.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police were hunting for an escaped convict.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Low-risk convicts help to fight forest fires and clean up public lands.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>inmate</EXP><DEF>someone who is kept in a prison or a mental hospital</DEF><THESEXA>Some inmates are allowed to have special privileges.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was described by a fellow inmate as a quiet man. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>captive</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF> someone who is kept somewhere and not allowed to go free, especially in a war or fighting. <DEFBOLD>Captive</DEFBOLD> is a rather formal word which is used especially in literature</DEF><THESEXA>Their objective was to disarm the enemy and release the captives.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She <COLLOINEXA>was held captive</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>kept as a prisoner</GLOSS> in the jungle for over three years.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>prisoner of war</EXP><DEF>a soldier, member of the navy etc who is caught by the enemy during a war and kept in the enemy’s country</DEF><THESEXA>My grandad was a prisoner of war in Germany.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They agreed to release two Iranian prisoners of war.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hostage</EXP><DEF> someone who is kept somewhere as a prisoner, in order to force people to agree to do something, for example in order to get money or to achieve a political aim</DEF><THESEXA>Diplomats are continuing their efforts to secure the release of the hostages.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The US hostages were held in Tehran for over a year.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>detainee/internee</EXP><DEF>someone who is kept in a prison, usually because of their political views and often without a trial</DEF><THESEXA>In some cases, detainees at Guantanamo Bay</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2e58"><Section><Exponent><EXP>private</EXP><DEF>if something is private, you do not want most people to know about it because it concerns your feelings, your relationships etc</DEF><THESEXA>He didn’t want to discuss his private life.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What happens in the bedroom is strictly private.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>personal</EXP><DEF>relating to your private life – used especially about problems, feelings, and questions</DEF><THESEXA>They asked a lot of personal questions.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She talked about her own personal feelings.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’d rather not talk about it – it’s personal.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The singer has had a lot of problems in her personal life.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>secret</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>used about feelings and thoughts that you do not tell anyone about</DEF><THESEXA>Barclay had a secret desire to become an actor.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His secret fear was that Jenny would leave him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>intimate</EXP><DEF>very private – used about things relating to your relationships and sexual feelings</DEF><THESEXA>an intimate conversation</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many people share intimate details of their lives on the Internet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>their intimate moments together</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>innermost</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>your innermost feelings, thoughts etc are your most private ones</DEF><THESEXA>Counselling often encourages you to reveal your innermost thoughts.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be none of sb’s business</EXP><DEF>if something is none of your business, it is private and you should not ask about it</DEF><THESEXA>It’s none of your business how much I earn.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Are you married?’ ‘That’s none of your business.’</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2e47"><Section><Exponent><EXP>probably</EXP><POS>adv</POS><DEF>used when saying that you think something is true or will happen, although it is not completely definite</DEF><THESEXA>He’s probably gone home.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Rod is probably going to study law.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Is she lying?’ ‘Probably.’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>likely</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>probably going to happen or probably true</DEF><THESEXA>The most likely result is a win for the Democrats.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The price of petrol is likely to rise again this year.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>It is likely that</COLLOINEXA> we’ll lose.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>it looks as if/it looks like</EXP><DEF>used when saying that the present situation makes you think that something will probably happen or is probably true</DEF><THESEXA>It looks like Greg has been delayed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There aren’t any taxis, so it looks as if we’ll have to walk.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>may/could/might well</EXP><DEF>used when saying that you think something will probably happen or is probably true</DEF><THESEXA>He may well come back.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Take an umbrella. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2e0c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>setback</EXP><DEF> a problem that stops you from making progress</DEF><THESEXA>The space program suffered a major setback when the space shuttle, Discovery, exploded.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>snag</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF> a problem, especially one that you had not expected</DEF><THESEXA>There’s a snag – I don’t have his number.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hitch</EXP><DEF> a small problem that delays or prevents something</DEF><THESEXA>There have been a few last-minute hitches.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trouble</EXP><DEF> when something does not work in the way it should</DEF><THESEXA>The plane developed engine trouble.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hassle</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF> a situation that is annoying because it causes problems</DEF><THESEXA>Just trying to store all this stuff is a hassle.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

programmeBrEprogramAmEˈprəʊɡræmsomething that you watch on television, or listen to on the radioWhat’s your favourite television programme?I watched an interesting programme about Egypt last night.showʃəʊa programme on television or the radio, especially an informal one in which people talk together, take part in a game etca late-night talk showgame showsShe hosts a weekly call-in radio show called ‘Got a question?’documentaryˌdɒkjɑˈmentəri◂ˌdɑːk-a programme that gives you facts and information about a serious subject, such as history, science, or social problemsa documentary about homeless peoplea 50-minute television documentarysoap opera/soapˈsəʊp ˌɒpərə-ˌɑː-, səʊpa television or radio programme that tells an imaginary story about a group of people and their lives, and is often broadcast regularly for many yearsthe Australian soap opera ‘Neighbours’the huge success of television soapssitcomˈsɪtkɒm-kɑːman amusing programme in which there is a different story each week about the same group of peoplethe American sitcom ‘Friends’reality TVtelevision programmes that show real people in funny situations or situations in which they must compete with each other. Often the people are filmed continuously for weeks or monthsthe reality TV show ‘Big Brother’The trouble with reality TV is that a lot of the time it’s really boring.webcasta programme, event etc that is broadcast on the InternetUniversities may record and broadcast some lectures as webcasts.podcasta file of recorded sound and sometimes pictures that you can download from the InternetThe interview is available as a podcast.

<Thesaurus><ThesBox </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>impasse</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>æmˈpɑːs</PRON><AMEVARPRON>ˈɪmpæs</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><GRAM>singular</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a situation in
