reached an impasse.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grind to a halt</EXP><DEF>to slowly stop making any progress</DEF><THESEXA>The economy seems to be grinding to a halt.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was clear that the peace talks had ground to a halt.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2d2f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>prolong</EXP><DEF>to make something last longer</DEF><THESEXA>The issue divided the country and prolonged the civil war.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Successful treatment is prolonging people's lives.</THESEXA><THESEXA>No one wants to prolong the violence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This would only prolong the unhappiness she felt.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lengthen</EXP><DEF>to make something last longer than the usual time</DEF><THESEXA>They suggested lengthening the training period for new teachers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>New security measures have lengthened the time passengers spend in airports.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>extend</EXP><DEF>to make something that is coming to an end continue for a longer period of time – used especially about official arrangements such as <NonDV><REFHWD>contract</REFHWD><SUFFIX>s</SUFFIX></NonDV> and <NonDV><REFHWD>visa</REFHWD><SUFFIX>s</SUFFIX></NonDV></DEF><THESEXA>They agreed to extend his contract by a year.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The offer has been extended for a further fourteen days.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I applied to extend my visa.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>drag sth out</EXP><DEF> to make a situation or event last longer than necessary</DEF><THESEXA>I wish they'd make a decision and not keep dragging it out.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Each side accused the other of dragging out the negotiations.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2d20"><Section><Exponent><EXP>promise</EXP><DEF>to tell someone that you will definitely do something, or that something will happen</DEF><THESEXA>He promised to give the book back.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The organisers have promised that this year’s event will take place.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>swear</EXP><DEF>to make a very serious and sincere promise, for example in a law court</DEF><THESEXA>He had sworn to tell the truth.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I would never do that, I swear.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give sb your word</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to promise sincerely that you will do something or that something is true – often used when you are trying to persuade someone that they can trust you</DEF><THESEXA>I give you my word that your money is safe with me.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ll let you go out tonight if you give me your word that you’ll be home by 11.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>vow</EXP><DEF>to make a very serious promise, often to yourself</DEF><THESEXA>She vowed that she would never drink alcohol again.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They vowed to carry on their campaign for justice.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assure</EXP><DEF>to promise someone that something will happen or that something is true, in order to try to make them feel less worried</DEF><THESEXA>Police have assured the public that they are doing everything they can to find the killer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>guarantee</EXP><DEF>to promise that you will make sure that something will definitely happen, so that there is no question of it not happening</DEF><THESEXA>I guarantee that you will have the documents by tomorrow.</THESEXA><THESEXA>How can you guarantee that this won’t happen again?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pledge</EXP><DEF>to promise publicly or officially that you will do something, for example to give help or money</DEF><THESEXA>The government has pledged to increase overseas aid to $4 billion over the next five years.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>undertake</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make an official or legal promise to do something</DEF><THESEXA>The government undertook to keep price increases to a minimum.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The two sides undertook to respect each other’s territory.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>commit to sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make a firm and definite promise to do something important, which will affect you for a long time and which could result in serious effects if you fail</DEF><THESEXA>Bobby felt unready to commit to a romantic relationship.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A group of 11 companies has committed to developing a new passenger plane.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2d0a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>promise</EXP><DEF>a statement that you will definitely do or provide something, which may not be reliable</DEF><THESEXA>‘I’ll call you tomorrow.’ ‘Is that a promise?’</THESEXA><THESEXA>Politicians are always making promises.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pledge</EXP><DEF>a public or official promise to do a particular thing in the future</DEF><THESEXA>The Government has fulfilled at least 50% of its election pledges.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We have received pledges of help from various organizations.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>vow</EXP><DEF>a very serious promise to do something or not to do something that you choose to make</DEF><THESEXA>He made a vow never to drink alcohol again.</THESEXA><THESEXA>your marriage vows</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>oath</EXP><DEF>a formal promise, especially one that someone makes in a court of law</DEF><THESEXA>Witnesses swear a solemn oath to tell the truth.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Public officials must take an oath to support the US Constitution.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>undertaking</EXP><DEF>a serious or public promise to do something, especially something difficult which needs a lot of effort or money</DEF><THESEXA>The police have given an undertaking to reduce street crime in the city centre.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was made to sign a written undertaking that he would not go within a mile of her house.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assurance</EXP><DEF>a promise that something will happen or is true, made so that someone is less worried or more confident</DEF><THESEXA>You have my assurance that it won’t happen again.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The manager gave me his personal assurance that the goods would be delivered today.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>guarantee</EXP><DEF>a very definite promise that something will happen. A guarantee is also a formal written promise by a company to repair or replace a product free if it has a fault within a fixed period of time.</DEF><THESEXA>With any diet, there’s no guarantee of success.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m afraid I can’t give you a 100% guarantee.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Is the camera still under guarantee<GLOSS>within the period during which it can be repaired or replaced free</GLOSS>?</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2cc6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>property</EXP><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>the things that a person, organization etc owns</DEF><THESEXA>He left most of his property to his granddaughter.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They were arrested and charged with damaging school property.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>possessions</EXP><GRAM>plural</GRAM><DEF>all the things that you own or have with you at a particular time</DEF><THESEXA>He sold all his possessions and left the country.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The prisoners were allowed to have few personal possessions.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>belongings</EXP><GRAM>plural</GRAM><DEF>things you own such as clothes, books etc, especially things you take with you when travelling</DEF><THESEXA>I quickly packed a few of my belongings in an overnight bag.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>things</EXP><GRAM>plural</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>stuff</LEXVAR><GRAM>U</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>small things you own, such as clothes, books etc</DEF><THESEXA>Don’t leave your things all over the floor!</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve got so much stuff, I don’t know where to put it all.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>valuables</EXP><GRAM>plural</GRAM><DEF>things that you own that are worth a lot of money and may get stolen, for example jewellery or cameras</DEF><THESEXA>The advice from police is: if you have valuables don’t leave them in the car.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Keep valuables like TVs and computer equipment out of sight.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>personal effects</EXP><GRAM>plural</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>small things you own – used especially when there is an accident, a robbery, or the person who owns them dies</DEF><THESEXA>The insurance policy covers baggage and personal effects – up to £1,000 per person. </THESEXA><THESEXA>After Turing’s death in 1954, his mother received his personal effects, including the Order of the British Empire, awarded in 1946 for his code work.</THESEXA><THESEXA> Sections of aircraft wreckage and personal effects were scattered all around. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>assets</EXP><GRAM>plural</GRAM><DEF>houses, land, shares etc that a person or organization owns and would be able to sell if they needed money – used especially in legal or business contexts</DEF><THESEXA>Many homeowners now have assets of over £234,000 in the value of their home alone.</THESEXA><THESEXA>financial assets such as bonds and shares</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2cb2"><Section><Exponent><EXP>protect</EXP><DEF>to keep someone or something safe from harm, damage, or illness</DEF><THESEXA>Don’t worry, I’ll protect you.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The government wants to protect the environment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Eating healthily helps to protect against many diseases.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give/offer/provide protection</EXP><DEF>to protect someone from something harmful</DEF><THESEXA>Wearing a hat offers some protection from the sun.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The drug can give protection against cancer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The law provides no protection.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>guard</EXP><DEF>to protect a person, place, or object by staying near them and watching them</DEF><THESEXA>Police officers guarded the entrance to the building.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He is guarded by armed men. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>save</EXP><DEF>to protect someone or something when they are in danger of being harmed or destroyed</DEF><THESEXA>Local people are fighting to save the theatre from demolition.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Emergency aid could save millions of people who are threatened with starvation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>preserve</EXP><DEF>to keep something, especially buildings or the environment, from being harmed, destroyed, or changed too much</DEF><THESEXA>The organization works to preserve forests.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is little money for preserving historic buildings.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>safeguard</EXP><DEF>to protect something important, such as people’s rights, interests, jobs, health etc</DEF><THESEXA>The deal will safeguard 200 jobs at the factory.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Laws should do more to safeguard the rights of victims.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shield</EXP><DEF>to put something in front of something else to protect it. Also used to talk about protecting people from unpleasant situations</DEF><THESEXA>He lifted his hand to shield his eyes from the light.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They thought the public should be shielded from the truth.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shelter</EXP><DEF>to provide a place where someone or something is protected from the weather or from danger</DEF><THESEXA>The village is sheltered by a belt of trees.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His family had sheltered Jews during the war.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>harbour</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>harbor</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to help and protect someone who has done something illegal, and prevent the police from finding them</DEF><THESEXA>He is accused of harbouring suspected terrorists.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2c98"><Section><Exponent><EXP>protest</EXP><DEF>to do something to show publicly that you disagree with something – used especially when a large group of people do this together</DEF><THESEXA>Huge crowds gathered in the capital protesting against the war.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Drivers blocked roads around the capital to protest about the rising cost of fuel. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>march</EXP><EXP /><DEF>to walk in a large group from one place to another in order to protest about something</DEF><THESEXA>Hundreds of students marched through the city in protest against the employment laws.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>demonstrate</EXP><DEF>to walk or stand somewhere in a large group, in order to protest about something</DEF><THESEXA>About 200 people were demonstrating outside the US Embassy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Environmentalists have been demonstrating against plans to dump waste at sea.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>riot</EXP><DEF>to protest by behaving in a violent and uncontrolled way</DEF><THESEXA>Hundreds of workers rioted after pay negotiations broke down.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The prisoners were rioting against their appalling conditions.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>boycott</EXP><DEF>to protest about the actions of a company, country, or industry by refusing to buy something, or refusing to go to a place or event</DEF><THESEXA>They may boycott the next Olympic Games.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Shoppers are boycotting battery-farmed eggs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hold/stage a sit-in</EXP><DEF>to protest by refusing to leave a place</DEF><THESEXA>The students have been staging a sit-in to protest about overcrowding at the polytechnic.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go on a hunger strike</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>go on hunger strike</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to protest by refusing to eat</DEF><THESEXA>Maynard went on a hunger strike to protest his innocence.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2c7c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>proud</EXP><DEF>very pleased with what you, your family, or your country have achieved, or of something you own</DEF><THESEXA>I felt so proud when my son graduated from college.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Judith’s very proud of her new Ferrari.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pleased with yourself</EXP><DEF>feeling pleased because something good has happened, especially because you think you have been very clever, skilful etc</DEF><THESEXA>He was smoking a big cigar and was obviously pleased with himself.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’d made a big profit and was feeling pretty pleased with myself.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>arrogant</EXP><REGISTERLAB>disapproving</REGISTERLAB><DEF>behaving in an unpleasant and annoying way, because you think you are better or know more than other people, and that your opinions are always right</DEF><THESEXA>He was arrogant and regarded people who disagreed with him as fools.</THESEXA><THESEXA>his arrogant attitude to women </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>vain</EXP><REGISTERLAB>disapproving</REGISTERLAB><DEF>too proud of your appearance, in a way that annoys other people</DEF><THESEXA>He’s so vain – he thinks all the girls fancy him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conceited/big-headed</EXP><REGISTERLAB>disapproving</REGISTERLAB><DEF>proud of yourself because you think you are very intelligent, skilful, beautiful etc, especially without good reason and in a way that annoys people</DEF><THESEXA>Stewart’s the most arrogant conceited person I’ve ever known.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was offered a brilliant job and became incredibly big-headed overnight.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pompous</EXP><REGISTERLAB>disapproving</REGISTERLAB><DEF>thinking that you are much more important than you really are, and using very long and formal words to try to sound important</DEF><THESEXA>The clerk was a pompous little man with glasses.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a pompous speech</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>smug</EXP><REGISTERLAB>disapproving</REGISTERLAB><DEF>pleased with yourself in a quiet but annoying way because you think you are in a better position than other people</DEF><THESEXA>Milly was looking very smug about coming top of the class.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a smug expression</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>self-satisfied</EXP><REGISTERLAB>disapproving</REGISTERLAB><DEF>pleased with what you have achieved and showing it clearly in an annoying way</DEF><THESEXA>She glared angrily into his self-satisfied face.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a self-satisfied grin</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2c70"><Section><Exponent><EXP>pull</EXP><DEF>to make something or someone move in the direction that your hands are moving</DEF><THESEXA>He pulled her towards him and kissed her.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sam was pulling on his socks.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tug</EXP><DEF>to pull something suddenly with a short quick movement, often to get someone’s attention</DEF><THESEXA>‘Look,’ he said, tugging at his brother’s sleeve.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I tugged at the drawer but it wouldn’t open.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>drag</EXP><DEF>to pull something along the ground, especially because it is heavy</DEF><THESEXA>If we can’t lift the piano, we’ll have to drag it.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>haul</EXP><DEF>to pull something big and heavy using a lot of effort, especially upwards and using a rope</DEF><THESEXA>They hauled their boats further up the beach.</THESEXA><THESEXA> fishermen hauling in their nets</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>heave</EXP><DEF>to pull or lift something very heavy, especially with one movement</DEF><THESEXA>He heaved the sack of sand onto his shoulder.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>draw</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to pull something or someone gently in a particular direction</DEF><THESEXA>Lisa reached for his hand but he drew it away.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pull</EXP><DEF>to be attached to a vehicle or piece of machinery and make it move behind you in the direction you are going</DEF><THESEXA>Ten dogs were pulling a sledge over the ice.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a tractor pulling a plough</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tow</EXP><DEF>to pull a vehicle behind – used about a vehicle, a boat, or a horse pulling something using a rope or chain</DEF><THESEXA>The car in front of us was towing a caravan.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Horses were used to tow the boats along the canals.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>draw</EXP><DEF>to pull a vehicle such as a carriage – used especially about horses doing this</DEF><THESEXA>a carriage drawn by four horses</THESEXA><THESEXA>a horse-drawn cart</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2c5a"><Section><SECHEADING>types of punctuation mark</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>apostrophe</EXP><DEF>the sign (') that is used to show that one or more letters or numbers have been left out, as in <DEFBOLD>don’t</DEFBOLD>, or used before ‘s’ to show that something belongs to someone or something, as in <DEFBOLD>Mark’s dog</DEFBOLD></DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>brackets</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>parentheses</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE and BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>a pair of signs ( ) used for enclosing information that interrupts a sentence</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>colon</EXP><DEF>the sign : that is used to introduce an explanation, example, quotation etc</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>semicolon</EXP><DEF>the sign ; that is used to separate words in a list, or different parts of a sentence that can be understood separately</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>comma</EXP><DEF>the sign , that is used to separate things in a list, or between two clauses in a sentence</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hyphen</EXP><DEF>the sign – that is used to join words or syllables </DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dash</EXP><DEF>the sign — that is used to separate two closely related parts of a sentence, especially in more informal English</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>full stop</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><AmEVariant><LEXVAR>period</LEXVAR></AmEVariant><DEF>the sign . that is used to mark the end of a sentence or the short form of a word</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>exclamation mark</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>exclamation point</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>the sign ! that is used after a sentence or word that expresses surprise, anger, or excitement</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>question mark</EXP><DEF>the sign ? that is used at the end of a question</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>quotation marks</EXP><BrEVariant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>inverted commas</LEXVAR></BrEVariant><DEF>a pair of signs ‘ and ’ that are put around words, especially to show that you are quoting what someone has said</DEF></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>other marks used in writing</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>angle brackets</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a pair of signs <> used for enclosing information</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slash</EXP><DEF>a line / that is used to separate words, numbers, or letters</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>backslash</EXP><DEF>a line \ that is used to separate words, numbers, or letters</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>asterisk</EXP><DEF>the sign * that is used especially to mark something interesting or important</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>at sign</EXP><DEF>the sign @ that is used especially in email addresses</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ampersand</EXP><DEF>the sign & that means ‘and’</DEF></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2c11"><Section><Exponent><EXP>punish</EXP><DEF>to do something unpleasant to someone because they have done something wrong or broken the law</DEF><THESEXA>Drug smugglers are severely punished.</THESEXA><THESEXA> She wanted to punish him for deceiving her.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fine</EXP><DEF>to make someone pay money as a punishment</DEF><THESEXA>The company was fined for safety violations.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sentence</EXP><DEF>if a judge sentences a criminal, he or she gives them an official punishment, usually sending them to prison for a period of time</DEF><THESEXA>The judge sentenced Margolis to a year in prison.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>penalize</EXP><BrEVariant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>penalise</LEXVAR></BrEVariant><DEF>to officially punish someone, especially by taking away their right to do something or by limiting their freedom in some way</DEF><THESEXA>New laws will penalize  firms that continue to pollute the environment.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>discipline</EXP><DEF>to punish someone who has broken the rules of an organization that they belong to or work for</DEF><THESEXA>Officers are expected to discipline soldiers who do not keep their uniforms in good condition.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come down hard on sb</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to punish someone or criticize them severely</DEF><THESEXA>The judge came down hard on Harris, saying that his crime was ‘inexcusable’.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make an example of sb</EXP><DEF>to punish someone so that other people are afraid to do the same thing</DEF><THESEXA>Athletics officials felt they had to make an example of him for using banned drugs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>teach sb a lesson</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to do something in order to show someone that they must not do something again, when they have behaved very badly</DEF><THESEXA>I didn't want to hurt him - I just wanted teach him a lesson.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Maybe a night in jail will teach him a lesson.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make sb pay (for sth)</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make someone wish they had never done something, by making them suffer</DEF><THESEXA>We should make him pay for all the mischief he's caused!</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2bd9"><Section><Exponent><EXP>punishment </EXP><DEF>something that is done in order to punish someone, or the act of punishing them</DEF><THESEXA>I don’t think they deserved such a severe punishment. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The usual punishment is life in prison.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sentence</EXP><DEF>a punishment given by a judge in a court</DEF><THESEXA>He was given a long prison sentence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They asked for the maximum sentence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fine</EXP><DEF>an amount of money that you must pay as a punishment</DEF><THESEXA>I got an £80 fine for speeding.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are <COLLOINEXA>heavy fines</COLLOINEXA> for drink-driving.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>penalty</EXP><DEF>a general word for a punishment given to someone who has broken a law, rule, or agreement</DEF><THESEXA>What’s the penalty if you get caught?</THESEXA><THESEXA>He called for <COLLOINEXA>stiffer penalties</COLLOINEXA> for crimes involving guns.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the death penalty</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>capital punishment</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>the system in which people are killed as a punishment for crimes</DEF><THESEXA>If he is found guilty, he faces the death penalty.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A number of states have abolished capital punishment.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>community service</EXP><DEF>unpaid work helping other people that someone does as punishment for a crime</DEF><THESEXA>He was given a choice between doing 200 hours of community service, or a big fine.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>corporal punishment</EXP><DEF>the punishment of children by hitting them</DEF><THESEXA>I don’t agree with corporal punishment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Corporal punishment was abolished in schools in 1987.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2bc2"><Section><Exponent><EXP>purpose</EXP><DEF>the reason you do something, and the thing you want to achieve when you do it</DEF><THESEXA>What is the purpose of your visit to England?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The plant is used for medicinal purposes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>aim</EXP><DEF> what you want to achieve when you do something</DEF><THESEXA>The main aims of the project are as follows.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Their ultimate aim is to find a cure for cancer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>goal</EXP><DEF> something that you hope to achieve in the future, even though this may take a long time</DEF><THESEXA>It took Mandela over forty years to achieve his goal of a democratic South Africa.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the goal of ending child poverty</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>objective</EXP><DEF> something that you are working hard to achieve, especially in business or politics</DEF><THESEXA>The bank achieved its objective of increasing its share of the market.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The government’s long-term objective is to cut CO2 emissions by 50%.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the object of sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the specific purpose of an activity</DEF><THESEXA>The object of the game is to get as many points as possible.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The students will benefit, and that must be <COLLOINEXA>the object of the exercise</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>the main thing that you are trying to do</GLOSS>. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the point</EXP><DEF>the purpose of doing something and the reason why it is right or necessary</DEF><THESEXA>At fourteen, I couldn’t see the point of going to school.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>What’s the point</COLLOINEXA> in waiting? <GLOSS>I don’t think it is useful or necessary</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>intention</EXP><DEF>the purpose that you have in your mind when you do something</DEF><THESEXA>He kept his real intentions well hidden.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Although we made a lot of money, this wasn’t our original intention.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ends</EXP><DEF>the result that someone is trying to achieve – used especially when you disapprove of what someone is doing</DEF><THESEXA>They are using religion for political ends.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>The ends do not justify the means</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>you should not use violence, cruelty, dishonest behaviour etc to achieve your aims</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2bb3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>push</EXP><DEF>to make something or someone move by pressing them with your hands, arms etc</DEF><THESEXA>Push the door, don’t pull it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She pushed him away and walked out.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shove</EXP><DEF>to push someone or something in a rough or careless way</DEF><THESEXA>People were shoving to get to the front of the queue.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Tom shoved his suitcase under the bed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stuff</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to push something quickly and carelessly into a small space</DEF><THESEXA>She stuffed a few clothes into a bag and left.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>poke</EXP><DEF>to push someone or something with your finger or something sharp</DEF><THESEXA>I poked the snake with a stick but it was dead.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nudge</EXP><DEF>to push someone beside you gently with your elbow to get their attention</DEF><THESEXA>Toby nudged me and pointed out of the window.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>roll</EXP><DEF>to push something round or something on wheels so that it moves forward</DEF><THESEXA>They rolled the logs down the hill.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The car still didn’t start so we tried to roll it off the road.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wheel</EXP><DEF>to push something with wheels, for example a bicycle or a <NonDV><REFHWD>trolley</REFHWD></NonDV>, so that it moves forward, while guiding it with your hands</DEF><THESEXA>Rob wheeled his bike round the back of the house.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2b6f"><HEADING>Sense 1</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>question</EXP><DEF>something that you ask someone, either when speaking or when writing</DEF><THESEXA>Would anyone like to ask any questions?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve emailed the hotel with one or two questions.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>query</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈkwɪəri</PRON><AMEVARPRON>ˈkwɪri</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>a question that you ask when you are not sure you have understood something or that the information you have is correct</DEF><THESEXA>If you have any queries, please contact your travel agent.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Our staff are always available to answer customers’ queries.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>inquiry</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><ORTHVAR>enquiry</ORTHVAR><GEO>especially BrE</GEO></Variant><PronCodes><PRON>ɪnˈkwaɪəri</PRON><AMEVARPRON>ɪnˈkwaɪri, ˈɪŋkwəri</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>a question you ask in order to get information or find out the details about something</DEF><THESEXA>We’ve advertised the house, and we’ve already received lots of enquiries.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The police have been making some inquiries in the area.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>request</EXP><DEF>a polite or formal question asking for something that you want or need</DEF><THESEXA>The bank said ‘no’ to our request for more money.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You must make your request in writing.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2b4c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>question</EXP><DEF>to ask someone questions in order to get information about something such as a crime</DEF><THESEXA>The police questioned him for three hours before releasing him without charge.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When questioned by reporters, he denied all knowledge about the affair.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>interrogate</EXP><DEF>to keep asking a lot of questions for a long time, sometimes using threats, in order to get information</DEF><THESEXA>He was interrogated by US agents about his alleged links to al-Qaeda. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>interview</EXP><DEF>to ask someone questions for a newspaper, TV programme etc, or to ask someone questions to find out if they are suitable for a job, course etc</DEF><THESEXA>Did you hear him being interviewed on ‘the Today Programme’?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The woman who interviewed me offered me the job.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grill</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to ask someone a lot of difficult questions about something, in a way that is tiring or annoying</DEF><THESEXA>She started grilling me about why I was so late coming home.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cross-examine</EXP><DEF>to ask someone a series of questions in court about their previous statements, in order to find out whether they have been telling the truth</DEF><THESEXA>The defence lawyer cross-examined the witness in order to test his evidence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be helping police with their inquiries</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>to be questioned by the police about a crime – used especially in news reports when the police think that this person may be guilty of the crime</DEF><THESEXA>Last night, a 21-year-old woman was helping police with their inquiries.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2b2c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>quick</EXP><DEF>taking only a short time to do something</DEF><THESEXA>I took a quick look at the <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2aaa"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rain</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>water that falls in small drops from clouds in the sky</DEF><THESEXA>The rain has stopped at last.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There had been heavy rain during the night.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>drizzle</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>light rain with very small drops of water</DEF><THESEXA>A light drizzle was falling as I left the house.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shower</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a short period of rain that can be heavy or light</DEF><THESEXA>More heavy showers are forecast for tonight.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a light shower of rain</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>downpour</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C usually singular</GRAM><DEF>a short period of very heavy rain that starts suddenly</DEF><THESEXA>A sudden downpour sent us running for shelter.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a torrential downpour</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hail</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>frozen rain that falls in the form of <NonDV><REFHWD>hailstone</REFHWD></NonDV>s<GLOSS>small balls of ice</GLOSS></DEF><THESEXA>The hail and high winds have destroyed many of the county’s crops.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sleet</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>a mixture of snow and rain</DEF><THESEXA>The rain had turned to sleet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sleet and snow fell.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the rains</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>plural</GRAM><DEF>heavy rain that falls during a particular period in the year in tropical countries</DEF><THESEXA>The farmers are waiting for the rains to come.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>monsoon</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>the heavy rain that falls between April and October in India and other southern Asian countries</DEF><THESEXA>The monsoon is late this year.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the monsoon season</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2a87"><Section><Exponent><EXP>it’s raining</EXP><DEF>drops of water are falling from the sky</DEF><THESEXA>It’s raining – you’d better take an umbrella.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>it’s pouring (down)</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>it’s pouring (rain)</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>it is raining very heavily</DEF><THESEXA>We stayed at home because it was pouring down all day.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>it’s chucking it down</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>it is raining very heavily</DEF><THESEXA>Outside it was chucking it down and the streets were deserted. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>it’s drizzling</EXP><DEF>very gentle rain is falling</DEF><THESEXA>It’s only drizzling – let’s go for a walk anyway.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>it’s hailing</EXP><DEF>frozen rain in the form of small balls of ice are falling</DEF><THESEXA>It had been hailing and the roads were still slippery.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2a72"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rainy</EXP><DEF>a rainy period of time is one when it rains a lot</DEF><THESEXA>a cold rainy day in October</THESEXA><THESEXA>The weather continued to be rainy.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>The rainy season</COLLOINEXA> is in July.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wet</EXP><DEF>rainy. <DEFBOLD>Wet</DEFBOLD> and <DEFBOLD>rainy </DEFBOLD>mean the same and are used in the same way</DEF><THESEXA>In Cyprus during the winter, you’ll only have the occasional wet day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The wet weather is expected to continue.</THESEXA><THESEXA>another wet summer</THESEXA><THESEXA>It's been wet all week.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>damp</EXP><DEF>if the air feels damp, there is a lot of <NonDV><REFHWD>moisture</REFHWD></NonDV> in the air and it may be raining slightly</DEF><THESEXA>It was a cold damp day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a damp November morning</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>showery</EXP><DEF>raining for short periods</DEF><THESEXA>the cool, bright, showery weather of early April</THESEXA><THESEXA>The weather will be showery, with some sunny intervals.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>drizzly</EXP><DEF>raining slightly, with a lot of mist in the air</DEF><THESEXA>The morning was grey and drizzly.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grey</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>gray</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>if the sky is grey, there are a lot clouds, and it looks like it will rain</DEF><THESEXA>The next morning, the sky was grey.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Then, as if by magic, the sun burst through what had until then been a rather grey day.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2a3b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rare</EXP><DEF>not existing in large numbers or in large amounts</DEF><THESEXA>The law prevents the export of rare birds.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The plates are quite rare. Only about a hundred were made.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scarce</EXP><DEF>not available in large enough numbers or amounts at a particular time – used especially about things people need</DEF><THESEXA>After the war, food and clothing were scarce.</THESEXA><THESEXA>People are having to compete for scarce resources.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not common</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>fairly rare</DEF><THESEXA>Silver coins of this period are not common, and could be very valuable.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>infrequent</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>not happening often</DEF><THESEXA>As time went on, her visits became more and more infrequent.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be few and far between</EXP><DEF>to not be common – especially much less common than you might expect</DEF><THESEXA>Luckily, accidents such as these are few and far between.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Bargains are, unfortunately, few and far between.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be (something of) a rarity</EXP><DEF>if something or someone  is a rarity, it is surprising to find one, because very few exist</DEF><THESEXA>Women are still something of a rarity in senior management positions.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be like hen’s teeth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be extremely rare</DEF><THESEXA>Good Greek restaurants are like hen’s teeth around here.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2a0d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rarely</EXP><DEF>not often</DEF><THESEXA>These geese are rarely found on inland waters.</THESEXA><THESEXA>50% of Britons say they rarely eat meat.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not (very) often</EXP><DEF>often used in everyday English instead of saying <DEFBOLD>rarely</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Tina’s not often late.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t go there very often.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s not often that you get a chance like this.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>seldom</EXP><DEF>rarely. <DEFBOLD>Seldom</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>rarely</DEFBOLD> and is used especially in written English</DEF><THESEXA>He seldom slept well.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They seldom went out.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hardly ever/scarcely ever</EXP><DEF> almost never</DEF><THESEXA>Ben’s nineteen and he’s hardly ever at home these days.</THESEXA><THESEXA>For some reason, her name was scarcely ever mentioned.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>very occasionally</EXP><DEF>used when you want to emphasize that something only happens a few times over a long period of time</DEF><THESEXA>Very occasionally the temperature drops to below 30.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-29f5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rather/quite</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>more than a little, but less than very. British people often use these words before adjectives in conversation. In many cases they do not intend to change the meaning – it is just something that people say</DEF><THESEXA>She seemed rather unhappy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s rather a difficult question.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s getting quite late.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Malaria is rather common in this area.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fairly</EXP><DEF>rather. <DEFBOLD>Fairly</DEFBOLD> is used in both British and American English</DEF><THESEXA>The test was fairly easy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s a fairly long way to the next town.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pretty</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>rather. <DEFBOLD>Pretty</DEFBOLD> is more informal than the other words and is used in spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>Her French is pretty good.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We’re in a pretty strong position.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reasonably</EXP><DEF>to a satisfactory level or degree</DEF><THESEXA>He plays reasonably well.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Let’s just say that I am reasonably confident we’ll win.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>moderately</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>more than a little, but not very</DEF><THESEXA>Her family was moderately wealthy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The food was moderately good, but not as good as the food in the other restaurants.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Use a moderately high heat.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a moderately difficult climb</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>somewhat</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>fairly or to a small degree. <DEFBOLD>Somewhat</DEFBOLD> is used especially when talking about the size or degree of something. It is often used in comparatives</DEF><THESEXA>The celebrations were somewhat larger than last year’s.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He looked somewhat irritated. </THESEXA><THESEXA>a somewhat surprising decision</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-29d5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>reaction</EXP><DEF>someone’s feelings, and how they behave, because of something that has happened or been <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-29a1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>read</EXP><DEF>to look at and understand the words in a book, magazine, letter etc for interest, <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-298a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>ready</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>having done everything that needs to be done in order to prepare for something</DEF><THESEXA>Are you ready? The taxi’s here.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t feel that I’m ready for the test yet.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>prepared</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>ready to deal with a situation, because you are expecting it or have made careful preparations</DEF><THESEXA>The police were prepared for trouble.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The team looked <COLLOINEXA>well-prepared</COLLOINEXA> for the game.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be all set</EXP><DEF>to be ready to start doing something that you have planned to do, and be just about to do it</DEF><THESEXA>We were all set for a barbecue when it started to rain.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be good to go</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>be ready to start doing something after completing all the necessary preparations</DEF><THESEXA>We just need to get you a pair of skis and you’re good to go.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ripe</EXP><DEF>ripe fruit are soft, sweet, and ready to eat</DEF><THESEXA>Don’t pick the apples until they’re really ripe.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a delicious ripe plum</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be in place</EXP><DEF>if the arrangements or the equipment for doing something are in place, they are ready to start being used</DEF><THESEXA>The television cameras were in place for the wedding.</THESEXA><THESEXA>All the arrangements are in place for a new constitution and democratic elections.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be standing by</EXP><DEF>if people are standing by, they are ready to take action and help if they are needed – used especially about medical teams, police, the army etc</DEF><THESEXA>Officers in full riot gear were standing by outside the police station.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Several ambulances were standing by.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2951"><Section><Exponent><EXP>realize</EXP><DEF>to begin to understand, notice, or know something that you did not understand etc before</DEF><THESEXA>I hadn’t realized that Ben was his brother.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She suddenly realized who the man in the photograph was.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>become aware</EXP><DEF> to gradually realize that something is happening or is true, over a period of time</DEF><THESEXA>He slowly became aware that he was not alone</THESEXA><THESEXA>People are becoming more aware of the harmful effects of cars on the environment. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dawn on sb</EXP><DEF> if something dawns on you, you realize it for the first time – often used in the phrase <DEFBOLD>it dawned on sb</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>It dawned on me that he could be lying.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It only dawned on her that she was in danger when she saw rescue workers running away from the building.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He thought about the dream for a long time before its meaning began to dawn on him. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sink in</EXP><DEF> if something sinks in, you begin to realize its full meaning or importance, especially gradually</DEF><THESEXA>It took a few minutes for the doctor’s words to sink in.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The reality of what I had done slowly began to sink in.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hit</EXP><DEF>if a fact hits you, you suddenly understand it and how important it is</DEF><THESEXA>It hit me one day that he didn’t care. He’d talk when I phoned him, but he’d never call me.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>strike</EXP><DEF>if an idea or thought strikes you, you suddenly think of it</DEF><THESEXA>It suddenly struck her what a risk she was taking.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A thought has just struck me - there must be other people with the same problem.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2943"><Section><Exponent><EXP>reason</EXP><DEF>why something happens, or why someone does something</DEF><THESEXA>What was the reason for the delay?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t know the reason why he left his last job.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cause</EXP><DEF>the reason why something happens, especially something bad</DEF><THESEXA>What was the cause of his death?</THESEXA><THESEXA>We studied the causes of the First World War. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>explanation</EXP><DEF> a set of reasons that helps you to understand why something happens, especially when it seems difficult to understand</DEF><THESEXA>There are various possible explanations for climate change.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Is there any explanation for his behavior?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>motive</EXP><DEF> a reason that makes someone decide to do something – often used about crimes</DEF><THESEXA>Police say that there is no obvious motive for the attack.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>justification</EXP><DEF>a good reason for doing something that seems wrong</DEF><THESEXA>There is never any justification for torture or abuse.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They try to use the situation in the Middle East as a justification for killing innocent civilians.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There’s no justification for this type of behaviour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grounds</EXP><DEF>a reason that makes it right or fair to do something, especially according to legal, official, or moral rules</DEF><THESEXA>The court will decide if she has grounds for divorce.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They claim the war is justified on moral grounds<GLOSS>because of moral reasons</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>basis </EXP><DEF>the main ideas or reasons on which something is based</DEF><THESEXA>The doctor makes his decisions purely on the basis of clinical observation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What do you think is the basis for this advice? </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rationale</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˌræʃəˈnɑːl</PRON><AMEVARPRON> -ˈnæl</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a set of reasons that are used to explain why someone does something in a particular way</DEF><THESEXA>the rationale behind the government’s economic reforms</THESEXA><THESEXA>This chapter explains the nature of yearly plans, and provides a rationale for their use.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>a reason that does not seem believable</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>excuse</EXP><DEF>a reason that you give to explain why you have done something bad, or not done something that you should have done – especially one that is not completely true</DEF><THESEXA>She said she couldn’t come because she had to work late, but it was just an excuse.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a feeble excuse<GLOSS>one that is hard to believe</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pretext</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an untrue reason that you give for doing or not doing something, in order to hide the real reason</DEF><THESEXA>He would often find some pretext to go out in the evening alone.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They used this as a pretext for taking military action.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2934"><Section><Exponent><EXP>recently</EXP><DEF>not long ago, especially a few days, weeks, or months ago</DEF><THESEXA>The president has recently returned from a tour of South America.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A new species of plant was recently discovered in Brazil.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lately</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>during the recent past, especially during the weeks or days closest to now</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve been really busy lately so I haven’t been out much.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There hasn’t been much in the news lately.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>just</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a very short time ago, especially only a few minutes, hours, days, or weeks ago</DEF><THESEXA>John’s just gone out.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They’ve just had a new baby.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>a short/little while ago</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>not long ago – often used when you are not sure exactly when</DEF><THESEXA>That house was sold a short while ago.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I looked in on her a little while ago and she was fast asleep.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the other day</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>recently, especially only a few days ago</DEF><THESEXA>I met Lucy in town the other day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You’ll never guess what happened to me the other day.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>freshly</EXP><DEF>made, prepared, done etc not long ago – used especially about food and drink. Also used about things that have just been painted, washed, or dug</DEF><THESEXA>freshly baked bread</THESEXA><THESEXA>freshly squeezed orange juice</THESEXA><THESEXA>The boat had been freshly painted.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>newly</EXP><DEF>created, built, married etc not long ago</DEF><THESEXA>the newly created position of Chief Designer</THESEXA><THESEXA>a newly married couple</THESEXA><THESEXA>their newly elected president</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-291d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>recession</EXP><DEF>a period when a country’s economic growth stops and there is less trade</DEF><THESEXA>The industry has cut jobs due to the recession.</THESEXA><THESEXA>fears that the economy may be sliding into recession</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>depression</EXP><DEF>a long period during which there is a bad recession, so that there is very little business activity and a lot of people do not have jobs</DEF><THESEXA>During the depression of the 1930s, as many as 20% of the population were jobless.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slump</EXP><DEF>a fairly short period when there is a reduction in business and many people lose their jobs</DEF><THESEXA>The slump in the housing market is making it difficult for people to sell their homes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slowdown</EXP><DEF>a period when there is a reduction in business activity, that may be the start of a recession</DEF><THESEXA>High prices could tip the slowdown in the US into a world recession. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>downturn</EXP><DEF>a period during which there is a reduction in business activity and economic conditions become worse, when before the economy was growing</DEF><THESEXA>Public spending may reduce the effects of the downturn.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>crash</EXP><DEF>an occasion when the value of stocks and shares on a stock market falls suddenly and by a large amount, causing economic problems</DEF><THESEXA>The Wall Street Crash of 1929 was disastrous for not only the American economy, but for the world economy.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2911"><Section><Exponent><EXP>recognize</EXP><DEF>to know who someone is or what something is, especially because you have seen or heard them before</DEF><THESEXA>I hadn’t seen her for ten years, but I recognized her immediately.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you recognize this song?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>identify</EXP><DEF>to recognize someone or something and say who or what they are</DEF><THESEXA>As they came closer, I was able to identify two of the group.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s delicious but I can’t quite identify the taste.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>distinguish</EXP><DEF>to recognize and understand the difference between two or more things or people</DEF><THESEXA>By this age, kids can usually distinguish between right and wrong.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s often difficult to distinguish identical twins from each other.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make sth/sb out</EXP><POS /><DEF>to be able to see or hear something or someone – used when it is very difficult to do this</DEF><THESEXA>In the distance, I could just make out the outline of an island.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He whispered something but I couldn’t make out what it was.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>discern</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>dɪˈsɜːn </PRON><AMEVARPRON> -ɜːrn</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to notice or understand something by looking carefully or thinking about it carefully</DEF><THESEXA>I thought I discerned a faint gleam of hope in his eyes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A number of differences can be discerned in the data for the three countries.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><Exponent><EXP>know</EXP><DEF>to recognize someone or something. <DEFBOLD>Know</DEFBOLD> is often used in everyday English instead of <DEFBOLD>recognize</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>How do you know that it is real gold? </THESEXA><THESEXA>I <COLLOINEXA>know </COLLOINEXA>him <COLLOINEXA>from somewhere</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I can’t remember his name, but I <COLLOINEXA>know his face</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>can tell</EXP><DEF>to be able to recognize someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>I could tell that it was him by his voice. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Can you tell that it’s not real leather?</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s difficult to be able to <COLLOINEXA>tell them apart</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>to recognize that they are different</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-28fb"><Section><Exponent><EXP>recommend</EXP><DEF>to advise someone to do something, especially when you have special knowledge of a situation or subject</DEF><THESEXA>I would strongly recommend buying a good quality bicycle rather than a cheap one.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The report recommended stricter supervision of the trade in live animals.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tell</EXP><DEF>to tell  someone that you think they should do something, especially in order to avoid problems</DEF><THESEXA>We’ve been told that we should start revising early.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I told you not to drink the water here.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>urge</EXP><DEF>to strongly advise someone to do something because you think it is very important</DEF><THESEXA>Her doctor has urged her to see a specialist.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police are urging drivers not to come into London this weekend.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>advocate</EXP><DEF>to say publicly that something should be done, often something that a lot of people disagree about</DEF><THESEXA>In 1984, he advocated the use of force against Nicaragua.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I am surprised that dentists don’t advocate the use of fluoride tablets.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>endorse</EXP><DEF>to say in an advertisement that you like a product and think that people should use it</DEF><THESEXA>A lot of companies use sports stars to endorse their products.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-28db"><Section><Exponent><EXP>record</EXP><DEF>information about something that is written down</DEF><THESEXA>your medical records</THESEXA><THESEXA>the public records office</THESEXA><THESEXA>I have to keep a record of all my spending when I’m travelling on business. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>file</EXP><DEF>a books all seemed to be in order.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ledger</EXP><DEF>one of the official books in which a company’s financial records are kept, which show how much it has received and things that have happened to you</DEF><THESEXA>In his diary he wrote, ‘It s lovely having him here, we’ve had so many cosy talks.’ </THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ll just check in my diary to see if I’m free.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blog</EXP><DEF>a web page on the Internet on which someone regularly writes about their life, opinions, or a particular subject</DEF><THESEXA>I may not always agree with David, but I always read his blog. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>register</EXP><DEF>an official list of names of people, organizations etc</DEF><THESEXA>Guests must sign the hotel register.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the national register of births, deaths, and marriages</THESEXA><THESEXA>Lloyds Register of Shipping</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>roll</EXP><DEF>an official list of area</GLOSS></THESEXA><THESEXA>The teacher <COLLOINEXA>called the roll</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>read out the list of the names of the students, who then have to say if they are present</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>log</EXP><DEF>an official record that is kept on a ship or plane</DEF><THESEXA>Mr Appleby said he complained to a senior officer, who made a note in <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-28c7"><Section><Exponent><EXP>recover</EXP><DEF>to become healthy again after you have been seriously ill or injured, or had a bad experience</DEF><THESEXA>My mother’s been very ill, and she’s still in hospital recovering.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She needed time to recover from the shock.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get better</EXP><DEF>to recover from an injury or illness. In everyday English, people usually say <DEFBOLD>get better</DEFBOLD> rather than <DEFBOLD>recover</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>I hope you get better soon.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My back’s been quite bad recently, but it’s getting better slowly.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get over sth</EXP><DEF>to recover from a bad experience, or a minor illness. In everyday English, people usually say <DEFBOLD>get over</DEFBOLD> rather than <DEFBOLD>recover</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>She never got over his death. </THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m getting over my cold.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get well</EXP><DEF>to recover from an illness or operation – used especially when you are writing to encourage someone to recover</DEF><THESEXA>Get well soon – we all miss you!</THESEXA><THESEXA>I hope you get well quickly.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be back on your feet</EXP><DEF>to have recovered and be able to live life as usual again</DEF><THESEXA>It may take a week or two until you’re back on your feet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s great to see you back on your feet!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be on the mend</EXP><DEF>to be showing definite signs of recovering after an illness or injury</DEF><THESEXA>I’m glad to see you’re on the mend again.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Kathy’s been quite ill with flu, but she seems to be on the mend now.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>recuperate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to spend time resting and getting your health or energy back, after you have had an illness or had a difficult or tiring experience</DEF><THESEXA>It had been a hard year, and I needed a few weeks in the sun in order to recuperate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He is recuperating from a heart attack.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>convalesce</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˌkɒnvəˈles </PRON><AMEVARPRON> ˌkɑːn-</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to spend a long period of time recovering from a serious illness, especially by resting in a comfortable or warm place</DEF><THESEXA>She was at home convalescing after major surgery.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2897"><Section><SECHEADING>to reduce prices, numbers, or amounts</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>reduce</EXP><DEF>to make the price, amount, or size of something less or smaller</DEF><THESEXA>The price was reduced by 50%.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We need to reduce the amount of salt in our diet.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cut</EXP><DEF>to reduce something, especially by a large amount – used about prices, costs, jobs, or the time needed to do something</DEF><THESEXA>Companies are always looking for ways to cut costs, or reduce the level of something. <DEFBOLD>Bring sth down</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>lower</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The government wants to bring down the level of inflation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company is trying to bring its costs down.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slash</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to reduce an amount or price by a very only!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cut sth back</EXP><DEF>to reduce the amount of something – used especially about people deciding to spend less, do less, or use less of something</DEF><THESEXA>The education budget has been cut back again.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I need to cut back on my workload.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>downsize</EXP><DEF>to reduce the number of people employed in order to reduce costs – used about a company or organization</DEF><THESEXA>The company is planning to downsize its European operations.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scale sth down/back</EXP><DEF>to reduce the size or the amount of money that is being spent on something</DEF><THESEXA>The research programme has been scaled down.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The navy is being scaled down.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>relieve/ease</EXP><DEF>to make pain or feelings less unpleasant</DEF><THESEXA>The drug is used to relieve pain.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A joke can help to ease the tension.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>alleviate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to reduce pain or suffering, or make a problem less serious</DEF><THESEXA>You can buy various medicines to alleviate the symptoms of flu.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The new road was supposed to alleviate the congestion problem.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2841"><Section><Exponent><EXP>referee</EXP><DEF>someone who makes sure that the rules of a sport are obeyed in soccer, American football, rugby, ice hockey, basketball, boxing, or wrestling</DEF><THESEXA>a football referee</THESEXA><THESEXA>The referee blew his whistle to stop the game.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>umpire</EXP><DEF> someone who makes sure that the rules are obeyed in baseball, cricket, tennis, or hockey</DEF><THESEXA>He was given several warnings by the umpire.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a tennis umpire</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>judge</EXP><DEF>someone who decides the result of a competition or sporting contest</DEF><THESEXA>There will be only one winner and the judges’ decision is final.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A panel of judges will evaluate the dancers' performance. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>arbitrator</EXP><DEF>someone who officially decides how an argument between two opposing sides should be settled</DEF><THESEXA>They insisted that an independent arbitrator must settle the dispute.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-282f" fullpage="yes"><Section><Exponent><EXP>refuse</EXP><DEF>to say firmly that you will not do something that someone has asked you to to disallow his evidence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rebuff</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to refuse to accept someone’s offer, request, or suggestion</DEF><THESEXA>The company <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-27f3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>related/connected</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>used about things that have a connection with each other. <DEFBOLD>Connected</DEFBOLD> is not used before a noun</DEF><THESEXA>Physics and Maths are closely related.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The two problems are connected.</THESEXA><THESEXA>homelessness and other related issues</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>linked</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>having a direct connection – often used when one thing is the cause of the other</DEF><THESEXA>Skin cancer is directly two or more things that are connected with each other and affect each other in a complicated series of ways</DEF><THESEXA>The various parts of society are closely interrelated. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The experience.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be bound up with sth</EXP><DEF>to be very closely connected – used about two things that need to be considered together</DEF><THESEXA>The history of the <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-27bc"><Section><Exponent><EXP>relationship</EXP><DEF>when two people spend time together or live together because they are romantically dancer.</THESEXA><THESPROPFORM>have a fling</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>They had a fling years ago.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>romance</EXP><DEF>an exciting and often short relationship between two
