<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-278f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>relative</EXP><DEF>a member of your family, especially one who does not live with you</DEF><THESEXA>Most of her relatives were able to come to the wedding.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We have some <COLLOINEXA>distant relatives</COLLOINEXA> in Australia.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>relation</EXP><DEF>a member of your family. <DEFBOLD>Relation</DEFBOLD> means the same as <DEFBOLD>relative</DEFBOLD>. It is often used when talking about whether someone is in the same family as another person</DEF><THESEXA>Big cities can be lonely places if you have no friends or relations there.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘<COLLOINEXA>What relation is she to you</COLLOINEXA>?’ ‘She’s my half sister.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s no relation to the singer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>descendant</EXP><DEF>someone who is a family member of a person who lived and died a long time ago, for example the great-grandchild of that person</DEF><THESEXA>The people are the descendants of slaves who were brought over from Africa.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ancestor</EXP><DEF>a member of your family who lived a long time ago, especially hundreds of years ago</DEF><THESEXA>My ancestors originally came from Ireland.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>forefathers</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>people in your family who lived a long time ago – often used in historical descriptions</DEF><THESEXA>His forefathers came to America over a century ago.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>extended family</EXP><DEF>a family group that consists not only of parents and children, but also includes grandparents, aunts etc</DEF><THESEXA>Extended families rarely live together in Britain, but they are still important.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>folks</EXP><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>your family, especially your parents</DEF><THESEXA>Are you going to see your folks at Christmas?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>next of kin</EXP><DEF>the person or people who are most closely related to you, for example your husband or mother, and who need to be told if something serious happens to you</DEF><THESEXA>The next of kin must be notified of his death before his name is released to the press.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-275d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>relax</EXP><DEF>to rest or do something that is enjoyable after you have been working</DEF><THESEXA>In the evenings, I like to relax with some music and a nice meal.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In two weeks’ time I’ll be relaxing on a beach in Greece.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rest</EXP><DEF>to stop working or stop being active, and sit down or lie down so that you become less tired</DEF><THESEXA>If you’re tired, we’ll stop and rest for a while.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The doctor told me to take some time off work and try to rest.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unwind</EXP><DEF>to gradually relax after you have been working hard or feeling anxious</DEF><THESEXA>It had been a bad day and he just wanted to get home and unwind.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Set in spectacular countryside, the Shiga Hotel is the perfect place to unwind. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take it/things easy</EXP><DEF>to relax and not do very much, especially after working very hard or being ill</DEF><THESEXA>Now that you’ve finished your exams, you can take it easy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The doctor said he’ll have to take things easy for while.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put your feet up</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to rest for a short time after a tiring activity, especially by sitting with your feet resting on something</DEF><THESEXA>Kate poured herself a drink and put her feet up.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When you’re pregnant and doing a full-time job, you must find time to put your feet up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>chill out/chill</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to relax completely, or stop worrying and getting annoyed about things – used mainly by young people</DEF><THESEXA>We spent the day chilling out by the pool.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Hey dude, chill out! It’s only a car! </THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s a great place to chill out.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Let’s go back to my place and chill.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP> loosen up</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to become more relaxed and stop worrying about things</DEF><THESEXA>I keep telling Mum that she needs to loosen up a bit.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Tom had had a few drinks and was beginning to loosen up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put sb at ease/make sb feel at ease</EXP><DEF>to make someone feel relaxed, especially in a situation in which they might feel a little nervous</DEF><THESEXA>A good interviewer will try to make you feel at ease.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His friendly manner always puts people at their ease.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2731"><Section><Exponent><EXP>relaxed</EXP><DEF>feeling calm, comfortable, and not worried or annoyed</DEF><THESEXA>I was feeling relaxed after my holiday.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Looking relaxed and confident, the president answered questions from the press. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He seems very relaxed about the whole situation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>easy-going</EXP><DEF>relaxed and not getting annoyed or angry with people – used when this is a part of someone’s character. An <DEFBOLD>easy-going</DEFBOLD> person likes to have good friendly relationships with other people, and is not strict about rules</DEF><THESEXA>Fred MacMurray was very easy-going and a pleasure to work with.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The hotel owner was an easy-going woman who let visitors come and go pretty well when they pleased.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The children were brought up in an easy-going atmosphere at home.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>laid-back</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>relaxed and not getting worried or annoyed about things – used when this is part of someone’s character. A <DEFBOLD>laid-back</DEFBOLD> person likes to take life slowly</DEF><THESEXA>He seemed like a cool laid-back kind of a guy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Life on the farm was more laid-back compared to life in the big city.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP> comfortable</EXP><DEF>feeling happy and not worried about doing something, talking about something, or being with someone</DEF><THESEXA> The teachers were comfortable with the new technology and the students loved it. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Some female patients are not comfortable with the idea of seeing a male doctor.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This is an emotional issue, which most people aren’t comfortable talking about.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>at ease</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>feeling relaxed in a situation – used especially when someone feels less worried than before, or when many other people would not feel relaxed in that situation</DEF><THESEXA>After a few weeks at college, I started to feel more at ease.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He looked completely at ease on a horse.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was surprised to find himself so at ease with her father.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>feel at home</EXP><DEF>to feel relaxed in a place or with a person</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve always felt at home in Tokyo.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He felt instantly at home with her.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-26fd"><Section><Exponent><EXP>religion</EXP><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a set of beliefs about a god, and the ceremonies and customs that go with these beliefs. Also used when talking about all religions in general</DEF><THESEXA>Judaism is one of the great religions of the world.</THESEXA><THESEXA>African tribal religions</THESEXA><THESEXA>a lecture on the role of religion in society</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>faith</EXP><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a religion, especially one of the large important world religions. Also used when talking about religious belief in general</DEF><THESEXA>The school welcomes children of all faiths.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His religious faith was always deeply important to him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>belief</EXP><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>the act of believing in a god or gods, or the ideas that someone believes because of their religion</DEF><THESEXA>We all have the right to freedom of belief.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They were persecuted because of their religious beliefs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>denomination</EXP><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a religious group that has slightly different beliefs from other groups who belong to the same religion</DEF><THESEXA>The church is the largest Christian denomination in the United States.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sect</EXP><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a group of people who have their own set of religious beliefs and customs, especially a group that has separated from a larger group</DEF><THESEXA>He became a member of a Buddhist sect.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are two sects in Islam - the Sunni and the Shi'a.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an obscure religious sect</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the church</EXP><GRAM>singular, U</GRAM><DEF>the Christian religion in general</DEF><THESEXA>How great is the influence of the church in present-day society?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The church has a lot to say about this.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the Catholic/Protestant etc church</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><ORTHVAR>Church</ORTHVAR></Variant><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>one of the separate groups within the Christian religion</DEF><THESEXA>the Methodist Church</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cult</EXP><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>an extreme religious group that is not part of an established religion – often used to show disapproval</DEF><THESEXA>The suicides have been linked to a strange religious cult.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the traditional pagan cults of Ancient Rome</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>creed</EXP><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the religion that you belong to, and the set of beliefs that you accept in order to belong to it – often used in phrases when saying that your religion should not affect how you are treated</DEF><THESEXA>There must be no discrimination because of race, colour, or creed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You will be rewarded whatever your race or creed. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>faith community</EXP><GRAM>C</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a group of people who share the same religious beliefs – used especially in official contexts</DEF><THESEXA>the benefits of living within a supportive faith community</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-26bd"><Section><SECHEADING>relating to religion</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>religious</EXP><DEF>relating to religion</DEF><THESEXA>the used especially in the following phrases</DEF><THESEXA>the Holy Bible</THESEXA><THESEXA>the holy city of Mecca</THESEXA><THESEXA>a Hindu holy man</THESEXA><THESEXA>They believe they are fighting a holy war.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The priest puts some holy water on the child's head.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sacred</EXP><DEF>connected with God and religion, and therefore treated in a special way – used especially in the following phrases</DEF><THESEXA>This place is <COLLOINEXA>sacred to</COLLOINEXA> both Jews and Muslims.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In India, cows are <COLLOINEXA>considered sacred</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the Hindu sacred texts</THESEXA><THESEXA>sacred music</THESEXA><THESEXA>a sacred ritual</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>theological</EXP><DEF>relating to the controlled by a religious authority</DEF><THESEXA>secular education</THESEXA><THESEXA>secular matters</THESEXA><THESEXA>In the UK we live in a much more secular know whether God exists or not</DEF><THESEXA>I think I'd describe myself as an agnostic.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a group of prominent agnostic scientists </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-26ab"><Section><Exponent><EXP>remember</EXP><DEF>to form an idea in your mind of people, events, places etc from the past</DEF><THESEXA>I remember Janine – she lived in that house on the corner.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I can’t remember how the film ends. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He remembered meeting her at a party once.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>recall</EXP><DEF>to remember a particular fact, event, or situation, especially in order to tell someone about it</DEF><THESEXA>Can you recall where your husband was that night?</THESEXA><THESEXA>She recalled that he had seemed a strange, lonely man.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>recollect</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to remember an event or situation</DEF><THESEXA>Harry smiled as he recollected the scene.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She tried to recollect what had happened next in her dream.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>memorize</EXP><DEF>to learn facts, a piece of writing or music etc, so that you can remember them later</DEF><THESEXA>He’s trying to memorize his speech.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Don’t write down your PIN number, memorize it.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>think back/look back</EXP><DEF>to think about something that happened in the past</DEF><THESEXA>I thought back to when I was his age.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Looking back, I should have been more patient with her.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We need to stop looking back and start thinking about the future.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reminisce</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˌreməˈnɪs</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to talk about pleasant events, people, experiences etc from the past, because you want to remember them or enjoy talking about them</DEF><THESEXA>They were reminiscing about old times.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I used to spend hours listening to my grandfather reminisce.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bear sth in mind</EXP><DEF>to remember something important when you are doing something, because it could affect what you do</DEF><THESEXA>Bear in mind that this is the first time he’s done this.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sth is on the tip of your tongue</EXP><DEF>used to say that you know a word or a name but that you have difficulty remembering it at this exact moment</DEF><THESEXA>His name’s on the tip of my tongue. I’ll think of it in a minute.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>remind sb of sth</EXP><DEF>to make you think of another person, thing, or time, because they are similar</DEF><THESEXA>It reminds me of the time when I first started teaching.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The taste reminded him of school dinners.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2660"><HEADING>Sense 2</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>remove</EXP><DEF>to make something no longer exist, especially something that was causing problems</DEF><THESEXA>Some stains are difficult to remove with ordinary washing powder.</THESEXA><THESEXA>All the obstacles to an agreement have now been removed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get rid of sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to remove someone or something that you do not want. <DEFBOLD>Get rid of </DEFBOLD> is much more common than <DEFBOLD>remove</DEFBOLD> in everyday English, but is usually only used in active sentences</DEF><THESEXA>They managed to get rid of all the weeds. </THESEXA><THESEXA>It was almost impossible to get rid of him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>eliminate</EXP><DEF>to completely get rid of something that you do not want, especially because it is unnecessary or causing problems</DEF><THESEXA>If you book online, this eliminates the need for a ticket.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The new system will help to eliminate costly delays.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>eradicate</EXP><DEF>to completely get rid of a disease or a problem</DEF><THESEXA>The disease has been eradicated from most of Europe.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Street crime has almost been eradicated. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>delete</EXP><DEF>to remove something that has been written on a computer, or stored in a computer</DEF><THESEXA>Do you want to delete this file?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Press 3 to delete the messages on your answerphone.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>erase</EXP><DEF>to remove recorded sounds or pictures from a tape, or writing from paper</DEF><THESEXA>Shall I erase this video?</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s better to cross out a mistake than to try to erase it.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cut</EXP><DEF>to remove a part from a film, book, speech etc</DEF><THESEXA>The most violent scenes were cut.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Parts of his original speech were cut.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2640"><Section><SECHEADING>to pay money to use sth</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>rent</EXP><DEF>to pay money to use a house, room, vehicle, piece of equipment, area of land etc</DEF><THESEXA>He rented a room in a house on the Old Kent Road.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They flew out to New York and rented a car at the airport.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hire</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>to pay money to use a car or a piece of clothing or equipment for a short period of time</DEF><THESEXA>Why don't we hire a van for the day?</THESEXA><THESEXA>You can hire suits for weddings.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lease</EXP><DEF>to have a legal agreement under which you pay money to a person or company in order to use a building, area of land, vehicle, piece of equipment etc for a fixed period of time </DEF><THESEXA>They leased the offices from an American company.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The car is leased from BMW.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to allow sb to use sth for money</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>rent/rent sth out</EXP><DEF>to allow someone to use a house, room, vehicle, piece of equipment, area of land etc in return for money</DEF><THESEXA>She rents the flat out to students.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>let/let sth out</EXP><DEF>to allow someone to use a room, house, building etc in return for money</DEF><THESEXA>Some people don't want to let rooms to foreigners.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They let the house out while they were on holiday.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lease/lease sth out</EXP><DEF>to make a legal agreement which allows a person or company to use something that you own for a fixed period of time</DEF><THESEXA>Santa Clara ' s Redevelopment Agency leased the existing city golf course to developers.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-262e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>repair</EXP><DEF>to do some work on something that is damaged or not working properly, so that it is in yet?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fix</EXP><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><DEF>to repair something</DEF><THESEXA>I’m taking the car in to get it fixed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The chain on the bike needs fixing.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mend</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>to repair something that is damaged, torn, or not working</DEF><THESEXA> I’ve found someone who’ll mend the fence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Can you mend this sweater for me?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Fishermen sat mending their nets in the sunshine.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>service</EXP><DEF>to check a vehicle or machine and repair it if necessary, especially regularly</DEF><THESEXA>You should have your car serviced every six months.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When was the last time we had the gas boiler serviced?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>renovate</EXP><DEF>to repair an old building so that it looks in good condition again</DEF><THESEXA>They bought an old house and renovated it themselves.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>restore</EXP><DEF>to repair something old and valuable, especially a building, piece of furniture, painting etc, so that it looks the same as it did originally</DEF><THESEXA>Many paintings were damaged in the fire but have now been restored.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The hotel was restored to its original Victorian splendour in 1984.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>do up</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><Variant><LEXVAR>fix up</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>to repair an old building or vehicle, so that it looks in good condition again</DEF><THESEXA>He does up old cars and sells them.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A builder bought the house and fixed it up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>patch sth up</EXP><DEF>to quickly repair something that has a hole in it, by putting a piece of material on it, especially temporarily</DEF><THESEXA>They patched up the wall with bits of cement. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>darn</EXP><DEF>to repair holes in clothes</DEF><THESEXA>Are you any good at darning <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-261a"><Section><SECHEADING>a person</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>replace</EXP><DEF>if one person replaces another, they do a job or activity instead of the other person, usually permanently</DEF><THESEXA>The opposition leader Zhelyu Zhelev was elected to replace him. </THESEXA><THESEXA>How will we ever find anyone who can replace you?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The lead singer was replaced by Ray Willis back in 1992.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take sb's place/take the place of sb</EXP><DEF>to do something instead of someone</DEF><THESEXA>It will be difficult to find someone to take her place.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Doctor Rice is on holiday. I'm taking his place.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The court may allow another relative to take the place of the parent. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take over</EXP><DEF>to replace someone in a job or position, and continue their work</DEF><THESEXA>The new manager took over in July.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sales are up <GLOSS>they have increased</GLOSS>since he took over the company. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stand in for sb</EXP><DEF>to replace someone at work on a particular occasion</DEF><THESEXA>She was filming in Australia, so she asked a friend to stand in for her at the awards ceremony.</THESEXA><THESEXA>During the dangerous scenes, stuntmen and women stand in for the actors.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fill in for sb</EXP><DEF>to replace someone in a job for a short time</DEF><THESEXA>Susan will fill in for me while I'm away. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>a thing</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>replace</EXP><DEF>if one thing replaces another, it is used instead of the other thing, usually permanently</DEF><THESEXA>The car was old and needed replacing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Computers have replaced typewriters.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take sth's place/take the place of sth</EXP><DEF>to be used instead of another thing</DEF><THESEXA>If any of the eight units fails, its place will be taken by the back-up unit.</THESEXA><THESEXA>MP3 players are taking the place of CDs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Ugly concrete apartment buildings have taken the place of the old houses.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>supersede</EXP><DEF>to take the place of something – used especially about inventions, methods, organizations etc</DEF><THESEXA>The League of Nations was superseded by the United Nations in 1946. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Analysis by manual calculation has been superseded by more modern computer-based methods. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Goose used to be a traditional Christmas bird until turkey superseded it in popularity. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Iron began to supersede bronze for tool making about 3000 years ago.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>substitute sth for sth</EXP><DEF>to use something instead of the thing that you usually use, because the usual thing is not available – used especially about food</DEF><THESEXA>You can substitute margarine for butter in most recipes.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-25f3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>reprimand</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to tell someone that they have done something wrong or illegal and are being punished for it – used especially in official contexts</DEF><THESEXA>The police officers were officially reprimanded for their behaviour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The Swiss authorities severely reprimanded the banks for accepting $660 million from the former Nigerian dictator Sani Abacha.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Debra remembered as a very young child being reprimanded by her father.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scold</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if a parent, teacher, or other adult scolds a child, they talk to them angrily because they have done something wrong. <DEFBOLD>Scold</DEFBOLD> sounds rather formal and old-fashioned. In everyday English people usually say <DEFBOLD>tell sb off</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>I dreaded the thought of going home and being scolded by my father.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tell sb off</EXP><DEF>to talk angrily to someone because they have done something wrong. <DEFBOLD>Tell sb off</DEFBOLD> is more common in British English than American English</DEF><THESEXA>Dad told me off for getting home late.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give sb a talking-to</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to talk angrily to a child because they have done something wrong</DEF><THESEXA>The boy was given a good talking-to and sent home.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lecture</EXP><DEF>to talk angrily to someone for a long time about something they have done wrong, especially in a way that they think is not necessary or fair</DEF><THESEXA>Stop lecturing me, will you!</THESEXA><THESEXA>He began to lecture her about her duties as a citizen.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rebuke</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to tell someone that they should not have done something</DEF><THESEXA>She rebuked him for being late.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sheerman rebuked his colleague for suggesting that he was too stupid to understand what he was saying. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reproach</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to talk to someone in a way that shows you are disappointed at what they have done. <DEFBOLD>Reproach </DEFBOLD> sounds much gentler than criticizing someone or reprimanding them</DEF><THESEXA>He felt he had to reproach his friend for his excessive drinking.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>berate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to publicly criticize someone for a long time, in a way that shows you strongly disapprove of what they have done</DEF><THESEXA>She berated the paper for its 'misleading front-page story.'</THESEXA><THESEXA>Kennedy berated the Eisenhower administration and its vice president Richard Nixon, during the 1960 campaign.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-25d4"><Section><Exponent><EXP>reputation</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>the opinion that people have about a person, organization etc because of what has happened in the past</DEF><THESEXA>She was a good lawyer with a reputation for honesty and diligence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The school had an excellent reputation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The lawsuit has damaged the company’s reputation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>image</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>the idea that people have about what something is like, especially when this is created through newspaper stories, advertising etc</DEF><THESEXA>A PR campaign was launched in an effort to improve the company’s image.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Boxing has rather a <COLLOINEXA>negative image</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The princess tried to <COLLOINEXA>project</COLLOINEXA> an <COLLOINEXA>image</COLLOINEXA> of herself as serious and hard-working<GLOSS>she tried to give people the idea that she was serious and hard-working</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>name</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>singular</GRAM><DEF>the reputation that a person, organization etc has – used especially in the following phrases</DEF><THESEXA>The company is anxious to protect its <COLLOINEXA>good name</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Cyclists who ignore traffic rules give other cyclists <COLLOINEXA>a bad name</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Electrolux<COLLOINEXA> has a name for</COLLOINEXA> making top quality vacuum cleaners.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He went to court in order to try to <COLLOINEXA>clear his name</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>prove that he is innocent</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>standing</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>someone’s reputation and position compared to other people in a group or society, based on other people’s opinion of them</DEF><THESEXA>The class system in Great Britain encourages people to be very aware of their <COLLOINEXA>social standing</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He needs to improve his standing among female voters.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Jacques Tati was a man of international standing in the world of screen comedy.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>prestige</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>the good reputation that a company, organization, group etc has, which makes people respect and admire them</DEF><THESEXA>the prestige of a carmaker such as Rolls-Royce</THESEXA><THESEXA>Does Stanford University <COLLOINEXA>carry</COLLOINEXA> the same <COLLOINEXA>prestige</COLLOINEXA> as Harvard or Yale?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Hosting the Olympic Games would enhance our country’s international prestige.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The teaching profession has lost the prestige it had in the past.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stature</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the importance and respect that a person or organization has, because of their achievements or their influence</DEF><THESEXA>As he got older, Picasso’s stature as an artist increased.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Their work is <COLLOINEXA>equal in stature</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an actor <COLLOINEXA>of international stature</COLLOINEXA></THESEXA><THESEXA>The party’s stature has increased in recent years.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-257e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rescue</EXP><DEF>to remove someone from a dangerous, difficult, or unpleasant situation</DEF><THESEXA>Firefighters worked for two hours to rescue people from the building.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Will you rescue me if I get stuck talking to Sam?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come to the rescue/sb’s rescue</EXP><DEF>to come and rescue or help someone</DEF><THESEXA>It was an embarrassing moment, but fortunately Paul came to the rescue.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Several people saw I was in trouble, but no one came to my rescue.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>save</EXP><DEF>to prevent someone from being killed, harmed, or losing something, or to make it possible for something to continue</DEF><THESEXA>Wearing a seat belt can help save your life.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They saved the hospital from closure.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If you break down in the desert, there is no one there to save you.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pick sb up</EXP><DEF>to rescue someone from a dangerous place by taking them away in a boat or aircraft</DEF><THESEXA>A lifeboat picked them up two miles from the coast.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They spent the night near the top of the mountain, before being picked up by a helicopter.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bail sb out</EXP><DEF>to rescue a person, company etc from a difficult situation, by providing them with the money they need</DEF><THESEXA>A number of state-owned enterprises have been bailed out by the central bank.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He owed thousands of pounds and his mother had to bail him out.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-255f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>research</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>careful detailed work that is done in order to find out more about a subject, especially as a part of a scientific or academic project</DEF><THESEXA>Billions of dollars have been spent on research into the causes and treatment of cancer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The University has for a long time been a leading centre for research in this field.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>work</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>the studies that have been done on a particular subject</DEF><THESEXA>Faraday is famous for his work on electricity.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A lot of work has been done on hydrogen-powered cars.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Their work had an enormous influence on the study of genetics.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>study</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a piece of work in which someone examines a particular subject in order to find out more about it, and writes about what they have found</DEF><THESEXA>The study showed that 25 percent of adults do not eat breakfast at all, compared with 14 percent in 1961.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Recent studies suggest that our sense of smell is closely linked with the part of the brain that deals with memory.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>experiment</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a scientific test in order to find out what happens when you do something</DEF><THESEXA>They carried out a series of experiments<GLOSS>they did a series of experiments</GLOSS> in order to try to prove their theory.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Experiments have shown that there is an increased risk of some forms of cancer.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-14c1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rest</EXP><DEF>to stop working or stop being active, and sit down or lie down so that you become less tired</DEF><THESEXA>If you’re tired, we’ll stop and rest for a while.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The doctor told me to take some time off work and try to rest.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take a rest</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>have a rest</LEXVAR><GEO>especially BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to stop what you are doing for a period of time so that you can relax</DEF><THESEXA>Murray will now take a rest before the competition next month.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There was a TV in the hotel room, and sometimes I escaped up there to have a rest.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A spokesman said that the Senator needed to take a rest from the campaign activity.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take a break</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>have a break</LEXVAR><GEO>especially BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to stop what you are doing for a short time, so that you can rest or do something else – often used about stopping a meeting, class etc for a short time</DEF><THESEXA>Is it all right if we have a short break at about 10:30?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I spoke to the actor as he was taking a break from rehearsals.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Let’s take a break now for coffee.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take it easy</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>take things easy</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to do things gently and with less effort than usual, in order to avoid becoming worried or tired, especially because you are not feeling strong or healthy</DEF><THESEXA>After the operation, she decided to take things easy for a month or two.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I thought I’d take it easy tonight.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put your feet up</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to rest for a short time after a tiring activity, especially by sitting with your feet resting on something</DEF><THESEXA>Kate poured herself a drink and put her feet up.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When you’re pregnant and doing a full-time job, you must find time to put your feet up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>chill/chill out</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to spend time relaxing and doing things that do not need a lot of effort – used by young people</DEF><THESEXA>We chilled out in front of the TV with a couple of beers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I like chilling with the guys and playing pool or watching movies.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-24ef"><Section><Exponent><EXP>cafe/coffee shop</EXP><DEF>a place where you can get coffee and other non-alcoholic drinks, cakes, and small meals</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fast food restaurant</EXP><DEF>one where you can get meals such as hamburgers, french fries etc</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>self-service restaurant</EXP><DEF>one where you collect the food yourself</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>diner</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>a restaurant where you can eat cheap and simple food</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bistro</EXP><DEF>a small restaurant or bar, especially one serving French-style food</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cafeteria</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>canteen</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a place at work or school where you can collect and eat meals</DEF></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-24bb"><Section><Exponent><EXP>result</EXP><DEF>something that happens because of something else</DEF><THESEXA>The fire at the house was the result of a dropped cigarette.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many people find that herbal remedies produce a good result. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>consequence</EXP><DEF>something important that happens as the result of a decision or action</DEF><THESEXA>Global warming will have serious consequences for the environment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Rising prices are the inevitable consequence of the grain shortage. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>repercussions</EXP><DEF>the bad effects that happen later as a result of an event, often a long time afterwards</DEF><THESEXA>The economic crisis in the US is likely to have serious repercussions for the rest of the world. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The scandal could have major repercussions for his career.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>outcome</EXP><DEF>the final result of a meeting, election, war etc</DEF><THESEXA>The final outcome of the election remained in doubt for several days.</THESEXA><THESEXA>So, what is the likely outcome for Spain?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the upshot </EXP><DEF> the final result of a situation, especially when this was unexpected</DEF><THESEXA>The upshot of all this was that the trial had to be delayed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the fruits of sth</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the things that have been achieved as a result of someone’s efforts – used especially in the following phrases</DEF><THESEXA> He did not live to see <COLLOINEXA>the fruits of his labours</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They can now enjoy <COLLOINEXA>the fruits of their success</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-24a8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>return</EXP><DEF>to go back or come back to a place where you were before. <DEFBOLD>Return</DEFBOLD> sounds more formal than <DEFBOLD>go back </DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD>come back</DEFBOLD>, and is more commonly used in written English</DEF><THESEXA>She returned to the hotel hoping to find a message.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Alastair returned from the office late that night.</THESEXA><THESEXA>On Friday, I returned home around six o'clock.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go back</EXP><DEF>to go to the place where you were before, or to the place where you usually live</DEF><THESEXA>It’s cold out here – shall we go back inside?</THESEXA><THESEXA>When are you going back to Japan?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go home</EXP><DEF>to go to your home again, or to the country where you were born, after you have been away from it</DEF><THESEXA>I did a bit of shopping and then went home.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Are you going home to Hong Kong when the course finishes?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come back</EXP><DEF>to come to the place where you are again, after going away from it</DEF><THESEXA>I’ll be away for two days – coming back on Thursday night.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s just come back from a vacation in Miami.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get back</EXP><DEF>to arrive somewhere where you were before, especially your home or the place where you are staying</DEF><THESEXA>We got back at about 9 o'clock.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She couldn’t wait to get back to London.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>turn back</EXP><DEF>to turn around and go back in the direction you came from</DEF><THESEXA>We took the wrong road and had to turn back.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He ordered the soldiers to turn back and march south.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2478"><Section><Exponent><EXP>reveal</EXP><DEF>to let someone know about something that is secret or has not been known until now</DEF><THESEXA>Doctors are not allowed to reveal confidential information.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was revealed that he had smoked marijuana at college.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tell</EXP><DEF>to talk about something to someone, so that they know about it</DEF><THESEXA>Don’t tell anyone about this just yet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Shall I tell you a secret?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>disclose</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to publicly reveal something such as a fact or a name that has been kept secret</DEF><THESEXA>The terms of the agreement have not yet been disclosed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The agent did not disclose that there had been a violent crime in the house.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>divulge</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>daɪˈvʌldʒ, də-</PRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to reveal important or personal information which was previously secret or unknown</DEF><THESEXA>The bank has refused to divulge its plans.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m afraid I cannot divulge what was said to me.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make sth public</EXP><DEF>to tell people about important information, especially after it has been kept secret</DEF><THESEXA>Apparently they were engaged for some time before making it public.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The government has agreed that sources of financing should in future be made public.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>leak</EXP><DEF>to deliberately give secret information to a newspaper, television company etc, when a government or other organization wants to keep it secret</DEF><THESEXA>The contents of the email were leaked to the press.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A man was charged today with leaking official secrets.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give sth away</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>spill the beans</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>to tell someone something that you want to keep secret</DEF><THESEXA>He was careful not to give away any trade secrets.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m not going to give away how much I paid for it!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>let slip</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to accidentally tell someone about something</DEF><THESEXA>He let slip that he was envious of his older brother.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-241a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>revolt/rebellion/uprising</EXP><DEF>an attempt by a large group of people at revolution</DEF><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>a popular uprising</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>involving ordinary people, not the army</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>coup</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>kuː</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>an occasion when a group of people, especially soldiers, suddenly take control of a country</DEF><THESEXA>a military coup</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-240e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rich</EXP><DEF>having a lot of money – used about people and places</DEF><THESEXA>She married a rich Greek there was no necessity for work.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>privileged</EXP><DEF>having special advantages because your family have a lot of money and a high position in <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-23f4"><Section><Exponent><EXP>right</EXP><DEF>not wrong – used about something someone says, or about the person who says it</DEF><THESEXA>the right answer</THESEXA><THESEXA>You were right about the colour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘He’s about thirty, isn’t he?’ ‘That’s right.’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>correct</EXP><DEF>right. <DEFBOLD>Correct</DEFBOLD> sounds more formal than <DEFBOLD>right</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>the correct answer</THESEXA><THESEXA>He is absolutely correct.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Unfortunately, this information is not correct.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>accurate</EXP><DEF>right – used about information, measurements, descriptions etc</DEF><THESEXA>Make sure that your measurements are accurate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an accurate description of the suspect</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>exact</EXP><DEF>an exact number, amount, or time is completely correct, and is no more and no less than it should be</DEF><THESEXA>The exact time is 9.28 a.m.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The exact weight of the baby was 3.3 kilos.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spot-on</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>exactly right – used especially about guesses or things people say</DEF><THESEXA>His answer was spot-on.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You’re spot-on.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-23c3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>river</EXP><DEF>a line of water that flows into the sea</DEF><THESEXA>They crossed the river by stream</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>brook</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a small stream</DEF><THESEXA>There was a small brook, rushing and sparkling along between green <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-23aa"><Section><SECHEADING>types of road</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>road</EXP><DEF>a hard surface for cars, buses etc to drive street</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>the street in the middle of a town, where most of the shops are – used in British English</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>avenue</EXP><DEF>a road in a town, often with trees on each side</DEF><THESEXA>the busy avenue in front of the cathedral</THESEXA><THESEXA>He lived on Park Avenue. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>boulevard</EXP><DEF>a wide road in a city or town – used especially in street names in the US, France etc. In the UK, streets are usually called <DEFBOLD>avenue</DEFBOLD> rather than <DEFBOLD>boulevard</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>the world-famous Sunset Boulevard in Los Angeles. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lane</EXP><DEF>a narrow road in the country</DEF><THESEXA>a winding country lane</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cul-de-sac</EXP><DEF>a short street which is closed at one end</DEF><THESEXA>The house is situated in a quiet cul-de-sac in North Oxford.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>track</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>dirt road</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a narrow road in the country, usually without a hard surface</DEF><THESEXA>The farm was down a bumpy track.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ring road</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a road that goes around a town</DEF><THESEXA>The airport is on the ring road.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bypass</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a road that goes past a town, allowing traffic to avoid the centre</DEF><THESEXA>The bypass would take heavy traffic out of the old city centre.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dual carriageway</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>divided highway</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a road with a barrier or strip of land in the middle that has lines of traffic travelling in each direction</DEF><THESEXA>I waited until we were on the dual carriageway before I overtook him.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>freeway/expressway</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>a very wide road in a city or between cities, on which cars can travel very fast without stopping</DEF><THESEXA>Take the Hollywood Freeway (101) south, exit at Vine Street and drive east on Franklin Avenue.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Over on the side of the expressway, he saw an enormous sedan, up against a stone wall. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>motorway</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>highway</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a very wide road for travelling fast over long distances</DEF><THESEXA>The between states</DEF><THESEXA>The accident happened on Interstate 84, about 10 miles east of Hartford.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>toll road</EXP><DEF>a road that you pay to use</DEF><THESEXA>The government is planning to introduce toll roads, in an effort to cut traffic congestion.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>turnpike</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>a large road for fast traffic that you pay to use</DEF><THESEXA> He dropped her off at an entrance to the New Jersey Turnpike.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2361"><Section><Exponent><EXP>robbery</EXP><DEF>the crime of stealing money or other things from a bank, shop etc, especially by using threats or violence</DEF><THESEXA>Mobile phones are a common target in street robberies.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Armed robbery is a serious offence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>theft</EXP><DEF>the crime of stealing something, especially when the person they are stolen from is not present</DEF><THESEXA>Car thefts are on the increase.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Security has been tightened since the theft of a $150,000 oil painting.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>burglary</EXP><DEF>the crime of entering a house or other building illegally and stealing things</DEF><THESEXA>Most burglaries occur when a house or apartment is empty.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was charged with burglary.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>break-in</EXP><DEF>an occasion when someone breaks a door or window in order to enter a place and steal things</DEF><THESEXA>The break-in was the eighth on our street this year.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There’s been a break-in at the newsagents.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mugging</EXP><DEF>a violent attack on someone in the street in order to rob them</DEF><THESEXA>There have been a number of muggings outside downtown hotels.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>raid</EXP><DEF>an attack on a bank, shop etc, especially one in which the thieves use weapons</DEF><THESEXA>The gang carried out an armed raid on a post office.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP> bank job</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a carefully planned robbery of a bank</DEF><THESEXA>The money from the bank job was quickly taken out of the country.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>larceny</EXP><REGISTERLAB>law</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the crime of stealing something from someone, without using force or threats. This word is used mainly in American English. It is now old-fashioned in British English</DEF><THESEXA>He pleaded guilty in New York to nine counts of <COLLOINEXA>grand larceny</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>stealing things that are worth a lot of money</GLOSS>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They were suspected of being involved in <COLLOINEXA>petty larceny</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>stealing things that are not worth a lot of money</GLOSS>. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-233a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rock</EXP><DEF>a piece of the hard substance that forms the main surface of the Earth. In British English, <DEFBOLD>rocks</DEFBOLD> are too large to pick up, but in American English, they can either be large or small</DEF><THESEXA>the rocks along the riverbanks</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stone</EXP><DEF>a small piece of rock, found on the ground or near the surface of the ground. Speakers of American English are more likely to use the word <DEFBOLD>rock</DEFBOLD> than <DEFBOLD>stone</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The children were throwing stones into the water.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>boulder</EXP><DEF>a large round piece of rock</DEF><THESEXA>She climbed over a few boulders at the edge of the sea.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pebble</EXP><DEF>a small smooth stone found especially on a beach or on the bottom of a river</DEF><THESEXA>The beach was covered with smooth white pebbles.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fossil</EXP><DEF>a rock which has the shape of an animal or plant that lived many thousands of years ago</DEF><THESEXA>fossils of early reptiles</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2320"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rough</EXP><DEF>having a surface that is not flat or smooth</DEF><THESEXA>rough ground</THESEXA><THESEXA>a rough mountain path</THESEXA><THESEXA>The walls were all rough.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her hands were rough and work-hardened.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>uneven</EXP><DEF>an uneven  surface has areas that are not flat or not all at the same level</DEF><THESEXA>The floor was uneven.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She climbed the uneven steps with great care.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bumpy</EXP><DEF>a bumpy  road, path, or area of land has a lot of holes and raised parts in it</DEF><THESEXA>the bumpy track down to the farm</THESEXA><THESEXA>The field was too bumpy to play football on.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a bumpy journey<GLOSS>on a road that has a very rough surface</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>coarse</EXP><DEF>having a rough surface that feels slightly hard – used especially about materials such as cloth or wool</DEF><THESEXA>coarse woollen blankets</THESEXA><THESEXA>coarse grass</THESEXA><THESEXA>The wool felt rather coarse.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rugged</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈrʌɡəd</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>land that is rugged is very rough and uneven and is often in a high place</DEF><THESEXA>the rugged terrain near the mountains</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><ThesColloBox><SECHEADING>COLLOCATIONS CHECK</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>rough</EXP><COLLORANGE>ground/sea/stone/skin</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>uneven</EXP><COLLORANGE>surface/floor/pavement</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bumpy</EXP><COLLORANGE>road/field/journey</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>coarse</EXP><COLLORANGE>cloth/wool/paper/grass</COLLORANGE></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rugged</EXP><COLLORANGE>terrain/mountain/landscape/coastline</COLLORANGE></Exponent></ThesColloBox></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2307"><HEADING>Sense 1</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>rubbish</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>things that people throw away, such as old food, dirty paper etc</DEF><THESEXA>People are being encouraged to recycle their household rubbish.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the rubbish bin</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>garbage/trash</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>rubbish</DEF><THESEXA>The garbage is collected every Tuesday.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There were piles of trash in the backyard.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a black plastic garbage bag</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>refuse</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>rubbish</DEF><THESEXA>The strike has disrupted <COLLOINEXA>refuse collection</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s a site which is used for domestic refuse.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>litter</EXP><DEF>empty bottles, pieces of paper etc that people have dropped on the ground</DEF><THESEXA>Parents should teach children not to drop litter.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There was a lot of litter on the beach.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>waste</EXP><DEF>rubbish, or materials that need to be dealt with after they have been used in industrial processes</DEF><THESEXA>nuclear waste</THESEXA><THESEXA>toxic waste</THESEXA><THESEXA>household waste</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company was fined for dumping toxic waste in the sea.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-22f1" fullpage="yes"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rude</EXP><DEF>not following the rules of good social behaviour or treating other people with a lack of respect</DEF><THESEXA>It’s rude to interrupt.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s the rudest man I’ve ever met.</THESEXA><THESEXA>rude remarks about her stomach </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>impolite/not polite</EXP><DEF>not following the rules of good social behaviour. <DEFBOLD>Impolite</DEFBOLD> sounds rather formal</DEF><THESEXA>It is impolite to food.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cheeky</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>smart/sassy</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>behaving in a way that is a little rude, especially when this is me!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tactless</EXP><DEF>saying things that are likely to upset or embarrass someone, without intending to</DEF><THESEXA>a tactless remark</THESEXA><THESEXA>How could someone</DEF><THESEXA>comments that are insulting to women</THESEXA><THESEXA>The article was full of insulting language.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>discourteous</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>dɪsˈkɜːtiəs </PRON><AMEVARPRON> -ɜːr-</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>rather rude. <DEFBOLD>Discourteous </DEFBOLD> sounds very formal and is often used when talking about being careful not to upset someone’s feelings</DEF><THESEXA>He did not wish to appear discourteous towards his host.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It would seem discourteous to refuse her talking about subjects that you do not have a right to know about</DEF><THESEXA>an impertinent question about his private life</THESEXA><THESEXA>an impertinent young man</THESEXA><THESEXA>Would it be impertinent to ask how old you are?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>insolent</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>behaving in a way that is deliberately very rude to someone in and newspaper articles</DEF><THESEXA>an irreverent look at the week’s events</THESEXA><THESEXA>his irreverent sense of humour</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><ThesColloBox><SECHEADING>COLLOCATIONS <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-22ce"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rule</EXP><DEF>an instruction that says what people are allowed to do or not allowed to do, for example in a game, school, or company</DEF><THESEXA>the rules of baseball</THESEXA><THESEXA>He disobeyed the school rules.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>law</EXP><DEF>an official rule that everyone in a country, city, or state must obey</DEF><THESEXA>It is against the law to carry a concealed weapon.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The law requires motorcyclists to wear helmets.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>regulation</EXP><DEF>an official rule or order, which is part of a set of rules made by a government or organization</DEF><THESEXA>the regulations for applying for a passport</THESEXA><THESEXA>building regulations</THESEXA><THESEXA>environmental regulations on air pollution</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>restriction</EXP><DEF>an official rule that limits what people can do</DEF><THESEXA>new restrictions on immigration</THESEXA><THESEXA>The government is planning to impose regulations on the amount of alcohol you can bring into the country.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>guidelines</EXP><DEF>rules or instructions about the best way to do something</DEF><THESEXA>the Department of Health’s guidelines for a healthy diet</THESEXA><THESEXA>guidelines for classroom teachers</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>code</EXP><DEF>a set of rules that people or organizations agree to obey but are not forced to obey</DEF><THESEXA>The school has a <COLLOINEXA>dress code</COLLOINEXA> for its students.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the company’s <COLLOINEXA>code of conduct</COLLOINEXA></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>statute</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a law that has been officially approved by a parliament, council etc, and written down</DEF><THESEXA>The statute banned corporal punishment.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ordinance</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>a law, made by a city or town, that forbids or restricts an activity</DEF><THESEXA>A local ordinance limited speed in the parks to ten miles an hour.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-22b7"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rumour</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>rumor</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>information or a story that is passed from one person to another and which may or may not be true</DEF><THESEXA>The band denied the rumours that they may be splitting up.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The truth finally came out after months of rumour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve heard rumours about a ghost in the building.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>speculation</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>a situation in which a lot of people are talking about something that is happening, especially something that is happening in politics or public life, and trying to guess what the truth is</DEF><THESEXA>There was a great deal of speculation about a possible merger involving Belgium’s largest banks.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The report <COLLOINEXA>fuelled speculation</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>caused more speculation</GLOSS> that he was about to resign.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His future as a player has been the subject of <COLLOINEXA>intense speculation</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gossip</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>things that people say about what they think has happened in other people’s private lives, which is usually not true</DEF><THESEXA>She tells me all the latest gossip from the office.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The magazine was full of gossip about celebrities.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You shouldn’t believe every <COLLOINEXA>piece of gossip</COLLOINEXA> you hear.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>talk</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>something that people talk about a lot but which is not official</DEF><THESEXA>The government has dismissed talk of a military strike on the country.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There’s been a lot of talk of him resigning.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hearsay</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>something that you have heard from someone else, but cannot prove whether it is true or untrue – often used in legal contexts</DEF><THESEXA>All the accounts were based on hearsay rather than eye-witness reports.</THESEXA><THESEXA>hearsay evidence</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2269"><Section><Exponent><EXP>run</EXP><DEF>to move very quickly, by moving your legs more quickly than when you walk</DEF><THESEXA>My five-year-old son runs everywhere.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I go running twice a week.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>jog</EXP><DEF>to run quite slowly for exercise over a long distance</DEF><THESEXA>A few people were jogging in the park.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>race/dash</EXP><DEF>to run somewhere as quickly as you can, especially because you have to do something urgently</DEF><THESEXA>He dashed across the road to the police station.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We raced to the bus stop and got there just in time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sprint</EXP><DEF>to run as fast as you can for a short distance</DEF><THESEXA>I saw the runners sprinting past.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He sprinted up the stairs.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tear</EXP><DEF>to run very quickly and without really looking where you are going, because you are in a hurry</DEF><THESEXA>He tore down the street and around the corner.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>charge</EXP><DEF>to run quickly and with a lot of energy, so that you might knock down anyone or anything that gets in your way</DEF><THESEXA>They all charged out of the school gates at 4 o'clock.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Dennis charged through the door into my office.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take to your heels</EXP><DEF>to start running away very quickly, especially to escape or because you are afraid</DEF><THESEXA>The men took to their heels as soon as they saw the police.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>leg it</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to run away very quickly, in order to escape from someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>I legged it before the cops came.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lope</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to run easily with long steps – used especially about tall people with long legs</DEF><THESEXA>John loped across the street to meet me.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>animals running</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>trot</EXP><DEF>to run fairly slowly, taking short steps – used especially about horses and dogs</DEF><THESEXA>A little dog was trotting behind her.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gallop</EXP><DEF>if a horse gallops, it runs very quickly</DEF><THESEXA>The horse galloped off across the field.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bolt</EXP><DEF>to suddenly run somewhere very fast, especially in order to escape</DEF><THESEXA>Suddenly a fox bolted out from beneath a hedge.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2256"><Section><Exponent><EXP>rush</EXP><DEF>to move very quickly, especially because you need to be somewhere soon</DEF><THESEXA>He was rushing out of his office in order to go to a meeting.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There’s no need to rush - we have plenty of time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hurry</EXP><DEF>to do something or go somewhere more quickly than usual, especially because there is not much time</DEF><THESEXA>People hurried into stores to escape the rain.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You ll have to hurry or we 'll be late for breakfast</THESEXA><THESEXA>I hurried through the rest of my workout and showered as quickly as I could.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>race</EXP><DEF> to go somewhere as fast as you can</DEF><THESEXA>She raced downstairs to tell her mother.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He raced back to his car and called for help.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tear</EXP><DEF>to run very quickly and without really looking where you are going, because you are in a hurry</DEF><THESEXA>I saw two boys tearing across the field towards the tree.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He tore down the stairs and out of the house.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They tore out of the building.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dash</EXP><DEF> to run somewhere very fast, especially only a short distance</DEF><THESEXA>Bob dashed across the road to his friend’s house.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her heart was pumping furiously as she dashed through the kitchen to the front door.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I dashed outside to try to rescue the unfortunate creature.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hustle</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to hurry when you are doing something or going somewhere</DEF><THESEXA>You better hustle or you’re going to miss the school bus.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hasten</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to hurry somewhere, especially because you need to do something</DEF><THESEXA>Suddenly frightened, she hastened back to where her friends were standing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She took a deep breath and then hastened after him.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-223c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>sad</EXP><DEF>not happy</DEF><THESEXA>She felt sad as she waved goodbye.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a sad and Maybe you’ll win next time.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They sat in glum silence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wistful</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>looking a little sad and sad</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>miserable</EXP><DEF>very sad, especially because you are lonely, cold, ill, or upset – used about people and periods of time</DEF><THESEXA>I felt miserable and blamed <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2217"><Section><Exponent><EXP>sadness</EXP><DEF>a sad feeling, caused especially when a happy time is ending, or when you feel sorry about someone else’s unhappiness</DEF><THESEXA>Charles felt a great sense of sadness and loss.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I noticed a little sadness in her eyes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unhappiness</EXP><DEF>the unhappy feeling you have when you are in a very difficult or unpleasant situation, especially when this lasts for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>After years of unhappiness, she finally decided to leave him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was a tense, nervous young woman, whose deep unhappiness was obvious to all those around her.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You do not know how much pain and unhappiness you have caused.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sorrow</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the feeling of being very sad, especially because someone has died or because terrible things have happened to you</DEF><THESEXA>There seemed to be nowhere to go to be alone with her sorrow.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His heart was filled with great sorrow after her death.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>misery</EXP><DEF>great unhappiness, caused especially by living or working in very bad conditions</DEF><THESEXA>The cold weather is with us again and the misery of the homeless is increasing. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Thousands of families were destined to a life of misery. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The misery and pain he caused were, for him, merely a measure of his success. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>despair</EXP><DEF>a feeling of great unhappiness, because very bad things have happened and you have no hope that anything will change</DEF><THESEXA>At the end of the month, she still had no job and was tired, frustrated, and close to despair.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grief</EXP><DEF>great sadness that you feel when someone you love has died</DEF><THESEXA>He was overcome with grief when his wife died.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>heartache</EXP><DEF>a strong feeling of great sadness, especially because you miss someone you love</DEF><THESEXA>She remembered the heartache of the first Christmas spent away from her sons.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>depression</EXP><DEF>a mental illness that makes someone feel so unhappy that they  have no energy or hope for the future, and they cannot live a normal life</DEF><THESEXA>He slipped into a depression in which he hardly ate or even left his room.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>despondency</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a feeling of being very unhappy and without hope</DEF><THESEXA>She felt useless, and this contributed to her despondency.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>melancholy</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a feeling of sadness, that you feel even though there is no particular reason for it</DEF><THESEXA>Modigliani expressed his melancholy through his painting.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-21d8"><Section><SECHEADING>not in danger</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>safe</EXP><DEF>not in danger of being harmed, lost, or stolen. Also used about a place where someone or something is safe</DEF><THESEXA>I don’t feel safe around here.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Keep your valuables in a safe place.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>secure</EXP><DEF>if something is secure, it is safe from thieves and criminals. Also used about a place where something is safe</DEF><THESEXA>How do I know that my personal details are secure?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The money is in a very secure place.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>well protected</EXP><DEF>not likely to be damaged or harmed by something</DEF><THESEXA>The equipment was well protected from the rain.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Always make sure that you are well protected from the sun.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>out of harm’s way</EXP><DEF>in a place where someone or something cannot be hurt or damaged</DEF><THESEXA>She put the glass vases on the top shelf, out of harm’s way.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Make sure that he stays out of harm’s way.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>not harmed or damaged</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>safe</EXP><DEF>not harmed or damaged</DEF><THESEXA>The children are all safe.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>OK/all right</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>safe and not hurt</DEF><THESEXA>I was glad to hear that you were OK.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unharmed</EXP><DEF>not hurt</DEF><THESEXA>The kidnappers released the young man unharmed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unscathed</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ʌnˈskeɪðd</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>not hurt or damaged, after an accident, an attack, or a dangerous experience – used when you are surprised by this</DEF><THESEXA>The baby escaped unscathed. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Miraculously, the old part of the city remained unscathed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in one piece</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>not harmed or damaged, especially after a journey or a dangerous experience</DEF><THESEXA>I’m glad you’re home in one piece.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>out of danger</EXP><DEF>safe, after being in a dangerous situation</DEF><THESEXA>As soon as they were out of danger, they stopped for a rest. </THESEXA><THESEXA>His doctors say he is out of danger.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-4888"><Section><Exponent><EXP>salary</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>the money that you receive regularly for doing your job, usually paid to you every month. <DEFBOLD>Salary</DEFBOLD> is usually used for professional jobs such as teachers, managers, doctors etc</DEF><THESEXA>Nurses earn a basic salary of £21,250.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her salary is paid directly into her bank account.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pay</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>the money you receive for doing a job</DEF><THESEXA>The pay is pretty good.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Teachers are asking for higher pay.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wages</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>plural</GRAM><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>wage</LEXVAR><GRAM>singular</GRAM></Variant><DEF>the money that someone is paid every week by their employer, especially someone who works in a shop or factory</DEF><THESEXA>Practically all my wages go on housing and transport to work. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The average weekly wage was £350.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a wage increase</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>income</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>the money that you receive regularly for doing your job, and from things such as a business or investments</DEF><THESEXA>The amount of tax you have to pay depends on your income.</THESEXA><THESEXA>People <COLLOINEXA>on low incomes</COLLOINEXA> are finding it difficult to pay their fuel bills.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>earnings</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>plural</GRAM><DEF>the total amount of money you earn from any job you do – used especially when the amount is different each month or year</DEF><THESEXA>The average worker’s earnings have not kept up with inflation.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-21b6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>the same</EXP><DEF>used to say that two people, things, events etc are exactly like each other</DEF><THESEXA>The houses on the street all look the same.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They were doing the same jobs as the men, but being paid less.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>just like/exactly painting.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>equal</EXP><DEF>two or more amounts, totals, levels etc that are equal are the same as each other</DEF><THESEXA>Spend an equal amount of time on each essay question.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be no different from sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to be the same, even though you expect them to be different</DEF><THESEXA>People often think that movie stars are
