apart</LEXVAR></Variant><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if you  can’t tell the difference  between two people or things, or if you  can’t tell them apart, they look, sound, or seem exactly the same to you</DEF><THESEXA>Emma and Louise sound so alike on the phone that I can’t tell the difference.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-217b"><HEADING>Senses 1 & 2</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>sand</EXP><DEF>a substance that forms beaches and deserts, or an area of this substance</DEF><THESEXA>sunbathers lying on the sand</THESEXA><THESEXA>He could see several small figures out on the sands. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>beach</EXP><DEF>an area of sand or small stones at the edge of the sea or a lake</DEF><THESEXA>the island's golden beaches</THESEXA><THESEXA>How about a day at the beach?</THESEXA><THESEXA>a pebble beach</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>desert</EXP><DEF>a large area of land where it is always very hot and dry, and there is a lot of sand</DEF><THESEXA>the Sahara desert</THESEXA><THESEXA>They crossed the desert by camel. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dune/sand dune</EXP><DEF>a hill made of sand near the sea or in the desert</DEF><THESEXA>We went for a long walk along the dunes. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The dunes are home to a wide variety of birds and plants.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2165"><Section><Exponent><EXP>satisfaction</EXP><DEF>a feeling of happiness or pleasure, especially because you have achieved something good or useful</DEF><THESEXA>Most teachers take great pride and satisfaction in their work.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pride</EXP><DEF>a feeling of pleasure and satisfaction that you get when you or someone connected with you has achieved something good</DEF><THESEXA>Her father’s pride in her accomplishments was clear.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I was blushing with pride because I had been chosen to be on the team. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>contentment</EXP><DEF>the feeling of being happy and satisfied because you have what you want or need. <DEFBOLD>Contentment</DEFBOLD> is rather a formal use</DEF><THESEXA>Only when you truly know yourself can you find contentment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He sat back with a look of deep contentment on his face.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fulfilment</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><AmEVariant><LEXVAR>fulfillment</LEXVAR></AmEVariant><DEF>a feeling of being satisfied and happy with your life. <DEFBOLD>Fulfilment</DEFBOLD> is rather a formal use</DEF><THESEXA>Some women find fulfillment in being a mother, but this is not true for all women.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2140"><Section><Exponent><EXP>satisfactory</EXP><DEF>good enough – often used when something reaches a fairly good standard, but is not of a high standard</DEF><THESEXA>Her grades are satisfactory.</THESEXA><THESEXA>For a beginner, this camera produces satisfactory results.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>all right/OK</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>not bad, but not very good</DEF><THESEXA>The meal was all right, but rather expensive.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘How was the film?’ ‘It was OK.’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reasonable</EXP><DEF>fairly good</DEF><THESEXA>a reasonable standard of living</THESEXA><THESEXA>The quality of the food was reasonable.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>acceptable</EXP><DEF>if something is acceptable to you, you think it is good enough and you are willing to take it</DEF><THESEXA>an acceptable offer </THESEXA><THESEXA>an acceptable level of risk </THESEXA><THESEXA>They can't find a solution that is acceptable to both sides. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>adequate</EXP><DEF>enough in quantity, or of a good enough standard. <DEFBOLD>Adequate</DEFBOLD> sounds rather formal and is used especially in official contexts</DEF><THESEXA>an adequate supply of drinking water</THESEXA><THESEXA> adequate standards of hygiene </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>decent</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>good enough in quality – used especially when something is as good as most other things</DEF><THESEXA>I want my kids to get a decent education.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Where can I get a decent cup of coffee?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The food’s decent and the service is good.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>passable</EXP><DEF>satisfactory, but not of the best quality – used especially about food and drink, or someone’s skill at doing something. <DEFBOLD>Passable </DEFBOLD> sounds rather formal</DEF><THESEXA>a passable French wine</THESEXA><THESEXA>His Japanese was passable.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a passable imitation of Barack Obama</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be up to scratch</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be of a good enough standard</DEF><THESEXA>His work wasn’t up to scratch.</THESEXA><THESEXA>None of the hotels they suggested were up to scratch.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>will do</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to be good enough for a particular purpose</DEF><THESEXA>Any kind of paper will do. </THESEXA><THESEXA>‘How about Ken?’ ‘I suppose he’ll do.’</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-213e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>satisfied</EXP><DEF>feeling that something is as good as it should be, or that something has happened in the way that you want</DEF><THESEXA>The teacher is satisfied with his progress.</THESEXA><THESEXA>another satisfied customer</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>happy</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>satisfied. <DEFBOLD>Happy</DEFBOLD> is very commonly used instead of <DEFBOLD>satisfied </DEFBOLD> in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>The boss seems happy with my work.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m happy to work part-time until the kids are older.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pleased</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>very satisfied</DEF><THESEXA>I’m pleased with the results.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He came out looking pleased with himself.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>content</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>satisfied with what you are doing, so that you do not want to change anything</DEF><THESEXA>She seemed content to just sit and watch the others.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sam was quite content with his life on the farm.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fulfilled</EXP><GRAM>not usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>feeling that you have achieved enough in your life, and that you do not need things that are more interesting, important, or useful</DEF><THESEXA>I enjoy being a mother, but to feel fulfilled I need to work too.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-213d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>satisfying</EXP><DEF>making you feel satisfied, because you have got or achieved something you wanted</DEF><THESEXA>There’s something very satisfying about baking your own bread.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Teaching can be immensely satisfying.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rewarding</EXP><DEF>satisfying, because you feel you are doing something useful for yourself or for other people</DEF><THESEXA>Nursing is a very rewarding job.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It's been hard work, but very rewarding.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fulfilling</EXP><DEF>making you feel satisfied and happy with your life, because you are using your abilities in a useful way. <DEFBOLD>Fulfilling</DEFBOLD> and <DEFBOLD>rewarding</DEFBOLD> are very close in meaning. <DEFBOLD>Fulfilling</DEFBOLD> is used more about your feelings about your life in general, and that something makes you feel a happier person</DEF><THESEXA>We want to provide a more fulfilling life for people who are mentally ill.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a happier and more fulfilling relationship </THESEXA><THESEXA>What should be a happy and fulfilling experience all too often is not.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pleasing</EXP><DEF>making you feel pleased and satisfied, because something is good</DEF><THESEXA>The results of the training scheme were very pleasing.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gratifying</EXP><DEF>making you feel pleased and satisfied, especially because people like what you have done, or you feel that you were right about something</DEF><THESEXA>Their enthusiastic reception of his paintings was very gratifying.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It's gratifying to know that these videos are appreciated.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It is extremely gratifying to hear that something I did affected someone's life in such a positive way.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2127"><Section><Exponent><EXP>save</EXP><DEF>to gradually collect money by not spending all the money you have, especially when you regularly put some of it in a bank</DEF><THESEXA>She doesn’t earn much, but she still manages to save a few dollars each week.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We’re saving for a deposit to buy a house.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>set/put aside</EXP><DEF>to regularly save part of the money you earn, especially over a long period of time</DEF><THESEXA>You should start setting aside part of your earnings as retirement savings.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scrimp and save</EXP><DEF>to try to save money by spending less on the things you need and by saving what you can, especially when you do not earn very much</DEF><THESEXA>My parents scrimped and saved for years to send me to college.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>squirrel sth away</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to keep something, especially money, in a safe place to be used later</DEF><THESEXA>I wanted to surprise her, so I squirreled away a couple of dollars a week to spend on a present.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>economize</EXP><DEF>to spend less money by buying only the things that you really need, or by buying cheaper things</DEF><THESEXA>Weddings can be expensive, but you can economize by doing some things yourself.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2107" fullpage="yes"><Section><SECHEADING>to say something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>say</EXP><DEF>to tell someone something, using words</DEF><THESEXA>‘I really ought to go,’ she said.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Lauren said she’d probably be late.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>state</EXP><DEF>to say something, especially in a definite or formal way – used in official contexts</DEF><THESEXA>The witness stated that he had never seen the woman before.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Please state your name and address.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>announce</EXP><DEF>to publicly tell people about something</DEF><THESEXA>The chairman announced his resignation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The results will be announced tomorrow.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We will announce the winners next Sunday. </THESEXA><THESEXA>They were announcing the train times over the loudspeaker system.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>declare</EXP><DEF>to say something very firmly</DEF><THESEXA>‘My personal life is none of your business,’ she declared.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mention</EXP><DEF>to talk about someone or something, especially without giving many details</DEF><THESEXA>Did Tom mention anything about what happened at school?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Your name was mentioned!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>express</EXP><DEF>to let someone know your feelings by putting them into words</DEF><THESEXA>Young children often find it difficult to express their emotions.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>comment</EXP><DEF>to say what your opinion is about someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>The prime minister was asked to comment on the crisis.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>note/remark</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to say that you have noticed that something is true – used especially in formal writing</DEF><THESEXA>We have already noted that most old people live alone.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Someone once remarked that the problem with computers is that they only give you answers.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>add</EXP><DEF>to say something more, after what has already been said</DEF><THESEXA>He added that he thought it could be done fairly cheaply.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>point out</EXP><DEF>to mention something that seems particularly important or relevant</DEF><THESEXA>Dr Graham points out that most children show some signs of abnormal behaviour. </THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s worth pointing out that few people actually die of this disease.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>air</EXP><DEF>to talk about your opinions, worries, or the things you disagree about</DEF><THESPROPFORM>air your views/grievances/differences</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The programme will give listeners the chance to air their views about immigration.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Workers were able to air their grievances.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>voice</EXP><DEF>to talk publicly about your feelings or about whether you approve or disapprove of something</DEF><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><THESPROPFORM>voice concern/support/doubt/fears etc</THESPROPFORM><THESEXA>The president has already voiced his support for the proposal.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She voiced concern for the safety of the hostages.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>different ways of saying something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>whisper</EXP><DEF>to say something very quietly, using your breath rather than your full voice</DEF><THESEXA>‘Don’t wake the baby,’ Jenny whispered.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mumble</EXP><DEF>to say something quietly without pronouncing the words clearly</DEF><THESEXA>He mumbled his thanks.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mutter</EXP><DEF>to say something quietly, especially when you are annoyed but do not want someone to hear you complaining</DEF><THESEXA>‘This is ridiculous,’ he muttered under his breath.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She muttered something about having to go home early. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>murmur</EXP><DEF>to say something in a soft slow gentle voice</DEF><THESEXA>She stroked his hair and murmured, ‘Don’t worry. You’ll be all right.’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>growl</EXP><DEF>to say something in a low angry voice</DEF><THESEXA>‘As I was saying,’ Lewis growled, ‘it needs to be finished today.’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>snarl</EXP><DEF>to say something in a nasty angry way</DEF><THESEXA>‘Get out of my way!’ he snarled.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>exclaim</EXP><DEF>to say something suddenly and loudly</DEF><THESEXA>‘How beautiful!’ she exclaimed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blurt out</EXP><DEF>to suddenly say something without thinking, especially something embarrassing or secret</DEF><THESEXA>It was partly nervousness that had made him blurt out the question.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stammer/stutter</EXP><DEF>to speak with a lot of pauses and repeated sounds, because you have a speech problem, or because you are nervous or excited</DEF><THESEXA>‘I’ll, I’ll only be a m-moment,’ he stammered.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-20ef"><Section><Exponent><EXP>schedule</EXP><DEF>a detailed plan of what someone is going to do and when they will do it, especially someone important</DEF><THESEXA>He has a very busy schedule.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The president’s schedule includes a two-day visit to St Petersburg.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>timetable</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><AmEVariant><LEXVAR>schedule</LEXVAR></AmEVariant><DEF>a written list that shows the exact times when something will happen, for example when planes or buses leave, or when classes at school take place</DEF><THESEXA>The timetable said there was another train at 6.15.</THESEXA><THESEXA>According to the class schedule, English 104 is at 10 am in Royce Hall. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>programme</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><AmEVariant><LEXVAR>program</LEXVAR></AmEVariant><DEF>a plan that shows the order of activities at a ceremony, sports meeting, public event etc</DEF><THESEXA>Who is organizing the conference programme?</THESEXA><THESEXA>the next event on the program</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>agenda</EXP><DEF>a list of the subjects that will be discussed at a meeting</DEF><THESEXA>Attached is the agenda for the budget committee meeting.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the final item on the agenda</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>timeline</EXP><DEF>a plan for when things will happen or how long you think something will take – used especially in business English</DEF><THESEXA>The timeline for the project is less than six months from start to finish.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What is the usual timeline from the sale of a house to the day you can move in?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>itinerary</EXP><DEF>a plan or list of the places you will visit on a journey, usually with the date or time that you will be there</DEF><THESEXA>The Travel Pack includes a detailed itinerary, maps, and a travel guide.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Let me know your itinerary when you know it. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-20c0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>the sea</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>the large area of salty water that covers much of the Earth’s surface</DEF><THESEXA>She lives by the sea.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The sea was very rough.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the ocean</EXP><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><DEF>the large area of salty water that waves</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bay </EXP><DEF>an area of sea that is partly enclosed by a curve in the land</DEF><THESEXA>I swam across the bay.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the Bay of Biscay</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gulf</EXP><DEF>a very large area of sea partly enclosed by land</DEF><THESEXA>the Gulf of Mexico</THESEXA><THESEXA>oil from the Gulf <GLOSS>the area of water near Iran, Saudi Arabia etc</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tide</EXP><DEF>the regular rising and falling of the level of the sea</DEF><THESEXA>Is the tide going out or coming in ?</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>High tide</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>when the sea is at its highest level</GLOSS>is at 4 o'clock in the afternoon.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>low tide</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>when the sea is at its lowest level</GLOSS></THESEXA><THESEXA>The rocks are visible at low tide.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wave</EXP><DEF>a line of raised water that moves across the surface of the sea</DEF><THESEXA>The waves were crashing against the rocks.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-3c8f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>search </EXP><DEF>to look carefully for someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>Detectives continue to search for clues.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She searched through all his clothes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>look for sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to try to get someone or something you want or need</DEF><THESEXA>I’m looking for something to wear for my sister’s wedding.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The band is looking for a singer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s decided to look for a new career.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>try to find sb/sth</EXP><DEF>used especially when someone or something is difficult to find</DEF><THESEXA>I spent half an hour trying to find a parking space.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was in the kitchen, trying to find something to eat that wasn’t raw carrot.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>seek</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to try to find something or someone – used especially about jobs, help, or information. Also used in newspaper advertisements when trying to find a suitable person</DEF><THESEXA>They went there seeking work.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She decided to seek help.</THESEXA><THESEXA> Tall blond 18-year-old male seeks female 17+ for friendship.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hunt</EXP><DEF>to look for someone or something. Used when you look very carefully and thoroughly, or in the phrase <DEFBOLD>house/job/bargain hunting</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>I’ve hunted everywhere, but I can’t find a recipe for French onion soup. </THESEXA><THESEXA>She’s gone out bargain-hunting in the sales.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be on the lookout for sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to be continuously looking for someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>I’m always on the lookout for a good bargain.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>leave no stone unturned</EXP><DEF>to look for someone or something in every possible place</DEF><THESEXA>The police say they will leave no stone unturned in their search for the killer.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-206c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>secret</EXP><DEF>known about by only a few people, who have agreed not to tell anyone else</DEF><THESEXA>a secret meeting place</THESEXA><THESEXA>The details of the proposal must remain secret.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>confidential</EXP><DEF>used about information, especially in business or government, that is secret and not intended to be shown or told to other people</DEF><THESEXA>a highly confidential report</THESEXA><THESEXA>Employees’ personal details are treated as strictly confidential.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>classified</EXP><DEF>used about information that the government has ordered to be kept secret from most people</DEF><THESEXA>He was accused of passing on classified information to the Russians in the 1950s.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sensitive</EXP><DEF>used about information that is kept secret because there would be problems if the wrong people knew it</DEF><THESEXA>A teenager managed to hack into sensitive US Air Force files.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>covert</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>used about things that are about things that are done secretly by the police in order to catch criminals or find out information</DEF><THESEXA>Detectives arrested the suspect after a five-day undercover operation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>underground</EXP><DEF>an underground organization or newspaper is one that operates or is produced secretly and opposes the government</DEF><THESEXA>Her affair</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hush-hush</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used about information or activities that are kept officially secret</DEF><THESEXA>He was put in charge of some hush-hush military project.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve no idea what he does – it’s all very hush-hush.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><ThesColloBox><SECHEADING>COLLOCATIONS <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-2054"><Section><Exponent><EXP>see</EXP><DEF>to notice something with your eyes, usually without planning to. Also used when saying that you watched a particular programme, film, game etc</DEF><THESEXA>Have you seen my keys anywhere?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Did you see the basketball game last night?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>look at sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to keep your eyes pointed toward someone or something, especially someone or something that is not moving</DEF><THESEXA>I looked at the map.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was looking at him in a strange way.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>notice</EXP><DEF>to see something interesting or unusual</DEF><THESEXA>I noticed a police car outside their house.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spot</EXP><DEF>to suddenly see something, especially something you are looking for</DEF><THESEXA>Nick spotted the advertisement in the paper.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>catch sight of/catch a glimpse of</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>glimpse</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>to suddenly see someone or something for a short time, usually not clearly</DEF><THESEXA>I caught sight of him in the hotel lobby, and followed him out the door.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He glimpsed her face as she went into the courtroom.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make out sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to see something, but only with difficulty</DEF><THESEXA>Ahead, I could just make out the figure of a woman.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>witness</EXP><DEF>to see something happen, especially a crime or an accident</DEF><THESEXA>The police are asking anyone who witnessed the attack to come forward.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>observe</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to see and pay attention to something</DEF><THESEXA>Officers observed him driving at 100 mph.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sight</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to suddenly see something or someone from a long distance, especially when you have been looking for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>The missing boys were sighted by a rescue helicopter.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The crew finally sighted land.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>Unable to see</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>blind</EXP><DEF>unable to see anything</DEF><THESEXA>She has been blind from birth.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>partially-sighted</EXP><DEF>not able to see things very well, although not completely blind</DEF><THESEXA>Good lighting can be very important for partially sighted people.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>visually handicapped/impaired</EXP><DEF>completely blind or not able to see very much – used especially in official reports, forms etc</DEF><THESEXA>a special school for visually impaired children</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1fd6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>appear</EXP><DEF>a more formal word for ‘seem’</DEF><THESEXA>These reports appear to be unfounded.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>look</EXP><DEF>to seem to be something, often because of what can be seen</DEF><THESEXA>The future looks bleak.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He looked pleased.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sound</EXP><DEF>used to say that something or someone seems to be something because of what you have heard or read about them, or because of the noise or voice that you hear</DEF><THESEXA>This study sounds very ambitious.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They heard what sounded like a blow.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come across as sth</EXP><DEF>to seem to have particular qualities</DEF><THESEXA>He comes across as a very sensitive man.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1fc2"><Section><Exponent><EXP>selfish</EXP><DEF>thinking only about what you need or want, and not thinking about how other people feel or what other people want – used especially when criticizing a person or their behaviour</DEF><THESEXA>Amy, don’t be selfish. Let the others have a turn.</THESEXA><THESEXA>'I’m not doing this for selfish reasons,' he said.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a very selfish thing to do. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>egocentric</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>self-centred</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO><LEXVAR>self-centered</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>believing that what you do and think is the most important thing, and not paying attention to what anyone else thinks or does – used when describing someone’s personality</DEF><THESEXA>Teenagers can be highly egocentric, and sometimes find it difficult to see another person’s point of view.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Children start off as self-centred little beings and they do not naturally think of the other person.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>egotistical</EXP><DEF>believing that you are better or more important than other people</DEF><THESEXA>Like many famous writers I’ve met, he was arrogant and egotistical.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He’s just another young player who has gone out of control in the increasingly egotistical world of professional sports.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>self-serving</EXP><DEF>only thinking of getting advantages for yourself</DEF><THESEXA>People’s fears are exaggerated by self-serving politicians.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They described the report as ‘self-serving and contradictory’. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>think of nobody but yourself</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>only think about yourself</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to only think of what you want to do, and not consider what other people want – often used when criticizing someone who does this</DEF><THESEXA>The trouble with Alan is that he thinks of nobody but himself.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Before I had children, I only thought about myself and what I wanted.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>it’s always me me me!</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when criticizing someone for being selfish</DEF><THESEXA>It’s always me, me, me with you and I have had enough. I don’t need this – you’re welcome to yourself, my dear.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1fa7"><Section><Exponent><EXP>sell</EXP><DEF>to give something to someone in exchange for money</DEF><THESEXA>He sold his motorcycle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The shop sells old furniture.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you sell books on gardening?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>export</EXP><DEF>to send goods to another country to be sold</DEF><THESEXA>Which countries export oil to the United States?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>deal in sth</EXP><DEF>to buy and sell a particular type of goods as part of your business</DEF><THESEXA>He deals in antiques.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put sth up for sale/put sth on the market</EXP><DEF>to make something available to be bought</DEF><THESEXA>When the painting was first put up for sale, no one thought that it would be worth so much money.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The farm was put up for sale.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sell up</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>to sell your house or your business so that you can move to a different place or do something different</DEF><THESEXA>They’re thinking of selling up and moving to Canada.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>auction sth/sell sth at auction</EXP><DEF>to sell things at a special event to the person who offers the most money</DEF><THESEXA>The contents of his home will be auctioned.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>flog</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to sell something, especially something that is of low quality</DEF><THESEXA>A man at the market was flogging £10 watches.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>peddle</EXP><DEF>to sell cheap things in the street. Also used about selling illegal drugs and <NonDV><REFHWD>pornography</REFHWD></NonDV></DEF><THESEXA>Street vendors peddled American and British cigarettes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>People who peddle drugs to children should be severely punished.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>traffic in sth</EXP><DEF>to buy and sell large quantities of illegal goods or people</DEF><THESEXA>They trafficked in illegal weapons.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The gang were involved in people-trafficking.</THESEXA><THESEXA>drug-trafficking</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1f90"><Section><SECHEADING>to make something separate</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>separate</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to divide something into two or more parts or groups, or to divide one type of thing from another. You use <DEFBOLD>separate</DEFBOLD> especially when saying that the parts are different from each other</DEF><THESEXA> Motorola is planning to separate the company into two public companies.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The items are separated into recyclable and non-recyclable waste.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>divide</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to make something become two or more parts or groups</DEF><THESEXA>The teacher divided us into groups.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The money was divided between them.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The house is divided into three apartments.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>split</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to separate something into two or more groups, parts etc – used especially when each part is equal in size</DEF><THESEXA>The class was split into groups of six.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>break sth up</EXP><POS>phr v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF> to separate something into several smaller parts, especially to make it easier to deal with</DEF><THESEXA>The phone company was broken up to encourage competition.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police used tear gas to break up the crowd.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>segregate</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to separate one group of people from others because of race, sex, religion etc</DEF><THESEXA>Schools were separate</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>separate</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>to divide into different parts, especially in a natural way</DEF><THESEXA>A watery liquid separates from the milk during cheesemaking.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>split</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>to separate into two or more parts or groups – used especially when each part is equal in size</DEF><THESEXA>What happens when an atom splits?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>break up</EXP><POS>phr v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>to separate into several smaller parts</DEF><THESEXA>In spring, the icebergs begin to break up.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1f57" fullpage="yes"><Section><SECHEADING>very bad</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>serious</EXP><DEF>very bad – used about problems, accidents, illnesses, or crimes</DEF><THESEXA>Violent crime is a serious problem in and around the capital.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The boy was taken to hospital with serious head injuries.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Fortunately, the damage to the car was not serious.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>severe</EXP><DEF>very serious – used about problems, injuries, and illnesses</DEF><THESEXA>He suffered severe injuries in a car crash.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The problem became so severe that they had to bring water in from other countries. </THESEXA><THESEXA>severe epilepsy</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grave</EXP><DEF>used about a situation that is very serious and worrying, especially because it is dangerous or seems likely to get worse</DEF><THESEXA>A thick fog descended and I knew that we were in grave danger.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The situation is grave – war now seems inevitable.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>acute</EXP><DEF>used about an illness, problem, or situation that has become very serious or dangerous, and needs to be dealt with quickly</DEF><THESEXA>She was taken to the hospital suffering from acute appendicitis.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In San Diego, the shortage of skilled workers is acute.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>desperate</EXP><DEF>used about a situation or problem that is very serious or <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1f23"><Section><Exponent><EXP>several</EXP><DEF>more than a few people or things, but  not a large number</DEF><THESEXA>She’s been to Japan several times.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve read several of his books.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>a number of sth</EXP><DEF>several. <DEFBOLD>A number of</DEFBOLD> sounds more formal than <DEFBOLD>several</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>We have received a number of complaints about last night’s programme.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are a number of different airlines to choose from.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>quite a few</EXP><DEF>several – used when emphasizing that there are rather a lot of people, things etc. <DEFBOLD>Quite a few</DEFBOLD> sounds more informal than <DEFBOLD>several</DEFBOLD> and is more commonly used in spoken English</DEF><THESEXA>Quite a few people were already in the pool.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She made quite a few enemies.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It took him quite a few days to make up his mind.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1f0d"><Section><SECHEADING>person</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>shake</EXP><DEF>if a person or part of their body shakes, they make small sudden continuous movements from side to side or up and down, especially because they are very frightened, cold, ill etc</DEF><THESEXA>Suddenly he started to shake. ‘Don’t ever scare me like that again!’ he whispered.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The poor girl was shaking.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shudder</EXP><DEF>to shake for a short time, especially because you think of something very unpleasant, or because you feel frightened or cold</DEF><THESEXA>Corbett shuddered when he thought of what might have happened to them.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I shuddered when I read the article.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was still shuddering with the cold.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She clung to him, shuddering with emotion. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tremble</EXP><DEF>to shake slightly in a way that you cannot control, especially because you are frightened, worried, or angry</DEF><THESEXA>Ernest opened the letter in silence, his hands trembling.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her whole body trembled with fear.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He hadn’t dared to move. He was trembling with shock. </THESEXA><THESEXA>‘I won’t be coming back,’ she said, her body trembling with anger.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shiver</EXP><DEF>to shake slightly, especially that.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>quiver</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to shake slightly and continuously because you are very worried or excited – used especially about someone’s lips, mouth, or body</DEF><THESEXA>Her bottom lip began to quiver, and she turned away to hide her tears.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Alice’s eyes began to fill with tears and her mouth heels.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rock</EXP><DEF>to move gently backwards and forwards or from side to side</DEF><THESEXA>He rocked to and fro in his chair.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>object/vehicle/the ground etc</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>shake</EXP><DEF>to move suddenly from side to side or up and down, usually with a lot of force</DEF><THESEXA>The floor shook from a distant explosion.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The walls were still shaking.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The trees were shaking in the wind.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rattle</EXP><DEF>to shake and make a noise</DEF><THESEXA>The windows rattled in the wind.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The train was rattling over the atoms vibrate at different frequencies.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wobble</EXP><DEF>to move unsteadily from side to side</DEF><THESEXA>The bike began to wobble alarmingly as she fought to control it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The cup wobbled and he grabbed it to stop it from falling.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rock</EXP><DEF>to move gently backwards and forwards or from side to side</DEF><THESEXA>The trailer rocked in the wind.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The boat was rocking from side to side with the <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1efe"><Section><Exponent><EXP>shame</EXP><DEF>the feeling you have when you feel guilty and embarrassed because you, or someone who is close to you, have done something wrong</DEF><THESEXA>She never overcame the shame of having abandoned her children.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He remembered his angry words with a deep sense of shame.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Following the scandal, Garrison resigned in shame.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>humiliation</EXP><DEF>a feeling of shame and embarrassment because you have been made to look weak or stupid in front of other people</DEF><THESEXA>What really upset me was the humiliation of having to ask her for money.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He suffered the humiliation of defeat in the first round of the competition.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dishonour</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>dishonor</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the loss of other people’s respect because you have done something bad, or you have been unsuccessful</DEF><THESEXA>His comments have <COLLOINEXA>brought</COLLOINEXA> shame and <COLLOINEXA>dishonour on</COLLOINEXA> him and his profession.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is no dishonour in failure when you have done everything you possibly can to succeed. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stigma</EXP><DEF>the feeling that other people in society disapprove of you because of something that has happened to you, or because you feel different from most other people in some way – used especially when this seems unfair and unreasonable</DEF><THESEXA>Even when someone has been found innocent of a crime, the stigma often remains.</THESEXA><THESEXA>At first I found the stigma of being unemployed very difficult to cope with.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In many countries there is still a strong social stigma attached to homosexuality.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>great shame</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>disgrace</EXP><DEF>a complete loss of people’s respect because you have done something very bad and shocking</DEF><THESEXA>His actions <COLLOINEXA>brought disgrace on</COLLOINEXA> the family.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The players were sent home <COLLOINEXA>in disgrace</COLLOINEXA> after admitting taking drugs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Garton killed himself because he could not bear the disgrace of being charged with corruption.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ignominy</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a feeling of great shame and embarrassment because you have been made to look weak or stupid – a very formal use</DEF><THESEXA>The team suffered the ignominy of losing five games in a row.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She hoped to avoid the ignominy of having to appear in court.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1d44" fullpage="yes"><Section><SECHEADING>time</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>short</EXP><DEF>not long</DEF><THESEXA>I lived in Tokyo for a <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1cfd"><Section><Exponent><EXP>shout</EXP><DEF>to say something very loudly</DEF><THESEXA>The two men were shouting angrily at each <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1ce0" fullpage="yes"><Section><SECHEADING>let someone see </SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>show</EXP><DEF>to let someone see something, <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1ca7"><Section><Exponent><EXP>bashful</EXP><DEF>shy and not willing to say very much</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>self-conscious</EXP><DEF>worried and embarrassed about what you look like or what other people think of you</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>timid</EXP><DEF>not brave or confident</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reserved</EXP><DEF>not liking to express your emotions or talk about your problems</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>introverted</EXP><DEF>thinking a lot about your own interests, problems etc, and not liking to be with other people</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>withdrawn</EXP><DEF>quiet and not wanting to talk to other people, especially because you are unhappy</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>antisocial</EXP><DEF>not liking to meet people and talk to them</DEF></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>retiring</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>not wanting to be with other people</DEF></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-78ef4d03.118e0988fe1.5d4c"><Section><SECHEADING>something that you see</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>sight</EXP><DEF>something that you see</DEF><THESEXA>A herd of elephants is a magnificent sight.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Even Charles cheered up at the sight of the food.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>view</EXP><DEF>the area you can see from a window or place, especially when it is beautiful</DEF><THESEXA>The view from the top of the mountain is amazing. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The hotel has great views of Lake Windermere.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We had a good view of the firework display.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>panorama</EXP><DEF>an impressive view of a very large area that stretches a long way across in front of you</DEF><THESEXA>a panorama of snow-covered hills and mountains</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>vista</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a view of a large area of beautiful scenery – used in written descriptions</DEF><THESEXA>The road around the island offers some spectacular vistas.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scene</EXP><DEF>what you see in a place, especially where people are moving around and doing things</DEF><THESEXA>Reporters described the horrific scenes which followed the bombing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His pictures are mainly of local scenes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spectacle</EXP><DEF>something that you see that is very unusual, surprising, or strange</DEF><THESEXA>It must have been an unusual spectacle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I leaned over the balcony to get a look at the spectacle below.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>visuals</EXP><GRAM>plural</GRAM><DEF>pictures or parts of a film, video etc that people can see, as opposed to the parts you can hear</DEF><THESEXA>Good visuals will help keep your audience’s attention.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1c70"><Section><Exponent><EXP>sign</EXP><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>an event, fact etc that shows that something is happening or that something <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1b67"><Section><Exponent><EXP>sleep</EXP><DEF>to rest your mind and body with your eyes closed. <DEFBOLD>Sleep </DEFBOLD> is usually used when talking about how long, how deeply, or where someone sleeps. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1b46"><Section><Exponent><EXP>sleep</EXP><DEF>the natural state of resting your mind and body, when your eyes are closed and you do not notice anything happening around you</DEF><THESEXA>I hardly got any sleep at all last night.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He woke suddenly from a <COLLOINEXA>deep sleep</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slumber/slumbers</EXP><REGISTERLAB>literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>sleep</DEF><THESEXA>She <COLLOINEXA>fell into </COLLOINEXA>an uneasy <COLLOINEXA>slumber</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He awoke from his slumbers.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shut-eye</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><REGISTERLAB>especially humorous</REGISTERLAB><DEF>sleep</DEF><THESEXA>I really need to <COLLOINEXA>get some shut-eye</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>doze</EXP><DEF>a period in which you sleep lightly, especially when you are not in your bed</DEF><THESEXA>Edward was so tired he <COLLOINEXA>fell into a doze</COLLOINEXA> on the settee.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>snooze</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a short period when you sleep lightly, especially when you are not in your bed</DEF><THESEXA>He decided to <COLLOINEXA>have a snooze</COLLOINEXA> on the sofa while he was waiting for the others to get ready.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nap</EXP><DEF>a short sleep, especially during the day</DEF><THESEXA>He’s <COLLOINEXA>taking a nap</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Helen put the baby down for a nap after lunch.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>forty winks</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a short sleep, especially during the day</DEF><THESEXA>I’m just going to <COLLOINEXA>have forty winks</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I felt a lot better after I had had forty winks.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1b1d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>slide</EXP><DEF>to move smoothly over a surface while continuing to touch it</DEF><THESEXA>The glass slid off the tray and crashed to the floor.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The kids were having fun sliding around on the polished floor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slip</EXP><DEF>to slide a short distance accidentally, and fall or lose your balance slightly</DEF><THESEXA>Be careful you don’t slip on the ice.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She slipped and broke her ankle.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>skid</EXP><DEF>to slide sideways or forwards in a way that is difficult to control – used especially about a moving vehicle</DEF><THESEXA> He slammed on the brakes and the car skidded to a halt. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Go slowly in wet or icy weather, because it’s easy to skid.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>glide</EXP><DEF>to move smoothly and quietly across water or a smooth surface, especially in a graceful way</DEF><THESEXA>A swan was gliding across the lake.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The ship glided into port.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slither</EXP><DEF>to slide in an awkward way, for example on a rough or muddy surface. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1b07"><Section><Exponent><EXP>slow</EXP><DEF>not moving quickly or not doing something quickly</DEF><THESEXA>I was always one of the slowest runners in my class.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My computer’s really slow compared to the ones at school.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gradual</EXP><DEF>happening, developing, or changing slowly over a long period of time</DEF><THESEXA>a gradual rise in the Earth’s temperature</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve noticed a gradual improvement in his work.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>leisurely</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>moving or doing something slowly, especially because you are enjoying what you are doing and do not have to hurry</DEF><THESEXA>a leisurely breakfast</THESEXA><THESEXA>They walked at a leisurely pace.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unhurried</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>moving or doing something in a slow and calm way, without rushing at all</DEF><THESEXA>She continued to listen, seeming relaxed and unhurried.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the doctor’s calm unhurried manner</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sluggish</EXP><DEF>moving or reacting more slowly than usual, especially because of a loss of power or energy. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1a97"><Section><Exponent><EXP>smile</EXP><DEF>to make your mouth curve upwards, in order to be friendly or because you are happy or amused</DEF><THESEXA>She smiled when she read his email.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘I’m delighted to meet you,' George said, smiling at the girl.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grin</EXP><DEF>to give a big smile</DEF><THESEXA>The two boys were grinning at each other.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The coach was <COLLOINEXA>grinning from ear to ear</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>was grinning a lot</GLOSS> when the team scored.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>beam</EXP><DEF>to give a big happy smile for a long time, because you are very pleased or proud</DEF><THESEXA>She beamed with pride as her son collected the award.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The wedding couple were outside beaming at the camera.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>smirk</EXP><DEF>to smile in an unpleasant way, for example because you are pleased about someone else’s bad luck or because you know something that someone else does not know</DEF><THESEXA>The children smirked when the teacher dropped all the books on the floor.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What are you smirking about?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>simper</EXP><REGISTERLAB>disapproving written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to smile in a silly and annoying way</DEF><THESEXA>a group of simpering schoolgirls</THESEXA><THESEXA>I hated the way she simpered every time a man spoke to her.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1a3c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>some</EXP><DEF>a number of people or things or an amount of something, but not a large number or amount</DEF><THESEXA>There were some children playing in the street.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘Have we got any biscuits?’ ‘Yes, I’ve just bought some.’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>a few</EXP><DEF>a small number of people, things etc. <DEFBOLD>A few</DEFBOLD> is used when there is a smaller number than <DEFBOLD>some</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>‘Are there any chocolates left?’ ‘Only a few.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m going out with a few of my friends.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>a couple of</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>two or a very small number</DEF><THESEXA>Can I ask you a couple of questions?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ll just have a couple of drinks.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>a little</EXP><DEF>a small amount of something</DEF><THESEXA>I speak a little Spanish.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He just wants a little sugar.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>a certain amount of</EXP><DEF>a fairly large amount – use this to talk about people’s feelings, abilities etc</DEF><THESEXA>It’s a job that requires a certain amount of skill.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-187f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>spend</EXP><DEF>to use money to buy things</DEF><THESEXA>I bought two skirts and a T-shirt and I only <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1853"><Section><Exponent><EXP>spending</EXP><DEF>the amount of money that is spent, especially by a government or other </THESEXA><THESEXA>military spending</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>expenditure</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the amount of money that a government, organization, or person spends <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1811"><Section><Exponent><EXP>spoil</EXP><DEF>to have a bad effect on something so that it is much less attractive, enjoyable <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-2c371bee.11a8d8e2f61.-6094"><Section><Exponent><EXP>spoken</EXP><DEF>used about the language that people speak rather than write</DEF><THESEXA>Spoken English is often less formal than written English.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I can understand classical Arabic but not spoken Arabic.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>oral</EXP><DEF>an oral test is one in which you have to speak rather than write. <DEFBOLD>Oral</DEFBOLD> is also used about culture, traditions, and history that are based on spoken English rather than being written down</DEF><THESEXA>We had a 15-minute oral exam in German.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Anglo-Saxon stories and poems were part of a largely oral culture.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>verbal</EXP><DEF>a verbal agreement, warning, form of communication etc is spoken rather than written down</DEF><THESEXA>We had a verbal agreement but no written contract.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company have received verbal approval to begin the project.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>by/through word of mouth</EXP><DEF>by someone telling you about something rather than by reading about it somewhere – used to say how you got some information</DEF><THESEXA>He found out about the job by word of mouth.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A lot of our customers hear about us through word of mouth.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-17c3"><Section><SECHEADING>places where people do sport </SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>field</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>pitch</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF> a large area of ground, usually covered with grass, where team sports are played</DEF><THESEXA>A few kids were playing on the football field.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a hockey field</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stadium</EXP><DEF>a large sports field with seats all around it for people to watch team sports or track and field competitions</DEF><THESEXA>The atmosphere in the Olympic Stadium was amazing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a football stadium</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ground</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a sports field and the seats and buildings around it, belonging to a particular football, rugby, or cricket team</DEF><THESEXA>I met my friends inside the ground.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ballpark/park</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>a stadium where baseball is played</DEF><THESEXA>Busch Stadium is one of the newest ballparks in Major League.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>court</EXP><DEF>an area with lines painted on the ground, for tennis, badminton, basketball, or volleyball</DEF><THESEXA>The hotel has four tennis courts and a volleyball court.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>diamond</EXP><DEF> the area in a baseball field that is within the shape formed by the four bases. The <DEFBOLD>diamond</DEFBOLD> can also be the whole field</DEF><THESEXA>The pitcher stands in the middle of the diamond.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There’s a playground, a picnic area, and a baseball diamond.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>track</EXP><DEF> a circular path with lines on it, for running on</DEF><THESEXA>In the relay, each runner runs once around the track. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>gym</EXP><DEF> a room with machines which you can use to do exercises</DEF><THESEXA>He spends an hour in the gym every day.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pool/swimming pool</EXP><DEF>a place where you can swim</DEF><THESEXA>He jumped into the deep end of the pool.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ll meet you outside the swimming pool.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sports centre</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>sports center</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a building where you can play many types of indoor sports</DEF><THESEXA>Why not do exercise classes at your local sports centre?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>health club</EXP><DEF>a small sports centre, where you pay to be a member</DEF><THESEXA>How much does it cost to join a health club?</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-17a1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>spy</EXP><DEF>someone whose job is to find out secret information about another country</DEF><THESEXA>Stalin controlled a network of spies.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The film is basically a spy story.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>agent/secret agent</EXP><DEF>someone who works for a government or police department in order to get secret information about another country or organization</DEF><THESEXA>a secret agent working for MI5</THESEXA><THESEXA>He is the FBI’s best <COLLOINEXA>undercover agent</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>one who works secretly and pretends to be someone else</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>double agent</EXP><DEF>someone who finds out an enemy country’s secrets for their own country but who also gives secrets to the enemy</DEF><THESEXA>a former CIA double agent who also worked for the KGB</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mole</EXP><DEF>someone who works for an organization while secretly giving information to its enemies</DEF><THESEXA>A mole in the government was leaking information to the press.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>informer</EXP><DEF>someone who secretly tells the police about criminal activities, especially for money</DEF><THESEXA>Acting on information from an informer, the police raided the house.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>espionage</EXP><DEF>the work that spies do</DEF><THESEXA>He is serving a 20-year prison sentence for espionage.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1784"><Section><SECHEADING>work that a spy does</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>spying</EXP><DEF>the action of secretly collecting information about a person, country, or organization</DEF><THESEXA>Several embassy officials had been arrested for spying.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>espionage</EXP><DEF>spying. <DEFBOLD>Espionage</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>spying</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Zakharov was charged with espionage.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company carried out a campaign of <COLLOINEXA>industrial espionage</COLLOINEXA> against its main rival.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Double agents are quite commonplace in the world of espionage.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>surveillance</EXP><DEF>activity in which the police, army, etc watch a person or place carefully because they may be connected with criminal activities</DEF><THESEXA>24-hour surveillance of the building</THESEXA><THESEXA>The police have <COLLOINEXA>had him under surveillance</COLLOINEXA> <GLOSS>have been watching him</GLOSS>for months.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>covert operations</EXP><DEF>secret military activities against an enemy</DEF><THESEXA>These planes are used by British Intelligence for covert operations.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1778"><Section><Exponent><EXP>stage</EXP><DEF>one of several parts of a long process, which happen one after another</DEF><THESEXA>At this stage of the election campaign, it is impossible to predict who will win.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She is still in the early stages of pregnancy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Piaget famously divided childhood into four separate stages.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the opening stages of the race</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>step</EXP><DEF>one of the parts of a process that you have to do or deal with in order to go on to the next one</DEF><THESEXA>The first step is to make a list of what you need.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What’s the next step?</THESEXA><THESEXA>You have to do this one step at a time.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>phase</EXP><DEF>one of the clearly separate stages of a process or activity, during which a type of activity takes place that is different from those in other phases</DEF><THESEXA>the initial phase of the campaign</THESEXA><THESEXA>They were now entering the final phase of their journey.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>round</EXP><DEF>one of the parts that an event or activity is divided into, especially talks or a sports competition</DEF><THESEXA>the first round of the negotiations</THESEXA><THESEXA>the final round of the competition</THESEXA><THESEXA>The next round of the trade talks will be held in Geneva.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>point</EXP><DEF>a specific time or moment during the course of something</DEF><THESEXA>What do you really want at this point in your life?</THESEXA><THESEXA>By this point they were starting to feel more confident.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1765"><Section><Exponent><EXP>stand</EXP><DEF>to be on your feet in an upright position</DEF><THESEXA>There were no seats, so we had to stand.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When we entered, Stephen was standing by his desk.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be on your feet</EXP><DEF>to be standing, especially for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>If you have young kids, you’re on your feet all day.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’d been on my feet since 7 o'clock and I needed to sit down.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The crowd were all on their feet clapping and calling for more.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get up</EXP><DEF>to stand after you have been sitting or lying down</DEF><THESEXA>He got up and turned off the TV.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Mum fell in her flat and was unable to get up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stand up</EXP><DEF>to stand after you have been sitting, or to be in a standing position</DEF><THESEXA>I stood up when she came in and shook her hand.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s generally better to do this exercise standing up.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get to your feet</EXP><REGISTERLAB>written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to stand up, especially slowly or when it is difficult for you</DEF><THESEXA>My attorney got slowly to his feet, breathing heavily.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rise</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to stand after you have been sitting, especially at a formal event</DEF><THESEXA>As the bride entered the cathedral, the congregation rose.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Audience members rose to their feet, cheering and clapping.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

out on the third floor of the hotel.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>kick off</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to start – used especially about a football game or a <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-16e3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>statement</EXP><DEF>something that someone says or writes publicly in order to tell people what they intend to do, what their opinion is etc</DEF><THESEXA>The president will make a statement to the press this afternoon.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>announcement</EXP><DEF>a public or official statement telling people what has happened or what will happen</DEF><THESEXA>The announcement was heard by millions of radio listeners this morning.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The company made an announcement yesterday that Rogers has resigned as managing director.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>declaration</EXP><DEF>an important official statement, especially about what a government or organization intends to do</DEF><THESEXA>On the 19th of July a declaration of war was delivered in Berlin.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Independence Hall is where the delegates met to sign the Declaration of Independence and write the Constitution. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>press release</EXP><DEF>an official statement giving information to the newspapers, radio, or television</DEF><THESEXA>The singer issued a press release saying that she was too ill to continue with the tour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>testimony</EXP><DEF>a formal statement saying that something is true, especially one a witness makes in a court of law</DEF><THESEXA>The testimony of the two arresting officers was an important part of the prosecution case.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The jury based their decision almost entirely on the testimony of one witness.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>affidavit</EXP><REGISTERLAB>law</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a written statement that you swear is true, for use as proof in a court of law</DEF><THESEXA>A prison doctor who treated the accused sent an affidavit about his mental state.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-16c7"><Section><Exponent><EXP>station</EXP><DEF>a place where trains or buses regularly stop</DEF><THESEXA>The town has its own railway station.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Paddington Station in west London</THESEXA><THESEXA>the bus station</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>terminus</EXP><DEF>the station or stop at the end of a railway or bus line</DEF><THESEXA>We’ve arranged to meet her at the Victoria bus terminus.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the railway terminus in central Calcutta</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>track</EXP><GRAM>usually plural</GRAM><DEF>the metal lines along which trains travel. This is sometimes used in American English to say which part of a station a train will leave from</DEF><THESEXA>The passenger train, traveling at 120 mph, careered off the tracks. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>platform</EXP><DEF>the raised place beside a railway track where you get on and off a train in a station – used especially to say which part of a station a train will leave from</DEF><THESEXA>Trains for Oxford leave from Platform 2.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ticket office</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>booking office</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>the place at a station where tickets are sold</DEF><THESEXA>You can buy rail tickets online or at the ticket office.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>departures board</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>departure board</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a board saying when and from which part of a station each train will leave</DEF><THESEXA>The departures board said that the train was ten minutes late.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-16b1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>stay</EXP><DEF>to not leave a place, or to be in a place for a particular period of time</DEF><THESEXA>Stay where you are and don’t move.</THESEXA><THESEXA>John only stayed at the party for a couple of hours.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>remain</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to stay somewhere. In written English, people often prefer to use <DEFBOLD>remain </DEFBOLD> rather than <DEFBOLD>stay</DEFBOLD>, because it sounds more formal</DEF><THESEXA>Some 2,000 protesters remained outside the building and refused to leave.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The judge recommended that he remain in jail for the rest of his life.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>linger</EXP><DEF>to stay in a place a little longer than you need to, because you are enjoying yourself, or because you hope to see someone or something</DEF><THESEXA>He lingered outside the lecture hall, hoping for a chance to talk to her.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are plenty of small cafés where you can linger over a cappuccino.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>loiter</EXP><DEF>to stay in a place not doing anything – used when you think someone is waiting for the chance to do something bad or illegal</DEF><THESEXA>The two men had been seen loitering in the area on the day that the car was stolen. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hang around</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to stay somewhere not doing anything</DEF><THESEXA>There are gangs of boys hanging around on street corners.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t mind hanging around for a few minutes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The boss doesn’t like being kept hanging around.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stick around</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to stay in the same place or situation for a period of time, especially while you are waiting for something to happen or someone to arrive</DEF><THESEXA>I decided to stick around and see how it all turned out.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Make up your mind. I’m not going to stick around forever.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1683"><Section><Exponent><EXP>steal</EXP><DEF>to illegally take something that belongs to someone else</DEF><THESEXA>The thieves stole organization you work for, especially money that you are responsible for</DEF><THESEXA>Government officials embezzled more than $2.5 million from the department.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shoplifting</EXP><DEF>stealing things from a shop by taking them when you think no one is looking</DEF><THESEXA>Shoplifting costs stores millions of pounds every year.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>phishing</EXP><DEF>the activity of dishonestly persuading people to give you their credit card details over the Internet, so that you can steal <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1662"><Section><Exponent><EXP>stomachache</EXP><DEF>pain in your stomach or near your stomach</DEF><THESEXA>He said he had a stomachache and didn't want to go to school.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I've got bad stomachache.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>indigestion</EXP><DEF>pain or uncomfortable feelings that you get when your stomach cannot break down food that you have eaten</DEF><THESEXA>She frequently suffered from indigestion.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If I eat onions, they give me indigestion. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>food poisoning</EXP><DEF>a stomach illness caused by eating food that contains harmful bacteria, so that you vomit</DEF><THESEXA>I got food poisoning while I was on holiday and had to spend the day in bed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Two of the customers has to be taken to hospital with suspected food poisoning.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nausea</EXP><DEF>the feeling that you have when you think you are going to vomit</DEF><THESEXA>A feeling of nausea suddenly came over me.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>constipation</EXP><DEF>the condition of having difficulty in getting rid of solid waste from your body</DEF><THESEXA>Do you suffer from constipation?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>diarrhoea</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>diarrhea</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>an illness in which waste from the bowels is watery and comes out often</DEF><THESEXA>The main symptoms are diarrhoea and vomiting.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some dairy products can cause diarrhoea.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1646" fullpage="yes"><Section><SECHEADING>to stop doing something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>stop</EXP><DEF>to not do something any something for a short time before starting again</DEF><THESEXA>He paused for a moment to consider the question.</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘I think it’s going to rain,’ she said, pausing to look up at the sky.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>have/take a break</EXP><DEF> to stop working, studying, or driving for a short time in order to rest</DEF><THESEXA>Okay, everyone. Take a ten-minute break.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If you’re feeling tired, you should have a break.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>break</EXP><DEF> to stop working, studying etc in order to rest or eat something – used about a group of people who are doing something together</DEF><THESEXA> After a couple of hours the committee broke for lunch.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to stop happening</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>stop</EXP><DEF>to stop happening</DEF><THESEXA>The noise suddenly stopped.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We waited for the rain to stop.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come to an end</EXP><DEF>to stop – used about something that has continued for a long time</DEF><THESEXA>The war finally came to an end in 1918. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wear off</EXP><DEF>to gradually stop – used about a pain, a feeling, or the effects of something</DEF><THESEXA>The pain will soon wear off.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The excitement was beginning to wear off.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The anaesthetic took a long time to wear off. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>peter out</EXP><DEF>to gradually stop happening or existing</DEF><THESEXA>The campaign petered out after only a few weeks.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cease</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to stop</DEF><THESEXA>The fighting has ceased.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Production at the factory has ceased.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to stop moving</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>stop</EXP><DEF>to stop moving</DEF><THESEXA>Can we stop soon? I’m tired.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The bus stops right in front of the hotel. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come to a halt</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to move more slowly and then stop – used about a vehicle</DEF><THESEXA>The train slowly came to a halt just outside the station. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The plane came to a halt less than twenty yards away from the limousines. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pull over</EXP><DEF>to move to the side of the road and stop – used about a vehicle or its driver</DEF><THESEXA>The bus pulled over to the side of the road, with smoke coming out of its engine. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The police officer was waving at him to pull over.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pull up</EXP><DEF>to stop close to something – used about a vehicle or its driver</DEF><THESEXA>The taxi pulled up outside her house. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He pulled up next to our car. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>come to a standstill</EXP><DEF>to go slower and then stop moving completely</DEF><THESEXA>The road was blocked by an accident, and the traffic quickly came to a standstill.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1637"><Section><Exponent><EXP>storm</EXP><DEF>a period of very bad weather when there is a lot of rain or snow, strong winds, and often  lightning</DEF><THESEXA>The ship sank in a violent storm.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They got caught in a storm on top of the mountain.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The storm hit the coast of Florida on Tuesday.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The cost of repairing storm damage will run into millions of pounds.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>thunderstorm</EXP><DEF>a storm in which there is a lot of <NonDV><REFHWD>thunder</REFHWD></NonDV><GLOSS>loud noise in the sky</GLOSS> and <NonDV><REFHWD>lightning</REFHWD></NonDV><GLOSS>flashes of light in the sky</GLOSS></DEF><THESEXA>When I was young I was terrified of thunderstorms.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hurricane</EXP><DEF>a storm that has very strong fast winds and that moves over water – used about storms in the North Atlantic Ocean</DEF><THESEXA>Hurricane Katrina battered the US Gulf Coast.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the hurricane season</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>typhoon</EXP><DEF>a very violent tropical storm – used about storms in the Western Pacific Ocean</DEF><THESEXA>A powerful typhoon hit southern China today.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Weather experts are monitoring typhoons in Hong Kong and China.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cyclone</EXP><DEF>a severe storm affecting a large area, in which the wind moves around in a big circle</DEF><THESEXA>Thousands of people died when a tropical cyclone hit Bangladesh.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Cyclone ‘Joy’ inflicted damage estimated at $40 million, with winds of up to 145 miles per hour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tornado</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>twister</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>an extremely violent storm that consists of air that spins very quickly and causes a lot of damage</DEF><THESEXA>The tornado ripped the roof off his house.</THESEXA><THESEXA>For the second time in a week deadly tornadoes have torn through Tennessee.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>snowstorm</EXP><DEF>a storm with strong winds and a lot of snow</DEF><THESEXA>A major snowstorm blew across Colorado.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>blizzard</EXP><DEF>a severe snowstorm in which the snow is blown around by strong winds, making it difficult to see anything</DEF><THESEXA>We got stuck in a blizzard.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Denver is bracing itself for <COLLOINEXA>blizzard conditions</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-161b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>story</EXP><DEF>a description of how something happened that is intended to entertain people, and may be true or imaginary</DEF><THESEXA>a ghost story</THESEXA><THESEXA>a love story</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s a story about a man who loses his memory.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a book of short stories</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tale</EXP><DEF>a story about strange imaginary events, or exciting events that happened in the past</DEF><THESEXA> a fairy tale by Hans Christian Andersen</THESEXA><THESEXA>I loved hearing tales of his travels.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>myth</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a very old imaginary story about gods and magical creatures</DEF><THESEXA>an ancient myth</THESEXA><THESEXA>Greek and Roman myths</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>legend</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>an old story about brave people or there.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fable</EXP><DEF>a traditional imaginary short story that teaches a moral lesson, especially a story about animals</DEF><THESEXA>the fable of the tortoise and
