<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1605" fullpage="yes"><Section><Exponent><EXP>strange</EXP><DEF>unusual or surprising, especially in a way that is difficult to man</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>funny/odd</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a little strange and making you feel slightly surprised or worried</DEF><THESEXA>There’s a funny especially in an interesting way. <DEFBOLD>Curious</DEFBOLD> is a little more formal than <DEFBOLD>strange</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>a curious fact</THESEXA><THESEXA>There’s something rather curious about small-town America.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She remembered curious little details.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mysterious</EXP><DEF>strange – used about something that people know little about and are unable to explain or understand</DEF><THESEXA>He had disappeared in mysterious circumstances.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There were reports of mysterious lights in the sky.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>eccentric</EXP><DEF>strange in a way that seems slightly crazy and amusing – used about people and their behaviour</DEF><THESEXA>He lived completely alone and used especially when this is either amusing or worrying</DEF><THESEXA>She sometimes wears rather peculiar clothes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He had a peculiar expression on his dream last night.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s a weird and wonderful place.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bizarre</EXP><DEF>extremely strange and different from what is usually considered <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-15f2"><Section><Exponent><EXP>strict</EXP><DEF>expecting people to obey rules or to do what you say – used especially about parents, teachers, or organizations</DEF><THESEXA>Our teachers were very strict.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Most schools are quite strict about the way students dress.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>firm</EXP><DEF>showing that you are in control of the situation and will not change your opinion, especially when you are telling someone what to money.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tough</EXP><DEF>determined that your orders or decisions will be obeyed, especially in order to make sure that a situation improves – used especially when you harsh.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>authoritarian</EXP><REGISTERLAB>disapproving </REGISTERLAB><DEF>very strict about forcing people to obey rules or laws, and punishing them very severely if government</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>rule/law/system</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>strict</EXP><DEF>a strict order or rule is one that must be obeyed</DEF><THESEXA>There are strict rules about keeping tax records.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He had strict instructions to return the key to me.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tight</EXP><DEF>tight  controls or limits are very strict about what is allowed and what is not allowed</DEF><THESEXA>The report recommends tighter controls on the advertising of alcohol.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are tight regulations governing waste disposal.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tough</EXP><DEF>tough laws or rules are very strict</DEF><THESEXA>They want tougher laws against drinking and driving.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The federal government is introducing tough new rules on immigration.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>harsh</EXP><DEF>harsh punishments or laws are very severe, often too severe</DEF><THESEXA>There are harsh penalties for drug trafficking.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The government has brought in harsh measures to combat the rioting taking place in many cities.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stringent</EXP><DEF>controlling what people can do with rules that have very high standards</DEF><THESEXA>There are now stringent controls on pollution from <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-159f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>strike</EXP><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a period of time when a group of workers stop working because of a disagreement about pay, working conditions etc</DEF><THESEXA>A teachers’ strike has been announced for next week.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The rail strike has resulted in major delays on roads across the country.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>industrial action</EXP><GRAM>U</GRAM><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>activities such as strikes, or doing less work than usual, as a way of trying to persuade an employer to improve pay, conditions etc</DEF><THESEXA>Lecturers voted to take industrial action over their workload.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Prison officers have threatened industrial action.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stoppage</EXP><GRAM>C</GRAM><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>work stoppage</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a short strike, especially one that lasts for one day</DEF><THESEXA>Customs officers will return to work today after a twenty-four hour stoppage.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>go-slow</EXP><GRAM>C</GRAM><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a period of time when a group of workers deliberately work more slowly than usual as a way of protesting about pay, conditions etc</DEF><THESEXA>The hospital go-slow comes into effect tomorrow.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The union carried out strikes and go-slows in support of a wage claim. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-414b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>stripe</EXP><DEF>a line of colour, especially one of several lines of colour all close together</DEF><THESEXA>the red and white stripes on the US flag</THESEXA><THESEXA>horizontal stripes</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>streak</EXP><DEF> a coloured line or thin mark, especially one that is not straight or has been made accidentally</DEF><THESEXA>His hair was black with streaks of grey.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He saw the red streaks of sunrise in the sky.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>band</EXP><DEF> a thick line of colour that is different from the areas around it</DEF><THESEXA>The fish has a black band on its fin.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-78ef4d03.118e0988fe1.4ef1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>stubborn</EXP><DEF>refusing to change your mind, even when people think you are wrong or are being unreasonable</DEF><THESEXA>Dave can be really stubborn once he’s made up his mind.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a stubborn old man</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>obstinate</EXP><DEF>very stubborn, in way that is annoying and unreasonable</DEF><THESEXA>I have never met anyone so obstinate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>his obstinate refusal to compromise</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pig-headed</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>refusing to change your mind, even when people think that what you want to do is stupid</DEF><THESEXA>I told her she was making a big mistake but she was too pig-headed to listen.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I wish you’d stop being so pig-headed!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>headstrong</EXP><DEF>very determined to do what you want, often without thinking about the results of your actions – used especially about young people</DEF><THESEXA>As a girl, she had been lively and headstrong.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the headstrong impulsiveness of youth</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wilful</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><AmEVariant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>willful</LEXVAR></AmEVariant><DEF>doing what you want, even after you have been told to stop, or when you know that it is wrong – used especially about children</DEF><THESEXA>He was a spoiled and wilful child, who always got his own way.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was passionate and wilful – exactly the sort of creature a man ought to avoid. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ornery</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>behaving in an unreasonable and often angry way, especially by doing the opposite of what people want you to do</DEF><THESEXA>an ornery kid</THESEXA><THESEXA>Teenagers can be ornery and rude.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-9b9"><Section><Exponent><EXP>stuck</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>fixed or trapped in a particular position or place and unable to move or be moved</DEF><THESEXA>I can’t open this drawer – it’s stuck.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sorry I’m late – I got stuck in traffic.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>jammed</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>stuck and impossible to move – used especially about parts of a machine, or something trapped between two surfaces</DEF><THESEXA>The photocopier is jammed again.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I put in a 50p coin but it got jammed in the slot.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>entangled</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>twisted together and unable to be separated – used especially about things such as hair, wire, or string getting caught around something</DEF><THESEXA>The chain of her necklace was entangled in her hair.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Swimming in the river is dangerous because you might get entangled in the weeds.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stranded</EXP><DEF>stuck and unable to move from or leave a place – used about people, vehicles, and animals</DEF><THESEXA>Hundreds of motorists were left stranded by the snowstorms.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We were stranded at Moscow airport.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The jellyfish were stranded on the beach.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>beached</EXP><DEF>stuck in shallow water or on the shore, and unable to move – used about whales, sea creatures, and boats</DEF><THESEXA>They helped push the beached whale back into the sea.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the owner of the beached vessel </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1527"><Section><Exponent><EXP>student</EXP><DEF>someone who is studying at a university or school. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-14da"><Section><Exponent><EXP>stupid</EXP><DEF>showing a total lack of good sense or good judgment. <DEFBOLD>Stupid </DEFBOLD> sounds very strong and is often used when you are annoyed or strongly criticizing someone’s behaviour</DEF><THESEXA>I wish you’d stop asking stupid questions.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was stupid of me to leave the door unlocked.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Well, if you’re stupid enough to skate on the lake, you deserve to fall in.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>silly</EXP><DEF>doing or saying things that are not sensible or serious, and that may make you feel embarrassed later. <DEFBOLD>Silly</DEFBOLD> sounds much gentler than <DEFBOLD>stupid</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>a silly mistake</THESEXA><THESEXA>Don’t be so silly! There’s nothing wrong with you.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I think you’re silly to worry so much about your hair.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>daft</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>not sensible, often in a way that is also amusing</DEF><THESEXA>Is this another of your daft ideas?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Don’t be daft! Of course you’re not too old to go clubbing.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dumb</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><DEF>stupid</DEF><THESEXA>a dumb question</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was dumb enough to believe her.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Oh, I just did the dumbest thing back there, I forgot my purse.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>foolish</EXP><DEF>stupid. <DEFBOLD>Foolish</DEFBOLD> sounds rather formal and is used mainly in written English. The usual words to use in everyday English are <DEFBOLD>silly</DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD>stupid</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>It was a foolish thing to say.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They did not want to look foolish.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was all a foolish dream.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I think the board of directors made a foolish choice that it will later regret.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unwise</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>done without thinking carefully enough about the possible disadvantages that may result</DEF><THESEXA>She knew the marriage was unwise.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an unwise choice of words </THESEXA><THESEXA>It would be very unwise to speculate. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>brave</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>often humorous</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when you think that what someone is planning or suggesting is certain to fail, but you do not want to say directly that they are behaving in a stupid way</DEF><THESEXA>The leader of the opposition described it as ‘a brave decision.’</THESEXA><THESEXA>I think he’s being very brave.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>very stupid</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>crazy</EXP><DEF>not at all sensible or reasonable – used when you are very surprised by someone’s behaviour or what they have said</DEF><THESEXA>Ian’s got some crazy plan to drive across Africa.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She looked at me as if I was crazy!</THESEXA><THESEXA>You’re crazy to think of hitch-hiking on your own.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ridiculous</EXP><DEF>extremely stupid</DEF><THESEXA>You look ridiculous in that hat.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some people spend a ridiculous amount of money on cars.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s absolutely ridiculous to suggest that he would do something like that.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>absurd/ludicrous</EXP><DEF>extremely stupid – used especially when an idea or situation seems strange or illogical</DEF><THESEXA>How can a return ticket cost less than a single? It’s totally absurd!</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a ludicrous idea.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some of the objections to the theory are simply absurd.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>laughable</EXP><DEF>so stupid that you cannot believe someone is telling the truth or being serious</DEF><THESEXA>The accusations were almost laughable.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a laughable suggestion</THESEXA><THESEXA>It would be laughable if it wasn’t so serious.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1470"><Section><Exponent><EXP>the subway</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>the railway system that runs under the ground below a big city. British people use <DEFBOLD>the underground</DEFBOLD> when talking about the railway system in London, and many systems outside the US</DEF><THESEXA>the New York subway</THESEXA><THESEXA>We got on the next subway train.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the underground</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>the railway system that runs under the ground below a big city</DEF><THESEXA>the London underground</THESEXA><THESEXA>an underground map</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s quickest to travel by underground.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the tube</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>the railway system that runs under London</DEF><THESEXA>We took the tube to Trafalgar Square.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a crowded tube train</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the metro</EXP><DEF>used especially to talk about the railway system that runs under Paris. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.4e6eb300.1194c690b1c.-77dc"><Section><SECHEADING>succeed in doing sth</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>succeed</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>I</GRAM><DEF>to do something we’ll manage somehow.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>achieve</EXP><POS>v</POS><GRAM>T</GRAM><DEF>to succeed in doing something good or important</DEF><THESEXA>She’s achieved a lot in the short <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-833"><Section><Exponent><EXP>successful</EXP><DEF>achieving what you wanted, or having the effect or result you intended</DEF><THESEXA>He applied for a visa three times and in the end he was successful.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The treatment was successful.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a highly successful campaign.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>effective</EXP><DEF>having the effect or result that was wanted – used especially about treatments, drugs, or methods</DEF><THESEXA>The drug is effective against a range of diseases.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We still haven't found an effective way to solve the problem.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>victorious</EXP><DEF>successful as a result of winning in a game, election, war etc</DEF><THESEXA>the captain of the victorious team</THESEXA><THESEXA>She emerged victorious in the second round of voting.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>promising</EXP><DEF>likely to be very successful in the future, used especially about someone who is good at a job, sport, art etc</DEF><THESEXA>a promising young athlete</THESEXA><THESEXA> He gave up a promising career in banking.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>thriving</EXP><DEF>very successful – used about a company, industry, or organization that is doing well at a particular time</DEF><THESEXA>The area has a thriving tourist industry.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The school is thriving academically.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>booming</EXP><DEF>extremely successful – used about an economy, or when business or trade is increasing</DEF><THESEXA>the booming economy in China</THESEXA><THESEXA>Business is booming.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1457"><Section><Exponent><EXP>suddenly</EXP><DEF>used when something happens very quickly and unexpectedly</DEF><THESEXA>Suddenly, there was a loud bang.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I suddenly realized what had happened.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her husband <COLLOINEXA>died suddenly</COLLOINEXA> at the age of 64.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>all of a sudden</EXP><DEF>suddenly – used especially in stories or descriptions of past events</DEF><THESEXA>All of a sudden, he takes a gun out of his pocket and shoots.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He just all of a sudden decided to leave.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>without warning</EXP><DEF>suddenly and with no signs that it was going to happen – used about bad or dangerous things</DEF><THESEXA>One day, he collapsed without warning.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Then, without warning, her husband left her.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>out of the blue</EXP><DEF>suddenly and unexpectedly – used especially when you hear from someone you have not seen for a long time or when someone tells you something that surprises you</DEF><THESEXA>She turned up on my doorstep, out of the blue.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The offer had come completely out of the blue.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>at short notice</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>on short notice</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>suddenly, so that there is not much time to prepare or change arrangements</DEF><THESEXA>He came into the side at short notice, when another player was injured.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Occasionally, tours may have to be cancelled at short notice.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>on the spur of the moment</EXP><DEF>used when talking about things you decide to do suddenly, without planning them beforehand</DEF><THESEXA>We all buy things on the spur of the moment.</THESEXA><THESEXA>On the spur of the moment, I decided to go and talk to her about it. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1433"><Section><Exponent><EXP>suggest</EXP><DEF>to tell someone your ideas about what they should do</DEF><THESEXA>'Why don’t you come with us?', Alan suggested.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a sunny afternoon and so I suggested going to the beach.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>recommend</EXP><DEF>to suggest that someone goes somewhere, tries something etc because you know that it is good and you think they will like it</DEF><THESEXA>Can you recommend a good restaurant near here?</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s a brilliant book - I’d recommend it to anyone.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>propose</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to formally suggest a plan or course of action, especially at a meeting</DEF><THESEXA>The government has proposed building a new town just north of the city.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I propose that we send a delegation to London to discuss our concerns.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>put forward</EXP><DEF>to suggest an idea, plan, reason etc</DEF><THESEXA>The party has put forward new proposals to reduce the number of cars in the town centre.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The idea was first put forward by Aristotle.</THESEXA><THESEXA>A number of reasons have been put forward to explain these negative results.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nominate</EXP><DEF>to officially suggest someone for an important job or position, or a person, film etc for a prize, especially when people will vote to make a decision</DEF><THESEXA>He’s been nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature several times.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Three candidates have been nominated for the position of chairman.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1401"><Section><Exponent><EXP>suggestion</EXP><DEF>an idea, plan, or possibility that someone suggests</DEF><THESEXA>We welcome any suggestions as to how to improve our service.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sally made a few useful suggestions about places we could visit.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>proposal</EXP><DEF>a formal or official suggestion</DEF><THESEXA>They will consider our proposal at their next meeting.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The proposal to build a new airport has finally been rejected.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>recommendation</EXP><DEF>an official suggestion about what to do, especially one that is contained in a report</DEF><THESEXA>The safety board has made some recommendations.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The Government decided to accept the recommendation of the Lockwood Report.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.1180d18c85a.-604a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>suitable</EXP><DEF>having the right qualities for a particular purpose or person</DEF><THESEXA>a suitable place for a picnic</THESEXA><THESEXA>They don’t consider him a suitable husband for their daughter.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>right</EXP><DEF>completely suitable in every way</DEF><THESEXA>It’s a nice house, but it isn’t right for us.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We’ll tell her when the time is right.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>appropriate</EXP><DEF>suitable for a particular purpose. <DEFBOLD>Appropriate</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>suitable</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>She filled out all the appropriate forms.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It may not be an appropriate time to ask him about it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s not appropriate to wear a short skirt for an interview.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the appropriate way to end a business letter</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>proper</EXP><DEF>the proper tool, piece of equipment, or way of doing something is the one that most people think is most suitable</DEF><THESEXA>You can’t change a wheel without the proper tools.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the proper procedure for hiring staff</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>suited to sth</EXP><DEF>if someone is suited to something, he or she has the right qualities to do it</DEF><THESEXA>He’d be well suited to the job.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-13d6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>sunny</EXP><DEF>if the weather is sunny, the sun is shining</DEF><THESEXA>When we arrived in Hong Kong, it was warm and sunny.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a lovely sunny afternoon</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fine</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>not raining, and with no clouds or only a few clouds in the sky</DEF><THESEXA>a period of fine weather</THESEXA><THESEXA>Next week will be fine but a little cooler.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>nice</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>lovely</LEXVAR><GEO>especially BrE</GEO></Variant><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>pleasantly warm and sunny</DEF><THESEXA>It’s a lovely day – why don’t we go for a walk?</THESEXA><THESEXA>If it’s nice, we’ll go to the beach.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bright</EXP><DEF>if the weather is bright, the sun is shining and there is plenty of light</DEF><THESEXA>a bright sunny morning</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was bright earlier on in the day.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>beautiful/glorious</EXP><DEF>used when the weather is very good and there is a lot of sunshine</DEF><THESEXA>a glorious autumn day</THESEXA><THESEXA>The weather has been beautiful all week.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>clear</EXP><DEF>with no clouds or mist</DEF><THESEXA>It was a clear June morning.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a clear blue sky</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cloudless</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially literary</REGISTERLAB><DEF>with no clouds</DEF><THESEXA>The sun shone from a cloudless sky.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was a beautiful cloudless morning.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dry</EXP><DEF>with no rain</DEF><THESEXA>a period of dry weather</THESEXA><THESEXA>Tomorrow will be mostly dry.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-13b5"><Section><SECHEADING>to agree with and help sb/sth</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>support</EXP><DEF>to say that you agree with a <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1383"><Section><Exponent><EXP>sure</EXP><POS>adj</POS><DEF>believing that something is definitely true or correct</DEF><THESEXA>I’m sure <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.c8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>surprise</EXP><DEF>to make someone feel surprised, especially because they did not expect something</DEF><THESEXA>Her reaction surprised me – I didn’t realize how strongly she felt.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What surprised me was how cheap everything was compared to at home.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>amaze</EXP><DEF>to greatly surprise someone, for example because something is very good, unusual, or hard to believe</DEF><THESEXA>He amazed everyone with his skill.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It amazes me that no one has thought of the idea before.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>astonish</EXP><DEF>to greatly surprise someone</DEF><THESEXA>Myra astonished her doctors by recovering so quickly.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>astound</EXP><DEF>to greatly surprise or shock someone. <DEFBOLD>Astound</DEFBOLD> sounds a little more formal and a little stronger than <DEFBOLD>astonish</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>What astounded him was their inefficiency.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take sb by surprise</EXP><DEF>to happen at an unexpected time, so that people are surprised or unprepared</DEF><THESEXA>Security men were taken by surprise as the man ran onto the stage.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His resignation took us by surprise.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>startle</EXP><DEF>to make someone feel surprised or slightly frightened by doing something they did not expect</DEF><THESEXA>The wind made the door shut with a crash, which startled her.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sudden movements may startle the horse.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1310"><Section><Exponent><EXP>surprised</EXP><DEF>having the feeling you get when something happens that you did not <DEFBOLD>Astounded</DEFBOLD> sounds a little more formal and a little stronger than <DEFBOLD>astonished</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>She was astounded by how much it <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-12f8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>surprising</EXP><DEF>making you feel surprised</DEF><THESEXA>It’s surprising how quickly you get used to it.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We got some very surprising results.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>astonishing/astounding</EXP><DEF>very surprising. <DEFBOLD>Astounding</DEFBOLD> sounds a little more formal and a little stronger than <DEFBOLD>astonishing</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The results of the tests were astounding.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Things are changing <COLLOINEXA>at an astonishing speed</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>extraordinary</EXP><DEF>very unusual and surprising</DEF><THESEXA>It is extraordinary that such a young boy should be so good at maths. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>amazing</EXP><DEF>very surprising – used especially about good or impressive things</DEF><THESEXA>an amazing achievement</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s amazing what you can do when you really try.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>staggering</EXP><DEF>very surprising, especially by being so large</DEF><THESEXA>The project cost a staggering $8 million.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unbelievable</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>incredible</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>so surprising that you can hardly believe it</DEF><THESEXA>It is unbelievable that she is only the same age as me.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The incredible thing is that he thinks he is ugly. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-12e9"><Section><Exponent><EXP>surrender</EXP><DEF>to say officially that you want to stop fighting, especially in a war, because you realize that you cannot win – used about people and countries</DEF><THESEXA>Two days later, the rebels surrendered.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Japan surrendered in August 1945.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give in</EXP><DEF>to accept that you cannot win a game, argument, fight etc and stop trying to win it</DEF><THESEXA> The players refused to give in and eventually won the game 4-3 in extra time.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The negotiations went on for days and neither side was prepared to give in.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>admit/accept defeat</EXP><DEF> to accept that you have not won something</DEF><THESEXA>In July 1905, Russia admitted defeat in its war with Japan.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She wanted to run for the Presidency and refused to accept defeat.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>concede</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to say that you are not going to win a game, argument, election etc, so that it officially ends</DEF><THESEXA>He was forced to concede the match.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Davis conceded defeat in the election.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-12db"><Section><Exponent><EXP>sweet</EXP><DEF>sweet food or drink has had sugar added or contains natural sugars</DEF><THESEXA>Italian oranges are very sweet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a cup of hot sweet tea</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sugary</EXP><DEF>sweet because a lot of sugar has been added</DEF><THESEXA>Sugary foods are bad for your teeth.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sickly</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>tasting unpleasantly sweet</DEF><THESEXA>The dessert was rather sweet and sickly.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a <COLLOINEXA>sickly sweet </COLLOINEXA>fruit drink</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cloying</EXP><DEF>tasting or smelling unpleasantly sweet</DEF><THESEXA>I find strawberry and peach drinks too cloying.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the cloying smell of fish oil</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-12c8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>sympathetic</EXP><DEF>kind to someone who is having problems</DEF><THESEXA>My friends were very sympathetic when I lost my job.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Jill was a very sympathetic listener.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a sympathetic attitude</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>compassionate</EXP><DEF>wanting to help people who are suffering or having problems</DEF><THESEXA>The doctor was a compassionate man who always acted in his patients’ best interests.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>understanding</EXP><DEF>not getting angry with someone or criticizing them, because you can see that they have problems</DEF><THESEXA>I have had a lot of time off work, but my boss is very understanding.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an understanding look</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>caring</EXP><DEF>caring about other people’s feelings and wanting to help them</DEF><THESEXA>I have very caring parents.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Most of the nurses are very caring.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-12ac"><Section><Exponent><EXP>sympathize</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><ORTHVAR>sympathise</ORTHVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><PronCodes><PRON>ˈsɪmpəθaɪz</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to feel sad for someone because you understand their problems</DEF><THESEXA>It must be awful for you – I do sympathize.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He told me how difficult it was dealing with his parents, and I sympathized with him. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>feel sorry for sb</EXP><DEF>to feel sad for someone because they have had bad luck or are in a difficult situation</DEF><THESEXA>I felt really sorry for him when he lost his job.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s his wife I feel sorry for – on her own with four kids.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>understand</EXP><DEF>to realize how someone feels and why they behave the way they do, and be kind to them</DEF><THESEXA>I’m sure if you talk to him, he’ll understand.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s important to try and understand teenagers.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pity</EXP><DEF>to feel sad for someone because they are in a very difficult situation</DEF><THESEXA>The man paled when they found him guilty, and I pitied him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I pity anyone who has to feed a family on such a low income.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1281"><Section><Exponent><EXP>system</EXP><DEF>an organized set of ideas, methods, actions etc that you use to do something</DEF><THESEXA>the airport security system</THESEXA><THESEXA>the system for dealing with refugees</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>process</EXP><DEF>a series of actions that are done in order to achieve a particular result</DEF><THESEXA>the process of teaching children to read</THESEXA><THESEXA>Repeat the same process, until all the sugar has dissolved.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>procedure</EXP><DEF>the official or usually agreed way of doing something</DEF><THESEXA>You must follow the normal complaints procedure.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Large companies have a standard procedure for informing new employees about conditions of work.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>formula</EXP><DEF>a method or set of principles that you use to solve a problem or make sure that something is successful</DEF><THESEXA>The team are hoping to use the same <COLLOINEXA>winning formula</COLLOINEXA> that they used last season.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is no <COLLOINEXA>magic formula</COLLOINEXA> for success<GLOSS>a method that is certain to succeed</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

talkto use words to communicate with someone about somethingI need to talk to you about your work.She always enjoyed talking to Jim.Can we talk?speakto talk. Speak is a little more formal than talk. You often use it when saying that someone speaks in a particular way, or that you will speak to someone in order to try to get something done. You only use speak when saying that someone speaks a languageHe spoke with confidence and authority.She was speaking quietly so she didn’t disturb anyone.I’d like to speak to the manager.Do you speak German?go on/drone on/rambleto talk too much or for too long about something, in a way that makes people boredHe went on about how great the team was. I’d better stop rambling and let you get on with your work.waffleˈwɒfəlBrEdisapproving informalto talk using a lot of words but without saying anything importantI wish he would stop waffling and get to the point!prattle ondisapproving informalto talk continuously about silly and unimportant thingsShe prattled on about her boyfriend for the entire journey.What’s he prattling on about now?
to talk about everyday thingshave a conversationto talk to someone for a long time about everyday thingsShe was having a conversation with one of her friends.When I arrived, Joe and Jane were deep in conversationvery involved in a conversation.I can order food in a restaurant in French, but not have a conversation.chat/have a chatinformalto have a friendly informal conversation about things that are not very importantThe girls were chatting outside the house.It’s been nice having a chat with you.gossipto talk about other people’s private lives when they are not there, especially about things that you have heard, which are not completely trueWhat are you two gossiping about?visit with sbAmEinformalto have a conversation with someoneI visited with him last week.converseformalto have a conversation with someoneWe met once and conversed briefly.
to talk seriouslydiscussto talk seriously about problems, ideas, or plansThey met to discuss how the building should be designed.She refuses to discuss the matter.talk sth overto discuss something with someone because it will affect them, especially someone close to youBefore you accept the job, talk it over with your family.You two need to sit down and talk things over.debateto discuss a subject formally when you are trying to make a decision or solve a problemThe issue will be debated on Tuesday.The UN Security Council debated whether to impose sanctions.

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-11f6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>talkative</EXP><DEF>a talkative person likes to talk a lot – also used about people’s moods</DEF><THESEXA>He’s very talkative with his friends and family.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Benjamin was in a talkative mood.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>chatty</EXP><DEF>liking to talk a lot in a friendly way – a rather informal use</DEF><THESEXA>I like her – she’s always cheerful and chatty.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The chatty girl on the phone answered most of my questions.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>articulate</EXP><DEF>able to talk easily and effectively about things, especially difficult subjects</DEF><THESEXA>All the applicants we interviewed were intelligent and articulate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>These were articulate people, well able to deal with the press and media. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>eloquent</EXP><DEF>able to express your ideas and opinions well – used especially about people who speak in public and are able to influence people</DEF><THESEXA>an eloquent public speaker </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>garrulous</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>talking a lot, especially about unimportant things</DEF><THESEXA>A garrulous old man was sitting near me in the bar.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He became more garrulous as the evening went on.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>you can’t shut him/her up</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used to say humorously that someone talks all the time</DEF><THESEXA>You can’t shut him up so I never get any work done when he’s here.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>chatterbox</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>someone, especially a child, who talks a lot </DEF><THESEXA>Cathy’s a real chatterbox.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-11a5"><Section><SECHEADING>describing the taste of something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>delicious</EXP><DEF>having a very good </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tangy</EXP><DEF>having a taste that stings your tongue slightly, like lemon does, in a way that seems good</DEF><THESEXA>The dressing was nice and taste.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>salty</EXP><DEF>containing a lot of salt</DEF><THESEXA>Danish salami has a salty flavour.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hot/spicy</EXP><DEF>having a This word can sound rather <NonDV><REFHWD>pretentious</REFHWD></NonDV> in everyday conversation</DEF><THESEXA>cooked vegetables in a piquant sauce </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mild</EXP><DEF>not having a strong or hot taste – usually used about foods that can sometimes be spicy</DEF><THESEXA>a mild curry</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>bland</EXP><DEF>not having an interesting taste</DEF><THESEXA>I found the sauce rather bland.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-117e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>tax</EXP><DEF>money that you must pay to the government, especially from the money you earn, or as an additional payment when you buy something</DEF><THESEXA>How much income tax do you pay each month?</THESEXA><THESEXA>The Republicans promised to reduce taxes before the last election.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Consumers are angry that the tax on petrol has gone up yet again.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>duty</EXP><DEF>a tax you pay on something you buy</DEF><THESEXA>The budget also sharply raised the duty on alcohol and tobacco.</THESEXA><THESEXA>customs duty<GLOSS>tax you pay on goods you buy and bring into the country</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tariff</EXP><DEF>a tax on goods coming into a country or going out of a country, especially to protect a country’s industry from cheap goods from other countries</DEF><THESEXA>the import tariffs on hi-tech equipment</THESEXA><THESEXA>The government’s tariff and trade policies came under fierce attack.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>levy</EXP><DEF>an extra amount of money that you have to pay the government, usually as a tax, often in order to encourage people not to use or do something</DEF><THESEXA>A new levy on fuel inefficient vehicles has been proposed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>surcharge</EXP><DEF>an amount of money that you have to pay in addition to the agreed or stated price of something</DEF><THESEXA>British Airways will increase its fuel surcharge on all airline tickets from June 3. </THESEXA><THESEXA>When you get cash at some machines, you have to pay an ATM surcharge.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1158"><Section><Exponent><EXP>teach</EXP><DEF>to give lessons in a school, college, or university</DEF><THESEXA>I taught for a year in France.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He teaches physics at York University.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lecture</EXP><DEF>to teach in a college or university by giving talks to groups of students on a subject</DEF><THESEXA>He lectures in engineering at a local college.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>instruct</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to teach someone how to do something, especially a particular practical skill</DEF><THESEXA>He was instructing them how to use the computer system.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Staff are instructed in how to respond in the event of a fire.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tutor</EXP><DEF>to teach one student or a small group</DEF><THESEXA>I found work tutoring Mexican students in English.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>coach</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>to give private lessons, especially so that someone can pass an important test</DEF><THESEXA>He coaches students for their university entrance exams.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>train</EXP><DEF>to teach a person or group of people in the particular skills or knowledge they need to do a job</DEF><THESEXA>It will take at least a month to train the new assistant.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>educate</EXP><DEF>to teach someone over a long period, usually at school or university</DEF><THESEXA>He was educated in England.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her parents want to educate her at home.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>show sb the ropes</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to show someone how to do a job or task that they have just started doing</DEF><THESEXA>Miss McGinley will show you the ropes and answer any questions you may have.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-1142"><Section><Exponent><EXP>teacher</EXP><DEF>someone who teaches as their job, especially in a school</DEF><THESEXA>a high school teacher</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>principal</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>headteacher</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>the teacher who is in charge of a school or college</DEF><THESEXA>The teacher sent him to the principal’s office.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tutor</EXP><DEF>someone who gives private lessons to one student or a small group of students. In Britain, a <DEFBOLD>professor </DEFBOLD> is a high-ranking university teacher, especially one who is head of a department</DEF><THESEXA>She was professor of linguistics at Cambridge University. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>instructor</EXP><DEF>someone who teaches a sport or a practical skill such as swimming or driving</DEF><THESEXA>He works as a ski instructor in the winter.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a driving instructor</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>coach</EXP><DEF>someone who helps a person or team improve in a sport</DEF><THESEXA>a professional tennis coach</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>educator</EXP><GEO>especially AmE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>someone whose job involves teaching people, or someone who is an expert on education</DEF><THESEXA>Most educators agree that class sizes are still too big.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trainer</EXP><DEF>someone who teaches people particular skills, especially the skills they need to do a job</DEF><THESEXA>a teacher trainer</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many companies pay outside trainers to teach management skills to their staff.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>governess</EXP><DEF>a woman who lived with a family and taught their children in past times</DEF><THESEXA>As a governess, Charlotte Brontë received twenty pounds a year. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-5c8a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>tear</EXP><DEF>to damage paper or cloth by pulling it too hard, or letting it touch something sharp</DEF><THESEXA>She unwrapped the present carefully, trying not to tear the paper.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I tore a hole in my jacket, climbing over the fence.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rip</EXP><DEF>to tear something quickly or violently</DEF><THESEXA>Beth excitedly ripped open the package.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Stop pulling my dress! You’ll rip it!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>split</EXP><DEF>to tear your trousers or shirt when you put them on, because they are too tight for you</DEF><THESEXA>He bent down and split his trousers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Oh no, now I’ve split my shirt.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ladder</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>if a woman ladders her <NonDV><REFHWD>tights</REFHWD></NonDV> or <NonDV><REFHWD>stockings</REFHWD></NonDV>, she tears them so that a long thin line appears in them</DEF><THESEXA>Damn! I’ve laddered my tights!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>snag</EXP><DEF>to catch a piece of clothing on something rough or sharp so that it tears slightly</DEF><THESEXA>I snagged my shirt on a nail.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shred</EXP><DEF>to deliberately destroy letters, documents etc by cutting them into thin pieces, often by using a special machine</DEF><THESEXA>In order to prevent fraud, it’s best to shred your bank statements.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I went through all my papers shredding things I didn’t need.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>frayed</EXP><DEF>torn a little along the edges – used about clothes, carpets etc that have been used a lot</DEF><THESEXA>He was wearing an old pair of frayed jeans.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The rug was a little frayed around the edges.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The jacket was a little frayed at the cuffs.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-10f1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>tease</EXP><DEF>to laugh at someone and make jokes in order to have fun by embarrassing them, either in a friendly way or in an unkind way. In everyday English, people often say <DEFBOLD>make fun of</DEFBOLD> rather than <DEFBOLD>tease</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>At work, we all tease her because she’s always late.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sam’s sisters used to tease him because he was overweight.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make fun of sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to tease someone, especially in an unkind way, by laughing at something they do and making them seem stupid</DEF><THESEXA>The boys at school used to make fun of me and call me names.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Everyone made fun of the way our Maths teacher walked.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>taunt</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>tɔːnt</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to tease someone in a very unpleasant way that shows you do not respect them, in order to make them angry or upset</DEF><THESEXA>In the end he hit the man for taunting him about his wife.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The other prisoners taunted him until he couldn’t bear it any more.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pull sb’s leg</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to tease someone in a friendly way, by trying to make them think something is true when it is not</DEF><THESEXA>I’m not really 18. I was only pulling your leg.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t believe you! You’re pulling my leg!</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wind sb up</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to deliberately say something to someone, in order to see if they become annoyed or worried</DEF><THESEXA>Are you trying to wind me up?</THESEXA><THESEXA>My friends are always winding me up about it. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take the mickey (out of sb)</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make someone look silly, often in a friendly way, for example by copying them or saying something that you do not really mean about them</DEF><THESEXA>I don’t speak like that – stop taking the mickey!</THESEXA><THESEXA>‘You’re a genius, we all know that!’ ‘Are you taking the mickey out of me?’</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-101f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>temporary</EXP><DEF>continuing for only a limited period of time</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve got a temporary office job, but I hope I’ll find something more permanent soon.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’m sure the problem is only temporary.</THESEXA><THESEXA>temporary staff</THESEXA><THESEXA>a temporary visa</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>provisional</EXP><DEF>temporary – used especially about arrangements or information that people may decide to change in the future. Also used about licences or governments which are only arranged to last for a short time</DEF><THESEXA>Does the hotel accept provisional bookings?</THESEXA><THESEXA>They have set a provisional date for the next meeting.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a provisional driving licence</THESEXA><THESEXA>In June, the rebels claimed to have formed a provisional government.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Provisional figures yesterday from the Department of Transport showed that 4,274 people were killed last year — 6 percent fewer than the previous year.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stopgap</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>temporary – used about something that you use for a short time until you can replace it with something better</DEF><THESEXA> The Republicans will approve the one-month budget as <COLLOINEXA>a stopgap measure</COLLOINEXA> to keep the state operating. </THESEXA><THESEXA>This is only <COLLOINEXA>a stopgap solution</COLLOINEXA> to the country’s debt problem.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>passing</EXP><GRAM>only before noun</GRAM><DEF>temporary – used about an interest, thought, or feeling that is short and not very serious</DEF><THESEXA>Tonight’s game will be exciting, even to those with only a passing interest in the sport.</THESEXA><THESEXA>As a student, I didn’t give working in finance even a passing thought.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ephemeral</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>existing or popular for only a short time</DEF><THESEXA>the ephemeral beauty of white snow on green trees and bare branches</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many new words, particularly slang, are fleeting and ephemeral.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>transient</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>continuing for only a limited time, or staying somewhere for only a short time – used especially to say that something is always changing, or people only stay somewhere for a short time</DEF><THESEXA>the transient nature of life</THESEXA><THESEXA>transient changes in the electrical properties of the neurons </THESEXA><THESEXA>Arizona has a highly transient population.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-101a"><HEADING>Senses 1 & 2</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>terrible/awful</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>dreadful</LEXVAR><GEO>especially BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>very bad</DEF><THESEXA>The journey was terrible – it took six hours.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The food was good but we had terrible service.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s such an awful programme! How can you watch it?</THESEXA><THESEXA>He looked dreadful.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>horrible</EXP><DEF>very bad and unpleasant – used especially when something has a strong effect on you and you feel shocked, annoyed, or sick</DEF><THESEXA>This soup tastes horrible.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I got a horrible shock when I saw the bill.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a horrible accident</THESEXA><THESEXA>a horrible thing to say to someone</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>appalling</EXP><DEF>terrible – especially in a way that is shocking. <DEFBOLD>Appalling</DEFBOLD> is stronger and a little more formal than <DEFBOLD>terrible</DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD>horrible</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The refugees are living in appalling conditions.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The teacher said my handwriting was appalling.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>disgusting</EXP><DEF>terrible – used about a taste, smell, habit etc, often one that makes you feel sick</DEF><THESEXA>The smell was disgusting and I had to go out.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you have to bite your nails? It’s a disgusting habit.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>lousy</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>terrible – used especially to express annoyance</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve had a lousy day at the office.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This area is a lousy place to live.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hopeless</EXP><DEF>very bad and difficult – used when there is no chance of success or improvement</DEF><THESEXA>We were trying to pay off our debts but it was a hopeless situation.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He was given the almost hopeless task of trying to negotiate a ceasefire.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>diabolical</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>extremely bad – used to express great disapproval of an action or event</DEF><THESEXA>The prices are diabolical.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a diabolical waste of money</THESEXA><THESEXA>McAndrew gave a diabolical performance on Saturday.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-100a"><HEADING>Sense 1</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>test</EXP><DEF>a set of questions or practical activities, which are intended to test?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>exam</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>examination</LEXVAR><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB></Variant><DEF>an important test that you do at the written examination at the end of the course.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>quiz</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>a quick test that a teacher gives to a class, usually to check that students are learning the things they should be learning</DEF><THESEXA>We have a math quiz every Monday.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a <COLLOINEXA>pop quiz</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>a quiz given by a teacher without any warning</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>finals</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>the last exams that you take at the end of a British university course</DEF><THESEXA>During my finals I was revising till 3 o'clock in the morning most days.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>final</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>an important test that you take at the end of a particular class in high school or college</DEF><THESEXA>The English final was pretty hard.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>midterm</EXP><GEO>AmE</GEO><DEF>an important test that you take in the middle of a term, covering what you have learned in a particular class in high school or college</DEF><THESEXA>He did badly in the midterm.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>oral exam</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>oral</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><DEF>an exam in which you answer questions by speaking, instead of writing, for example to test how good you are at speaking a foreign language</DEF><THESEXA>Nicky got an A in her Spanish oral.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You can either take an oral exam or do a 25 page essay.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>practical</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>an exam that tests your ability to do or make things, rather than your ability to write about them</DEF><THESEXA>The examinations</DEF><THESEXA>She did well in the mocks.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>paper</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a set of printed questions used as an examination in a particular subject, or the answers people write</DEF><THESEXA>The history paper was really difficult.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The papers are marked by the other teachers.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-fcc"><Section><Exponent><EXP>thank</EXP><DEF>to tell someone that you are pleased and grateful for something they have given you or done for you</DEF><THESEXA>She thanked them for the presents.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You don’t need to thank me.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He thanked her for coming and showed her to her car.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>say thank you</EXP><DEF>to thank someone by saying the words ‘thank you’, or by giving them something</DEF><THESEXA>I just wanted to say thank you for all your help.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This gift is our way of saying thank you to everyone who worked so hard.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Make sure you say thank you for your birthday presents.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>express your gratitude</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to thank someone for something they have done – used in speeches and letters</DEF><THESEXA>I would like to express my gratitude to all those who have sent me letters of support.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>show your appreciation</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to thank someone by giving them something, or by doing something special for them</DEF><THESEXA>To show our gratitude for all your hard work, we are paying you a special bonus.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>acknowledge</EXP><DEF>to publicly do or say something to thank someone for their help or support</DEF><THESEXA>The building will be named the John Laird Centre, to acknowledge the contribution of the Laird family to the area. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He acknowledged the applause with a small bow.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-fa4"><Section><Exponent><EXP>stage</EXP><DEF>the raised area on which the actors, musicians etc perform</DEF><THESEXA>He came <COLLOINEXA>on stage</COLLOINEXA> to rapturous applause.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The band will appear live on stage for the first time in three years.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the stalls</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>the orchestra</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>the lower level of seats</DEF><THESEXA>He had a seat in the stalls.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We paid $100 for a seat in the orchestra.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the circle</EXP><GEO> BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>the balcony</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>the higher level of seats</DEF><THESEXA>They were sitting in the balcony.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the orchestra pit</EXP><DEF>the space below the stage where the musicians sit</DEF><THESEXA>The actress fell into the orchestra pit.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>box office</EXP><DEF>the place in a theatre where you buy tickets</DEF><THESEXA>Collect your tickets at the box office.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>programme</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><ORTHVAR>program</ORTHVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a small book that you buy when you go to the theatre that gives information about the play and the performers</DEF><THESEXA>His name is not in the programme.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you want me to buy you a program?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>interval</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>intermission</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE formal and AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a short period of time between the parts of a play or show when the audience can talk or have a drink</DEF><THESEXA>We got a drink in the interval.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-f80"><Section><Exponent><EXP>therefore</EXP><DEF>for this reason</DEF><THESEXA>She already had a lot of experience and therefore seemed the best candidate for the job.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>so</EXP><DEF>therefore. <DEFBOLD>So</DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>therefore</DEFBOLD>, and is more common in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>They had not eaten all day,  so they were very hungry.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>thus</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>as a result of what you have just mentioned</DEF><THESEXA>The program is very simple and thus easy to run.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hence</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>for this reason</DEF><THESEXA>This material is highly poisonous, hence the importance of careful handling.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>as a result</EXP><DEF>used when saying that because of a particular situation, something else happens or is true</DEF><THESEXA>Some people suffer from stress at work and become ill  as a result.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Economic growth slowed down as a result of inflation.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>consequently/as a consequence</EXP><DEF>used when saying that because of a particular situation, something else happens or is true. <DEFBOLD>Consequently</DEFBOLD> and <DEFBOLD>as a consequence</DEFBOLD> are more formal than <DEFBOLD>as a result</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The disease attacks the plant, the flower does not open, and consequently  no seeds are produced.</THESEXA><THESEXA>As a consequence of global warming, our climate is already starting to change.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>this means that</EXP><DEF>used when saying what the result of something is</DEF><THESEXA>If students arrive late, this means that lesson time is wasted.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>for this reason</EXP><DEF>used when explaining the reason for something</DEF><THESEXA>Spell check programs do not recognize when you have used the wrong word. For this reason, you must still read over your work carefully. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-ea2"><Section><SECHEADING>types of travel tickets</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>single (ticket)/one-way ticket</EXP><DEF>a ticket that lets you go to a place but not back again</DEF><THESEXA>A single to Edinburgh, please.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He bought a one-way ticket to Los Angeles. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>return (ticket)</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>round-trip ticket</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>a ticket that lets you go to a place and back again</DEF><THESEXA>A return to London, please.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>season ticket</EXP><DEF>a ticket that lets you make the same journey every day for a fixed period of time</DEF><THESEXA>My company pays for my season ticket.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>e-ticket</EXP><DEF>a ticket that you buy over the Internet, in which you are given a number which you use when you check in at an airport</DEF><THESEXA>All you have to do is print off the e-ticket and show it at the check-in desk.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-e8c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>tidy</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a place, room etc that is tidy looks nice because everything has been arranged and put in the right place</DEF><THESEXA>a tidy desk</THESEXA><THESEXA>I want to leave the place clean and tidy before we go.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>neat</EXP><DEF>use this about things that are carefully arranged in a way that is nice to look at</DEF><THESEXA>a neat pile of towels</THESEXA><THESEXA>The room was neat and tidy.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>immaculate</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ɪˈmækjələt</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>a place or thing that is immaculate is perfectly clean and neat – use this to emphasize how clean something looks</DEF><THESEXA>Our house was always immaculate.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>well-kept</EXP><DEF>a well-kept building or garden is very well cared for and looks neat and clean</DEF><THESEXA>a large house on well-kept grounds</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>orderly</EXP><DEF>arranged or organized in a sensible or neat way</DEF><THESEXA>The room was orderly and uncluttered.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-78ef4d03.118e0988fe1.6bb5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>true</EXP><DEF>based on real facts, and not imagined or invented</DEF><THESEXA>The film was based on a true story.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you think the rumours are true?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>accurate</EXP><DEF>based on facts and not containing any mistakes – used about descriptions, information, and numbers</DEF><THESEXA>The measurements are accurate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His assessment of the current economic situation is accurate.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>undeniable/indisputable</EXP><DEF>definitely true, so that no one can argue or disagree about it</DEF><THESEXA>It is indisputable that the situation has got worse.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The decline in inflation was undeniable, even if the reasons for the decline were unclear.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>factual</EXP><DEF>based on facts, or involving facts</DEF><THESEXA>The court makes its decision based on factual evidence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is very little factual information about the incident.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a factual account of what happened</THESEXA><THESEXA>The questions ask for a purely factual answer, not for opinion.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>verifiable</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>able to be proven to be true or correct</DEF><THESEXA>The data was verifiable.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>it is a fact</EXP><DEF>used when saying that something is definitely true</DEF><THESEXA>It is a fact that women live longer than men.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be the truth</EXP><DEF>to be true – used when saying that someone is not lying</DEF><THESEXA>What I told you was the truth.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be the case</EXP><DEF>if a situation is the case, that is the way the situation truly is</DEF><THESEXA>It is certainly the case that crime rates are lower in Europe than in the US.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.-5592f786.1197a3eee79.-22ad"><Section><SECHEADING>people</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>trustworthy</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially written</REGISTERLAB><DEF>if <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-c7d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>try</EXP><DEF>to take action in order to do something that you may not be able to do</DEF><THESEXA>I tried <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-c4a"><HEADING>Sense 4</HEADING><Section><Exponent><EXP>turn</EXP><DEF>to move around a central or fixed point</DEF><THESEXA>The wheels way</DEF><THESEXA>The blades of the helicopter whirled overhead.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>twirl (around)</EXP><DEF>to spin around quickly, especially as part of a dance or performance</DEF><THESEXA>The couples were twirling around on the dance floor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>swirl (around)</EXP><DEF>to move around quickly in a circular movement, especially when the movement goes outwards or upwards from the center</DEF><THESEXA>Her white skirt swirled around her legs as she danced.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The leaves began to swirl around. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spiral</EXP><DEF>to move in a continuous curve that gets nearer to or further from its central point as it goes around</DEF><THESEXA>The smoke spiralled toward the
