<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-c35"><Section><Exponent><EXP>pair</EXP><DEF>two things of the same type that you use together. Also used about two people who do something together or who you often see together</DEF><THESEXA>a pair of shoes</THESEXA><THESEXA>a pair of socks</THESEXA><THESEXA>Winners will receive a pair of tickets for the show.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The pair were arrested six days after the killing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They're a funny pair!</THESEXA><THESEXA>The British pair will be playing in the final on Saturday.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>a couple (of sth)</EXP><DEF>two things of the same type, or a very small number of things</DEF><THESEXA>There were a couple of empty seats at the table.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you have any stamps? I just need a couple.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>couple</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>two people who are married or having a sexual relationship</DEF><THESEXA>a married couple</THESEXA><THESEXA>The couple met at university.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>twins</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>plural</GRAM><DEF>two children who were born on the same day to the same mother</DEF><THESEXA>The twins look very alike.</THESEXA><THESEXA><COLLOINEXA>identical twins</COLLOINEXA></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>duo</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>two people who perform together or who are often seen together</DEF><THESEXA>a comedy duo</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>duet </EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a piece of music written for two people to play</DEF><THESEXA>They played a duet by Brahms.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>twice</EXP><DEF>two times</DEF><POS>adv</POS><THESEXA>The group meets twice a week.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She sneezed twice.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>for two people</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>for two</EXP><DEF>for two people</DEF><THESEXA>A table for two, please.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>double bed/double room</EXP><DEF>a bed for two people, or a room that has a bed for two people in it</DEF><THESEXA>I'd like to book a double room.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>twin beds/twin room</EXP><DEF>two separate single beds, or a room with two separate single beds</DEF><THESEXA>We asked for a twin room, and they've give us a double room.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-c19"><Section><Exponent><EXP>type/kind/sort</EXP><DEF>one member of a group of people or things that have similar features or qualities. <DEFBOLD>Type</DEFBOLD> is the usual word to use in scientific or technical contexts. In everyday English, people usually use <DEFBOLD>kind</DEFBOLD> or <DEFBOLD>sort</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>What type of fish is this?</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are two main personality types.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>kind</EXP><DEF>a type of person or thing. <DEFBOLD>Kind </DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>type</DEFBOLD>, and is used especially in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>What kind of food do you like?</THESEXA><THESEXA>There were all kinds of people there.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The study is the first of its kind in Ireland.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sort</EXP><GEO>especially BrE</GEO><DEF>a type of person or thing. <DEFBOLD>Sort </DEFBOLD> is less formal than <DEFBOLD>type</DEFBOLD>, and is used especially in everyday British English</DEF><THESEXA>What sort of person is she?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I like all sorts of music.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>form</EXP><DEF>one type of something from all the ones that are possible – used especially when things have different physical characteristics, or in certain fixed phrases</DEF><THESEXA>There are many forms of heart disease. </THESEXA><THESEXA>Melanoma is a form of skin cancer.</THESEXA><THESEXA> The first primitive <COLLOINEXA>life forms</COLLOINEXA> consumed various materials, including hydrogen sulfide, and released oxygen. </THESEXA><THESEXA>In those days, horses were the commonest <COLLOINEXA>form of transport</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We need to use alternative <COLLOINEXA>forms of energy</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a popular <COLLOINEXA>form of entertainment</COLLOINEXA></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>variety</EXP><DEF>a type that is slightly different from others in the same group</DEF><THESEXA>The French make many varieties of cheese.</THESEXA><THESEXA>This is a new variety of apple.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>species</EXP><DEF>a type of plant or animal, which can breed together to produce plants or animals of the same type</DEF><THESEXA>These forests contain many species of trees. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The giant panda is an <COLLOINEXA>endangered species</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>of a ... nature</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when talking about a particular type of thing</DEF><THESEXA>Many people find it embarrassing to discuss problems of a sexual nature.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Minor incidents of this nature normally occur about once a month.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>category</EXP><DEF> a group of people or things that are all of the same type – used when there is a clear system for deciding which group something belongs to</DEF><THESEXA>The three major categories of rock are: igneous, metamorphic, and sedimentary.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She won the best actress category at the Oscars.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>brand</EXP><DEF>used when talking about the particular way that someone does something or thinks about something, when this is very different from that of other people</DEF><THESEXA>She has her own special brand of humour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He has called for a more positive brand of politics.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>genre</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a type of art, music, literature etc. that has a particular style or feature</DEF><THESEXA>He has written novels in several genres, most notably science fiction.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>a type of product</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>brand</EXP><DEF>the name of a type of product made by a company, especially one that you use every day such as food or cleaning products</DEF><THESEXA>a survey to find out which brand of toothpaste people prefer </THESEXA><THESEXA> advertising for a well-known brand of cigarettes</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>make</EXP><DEF> a type of product made by a particular company – used especially about things such as machines, equipment, or cars</DEF><THESEXA>‘What make of car do you drive?’ ‘A Ford.’</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>model</EXP><DEF>one particular type or design of a vehicle, machine etc</DEF><THESEXA>The new models are much faster.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-bfc"><Section><Exponent><EXP>typical</EXP><DEF>a typical person or thing is a good example of that type of person or thing</DEF><THESEXA>With his camera around his neck, he looked like a typical tourist. </THESEXA><THESEXA>The windows are typical of houses built during this period. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>classic</EXP><DEF>used to describe a very typical and very good example of something</DEF><THESEXA>It was a classic case of the cure being worse than the disease. </THESEXA><THESEXA>a classic mistake</THESEXA><THESEXA>The book is a classic example of great teamwork.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>archetypal</EXP><DEF>the archetypal  person or thing is the most typical example of that kind of person or thing, and has all their most important qualities</DEF><THESEXA>the archetypal English village</THESEXA><THESEXA>Indiana Jones is the archetypal adventure hero.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>quintessential</EXP><DEF>used when you want to emphasize that someone or something is the very best example of something – used especially when you admire them very much</DEF><THESEXA>the quintessential guide to New York </THESEXA><THESEXA>Robert Plant is the quintessential rock ‘n’ roll singer.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stereotypical</EXP><DEF>having the characteristics that many people believe a particular type of person or thing has – used when you think these beliefs are not true</DEF><THESEXA>Hollywood films are full of stereotypical images of women as wives and mothers. </THESEXA><THESEXA>He challenges stereotypical ideas about people with disabilities.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>representative</EXP><DEF>containing the most common types of people or things that are found in something, and showing what it is usually like</DEF><THESEXA>a representative sample of college students</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>characteristic</EXP><DEF>very typical of a particular type of thing, or of someone’s character or usual behaviour</DEF><THESEXA>Each species of bird has its own characteristic song.</THESEXA><THESEXA>What gives Paris its characteristic charm?</THESEXA><THESEXA>He played with his characteristic skill.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the characteristic symptoms of the disease</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be the epitome of sth</EXP><DEF>to be the best possible example of a particular type of person or thing or of a particular quality</DEF><THESEXA>His house was thought to be the epitome of good taste.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-bd5"><Section><Exponent><EXP>ugly</EXP><DEF>very unpleasant to look at – used about people, objects, and buildings</DEF><THESEXA>She thinks she's ugly but she's not.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an ugly block of flats</THESEXA><THESEXA>cheap ugly furniture</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unattractive</EXP><DEF>not pleasant to look at. <DEFBOLD>Unattractive</DEFBOLD> sounds more formal and less strong than <DEFBOLD>ugly</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>an unattractive modern town which was built during the 1960s</THESEXA><THESEXA>Women found him unattractive.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unsightly</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>not pleasant to look at, and spoiling the appearance of something</DEF><THESEXA>Unsightly red spots started to appear on her face.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The windows have been blocked off with unsightly metal bars. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>extremely ugly</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>hideous</EXP><DEF>extremely ugly</DEF><THESEXA>His hideous face twisted into a smile.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a hideous concrete shopping centre</THESEXA><THESEXA>a hideous monster</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>repulsive</EXP><DEF>extremely ugly, especially in a way that makes you want to look away</DEF><THESEXA>His appearance was so repulsive he had to wear a mask.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>grotesque</EXP><DEF>extremely ugly in a strange or unnatural way</DEF><THESEXA>A grotesque figure appeared out of the darkness.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>an eyesore</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>a blot on the landscape</LEXVAR></Variant><POS>n</POS><GRAM>singular</GRAM><DEF>something that is so ugly that it spoils the appearance of an area</DEF><THESEXA>Local residents regard the new office building as an eyesore.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I think wind turbines are a blot on the landscape - why can't people just use less electricity?</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-bb3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>under</EXP><DEF>something that is under something else has that thing directly above it or covering it</DEF><THESEXA>The pen was under the desk.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She had a T-shirt on under her sweater.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>below</EXP><DEF>in a lower position than something else, though not always directly under it</DEF><THESEXA>From the cliffs we could barely see the people on the beach below us.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His apartment is below ours on the left.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>underneath</EXP><DEF>under – used especially to emphasize that something covers, touches, or hides something</DEF><THESEXA>The girls wear shorts underneath their cheerleading skirts.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I found the book underneath the sofa.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>beneath</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>under or at a lower level</DEF><THESEXA>They strolled hand in hand beneath the summer moon.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The water lies just beneath the surface of the earth.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

understandto know the meaning of something, or the reasons for somethingI understand what you’re saying.Doctors are beginning to understand what causes the disease.seeespecially spokento understand something, especially the truth about a situation or the reasons for somethingI can see why you don’t like him.Do you see what I mean?Oh, I see!getinformalto understand a joke, what someone says, the reasons why something is true etcShe didn’t seem to get the joke.Do you get me?He explained the math homework twice, but I still don’t get it.I still don’t get why she left. comprehendformalto understand something, or understand why something is importantThe dream was easy to describe, but difficult to comprehend.They had failed to comprehend the significance of the problem.know what sb meansspokento understand what someone is telling you, or what a situation is like, especially because you have some experience or knowledge about this‘It can be really hard to give up smoking.’ ‘I know exactly what you mean.’We now know what the scientists meant when they warned about global warming.followto understand something such as an explanation or story as you hear it, read it etcThe plot is hard to follow.His instructions were easy to follow.
to understand something difficult graspto completely understand an idea or a fact, especially a complicated oneSome of his theories can be rather difficult for the ordinary reader to grasp.I don’t think Stuart really grasped the point I was making.fathomˈfæðəmformalto understand what something means or the reasons for something, after thinking carefully about itShe looked at him, puzzled, trying to fathom the reasons for his actions.make sense of sthto understand something that is not easy to understand, especially by thinking about itPeople are still trying to make sense of the news.can’t make head nor tail of sthspokenused when something seems impossible to understandI can’t make head nor tail of his plays, and I’m not sure that anyone else can either.

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-b8c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>undress/get undressed</EXP><DEF>to take off all your clothes, especially before going to bed. <DEFBOLD>Get undressed</DEFBOLD> is the usual phrase to use in everyday English</DEF><THESEXA>She got undressed and went to bed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Em felt uncomfortable undressing in front of other people.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>take sth off/remove</EXP><DEF>to remove a piece of clothing. <DEFBOLD>Remove</DEFBOLD> is more formal than <DEFBOLD>take off</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>'It’s warm in here,' said Michael, taking off his jacket.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Take your shoes off before you come in.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Police officers told him to remove his clothes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>strip (off)</EXP><DEF>to take off all or some of your clothes – used especially when you do this quickly, when someone has ordered you to do it, or in a way that is deliberately sexually exciting</DEF><THESEXA>Ben stripped off and got into the shower.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She stripped off her clothes and lay on the bed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The women in the club screamed and clapped as the male dancers began to strip.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>get changed</EXP><DEF>to take off your clothes and put on different clothes</DEF><THESEXA>The boys ran up the stairs to get changed.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ll be ready in a second, I just have to get changed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>undo</EXP><DEF>to open a piece of clothing that is fastened</DEF><THESEXA>He started to undo his shirt.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Amy was struggling to undo her belt.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-b6b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>unemployed</EXP><DEF>someone who is unemployed does not have a job</DEF><THESEXA>Fifty percent of the men in this town are unemployed.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>out of work</EXP><DEF>unemployed, especially for a long period of time, when you had a job before</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve been out of work for two years.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>redundant</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>if someone is redundant, they have been told that they no longer have a job</DEF><THESEXA>He was made redundant earlier this year.</THESEXA><THESEXA>redundant miners</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be on the dole</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>be on welfare/on unemployment</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to be receiving money from the government because you do not have a job</DEF><THESEXA>I didn’t want to go back on the dole.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Many people on welfare don’t have anyone to take care of the kids while they train for a job.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>be looking for work</EXP><DEF>if someone is looking for work, they do not have a job and are trying to find one</DEF><THESEXA>How long have you been looking for work?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>jobless</EXP><DEF>people who are jobless do not have jobs – used especially in news reports</DEF><THESEXA>The jobless totals have risen by 6% in the last year. </THESEXA><THESEXA>jobless youths</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-b46"><Section><Exponent><EXP>unfair/not fair</EXP><DEF>not right or fair, especially because not everyone has an equal opportunity</DEF><THESEXA>The present welfare system is grossly unfair.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It’s not fair that people are paying different prices for the same tickets.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unjust</EXP><DEF>not fair or right according to the principles of a particular society</DEF><THESEXA>He believed it was an illegal and unjust others</DEF><THESEXA>We live in a deeply unequal society.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the unequal distribution of global resources</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>inequitable</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>unfair because people are treated in different ways, or because some people have more power than others</DEF><THESEXA>inequitable tax laws</THESEXA><THESEXA>The system is inequitable, because it makes it possible for rich people to buy a place at university.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>biased</EXP><DEF>unfairly against or in favour of a particular group</DEF><THESEXA>biased reporting</THESEXA><THESEXA>There were claims that prison bosses were racially biased.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The policy was <COLLOINEXA>biased against</COLLOINEXA> women.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The trade laws are <COLLOINEXA>biased in favour of</COLLOINEXA> rich countries.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>treating people unfairly because of their race, sex, age etc</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>prejudiced</EXP><DEF>treating someone unfairly and having an unreasonable dislike of them because of their race, sex etc, or because they are old, disabled etc</DEF><THESEXA>The media had very prejudiced attitudes towards disabled people.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I don’t want to sound prejudiced, but I do think women are better at this type of job.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>racist</EXP><DEF>treating someone unfairly because of their race</DEF><THESEXA>racist remarks</THESEXA><THESEXA>Children pick up racist attitudes from their parents.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sexist</EXP><DEF>treating someone unfairly because of their sex</DEF><THESEXA>He had made sexist comments to several women in the office.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The show was about two female inspectors who had trouble with their sexist bosses.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>ageist</EXP><DEF>treating someone unfairly because of their age – used especially when old people are unfairly treated</DEF><THESEXA>Ageist attitudes result in older people being discriminated against in the workplace.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>homophobic</EXP><DEF>treating someone unfairly because they are homosexual</DEF><THESEXA>Many of his songs are homophobic.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-b1c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>unfashionable</EXP><DEF>not popular or fashionable at the present time – used especially about people’s ideas, beliefs, and way of life</DEF><THESEXA>They lived in an unfashionable part of London.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Marriage has become unfashionable in some countries.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>out of fashion</EXP><DEF>no longer fashionable – used especially about clothes, styles, and behaviour</DEF><THESEXA>Miniskirts have been in and out of fashion over the years.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Leather jackets had never <COLLOINEXA>fallen out of fashion</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>become unfashionable</GLOSS>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He used to be a smoker until smoking <COLLOINEXA>went out of fashion</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dated</EXP><DEF>used for describing things which were fashionable and modern at one time but are starting to look unfashionable or old now</DEF><THESEXA>The hotel needs to improve its dated interior.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The show seems dated now.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>passé</EXP><DEF>no longer fashionable – used especially about ways of doing and thinking about things. A rather formal use</DEF><THESEXA>The musicals and light comedies for which she was known have become passé.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some of his ideas about food are a bit passé.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sth is so last year</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when saying that something is now very unfashionable – a very informal use</DEF><THESEXA>Blogging is already starting to feel so last year.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The resort is overcrowded and so last year.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-b15"><Section><Exponent><EXP>unfasten</EXP><DEF>to open the two sides of a piece of clothing, bag, belt etc</DEF><THESEXA>It was hot in the waiting-room, so I unfastened my coat.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Please do not unfasten your seat belt while the vehicle is in motion.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>undo</EXP><DEF>to unfasten clothes or unfasten buttons, zips etc on clothes</DEF><THESEXA>My fingers were so cold that I couldn’t undo the buttons.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>untie</EXP><DEF>to unfasten the knot that fastens shoes, a tie, a scarf etc</DEF><THESEXA>He untied his shoes and kicked them off.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unbutton</EXP><DEF>to unfasten the buttons on a piece of clothing</DEF><THESEXA>She slowly unbuttoned her blouse.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>unzip</EXP><DEF>to unfasten clothes, bags etc by unfastening a zip</DEF><THESEXA>She unzipped the case and took out a thick file.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>loosen</EXP><DEF>to unfasten clothes a little, but not completely, in order to make yourself more comfortable</DEF><THESEXA>Carter took off his jacket and loosened his tie.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>open</EXP><DEF>to unfasten a bag, box, or other container</DEF><THESEXA>‘Could you open your bag, please?’ said the security guard.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-aee"><Section><SECHEADING>person/voice/behaviour</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>unfriendly/not friendly</EXP><DEF>behaving towards someone in a way that shows you are not interested in them or are not ready to talk to them or help them</DEF><THESEXA>The hotel staff were unfriendly and unhelpful.</THESEXA><THESEXA>an unfriendly tone of voice</THESEXA><THESEXA>They didn't seem very friendly to strangers.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She gave him an unfriendly glance.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hostile</EXP><DEF>very unfriendly, and embassy.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cold</EXP><DEF>behaving towards other people as if you do not like them or care about them</DEF><THESEXA>He gave her a cold stare.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a cold voice </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>frosty</EXP><DEF>unfriendly, especially because you are angry with someone</DEF><THESEXA>When she spoke, her tone was frosty.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He got <COLLOINEXA>a frosty reception</COLLOINEXA> from his wife when he finally returned home <GLOSS>she was not very friendly towards him</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>aloof</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>not wanting to talk to other people or spend time with them, especially because you think you are better than them</DEF><THESEXA>Some politicians are criticized for being too <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-aa8"><Section><Exponent><EXP>unimportant</EXP><DEF>not important</DEF><THESEXA>The exact details are unimportant.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Girls' education was seen as unimportant.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>of no/little importance</EXP><DEF>not important, or not very important. These phrases sound a little more formal than <DEFBOLD>unimportant</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>If you're capable of doing the job, your age is of no importance.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It's of little importance whether or not this story is true.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>minor</EXP><DEF>small and not very likely to have an important effect – used especially about changes, problems, injuries, damage, or differences</DEF><THESEXA>I've made a few minor changes.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The driver suffered minor injuries.</THESEXA><THESEXA>These are just minor problems.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trivial</EXP><DEF>very unimportant and not worth worrying about or spending time on</DEF><THESEXA>They had a disagreement about some trivial matter.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She tends to get upset about trivial things.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>insignificant</EXP><DEF>very small and unimportant, especially when compared to other things</DEF><THESEXA>Her own problems seemed insignificant.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The amount of carbon they produce is relatively insignificant.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>negligible</EXP><DEF>extremely small and not important – used especially about effects, amounts, differences, or risks</DEF><THESEXA>So far, the program has had a negligible effect.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The difference in price is negligible.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>secondary</EXP><DEF>not as important as something else</DEF><THESEXA>These issues are of secondary importance.</THESEXA><THESEXA>For many women, a career is secondary to being a mother.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-a88"><Section><Exponent><EXP>university</EXP><DEF>a place where you can study a subject at a high level to get a degree</DEF><THESEXA>Harvard University</THESEXA><THESEXA>About a third of the pupils go on to university.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>college</EXP><DEF>in Britain, a place where you can study after you finish secondary school, especially to train for a job. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-a3e"><Section><Exponent><EXP>unlucky</EXP><DEF>having bad luck, or causing bad luck</DEF><THESEXA>Matthews played well and was unlucky not to score.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Thirteen is an unlucky number.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We were unlucky with the weather. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-a24"><Section><Exponent><EXP>unsuitable/not suitable</EXP><DEF>not having the right qualities for a particular person, purpose, or situation</DEF><THESEXA>These toys are not suitable for children under 3.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The job was considered unsuitable for women.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Some dogs end up in totally unsuitable homes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>not appropriate/inappropriate</EXP><DEF>not suitable for a particular situation or purpose – used especially about someone’s behaviour or language</DEF><THESEXA>Slang is not appropriate in an academic essay.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Never reward inappropriate behaviour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It would not be appropriate for us to comment at this stage.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wrong</EXP><DEF>not the right thing or person for a particular job or purpose</DEF><THESEXA>You’re using the wrong spoon – this is the soup spoon.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She was simply the wrong person for the job.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>out of place</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF> not looking or seeming suitable for that place or situation</DEF><THESEXA>The horse drawn carriage looks a little out of place among the busy traffic.</THESEXA><THESEXA>At first I felt a bit out of place.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>incompatible</EXP><DEF>two ideas or things that are incompatible cannot exist or be done together. <Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-97d"><Section><Exponent><EXP>untrue</EXP><GRAM>not usually before noun</GRAM><DEF>not true, because the person does not know the facts, or idea that is false, especially a belief in something good about yourself or about the situation you are in</DEF><THESEXA>Alcohol gives people the illusion of being witty and events/situationsunusualdifferent from what usually happensWe had snow in May, which is very unusual.rarenot happening very often, or existing only in small numbersViolent crimes are rare.Hatton gathered many rare plants from all over the world.exceptionalɪkˈsepʃənəlvery unusual and happening very rarelyNinety-day visas can be extended only in exceptional circumstances.The presence of a jury in a civil trial is now quite exceptional.out of the ordinaryunusual and surprising or specialIt was a small village where nothing out of the ordinary ever seemed to happen.freakextremely unusual and unexpected – used about an accident, storm etcA freak wave wrecked most of the seafront.Their car was crushed by a tree in a freak accident.unprecedentedʌnˈpresədentədif something is unprecedented, it has never happened before – often used about successes and achievementsAn unprecedented number of students have received top grades.This kind of deal is unprecedented.unheard ofif something is unheard of, it has never happened or been done before – used especially when something seems very surprising to people at that timeIn our small town, this kind of crime was almost unheard of.unheard-of luxuries such as electric windows and air-conditioning
people/behaviour/methods etceccentricbehaving in a way that seems rather strange but not frighteningThe house was owned by an eccentric millionaire.eccentric behaviourunconventionalvery different from the way people usually behave, think, dress etc, often in a way that seems interestingHis parents had a rather unconventional lifestyle, and let their children do whatever they pleased.His approach to business may seem unconventional, but he certainly gets results. unorthodoxunorthodox ideas or methods are different from the usual ones, and therefore seem surprising to many peopleHe is known for his unorthodox political views.unorthodox teaching methods

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-91f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>unwilling/not willing</EXP><DEF>not wanting to do something and refusing to do it</DEF><THESEXA>She's unwilling to admit that she was wrong.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Stuart was an unwilling participant in the shooting.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Put  away any toys the child is not willing to share, to avoid any problems.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reluctant</EXP><DEF>not willing to do something, although you may be persuaded after a while. happen</DEF><THESEXA>The president has the right to veto any piece of legislation.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6d3"><Section><Exponent><EXP>wait</EXP><DEF>to stay somewhere or not do something until something else happens, someone arrives etc</DEF><THESEXA>I’ll wait here while you call him.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He said he was waiting for a friend.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hang around</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also </LINKWORD><LEXVAR>hang about</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to wait in a place not doing anything, especially so that you are wasting time</DEF><THESEXA>They kept us hanging around for hours at the hospital.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hold on/hang on</EXP><DEF>to wait because you are hoping that something will happen</DEF><THESEXA>The captain decided it was best to hold on and wait for the other ship to arrive.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We hung on until the very last moment.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>can you hold on/hang on?</EXP><REGISTERLAB>spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used when telling someone to wait</DEF><THESEXA>Can you hang on a minute? I just want to finish this email.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stand by/be on standby</EXP><DEF>to wait and be ready to do something if needed – used especially about soldiers, police, medical teams etc</DEF><THESEXA>The army are standing by.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Emergency services were on standby after someone called to say there was a bomb in the city centre. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>await</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to wait for something – used about something that you know will happen or arrive</DEF><THESEXA>I will await your reply<GLOSS>in a formal letter</GLOSS>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In February, nearly 200,000 prisoners were awaiting trial.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The soldiers awaited the order to advance.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-6c5" fullpage="yes"><Section><Exponent><EXP>walk</EXP><DEF>to move forward by putting one foot in front of the other</DEF><THESEXA>I missed the bus so I decided to walk.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We’ve walked about eight miles today.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wander</EXP><DEF>to walk without any clear purpose or direction</DEF><THESEXA>They wandered around the narrow streets of the old city.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stride</EXP><DEF>to walk with long steps in a determined, confident, or angry way</DEF><THESEXA>A man in a suit came striding purposefully into the hall.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She strode onto the stage and began to address the audience.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pace</EXP><DEF>to walk first in one direction and then in another many times, especially because you are nervous</DEF><THESEXA>Nick was pacing up and down, waiting for the phone to ring.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>march</EXP><DEF>to walk quickly with firm regular steps – used especially about soldiers or someone who is angry</DEF><THESEXA>The troops marched past with smart uniforms and good discipline.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Sheila marched into the office and demanded an apology.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wade</EXP><DEF>to walk through deep water</DEF><THESEXA>We had to wade across the river.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stomp</EXP><DEF>to walk putting your feet down very hard, especially because you are angry</DEF><THESEXA>She turned and stomped off without looking back.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to walk quietly</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>tiptoe</EXP><DEF>to walk quietly and carefully on your toes because you do not want to make a noise</DEF><THESEXA>I tiptoed out trying not to wake the baby.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>creep</EXP><DEF>to walk quietly and slowly because you do not want anyone to see or hear you</DEF><THESEXA>Stella crept up the stairs, hoping not to wake her parents.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sneak</EXP><DEF>to walk quietly so that no one notices you, especially because you are doing something you should not do</DEF><THESEXA>They sneaked off without paying.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I quickly sneaked out to have a cigarette.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>pad</EXP><DEF>to walk quietly without wearing shoes – also used about cats and dogs walking quietly</DEF><THESEXA>Michelle got up and padded barefoot down to the kitchen.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The cat padded in, asking for her food.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to walk slowly</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>trudge</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>trʌdʒ</PRON></PronCodes><DEF>to walk in a slow tired way because it is difficult to continue walking, or you do not want to go somewhere</DEF><THESEXA>The men trudged along the road, heads bent against the wind.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I’ve spent hours trudging around the shops looking for a present.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>plod</EXP><DEF>to walk slowly in a tired way – often used about a horse, donkey etc</DEF><THESEXA>The donkey was plodding slowly along under its heavy load.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I plodded on growing thirstier and hungrier.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>shuffle</EXP><DEF>to walk very slowly and noisily without lifting your feet off the ground</DEF><THESEXA>The old man got up and shuffled to the door.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to walk with difficulty</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>limp</EXP><DEF>to walk with difficulty because one leg hurts, so that you put most of your weight on the other leg</DEF><THESEXA>Jake was limping because of the injury to his knee.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stagger</EXP><DEF>to walk or move unsteadily, almost falling over, especially because you are drunk or have been injured</DEF><THESEXA>They finally staggered back to the hotel at 4 o'clock in the morning.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He hit her and she staggered and fell.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hobble</EXP><DEF>to walk with difficulty in a slow and unsteady way because your legs or feet hurt or have been injured</DEF><THESEXA>My new shoes were so painful I could only hobble along.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She hobbled out to the car on crutches.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>to walk for pleasure</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>take a walk</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>go for a walk</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to walk somewhere for pleasure</DEF><THESEXA>We went for a walk in the park.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stroll</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>go for a stroll</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to walk in a relaxed way, especially for pleasure</DEF><THESEXA>People were strolling along beside the river.</THESEXA><THESEXA>On Sunday, they went for a stroll in the park.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hike</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>go hiking</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to walk a long way in the mountains or countryside as an activity you enjoy</DEF><THESEXA>We’re going hiking in Scotland this summer.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They hiked around the Lake District.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Pat likes doing active things like hiking and horse-riding.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trek</EXP><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>go trekking</LEXVAR></Variant><DEF>to go for a walk lasting several days or weeks in a faraway place, carrying your clothes with you</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve always wanted to go trekking in Nepal.</THESEXA><THESEXA>They trekked up to Everest Base Camp.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-69a"><Section><Exponent><EXP>walk</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a journey that you make on foot, especially for exercise or enjoyment</DEF><THESEXA>I took the dog out for a long walk in the fresh air.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We went for some lovely walks.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you fancy going out for a walk?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hike</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a long walk in the mountains or countryside</DEF><THESEXA>We went for a hike in the woods.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There are some good hikes nearby.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>stroll</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>singular</GRAM><DEF>a slow, relaxed walk</DEF><THESEXA>Let’s take a stroll in the park.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We <COLLOINEXA>went for a stroll </COLLOINEXA>along the river.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>wander</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>a short relaxed walk, especially to look around a place</DEF><THESEXA>We <COLLOINEXA>had a wander round</COLLOINEXA> the town and then went to the beach.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>trek</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>singular</GRAM><DEF>used when talking about a long walk in the mountains, countryside etc which lasts for several days and which you do for pleasure. Also used about a long tiring walk somewhere, which you do not want to have to do </DEF><THESEXA>They <COLLOINEXA>went on a </COLLOINEXA>three week <COLLOINEXA>trek</COLLOINEXA> in the Atlas Mountains.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We then had a long trek back to our hotel with all our luggage.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>slog</EXP><GRAM>singular</GRAM><DEF>a long, tiring, and unpleasant walk, which continues for several hours</DEF><THESEXA>It was a dreary slog over bleak and windswept hills. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>march</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>an occasion when a group of people walk somewhere together, in order to protest about something</DEF><THESEXA>Demonstrators are planning a march through the capital.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a peace march </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-67f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>wall</EXP><DEF>an upright flat structure made of stone or brick, that divides one area from another or surrounds an area</DEF><THESEXA>The estate is surrounded by high stone walls.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a brick wall</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fence</EXP><DEF>a structure made of wood, metal etc that surrounds a piece of land</DEF><THESEXA>The garden was surrounded by an old wooden fence.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the chain link fence around the school</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>railings</EXP><DEF>a metal fence that is made of a series of upright bars</DEF><THESEXA>the iron railings in front of the house</THESEXA><THESEXA>The boy was leaning over the railing on the side of the boat.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>barrier</EXP><DEF>a type of fence or gate that prevents people from moving in a particular direction</DEF><THESEXA>A guard stood near the barrier.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The police had put up barriers to keep the crowd under control.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>screen</EXP><DEF>a piece of furniture like a thin wall that can be moved around and is used to divide one part of a room from another</DEF><THESEXA>the screen around his hospital bed</THESEXA><THESEXA>a Japanese bamboo screen</THESEXA><THESEXA>a fire screen <GLOSS>that you put near a fire</GLOSS></THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>partition</EXP><DEF>a thin wall that separates one part of a room from another</DEF><THESEXA>The room was divided into two by a thin partition.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The offices are separated by partitions and you can hear everything that is said in the next office.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>barricade</EXP><DEF>a line of objects that people have put across a road, to prevent people getting past, especially as part of a protest</DEF><THESEXA>The soldiers used tanks to smash through the barricades.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.208f4291.11b208d24ec.-6aec"><Section><Exponent><EXP>war</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a situation in which there is fighting between countries or opposing groups within a country, with large numbers of soldiers and weapons</DEF><THESEXA>He fought in World War II.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the horrors of war</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conflict</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>a situation in which there is fighting or a war – used especially in news reports</DEF><THESEXA>the conflict in the Middle East</THESEXA><THESEXA>There is increasing danger of armed conflict.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>fighting</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>a situation in which people or groups fight each other and try to kill each other</DEF><THESEXA>The fighting went on for months.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Fighting in the north has resulted in hundreds of deaths.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hostilities</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>plural</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>fighting in a war</DEF><THESEXA>The agreement called on the guerrillas to <COLLOINEXA>cease hostilities</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>stop fighting</GLOSS> and begin peace talks.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>warfare</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>the activity of fighting in a war – used especially to talk about a method of fighting</DEF><THESEXA>new and more advanced methods of warfare</THESEXA><THESEXA>chemical warfare</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>battle</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C,U</GRAM><DEF>an occasion when two armies, groups of ships etc fight each other in one place during a war</DEF><THESEXA>the great naval battles of the Napoleonic Wars</THESEXA><THESEXA>the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805</THESEXA><THESEXA>He died in battle.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>skirmish</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>ˈskɜːmɪʃ</PRON></PronCodes><POS>n</POS><GRAM>C</GRAM><DEF>a short fight between small groups of soldiers, ships etc, especially one that happens away from the main part of a war or battle</DEF><THESEXA>There were minor skirmishes between Indian and Pakistani troops across the border.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>combat</EXP><POS>n</POS><GRAM>U</GRAM><DEF>the act of fighting, especially during a war</DEF><THESEXA>Few of them had any experience of action</COLLOINEXA>.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her grandfather <COLLOINEXA>saw action</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>fought</GLOSS> in two world wars.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-61b"><Section><Exponent><EXP>warn</EXP><DEF>to tell someone about something bad or dangerous that might happen, so that they can avoid it or prevent it</DEF><THESEXA>I warned you about sitting out in the sun too long.</THESEXA><THESEXA>We were warned that there could be delays on the motorway, so we took another route.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>give sb a warning</EXP><DEF>to tell someone that if they continue to behave in an unsatisfactory way, they will be punished</DEF><THESEXA>He’s already been given several warnings about handing in his essays late.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The US gave a warning that if the hostages were not released, it would be forced to take military action.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>alert</EXP><DEF>to officially or publicly warn people of possible danger so that they can prevent it or be ready to deal with it</DEF><THESEXA>a campaign to alert people to the dangers of smoking</THESEXA><THESEXA>An anonymous caller alerted the police that a bomb was due to go off.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tip sb off</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to secretly warn someone about something that is going to happen – used especially about warning the police about a crime</DEF><THESEXA>The police found the drugs after being tipped off by local residents.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Informants tipped the FBI off.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>caution</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to warn someone to do or not to do something in order to avoid a dangerous or bad result</DEF><THESEXA>People are being cautioned against using credit cards abroad, in case of fraud.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Health officials have cautioned the public to wash fruit thoroughly before eating it.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>forewarn</EXP><PronCodes><PRON>fɔːˈwɔːn </PRON><AMEVARPRON>fɔːrˈwɔːrn</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><GRAM>usually passive</GRAM><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to warn someone about something that is going to happen, so that they are expecting it or ready for it</DEF><THESEXA>We had been forewarned that the roads weren’t very good. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5e4"><Section><Exponent><EXP>warning</EXP><DEF>something that you say or do to tell people about danger, or to tell them not to do something</DEF><THESEXA>All cigarette packets carry a government health warning.</THESEXA><THESEXA>She ignored her parents' warnings.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The army issued a warning that anyone who was out on the streets after dark was likely to be shot.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>caution</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an official warning or a piece of advice telling you to be careful</DEF><THESEXA>Caution: do not install electrical equipment near or around water sources.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The video begins with a caution that you must do some warm-up exercises first. </THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>tip-off</EXP><REGISTERLAB>informal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a warning that someone is about to do something, especially one given to the police about a crime</DEF><THESEXA>Police were called to the hotel after a tip-off.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>alert</EXP><DEF>a warning to be ready for possible danger that may happen soon</DEF><THESEXA>Twelve flood alerts have been issued to areas along the River Severn.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a fire alert</THESEXA><THESEXA>The ambulance services were <COLLOINEXA>on red alert</COLLOINEXA><GLOSS>they were ready to take action immediately</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>advisory</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>an official warning or notice that gives information about a dangerous situation</DEF><THESEXA>The air pollution gets so bad on some days that health advisories are posted at park entrances.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>caveat</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>a warning that something may not be completely true, effective etc. Also used when pointing out that it is important to remember something</DEF><THESEXA>The woman was offered treatment, but with the caveat that it had only a 30% chance of success.</THESEXA><THESEXA>One caveat is that you must take the goods back to the shop within 14 days. </THESEXA><THESEXA>There is one important caveat to this argument. </THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-5c6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>wash</EXP><DEF>to clean something with soap and water</DEF><THESEXA>Our car needs washing.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Make sure that you wash your hands.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>do the washing</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LEXVAR>do the laundry</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to wash clothes that need to be washed</DEF><THESEXA>Did you do the laundry this morning?</THESEXA><THESEXA>I do the washing on Wednesdays and Saturdays.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>do the washing up</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><Variant><LINKWORD>also</LINKWORD><LEXVAR>wash up</LEXVAR><GEO>BrE</GEO></Variant><Variant><LEXVAR>do the dishes</LEXVAR><GEO>AmE</GEO></Variant><DEF>to wash all the cups, plates, knives etc that you have used during a meal</DEF><THESEXA>If you do the cooking tonight, I’ll do the washing up.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Who’s going to do the dishes?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>cleanse</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to make something completely clean, especially using a special substance</DEF><THESEXA>Carefully cleanse the cut to get rid of any grit or dirt.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>rinse</EXP><DEF>to wash something with water in order to remove soap or dirt</DEF><THESEXA>I’ll just rinse the lettuce under the tap.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>scrub</EXP><DEF>to make something very clean, using a stiff brush and water, or soap and water</DEF><THESEXA>Lou was on her knees, scrubbing the kitchen floor.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>mop</EXP><DEF>to wash a floor with a wet <NonDV><REFHWD>mop</REFHWD></NonDV><GLOSS>special stick with thick threads on the end</GLOSS></DEF><THESEXA>A cleaner mopped the floor between the beds.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-58f"><Section><Exponent><EXP>watch</EXP><DEF>to look at someone or something for a period of time and pay attention to what is happening</DEF><THESEXA>They were all watching the game.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I watched him get out of his car. </THESEXA><THESEXA>We are watching carefully to see how the situation develops.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>keep an eye on sb/sth</EXP><DEF>to watch someone or something carefully so that you are prepared if anything bad happens</DEF><THESEXA>The doctors are keeping a careful eye on her.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Can you keep an eye on my bag for me?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Keep an eye on house prices before you decide to sell.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>observe</EXP><DEF>to watch someone or something carefully in order to learn more about them</DEF><THESEXA>A 24-hour camera is being used to observe the birds’ behaviour.</THESEXA><THESEXA>During your teacher training you will be encouraged to observe lessons.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>monitor</EXP><DEF>to watch a situation carefully to see how it changes over a period of time</DEF><THESEXA>Class teachers are responsible for monitoring the progress of each student.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Economists are monitoring the situation carefully.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>spy on sb</EXP><DEF>to watch someone secretly in order to find out what they are doing</DEF><THESEXA>He hired a private detective to spy on her.</THESEXA><THESEXA>In the 1960s, government agencies were spying on everybody.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>someone who watches something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>spectator</EXP><DEF>someone who watches an event, especially a sports event</DEF><THESEXA>There were 4,500 spectators at the game.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a crowd of spectators</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>viewer</EXP><DEF>someone who watches television</DEF><THESEXA>Millions of television viewers listened to the president’s speech.</THESEXA><THESEXA>programmes for younger viewers</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>audience</EXP><DEF>the people who watch a play or performance, or the people who watch a particular television programme</DEF><THESEXA>The audience roared with laughter and clapped.</THESEXA><THESEXA>It attracted a television audience of seven million.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>onlooker</EXP><DEF>someone who watches something happening without being involved in it, especially in the street</DEF><THESEXA>A man was standing on the roof, watched by a crowd of onlookers below.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>observer</EXP><DEF>someone who watches and pays attention to particular situations and events, because they are interested in them, or it is their job</DEF><THESEXA>She was a shrewd observer of human nature.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a political observer who writes for The Independent newspaper</THESEXA><THESEXA>The United Nations has sent military observers to the Sudan.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-53d"><Section><SECHEADING>a way of doing something</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>way</EXP><DEF>something you can do in order to achieve what you want or deal with a problem</DEF><THESEXA>Visiting a country is a great way to learn a language.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a good way to lose weight</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>method</EXP><DEF>a way of doing something, especially one that a lot of people know about and use</DEF><THESEXA>They still use traditional methods of farming.</THESEXA><THESEXA>modern teaching methods</THESEXA><THESEXA>Different research methods are used to gather data.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>approach</EXP><DEF>a general way of dealing with a particular problem or situation, especially a way that has been carefully thought about</DEF><THESEXA>We need a whole new approach to environmental issues.</THESEXA><THESEXA>There will be considerable advantages to adopting this approach.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>technique</EXP><DEF>a way of doing something for which you need a skill that must be learned and practised</DEF><THESEXA>I went to a class to learn relaxation techniques.</THESEXA><THESEXA>new surgical techniques</THESEXA><THESEXA>techniques for improving staff performance</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>strategy</EXP><DEF>a carefully planned way to achieve something difficult or complicated that may take a long time</DEF><THESEXA>They met to discuss the company’s business strategy.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the government’s long-term strategy for reducing crime</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>how to go to a place</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>way</EXP><DEF>the road, path, direction etc that you must take in order to get to a place</DEF><THESEXA>Are you sure this is the right way to the sea?</THESEXA><THESEXA>Will you come with me? I don’t know the way.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>route</EXP><DEF>a way from one place to another that people use regularly or that is shown on a map</DEF><THESEXA>There are two routes we could take but this is the quickest one.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the overland trade route between Europe and China</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>directions</EXP><DEF>instructions on how to get to a place</DEF><THESEXA>Let’s stop and ask someone for directions.</THESEXA><THESEXA>If you follow these directions you’ll have no problem finding the house.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>short cut</EXP><DEF>a way of getting somewhere that is shorter than the usual way</DEF><THESEXA>Let’s take a short cut across the field.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Taxi-drivers know all the short cuts.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>how to get to ...</EXP><REGISTERLAB>especially spoken</REGISTERLAB><DEF>used especially when you ask someone to tell you which is the right way</DEF><THESEXA>Can you tell me how to get to Grand Central Station?</THESEXA><THESEXA>It was getting dark and I wasn’t sure how to get home.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4ce"><Section><SECHEADING>not physically strong</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>weak</EXP><DEF>not physically strong, sometimes because you baby</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>delicate</EXP><DEF>weak and often becoming ill easily</DEF><THESEXA>a delicate child</THESEXA><THESEXA>She had rather a <COLLOINEXA>delicate guests.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>malnourished</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>weak or ill because you have not had enough good food to eat</DEF><THESEXA>Half a million people there are severely malnourished.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The organization provides emergency feeding for malnourished children.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>likely to etc</DEF><THESEXA>a rickety old bicycle</THESEXA><THESEXA>He lived in a rickety hut on the beach for several years.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-4b1"><Section><Exponent><EXP>weather</EXP><DEF>use this to talk about whether it is hot or cold outside or whether it is raining, snowing, windy etc</DEF><THESEXA>What was the weather like on your vacation?</THESEXA><THESEXA>a period of warm sunny weather</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>climate</EXP><DEF>the usual weather conditions in a particular country or area</DEF><THESEXA>Queensland has a warm tropical climate.</THESEXA><THESEXA>the climate of southern Florida</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the outlook</EXP><DEF>what the weather will probably be like for the next few days</DEF><THESEXA>The outlook for the weekend is for continued sunny weather.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>conditions</EXP><DEF>the weather at a particular time, especially when considering how this will affect a planned event or activity</DEF><THESEXA>Conditions are perfect for today’s boat race.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Freezing conditions are making the roads extremely hazardous.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>the elements</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>weather, especially bad weather</DEF><THESEXA>The equipment had been left exposed to the elements.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-484"><Section><Exponent><EXP>wedding</EXP><DEF>a ceremony in which two people get married, especially one with a religious service</DEF><THESEXA>We had our wedding in the local church.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a registry office wedding</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>marriage</EXP><DEF>a wedding. <DEFBOLD>Marriage</DEFBOLD> is less common and more formal than <DEFBOLD>wedding</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>Their marriage will take place in Westminster Abbey.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>reception</EXP><DEF>a large formal meal or party after a wedding</DEF><THESEXA>Where will you be having your reception?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>honeymoon</EXP><DEF>a holiday taken by two people who have just got married</DEF><THESEXA>We’re going to Barbados for our honeymoon.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-458" fullpage="yes"><Section><Exponent><EXP>wet</EXP><DEF>covered in water or another liquid</DEF><THESEXA>I’ve just washed my hair and it’s still wet.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You’d better change out of those wet clothes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>damp</EXP><DEF>slightly wet</DEF><THESEXA>Wipe the surfaces with a damp became moist<GLOSS>she was almost crying</GLOSS>.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>clammy</EXP><DEF>feeling slightly wet, cold, and sticky – used about someone’s skin, especially when they are underfoot.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>humid/muggy</EXP><DEF>used when the weather is hot but the air feels wet in a way that makes you uncomfortable</DEF><THESEXA>Summers in Tokyo are hot and humid.</THESEXA><THESEXA>a hot muggy day</THESEXA><THESEXA>the humid heat of a tropical forest</THESEXA></Exponent></Section><Section><SECHEADING>very wet</SECHEADING><Exponent><EXP>soaked</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>very wet all the way through – used especially about people and their clothes</DEF><THESEXA>It absolutely poured with rain and we got soaked.</THESEXA><THESEXA>His shirt was soaked with blood.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>drenched</EXP><GRAM>not before noun</GRAM><DEF>very wet – used about a person or area after a lot of rain or water has fallen on them</DEF><THESEXA>Everyone got drenched when a huge wave hit the boat.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The garden was completely drenched after the rain.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>saturated</EXP><DEF>extremely wet, and unable to take in any more water or liquid</DEF><THESEXA>His bandage was saturated with blood.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The -lɑːɡd</AMEVARPRON></PronCodes><DEF>used about ground that has water on its surface because it is so wet that it cannot take in any more</DEF><THESEXA>The game was cancelled because the field was waterlogged.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>sodden</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>very wet with water – used about clothes and the ground. <DEFBOLD>Sodden</DEFBOLD> is less common than <DEFBOLD>soaked</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>The ground was still sodden.</THESEXA><THESEXA>He took off his sodden shirt.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-43c"><Section><Exponent><EXP>wet</EXP><DEF>to put water or another liquid onto something to make it wet. In spoken English, people will often use <DEFBOLD>get something wet</DEFBOLD> rather than <DEFBOLD>wet</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>He wet the washcloth and washed Tom’s face.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>splash</EXP><DEF>to make someone or something wet by making a lot of small drops of water fall onto them</DEF><THESEXA>The kids were playing around in the pool, splashing each other.</THESEXA><THESEXA>I accidentally splashed soup onto my shirt.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>soak</EXP><DEF>to put something in water for a long time or to make something very wet – use this especially when something is put into water or the water comes up from underneath to make it wet</DEF><THESEXA>Soak the beans overnight before cooking.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The rain had come in through the bottom of our tent and completely soaked our clothes.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>drench</EXP><DEF>to make someone or something extremely wet with a large amount of water – use this especially when water is poured or falls on something</DEF><THESEXA>He drenched us all with the hose.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Her shirt was drenched in sweat.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>saturate</EXP><REGISTERLAB>formal</REGISTERLAB><DEF>to completely cover or fill something with liquid, so that it is wet all the way through</DEF><THESEXA>Heavy rains had saturated the ground.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>flood</EXP><DEF>to cover an area of land with a large amount of water</DEF><THESEXA>Farmers flood the fields in order to grow rice.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>moisten</EXP><DEF>to make something slightly wet by putting a small amount of water or another liquid on it, especially to stop it from getting too dry</DEF><THESEXA>Add just enough water to moisten the cake mixture.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Tom paused and moistened his lips.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>dampen</EXP><DEF>to make something slightly wet by putting a little water on it</DEF><THESEXA>Rain came in through the window, dampening the curtains.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3ff"><Section><Exponent><EXP>when</EXP><DEF>at or during the time that something happens</DEF><THESEXA>Call me when you get home.</THESEXA><THESEXA>When I got here, the place was empty.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>while</EXP><DEF>during the time that something is happening – used to emphasize that something is continuing</DEF><THESEXA>I’ll make the salad while you set the table.</THESEXA><THESEXA>While we were on holiday, our house was burgled.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>whenever</EXP><DEF>used in order to emphasize that something always happens when another thing happens</DEF><THESEXA>He visits his mother whenever he can.</THESEXA><THESEXA>You can come and talk to me whenever you have a problem.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>by the time</EXP><DEF> used in order to say that one thing has or will have already happened when something else happens</DEF><THESEXA>By the time a child is five, he will have watched hundreds of hours of television.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3f0"><Section><Exponent><EXP>while</EXP><DEF>during the time that you are doing something, or something is happening</DEF><THESEXA>I bought a magazine while I was waiting for the train.</THESEXA><THESEXA>While we were on holiday, my bag was stolen.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>meanwhile</EXP><DEF>at the same time as something else is happening</DEF><THESEXA>They’re still working on our bedroom. Meanwhile, we’re sleeping downstairs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>The Russians, meanwhile, declared war on August 8.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>in the meantime</EXP><DEF>during the period of time between now and a future event, or between two events in the past</DEF><THESEXA>More aid is expected soon, but in the meantime these people are going hungry.</THESEXA><THESEXA>My new job hadn’t started, so in the meantime I tried to write a book.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>whilst</EXP><GEO>BrE</GEO><DEF>while. <DEFBOLD>Whilst</DEFBOLD> sounds a little more formal than <DEFBOLD>while</DEFBOLD></DEF><THESEXA>One American plane was forced to land whilst flying over North Korea.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Whilst all this was going on, the performers were getting ready for the show.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3d9"><Section><Exponent><EXP>wig</EXP><DEF>an object covered with artificial hair that you wear on your head</DEF><THESEXA>In England judges still have to wear wigs.</THESEXA><THESEXA>Do you think it's real, or is it wig?</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>toupée</EXP><DEF>an area of artificial hair, used to cover a <NonDV><REFHWD>bald</REFHWD> </NonDV> part of your head</DEF><THESEXA>The singer used to wear a toupée, and then decided to shave all his hair off.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>hairpiece</EXP><DEF>a wig or a piece of false hair that you wear on your head to make your own hair look thicker</DEF><THESEXA>Unfortunately the hairpiece didn't quite match the colour of her hair.</THESEXA></Exponent><Exponent><EXP>extensions</EXP><DEF>long pieces of artificial hair that can be attached to your hair to make it look longer</DEF><THESEXA>I'm thinking of having extensions because my hair gets to a certain length and then becomes so thin that you can see through it.</THESEXA></Exponent></Section></ThesBox></Thesaurus>

<Thesaurus><ThesBox id="u2fc098491a42200a.262cc60a.117ee7c0f66.-3b6"><Section><Exponent><EXP>wind</EXP><DEF>air moving in a current, especially strongly or quickly</DEF><THESEXA>A cold wind was
